_comment: Vetted 'gold' case seeds authored by Claude Fable 5 for clinical fidelity. Loaded instantly by the sim (no model call); the bedside engine then runs dynamically on the local/cheap model using each seed's hidden 'context'. Colitis seeds are grounded in EBMedicine 'Colitis in the Adolescent Population' (Oct 2025): infectious colitis (IDSA stool panel), Shiga-toxin E. coli/HUS, C. difficile/pseudomembranous & fulminant disease, new-onset IBD/toxic megacolon, and STI proctitis (flagged there as a commonly-missed diagnosis).

cases.title: STI proctitis in an adolescent (the commonly-missed one)
cases.chiefComplaint: 16F — rectal bleeding, mucus, and pain; hidden sexual history
cases.diagnosis: Sexually transmitted infectious proctitis (Neisseria gonorrhoeae / Chlamydia trachomatis, consider LGV)
cases.context: 16-year-old girl with 10 days of rectal pain, mucoid and bloody rectal discharge, tenesmus, and painful defecation. The true diagnosis is sexually transmitted infectious proctitis from Neisseria gonorrhoeae and/or Chlamydia trachomatis (lymphogranuloma venereum is possible with severe proctocolitis). This is the article's flagged COMMONLY-MISSED diagnosis: it is missed when the clinician does not obtain a confidential, non-judgmental sexual history away from the parent. She is sexually active (receptive anal intercourse) but will not volunteer this in front of her mother, who is at the bedside; she WILL disclose it if interviewed privately and asked directly and supportively. RED HERRINGS: a family history of Crohn disease and the bloody mucoid stool strongly suggest new-onset inflammatory bowel disease, and infectious colitis is also on the differential — the learner must not anchor on IBD and must exclude STI. CORRECT PATH: obtain a private sexual history, perform an anorectal exam, send rectal/vaginal NAAT for gonorrhea and chlamydia (and HIV/syphilis screening), test/treat empirically for GC+chlamydia (ceftriaxone + doxycycline), counsel on partner treatment and reporting, and consider LGV if severe. She is hemodynamically stable and NOT septic; the teaching is diagnostic, not resuscitative. If the learner never interviews her privately, she stays guarded, the workup is misdirected toward IBD, and the STI is missed — surface the mother interjecting and the patient's evasiveness as pressure. If the learner does IBD workup that is fine as exclusion but the case is only 'solved' when STI is identified and treated.
criticalActions: Obtain a confidential sexual history with the parent stepped out of the room
Perform an anorectal/genital examination
Send NAAT for Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis (rectal/genital)
Screen for co-infections (HIV, syphilis)
Treat empirically for gonorrhea and chlamydia (ceftriaxone + doxycycline)
Consider lymphogranuloma venereum if severe proctocolitis
Counsel on partner notification/treatment and confidentiality
Avoid anchoring prematurely on inflammatory bowel disease
learningPoints: STI proctitis is a commonly-missed cause of adolescent proctocolitis — a confidential sexual history is the key diagnostic step.
Interview adolescents privately; disclosure of sexual activity often will not happen in front of a parent.
Rectal NAAT for GC/chlamydia plus empiric ceftriaxone + doxycycline; screen for HIV/syphilis and consider LGV.
A family history of IBD is a classic red herring — exclude STI before committing to an IBD diagnosis.
cases.intro: Room 4. A 16-year-old girl sits on the stretcher with her arms crossed, her mother close beside her answering most of the questions. The triage note reads: 10 days of rectal pain, blood and mucus with bowel movements. The girl avoids eye contact and gives short answers; her mother volunteers that there is Crohn disease in the family and she is worried it is the same thing.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's stable — vitals are fine. Mom's done most of the talking so far.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: Her uncle has Crohn's. Could she have that? She's had blood every time she goes.

cases.title: Shiga-toxin E. coli colitis evolving to HUS
cases.chiefComplaint: 14M — bloody diarrhea after a cookout, now pale and making less urine
cases.diagnosis: Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (O157:H7) colitis with evolving hemolytic uremic syndrome
cases.context: 14-year-old boy, 5 days of crampy abdominal pain and progressively BLOODY diarrhea beginning 3 days after a family cookout with undercooked ground beef (a sibling was mildly ill). The true diagnosis is Shiga toxin-producing E. coli (STEC/O157:H7) colitis now EVOLVING INTO HEMOLYTIC UREMIC SYNDROME (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + acute kidney injury). Early clues to HUS: pallor, decreasing urine output, mild edema, rising creatinine, dropping platelets, schistocytes on smear, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin. THE CRITICAL TEACHING POINT: in suspected STEC, ANTIBIOTICS and ANTIMOTILITY agents INCREASE the risk of HUS and are contraindicated — a well-meaning learner who reaches for a fluoroquinolone/azithromycin or loperamide is making the key error. CORRECT PATH: send stool for Shiga toxin/O157 (per IDSA panel: Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia, C diff, STEC), send CBC with smear, BMP/creatinine, LDH, haptoglobin, and urinalysis to screen for HUS; give careful ISOTONIC IV fluids with strict input/output monitoring (judicious volume support is nephroprotective); AVOID antibiotics and antimotility agents; consult nephrology and admit for HUS monitoring. DETERIORATION: if antibiotics or antimotility are given, or if he is under-resuscitated, the HUS worsens — platelets fall, creatinine climbs, he becomes oliguric, hypertensive, and encephalopathic. Correct supportive care stabilizes him.
criticalActions: Send stool studies including Shiga toxin / E. coli O157 (IDSA bloody-diarrhea panel)
Send CBC with peripheral smear, BMP/creatinine, LDH, haptoglobin, urinalysis to screen for HUS
Recognize evolving HUS (anemia + thrombocytopenia + acute kidney injury)
AVOID antibiotics in suspected Shiga toxin-producing E. coli
AVOID antimotility agents (e.g., loperamide)
Give careful isotonic IV fluids with strict input/output monitoring
Consult nephrology and admit for HUS monitoring
learningPoints: In suspected STEC (O157:H7), antibiotics AND antimotility agents increase HUS risk — do not give them.
HUS = microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + AKI; screen with smear, platelets, creatinine, LDH, haptoglobin.
Judicious isotonic volume support is nephroprotective in evolving HUS; monitor urine output closely.
Bloody diarrhea warrants the IDSA stool panel including Shiga toxin testing.
cases.intro: A 14-year-old boy lies curled on the stretcher, pale, with dark rings under his eyes. His mother reports five days of worsening belly cramps and diarrhea that turned bloody two days ago; today he seems washed out and has barely needed the bathroom to urinate. She mentions a weekend cookout — the burgers were 'a little pink' — and that his younger sister had a brief tummy bug afterward.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: The diarrhea's all blood now. But what scares me is he looks so pale today, and he's hardly peed since this morning.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Heart rate's up around 116, he's tachy. Want me to draw labs and send a stool study?

cases.title: C. difficile / pseudomembranous colitis after antibiotics
cases.chiefComplaint: 17M — profuse diarrhea and distension weeks after antibiotics for acne
cases.diagnosis: Clostridioides difficile (pseudomembranous) colitis, progressing toward fulminant disease
cases.context: 17-year-old boy with a week of profuse, foul, partly bloody diarrhea, crampy pain, fever, and now abdominal distension. He finished a prolonged course of oral clindamycin for severe acne about three weeks ago (recent antibiotic exposure is the key history — elicit it). The true diagnosis is Clostridioides difficile / pseudomembranous colitis, now showing early features of FULMINANT disease (the article defines fulminant C diff by hypotension/shock, ileus, or toxic megacolon). Warning signs to detect: rising WBC (may be markedly elevated), lactate, low albumin, abdominal distension with reduced bowel sounds, and colonic dilation on imaging. CORRECT PATH: elicit recent antibiotic use; send C difficile testing (PCR/toxin) as part of the stool workup; STOP the offending/unnecessary antibiotic; start ORAL VANCOMYCIN (or fidaxomicin) — not metronidazole first-line for severe disease; assess for fulminant colitis/toxic megacolon (serial abdominal exam, lactate, WBC, plain film or CT); AVOID antimotility agents (they precipitate ileus/megacolon); place contact precautions; resuscitate and admit, with early surgical consultation if fulminant/megacolon. DETERIORATION: antimotility agents, missed megacolon, or delayed vancomycin push him toward toxic megacolon and shock (distension, systemic toxicity, hypotension). Appropriate therapy and monitoring stabilize him.
criticalActions: Elicit recent antibiotic exposure (clindamycin course for acne)
Send Clostridioides difficile stool testing (PCR/toxin)
Start oral vancomycin (or fidaxomicin) for C. difficile colitis
Stop the offending/unnecessary antibiotic
Assess for fulminant disease / toxic megacolon (exam, lactate, WBC, abdominal imaging)
Avoid antimotility agents
Institute contact precautions
Resuscitate, admit, and involve surgery early if fulminant/megacolon
learningPoints: Recent antibiotic use plus worsening diarrhea should prompt C. difficile testing and treatment.
Severe C. diff is treated with oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin — not metronidazole first-line.
Fulminant C. diff = hypotension/shock, ileus, or toxic megacolon; screen for it and involve surgery.
Avoid antimotility agents in colitis — they can precipitate ileus and toxic megacolon.
cases.intro: A 17-year-old boy on the stretcher looks unwell and clammy, one hand on his distended abdomen. He describes a week of near-constant watery, foul-smelling diarrhea streaked with blood, cramping, and fevers. When asked about recent medications he shrugs — 'just some antibiotics my dermatologist gave me for my skin a few weeks ago.'

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I can't stop going to the bathroom, and now my stomach's all swollen and tight. I feel awful.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's febrile at 38.6 and tachycardic. Belly looks distended — want imaging and labs?

cases.title: New-onset ulcerative colitis with toxic megacolon risk
cases.chiefComplaint: 15F — weeks of bloody diarrhea and weight loss, now distended and toxic
cases.diagnosis: New-onset ulcerative colitis presenting as a severe flare with evolving toxic megacolon
cases.context: 15-year-old girl with 5 weeks of bloody diarrhea (up to 8-10 stools/day), crampy abdominal pain, fatigue, and 4 kg weight loss, now with worsening abdominal distension, fever, and tachycardia. The true diagnosis is NEW-ONSET ULCERATIVE COLITIS presenting as a severe flare with EVOLVING TOXIC MEGACOLON (a medical and potential surgical emergency that presents with vital-sign changes including fever and tachycardia). Expected findings: anemia, elevated ESR/CRP, low albumin, hypokalemia, and colonic dilation on plain film/CT. CORRECT PATH: recognize severe colitis with systemic toxicity; resuscitate and correct anemia/electrolytes; obtain abdominal imaging to assess for toxic megacolon; SEND STOOL STUDIES to exclude an infectious cause (including C diff and STEC) before/with immunosuppression; consult pediatric GI and surgery urgently; start IV corticosteroids for the IBD flare once infection is excluded or covered; keep NPO; and AVOID antimotility agents, opioids, and anticholinergics, which can precipitate megacolon. RED HERRINGS: severe infectious colitis can mimic this, so steroids must not be given blindly without excluding infection; the chronic weeks-long course, weight loss, and anemia point to IBD. DETERIORATION: antimotility/opioids/anticholinergics, a missed megacolon, or delayed resuscitation worsen distension and toxicity toward perforation. Correct resuscitation, imaging, and specialist involvement stabilize her.
criticalActions: Recognize severe colitis with systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, anemia)
Obtain abdominal imaging to assess for toxic megacolon
Send stool studies to exclude infectious causes (incl. C. diff, STEC) before immunosuppression
Resuscitate and correct anemia and electrolyte abnormalities (e.g., hypokalemia)
Consult pediatric GI and surgery urgently
Start IV corticosteroids for the IBD flare once infection is excluded/covered
Keep the patient NPO
Avoid antimotility agents, opioids, and anticholinergics
learningPoints: A chronic (weeks-long) course of bloody diarrhea with weight loss and anemia in an adolescent suggests new-onset IBD.
Toxic megacolon is a medical/surgical emergency — image for colonic dilation and involve surgery early.
Exclude infectious colitis (stool studies incl. C. diff) before starting immunosuppression.
Avoid antimotility agents, opioids, and anticholinergics — they can precipitate toxic megacolon.
cases.intro: A thin, tired-looking 15-year-old girl lies on the stretcher, knees drawn up. Her father says she has had bloody diarrhea for over a month — lately eight or more times a day — and has lost weight and stopped going to school. Today her belly is swollen and she spiked a fever; she looks exhausted and her heart is racing.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: This has been going on for weeks — blood every time, and she's lost so much weight. Today her stomach blew up and she's burning up.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's tachy at 124, febrile, looks pale. Her abdomen is distended and tender.

cases.title: Diabetic ketoacidosis
cases.chiefComplaint: 18M — polyuria, vomiting, abdominal pain, Kussmaul breathing
cases.diagnosis: Diabetic ketoacidosis (new-onset type 1 diabetes)
cases.context: 18-year-old with several days of polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, then vomiting, diffuse abdominal pain, and deep rapid breathing; this is NEW-ONSET type 1 diabetes presenting in diabetic ketoacidosis. Expected: glucose markedly elevated (e.g., 480), high anion-gap metabolic acidosis (pH ~7.10, low bicarbonate), positive serum/urine ketones, dehydration, and a normal-to-elevated measured potassium that will FALL sharply once insulin starts (total-body potassium is depleted). CORRECT PATH and its ORDER matter: (1) start isotonic IV FLUID resuscitation first; (2) CHECK POTASSIUM before insulin and replete it — if K < 3.3, HOLD insulin and give potassium first, or insulin will cause dangerous hypokalemia and arrhythmia; (3) start an insulin INFUSION (no IV bolus, particularly in younger patients); (4) add DEXTROSE to fluids as glucose falls toward ~250 so you can continue insulin to close the anion gap; (5) identify the precipitant (new diagnosis, infection, missed insulin); (6) monitor glucose/electrolytes frequently and watch for cerebral edema — avoid overly rapid correction and fluid overload, especially in adolescents (new headache, altered mental status, bradycardia/hypertension are ominous). RED HERRING: the abdominal pain can mimic a surgical abdomen but typically resolves with treatment of the DKA. DETERIORATION: giving insulin before checking/repleting potassium, bolusing insulin, or over-rapid correction cause hypokalemic arrhythmia or cerebral edema; the correct ordered approach stabilizes him.
criticalActions: Confirm DKA: glucose, venous blood gas/anion gap, serum or urine ketones
Start isotonic IV fluid resuscitation
Check potassium BEFORE insulin; if K < 3.3 hold insulin and replete potassium first
Start an insulin infusion (no IV bolus, especially in young patients)
Add dextrose to fluids as glucose approaches ~250 mg/dL
Identify and treat the precipitant (new diagnosis, infection, missed insulin)
Monitor glucose/electrolytes frequently and watch for cerebral edema; avoid over-rapid correction
Admit to an appropriate level of care
learningPoints: Fluids first, then check potassium before insulin — hold insulin if K < 3.3 mEq/L.
Use an insulin infusion, not a bolus, especially in pediatric/adolescent patients.
Add dextrose as glucose falls (~250) so insulin can continue to close the anion gap.
Watch for cerebral edema in young DKA patients; avoid overly rapid correction and fluid overload.
cases.intro: An 18-year-old is brought in by his roommate, breathing deeply and rapidly, with a sweet odor on his breath. He's been urinating constantly and drinking gallons of water for a week, and today started vomiting with belly pain. He's drowsy but rousable, mucous membranes dry, and he says he's lost weight recently without trying.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I can't stop throwing up… my stomach hurts and I'm so thirsty. I've been peeing nonstop for days.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Fingerstick reads 'high' — over the meter limit. He's breathing fast and deep. Two large-bore IVs are in.

cases.title: Septic shock (urosepsis)
cases.chiefComplaint: 72F — fever, confusion, flank pain, hypotension
cases.diagnosis: Septic shock from obstructing ureteral stone with pyelonephritis (urosepsis)
cases.context: 72-year-old woman, diabetic, brought in confused with fever, rigors, left flank pain, and dysuria for two days; she is hypotensive and tachycardic. The true diagnosis is SEPTIC SHOCK from urosepsis, with an OBSTRUCTING left ureteral stone and pyelonephritis (source control matters). Expected: elevated lactate, leukocytosis, pyuria/bacteriuria, acute kidney injury, and hydronephrosis with an obstructing stone on CT/ultrasound. CORRECT PATH (time-critical): obtain LACTATE and blood + urine cultures BEFORE antibiotics; give BROAD-SPECTRUM IV ANTIBIOTICS within 1 hour; resuscitate with 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid; reassess perfusion and start NOREPINEFRINE for MAP < 65 despite fluids; identify the source (urinalysis, imaging) and recognize that an OBSTRUCTED, infected system requires urgent urologic decompression (stent/nephrostomy) — antibiotics alone will not control the source; admit to ICU-level care. RED HERRING: anchoring on 'simple UTI' and under-treating; the obstruction is easy to miss without imaging. DETERIORATION: delayed antibiotics, inadequate fluids, or a missed obstruction worsen shock (falling MAP, rising lactate, worsening confusion). Prompt cultures→antibiotics→fluids→pressors and source control stabilize her.
criticalActions: Obtain lactate and blood + urine cultures before antibiotics
Give broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour
Resuscitate with 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid
Reassess perfusion and start norepinephrine for MAP < 65 despite fluids
Obtain imaging and recognize an obstructing stone requiring urologic decompression (source control)
Recognize septic shock (hypotension + elevated lactate + infection)
Admit to ICU / appropriate level of care
learningPoints: Sepsis bundle: lactate and cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, 30 mL/kg crystalloid.
Start norepinephrine for MAP < 65 mm Hg despite adequate fluids.
An obstructed, infected urinary system needs urgent decompression — antibiotics alone won't achieve source control.
Don't anchor on 'simple UTI' in an ill, hypotensive patient — image to find the source.
cases.intro: A 72-year-old woman is wheeled in from triage, flushed and shivering, confused about where she is. Her daughter says she's had burning urination and left-sided back pain for two days and became confused this afternoon. She's hot to the touch, her blood pressure is low at 82/48, and she's breathing fast.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She was fine two days ago — then the back pain and the burning when she pees, and now she doesn't know where she is.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Pressure's 82/48 and she's febrile at 39.1. She's altered. I've got two lines — what do you want first?

cases.title: Acute aortic dissection (Stanford A)
cases.chiefComplaint: 58M — sudden tearing chest/back pain, unequal pulses
cases.diagnosis: Acute Stanford type A aortic dissection
cases.context: 58-year-old man, hypertensive and noncompliant with meds, with abrupt severe 'tearing' anterior chest pain radiating to the interscapular back, maximal at onset. True diagnosis is acute type A aortic dissection. Findings: hypertension with a >20 mmHg systolic differential between arms, a soft early diastolic murmur (aortic regurgitation), possibly a widened mediastinum on CXR; can extend to cause tamponade, stroke, or acute AR. CORRECT PATH: two IVs, obtain blood pressure in BOTH arms, control heart rate FIRST with IV beta-blockade (esmolol/labetalol) to target HR <60 and SBP 100-120 BEFORE any vasodilator (avoid reflex tachycardia and increased shear), pain control, type and crossmatch, urgent CT angiography of the chest/abdomen/pelvis, and EMERGENT cardiothoracic surgery consult for type A. RED HERRINGS: looks like STEMI/ACS — but giving thrombolytics or aggressive anticoagulation/antiplatelets would be catastrophic; an inferior-lead ischemia pattern can occur if the dissection involves the RCA. DETERIORATION: nitroprusside without rate control, missed diagnosis, or delay to OR → extension, tamponade (becomes hypotensive/JVD/muffled tones), or death. Rate + pressure control and surgery stabilize.
criticalActions: Measure blood pressure in both arms and check for a pulse/pressure differential
Control heart rate first with IV beta-blockade (esmolol/labetalol) before any vasodilator
Target HR <60 and SBP 100-120 mmHg
Provide adequate analgesia
Type and crossmatch blood
Obtain emergent CT angiography (chest/abdomen/pelvis)
Emergent cardiothoracic surgery consult for type A dissection
Avoid thrombolytics/anticoagulation (do not treat as STEMI)
learningPoints: Rate control (beta-blockade) BEFORE vasodilators in dissection to reduce aortic wall shear (dP/dt).
Check bilateral BPs and look for a pulse deficit; a widened mediastinum supports the diagnosis.
Type A dissection is a surgical emergency; type B is often managed medically.
Don't anchor on STEMI — thrombolytics in dissection are lethal.
cases.intro: A 58-year-old man grips the side rails, diaphoretic and in obvious distress. He describes a sudden 'ripping' pain that started between his shoulder blades an hour ago and tore around to his chest. He has a long history of poorly controlled hypertension. He looks pale and anxious; his radial pulses feel different on each side.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It felt like something ripped inside my back — worst pain of my life, and it came on all at once.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Pressure's 188 over 96 in the right arm. Feels weaker on the left — want me to cycle both arms?

cases.title: Massive pulmonary embolism with obstructive shock
cases.chiefComplaint: 64F — sudden dyspnea and syncope, hypotensive and hypoxic
cases.diagnosis: Massive (high-risk) pulmonary embolism with obstructive shock
cases.context: 64-year-old woman, recent knee replacement 2 weeks ago, presents with sudden dyspnea, pleuritic pain, near-syncope, now hypotensive, tachycardic, and hypoxic. True diagnosis is high-risk (massive) pulmonary embolism causing obstructive shock and RV strain. Findings: sinus tach (may show S1Q3T3 or new RBBB), hypoxia, elevated troponin/BNP, RV dilation on bedside echo, clear lungs. CORRECT PATH: resuscitate cautiously (RV is preload-sensitive but volume overload worsens RV — give modest fluids, start norepinephrine early for hypotension), high-flow oxygen, obtain bedside echo showing RV strain, and because she is HEMODYNAMICALLY UNSTABLE give SYSTEMIC THROMBOLYSIS (tPA) unless absolutely contraindicated (or catheter-directed therapy/embolectomy if contraindicated); start anticoagulation (heparin). CTA confirms if the patient can tolerate it, but don't delay lysis in true arrest/peri-arrest. RED HERRING: could be mistaken for MI or sepsis; large fluid boluses worsen RV failure. DETERIORATION: aggressive fluids, delayed thrombolysis, or intubation with hypotension (peri-intubation arrest) → PEA arrest. Thrombolysis + pressors stabilize.
criticalActions: Recognize obstructive shock from high-risk PE (hypotension + hypoxia + RV strain)
Apply high-flow oxygen
Obtain bedside echocardiogram to assess RV strain
Start norepinephrine early for hypotension; give only modest fluids (avoid RV overload)
Start anticoagulation with heparin
Administer systemic thrombolysis (tPA) for the unstable patient, or catheter-directed therapy/embolectomy if contraindicated
Recognize peri-intubation arrest risk and optimize hemodynamics first
learningPoints: High-risk (massive) PE = PE with hypotension; treat with thrombolysis unless contraindicated.
The failing RV is preload-sensitive but intolerant of volume overload — modest fluids, early norepinephrine.
Bedside echo (RV dilation/strain) can guide therapy when the patient is too unstable for CTA.
Peri-intubation hypotension can precipitate arrest — resuscitate before you intubate.
cases.intro: A 64-year-old woman is wheeled in gasping, two weeks out from a total knee replacement. She stood up, felt sudden shortness of breath and chest tightness, and nearly passed out. She's mottled and diaphoretic, her oxygen saturation is in the mid-80s, and her blood pressure is low.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I can't… catch my breath. It came out of nowhere when I stood up.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Sat's 86 on room air, pressure 84 over 54, heart rate 124. She had knee surgery two weeks ago.

cases.title: Cardiac tamponade
cases.chiefComplaint: 51F — progressive dyspnea, hypotension, JVD, muffled tones
cases.diagnosis: Cardiac tamponade (pericardial effusion, likely malignant/uremic)
cases.context: 51-year-old woman with progressive dyspnea, fatigue, and near-syncope; now hypotensive with distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds (Beck triad), and pulsus paradoxus >12 mmHg. True diagnosis is cardiac tamponade from a pericardial effusion (consider malignancy, uremia, viral, post-MI). ECG may show low voltage and electrical alternans; CXR a large globular heart; bedside echo shows effusion with diastolic RV collapse and IVC plethora. CORRECT PATH: recognize obstructive shock, give a modest fluid bolus as a temporizing measure to augment preload, obtain bedside echo, and perform/arrange emergent PERICARDIOCENTESIS (echo-guided) or pericardial window; avoid intubation/positive-pressure ventilation if possible (drops preload → arrest). RED HERRING: dyspnea and hypotension can look like PE, sepsis, or heart failure; giving diuretics or nitrates would be harmful (they drop preload). DETERIORATION: positive-pressure ventilation, diuresis, or delay to drainage → PEA arrest. Pericardiocentesis is definitive.
criticalActions: Recognize tamponade/obstructive shock (Beck triad, pulsus paradoxus)
Obtain a bedside echocardiogram (effusion, diastolic RV collapse, IVC plethora)
Give a temporizing IV fluid bolus to support preload
Avoid diuretics and nitrates (preload-dependent physiology)
Avoid positive-pressure ventilation if possible
Perform or emergently arrange pericardiocentesis / pericardial window
Consult cardiology/cardiothoracic surgery
learningPoints: Tamponade is preload-dependent — support volume, avoid nitrates/diuretics and, if possible, positive-pressure ventilation.
Beck triad (hypotension, JVD, muffled tones) + pulsus paradoxus; confirm with bedside echo.
Electrical alternans and low voltage on ECG suggest a large effusion.
Pericardiocentesis is the definitive, life-saving intervention.
cases.intro: A 51-year-old woman sits bolt upright, breathing hard, looking frightened. Over a week she's grown more short of breath and lightheaded, and today she nearly fainted. Her neck veins are distended even sitting up, her heart sounds are distant, and her blood pressure sags noticeably each time she breathes in.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I feel like I can't get enough air, and my heart is pounding. I almost blacked out standing up.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Pressure's soft at 86 systolic and drops when she breathes in. Neck veins are way up and her heart sounds are hard to hear.

cases.title: Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm
cases.chiefComplaint: 72M — sudden flank/abdominal pain, hypotension, pulsatile mass
cases.diagnosis: Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm
cases.context: 72-year-old male smoker with sudden severe abdominal/flank/back pain and syncope, now hypotensive and diaphoretic with a tender pulsatile epigastric mass. True diagnosis is ruptured AAA. Classic triad: pain, hypotension, pulsatile mass. Often MISDIAGNOSED as renal colic — an older patient with 'first-ever kidney stone' and hypotension is a ruptured AAA until proven otherwise. CORRECT PATH: two large-bore IVs, type and crossmatch, activate massive transfusion / call vascular surgery IMMEDIATELY, bedside ultrasound to confirm aneurysm, PERMISSIVE HYPOTENSION (target SBP ~70-90 / mentating — do NOT over-resuscitate with crystalloid, which worsens bleeding and clot disruption), and emergent transfer to OR/endovascular repair. RED HERRING: renal colic, diverticulitis, MI. DETERIORATION: aggressive crystalloid, CT when unstable, or delay to OR → exsanguination. Blood products + immediate surgery are the only definitive path.
criticalActions: Recognize ruptured AAA (pain + hypotension + pulsatile mass; don't anchor on renal colic)
Place two large-bore IVs and type & crossmatch
Obtain bedside ultrasound to confirm the aneurysm
Activate massive transfusion and use blood products, not large crystalloid volumes
Practice permissive hypotension (SBP ~70-90 while mentating)
Call vascular surgery emergently
Expedite transfer to OR / endovascular repair
learningPoints: Consider ruptured AAA in any older patient with abdominal/flank/back pain and hypotension — it mimics renal colic.
Permissive hypotension and blood products, not crystalloid flooding, before definitive repair.
Bedside ultrasound rapidly confirms the aneurysm; an unstable patient goes to the OR, not the CT scanner.
Immediate vascular surgery involvement is the only definitive treatment.
cases.intro: A 72-year-old man clutches his belly, gray and sweating, after a sudden tearing pain in his flank and back made him collapse at home. He's a lifelong smoker. His abdomen is tender with a pulsatile fullness above the umbilicus, and his blood pressure is dangerously low.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: The pain hit like a hammer in my back and side, then everything went spinny and I hit the floor.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's pale and clammy, pressure 78 over 50. I can feel a pulsating mass in his belly.

cases.title: Unstable bradycardia / complete heart block
cases.chiefComplaint: 78M — syncope, bradycardia at 32, hypotension
cases.diagnosis: Complete (third-degree) AV block with unstable bradycardia
cases.context: 78-year-old man with syncope, fatigue, and dyspnea, found bradycardic at 32 with hypotension and altered perfusion. True diagnosis is complete (third-degree) heart block with AV dissociation on ECG (P waves march through independent of QRS, junctional/ventricular escape). May be from degenerative conduction disease, ischemia (esp. inferior MI), or drugs (beta-blocker/CCB/digoxin). CORRECT PATH: apply pads, give ATROPINE (often ineffective in high-grade/infranodal block but attempt), start TRANSCUTANEOUS PACING with sedation/analgesia, add a chronotropic infusion (epinephrine or dopamine) if pacing delayed/failing, get a 12-lead to look for ischemia and check electrolytes/drug causes (hyperkalemia, digoxin), and consult cardiology for TRANSVENOUS PACING and possible permanent pacemaker. RED HERRING: attributing symptoms to age; missing an inferior STEMI or hyperkalemia as the cause. DETERIORATION: delay in pacing → worsening hypoperfusion, escape rhythm failure, arrest. Pacing restores perfusion.
criticalActions: Recognize unstable bradycardia / complete heart block on ECG (AV dissociation)
Apply pacer pads and monitor
Give atropine (attempt, though often ineffective in infranodal block)
Initiate transcutaneous pacing with sedation and analgesia
Start a chronotropic infusion (epinephrine or dopamine) if pacing is delayed or failing
Obtain a 12-lead ECG and evaluate for ischemia, hyperkalemia, and drug causes
Consult cardiology for transvenous pacing / permanent pacemaker
learningPoints: Atropine is often ineffective in high-grade/infranodal AV block — move quickly to pacing.
Transcutaneous pacing is a bridge; secure transvenous pacing and cardiology involvement.
Look for reversible causes: inferior MI, hyperkalemia, and AV-nodal blocking drugs.
Sedate and provide analgesia for transcutaneous pacing — it is painful.
cases.intro: A 78-year-old man is brought in after passing out at the dinner table. He's pale and confused, his pulse slow and thready. The monitor shows a heart rate in the low 30s with P waves that seem to march independently of the QRS complexes. His blood pressure is low and he's cool to the touch.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He just slumped over at dinner. He's been so tired and dizzy the last few days.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Rate's 32 and he's hypotensive at 84 systolic. Pads are on — what do you want to do?

cases.title: Unstable ventricular tachycardia
cases.chiefComplaint: 66M — palpitations, chest pain, wide-complex tachy at 190, hypotension
cases.diagnosis: Unstable monomorphic ventricular tachycardia
cases.context: 66-year-old man with prior MI and reduced EF presents with palpitations and chest pressure; monitor shows a regular wide-complex tachycardia at ~190 with hypotension, diaphoresis, and chest pain — hemodynamically UNSTABLE. True diagnosis is monomorphic ventricular tachycardia (given structural heart disease, treat wide-complex tachycardia as VT). CORRECT PATH: because he is unstable (hypotension, ischemic chest pain, altered), proceed to SYNCHRONIZED CARDIOVERSION with pre-procedural sedation; if it degenerates to VF or is pulseless, defibrillate and run ACLS; after conversion, obtain 12-lead, treat ischemia/electrolytes (K, Mg), start antiarrhythmic (amiodarone) and consult cardiology. RED HERRING: calling it 'SVT with aberrancy' and giving a calcium channel blocker (verapamil) can precipitate arrest in VT. DETERIORATION: adenosine/verapamil, delay in cardioversion → degeneration to VF/arrest. Synchronized cardioversion is the immediate treatment.
criticalActions: Recognize unstable wide-complex tachycardia and treat as VT
Perform synchronized cardioversion with pre-procedural sedation
Defibrillate and run ACLS if it becomes pulseless VT/VF
Obtain a post-conversion 12-lead ECG
Correct electrolytes (potassium, magnesium) and evaluate for ischemia
Start an antiarrhythmic (amiodarone) after conversion
Avoid AV-nodal blockers/verapamil in wide-complex tachycardia of uncertain origin
Consult cardiology
learningPoints: Unstable wide-complex tachycardia → synchronized cardioversion, not drug trials.
In a patient with structural heart disease, treat wide-complex tachycardia as VT.
Verapamil/adenosine can be lethal if the rhythm is actually VT.
After conversion, correct K/Mg, evaluate for ischemia, and start amiodarone.
cases.intro: A 66-year-old man with a prior heart attack grabs his chest, pale and sweating, saying his heart is 'racing out of control.' The monitor shows a fast, regular, wide-complex rhythm near 190. His blood pressure is low and he's becoming confused.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My heart's slamming and my chest hurts… I feel like I'm going to pass out.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Wide complex at 190, pressure 82 over 54. He's diaphoretic and getting altered.

cases.title: Inferior STEMI with RV involvement
cases.chiefComplaint: 59M — crushing chest pain, inferior ST elevation, hypotension after nitro
cases.diagnosis: Inferior ST-elevation MI with right ventricular involvement
cases.context: 59-year-old man with 45 minutes of crushing substernal chest pain, diaphoresis, and nausea; 12-lead shows ST elevation in II, III, aVF (inferior STEMI). True diagnosis is inferior STEMI with RIGHT VENTRICULAR involvement (obtain a right-sided ECG — ST elevation in V4R). The RV infarct makes him PRELOAD-DEPENDENT: nitroglycerin causes profound hypotension. CORRECT PATH: aspirin, obtain right-sided leads, activate the cath lab for primary PCI (door-to-balloon), heparin/antiplatelet per protocol, and treat hypotension with IV FLUID BOLUSES (not nitrates); AVOID nitroglycerin and other preload reducers. Watch for bradyarrhythmias/heart block (inferior MI). RED HERRING: reflexively giving nitro for chest pain drops the pressure dangerously. DETERIORATION: nitrates, delayed reperfusion → cardiogenic shock, block, arrest. Fluids + rapid PCI stabilize.
criticalActions: Obtain a 12-lead ECG and recognize inferior STEMI
Obtain right-sided leads (V4R) to detect RV involvement
Give aspirin
Activate the cath lab for primary PCI (or thrombolysis if PCI unavailable)
Treat hypotension with IV fluid boluses
AVOID nitroglycerin and other preload-reducing agents
Anticoagulate/antiplatelet per ACS protocol
Monitor for bradyarrhythmias/AV block
learningPoints: Inferior STEMI → get right-sided leads; RV involvement makes the patient preload-dependent.
Avoid nitroglycerin in RV infarction — it can cause profound hypotension; treat with fluids.
Primary PCI within the door-to-balloon window is the goal.
Watch for bradycardia and AV block with inferior MI.
cases.intro: A 59-year-old man clutches his chest, gray and sweating, describing crushing pressure for the last 45 minutes with nausea. The paramedics handed you a 12-lead showing ST elevation in the inferior leads. His heart rate is on the slower side and he looks unwell.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Inferior ST elevation on our monitor. We gave aspirin. We held off on nitro — his pressure was borderline.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Feels like an elephant sitting on my chest… I feel sick to my stomach.

cases.title: Flash pulmonary edema (SCAPE)
cases.chiefComplaint: 70F — acute severe dyspnea, crackles, BP 220/120, hypoxia
cases.diagnosis: Sympathetic crashing acute pulmonary edema (hypertensive acute heart failure)
cases.context: 70-year-old woman with sudden severe respiratory distress, drowning-sensation, diffuse crackles, frothy sputum, markedly hypertensive (220/120), and hypoxic. True diagnosis is SCAPE (sympathetic crashing acute pulmonary edema) — hypertensive acute heart failure with rapid fluid redistribution, NOT primarily volume overload. CORRECT PATH: sit upright, immediate NON-INVASIVE POSITIVE-PRESSURE VENTILATION (CPAP/BiPAP), and aggressive AFTERLOAD REDUCTION with high-dose IV nitroglycerin (the key intervention); diuretics are secondary and often less important acutely. Avoid intubation if NIPPV is working. Evaluate for ischemic trigger (ECG, troponin). RED HERRING: treating as pneumonia/COPD or giving large fluid boluses worsens it; heavy reliance on furosemide alone under-treats the afterload problem. DETERIORATION: delay in NIPPV + nitroglycerin, or intubation with induction hypotension → decompensation. Nitro + NIPPV rapidly reverse it.
criticalActions: Sit the patient upright and apply high-flow oxygen
Start non-invasive positive-pressure ventilation (CPAP/BiPAP) early
Give aggressive afterload reduction with high-dose IV nitroglycerin
Recognize this as hypertensive acute heart failure (SCAPE), not simple volume overload
Obtain ECG and troponin to evaluate for an ischemic trigger
Avoid large fluid boluses
Reserve intubation for NIPPV failure
learningPoints: SCAPE is an afterload/redistribution problem — high-dose nitroglycerin + NIPPV are the mainstays.
Diuretics are secondary; these patients are often not truly volume overloaded.
NIPPV frequently prevents intubation in flash pulmonary edema.
Look for an ischemic trigger with ECG and troponin.
cases.intro: A 70-year-old woman is bolt upright on the stretcher, gasping and panicked, unable to speak in full sentences. She's coughing up frothy pink sputum, her lungs are full of crackles, and the monitor shows a blood pressure of 224/122 with an oxygen saturation in the low 80s.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I'm… drowning… can't… breathe…

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Sat's 82, pressure 224 over 122, respiratory rate in the 30s. She's soaking wet and coughing up pink froth.

cases.title: Tension pneumothorax
cases.chiefComplaint: 24M — chest trauma, severe dyspnea, absent breath sounds, hypotension
cases.diagnosis: Tension pneumothorax
cases.context: 24-year-old man after a motorcycle crash (or spontaneous in a tall thin smoker) with severe dyspnea, unilateral absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, tracheal deviation away from the affected side, distended neck veins, hypotension, and hypoxia. True diagnosis is tension pneumothorax — obstructive shock from progressive air trapping. This is a CLINICAL diagnosis; do NOT wait for a CXR when unstable. CORRECT PATH: immediate NEEDLE DECOMPRESSION (large-bore angiocath, 2nd intercostal space midclavicular line or 4th/5th at anterior axillary line) followed by TUBE THORACOSTOMY (chest tube); high-flow oxygen; then imaging. RED HERRING: delaying for a chest x-ray or CT in an unstable patient. DETERIORATION: any delay, or positive-pressure ventilation without decompression → PEA arrest. Decompression is immediately life-saving.
criticalActions: Recognize tension pneumothorax clinically (do not wait for imaging when unstable)
Perform immediate needle decompression
Follow with tube thoracostomy (chest tube)
Apply high-flow oxygen
Obtain a chest x-ray only after decompression / in stable patients
Reassess perfusion and oxygenation after decompression
learningPoints: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis — decompress before imaging in the unstable patient.
Needle decompression is a temporizing bridge to a chest tube.
Positive-pressure ventilation can rapidly convert a simple to a tension pneumothorax.
Look for tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, JVD, and hypotension.
cases.intro: A 24-year-old man is brought in after a high-speed motorcycle crash, struggling violently to breathe. His right chest doesn't move well and has no breath sounds, his neck veins are bulging, and his trachea looks shifted to the left. His blood pressure is crashing and his oxygen saturation is in the low 80s.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: He went downhill fast in the truck — breath sounds gone on the right, trachea's shifted, pressure dropping.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Can't… breathe… chest…

cases.title: Status asthmaticus (near-fatal asthma)
cases.chiefComplaint: 28F — severe wheeze then a quiet chest, tiring, hypoxic
cases.diagnosis: Near-fatal (status) asthmaticus
cases.context: 28-year-old woman with a severe asthma exacerbation not responding to home inhalers: tripoding, single-word dyspnea, initially loud wheeze now becoming a QUIET chest (ominous), rising CO2, and fatigue. True diagnosis is near-fatal status asthmaticus with impending respiratory failure. CORRECT PATH: continuous nebulized albuterol + ipratropium, EARLY systemic corticosteroids, IV MAGNESIUM sulfate, oxygen; escalate to IM epinephrine for the crashing patient, consider IV/nebulized epinephrine and NIPPV; if intubating, anticipate difficult ventilation — use ketamine, low respiratory rate, allow PERMISSIVE HYPERCAPNIA, watch for breath-stacking/auto-PEEP and sudden hypotension (disconnect + push on chest if it occurs). RED HERRING: a 'quiet chest' and normalizing/rising CO2 in a tiring patient signals failure, not improvement; over-sedation or high ventilator rates cause dynamic hyperinflation and arrest. DETERIORATION: missing the silent chest, aggressive ventilation → barotrauma/arrest. Aggressive bronchodilation + steroids + magnesium turn it around.
criticalActions: Give continuous nebulized albuterol plus ipratropium and high-flow oxygen
Administer systemic corticosteroids early
Give IV magnesium sulfate
Recognize the 'quiet chest' and rising CO2 as impending respiratory failure
Escalate to IM epinephrine for the crashing/peri-arrest patient; consider NIPPV
If intubating, use ketamine, low rate, and permissive hypercapnia; watch for auto-PEEP
Manage sudden post-intubation hypotension (disconnect circuit for auto-PEEP)
learningPoints: A quiet chest with a tiring patient and rising CO2 is near-fatal asthma, not improvement.
Steroids, magnesium, and continuous beta-agonists are core; epinephrine for the crashing patient.
Ventilate with a low rate and permissive hypercapnia to avoid breath-stacking/auto-PEEP.
Sudden post-intubation hypotension → suspect auto-PEEP; disconnect the circuit.
cases.intro: A 28-year-old woman with severe asthma is hunched forward, using every muscle to breathe, able to say only single words. Her inhaler stopped working hours ago. Earlier she was wheezing loudly; now her chest is ominously quiet and she looks exhausted, with an oxygen saturation of 87%.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Can't… talk… inhaler… not… working…

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's tiring out — barely any air movement now, sat's 87, heart rate 128. She looks like she's fading.

cases.title: Anaphylaxis
cases.chiefComplaint: 34F — hives, throat tightness, wheeze, hypotension after eating
cases.diagnosis: Anaphylaxis (IgE-mediated, likely food)
cases.context: 34-year-old woman with rapid-onset urticaria, angioedema, throat tightness, wheeze, vomiting, and hypotension minutes after eating at a restaurant (shellfish). True diagnosis is anaphylaxis (two organ systems involved, or hypotension after a likely allergen). CORRECT PATH: the FIRST and most important treatment is INTRAMUSCULAR EPINEPHRINE (0.3-0.5 mg IM, lateral thigh) — do not delay for antihistamines/steroids; repeat every 5-15 min as needed; place supine with legs raised, high-flow oxygen, aggressive IV fluids for hypotension; adjunct H1/H2 antihistamines, corticosteroids, and inhaled beta-agonist for bronchospasm; for refractory hypotension start an EPINEPHRINE INFUSION (and consider glucagon if on beta-blockers). Prepare for a difficult airway if angioedema progresses. Observe for biphasic reaction. RED HERRING: giving only antihistamines/steroids and withholding epinephrine (the commonest fatal error). DETERIORATION: delayed epinephrine → airway obstruction and cardiovascular collapse. IM epinephrine + fluids reverse it.
criticalActions: Give intramuscular epinephrine immediately (0.3-0.5 mg, lateral thigh) and repeat as needed
Position supine with legs elevated and apply high-flow oxygen
Give aggressive IV fluid resuscitation for hypotension
Add H1 and H2 antihistamines and corticosteroids as adjuncts
Treat bronchospasm with inhaled beta-agonists
Start an epinephrine infusion for refractory hypotension (glucagon if on beta-blockers)
Prepare for a difficult airway and observe for a biphasic reaction
learningPoints: Epinephrine IM is first-line and life-saving — antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts, never substitutes.
Refractory anaphylaxis needs an epinephrine infusion; consider glucagon if the patient takes beta-blockers.
Anticipate airway compromise from progressive angioedema.
Observe for biphasic reactions after initial improvement.
cases.intro: A 34-year-old woman is rushed in flushed and covered in hives, clutching her throat, wheezing and retching. She ate at a seafood restaurant twenty minutes ago and her lips and tongue are swelling. Her voice is muffled, her blood pressure is low, and she looks terrified.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My throat's closing… I can't… I ate shrimp…

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Covered in hives, lips swelling, wheezing, pressure 84 over 50. What do you want first?

cases.title: ACE-inhibitor angioedema (airway)
cases.chiefComplaint: 61M — progressive tongue/lip swelling, no hives, on lisinopril
cases.diagnosis: ACE-inhibitor-induced angioedema with airway threat
cases.context: 61-year-old man on lisinopril for years presents with progressive lip, tongue, and floor-of-mouth swelling over hours, no urticaria or pruritus, voice changes and difficulty handling secretions. True diagnosis is ACE-inhibitor-induced (bradykinin-mediated) angioedema — it does NOT reliably respond to epinephrine, antihistamines, or steroids the way allergic angioedema does, and the central threat is AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION. CORRECT PATH: stop the ACE inhibitor; the priority is AIRWAY ASSESSMENT and EARLY, CONTROLLED AIRWAY MANAGEMENT — prepare for a difficult airway (awake fiberoptic/flexible nasal intubation by the most experienced operator, double setup with surgical airway ready) before obstruction becomes complete; give epinephrine/antihistamines/steroids empirically (reasonable but often ineffective); specific agents (icatibant, C1-esterase inhibitor, FFP) may be considered. RED HERRING: treating exactly like allergic anaphylaxis and waiting for meds to work while the airway closes. DETERIORATION: delayed airway control → complete obstruction, can't-intubate-can't-oxygenate. Early definitive airway is key.
criticalActions: Recognize ACE-inhibitor (bradykinin-mediated) angioedema and stop the ACE inhibitor
Assess the airway continuously for progression (voice change, drooling, stridor)
Prepare for a difficult airway with a double setup (awake fiberoptic + surgical airway ready)
Secure the airway early and in a controlled fashion before obstruction is complete
Give epinephrine/antihistamines/steroids empirically (understanding they are often ineffective)
Consider specific therapy (icatibant, C1-esterase inhibitor, FFP)
Involve anesthesia/ENT early
learningPoints: ACE-inhibitor angioedema is bradykinin-mediated — it often doesn't respond to epinephrine/antihistamines/steroids.
The airway is the threat; manage it early and in a controlled way with a difficult-airway plan.
No hives/itching and an ACE-inhibitor history point away from allergic anaphylaxis.
Have a double setup with a surgical airway ready before the airway closes.
cases.intro: A 61-year-old man points urgently at his mouth — his lips and tongue have been swelling for the last few hours and now his words are thick and muffled. He has no rash and no itching. He takes lisinopril for blood pressure. He's beginning to drool and his voice sounds different than when he arrived.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My tongue keeps getting bigger… hard to talk… no rash, just the swelling.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: No hives anywhere, but his tongue is huge and he's starting to drool. He's on lisinopril.

cases.title: Acute ischemic stroke (large vessel occlusion)
cases.chiefComplaint: 67F — sudden left-sided weakness and neglect, last known well 90 min ago
cases.diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke from large vessel occlusion (right MCA)
cases.context: 67-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation (not anticoagulated) with sudden left face/arm/leg weakness, gaze deviation, and neglect; last known well 90 minutes ago. True diagnosis is acute ischemic stroke from a right MCA large vessel occlusion. CORRECT PATH: activate STROKE ALERT, immediate fingerstick GLUCOSE (rule out hypoglycemia mimic), NIH Stroke Scale, emergent noncontrast head CT + CTA (and CT perfusion), determine last-known-well and screen contraindications; give IV THROMBOLYSIS (tenecteplase/alteplase) if within window and eligible, and pursue ENDOVASCULAR THROMBECTOMY for the LVO; manage BP per protocol (permit up to 185/110 before lytics, don't over-lower); do not give aspirin until hemorrhage excluded and lytic decision made. RED HERRINGS: hypoglycemia, seizure with Todd paralysis, and complex migraine can mimic stroke — check glucose. DETERIORATION: delays to imaging/reperfusion enlarge the infarct ('time is brain'); over-aggressive BP lowering worsens penumbral perfusion. Rapid reperfusion is the treatment.
criticalActions: Activate a stroke alert and establish last-known-well time
Check a fingerstick glucose to exclude hypoglycemia
Perform an NIH Stroke Scale assessment
Obtain emergent noncontrast head CT plus CTA/CT perfusion
Give IV thrombolysis (tenecteplase/alteplase) if eligible and within window
Pursue endovascular thrombectomy for the large vessel occlusion
Manage blood pressure per protocol (allow up to 185/110 pre-lytic; avoid over-lowering)
Do not give aspirin until hemorrhage is excluded and the lytic decision is made
learningPoints: Time is brain — expedite imaging and reperfusion; establish last-known-well early.
Always check glucose; hypoglycemia is a classic stroke mimic.
Thrombolysis plus thrombectomy for eligible LVO; don't over-lower BP and starve the penumbra.
Withhold antiplatelets until hemorrhage is excluded and the thrombolysis decision is finalized.
cases.intro: A 67-year-old woman is brought in by her husband who says her face drooped and her left side went limp about 90 minutes ago while they were eating lunch. Her eyes are deviated to the right, she ignores her left side, and her left arm and leg are flaccid. She has atrial fibrillation and takes no blood thinners.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: One second she was fine, the next her face fell and her arm dropped. It's been about an hour and a half.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's got a dense left-sided weakness and gaze deviation. AFib on the monitor, pressure 178 over 98.

cases.title: Subarachnoid hemorrhage
cases.chiefComplaint: 45M — thunderclap 'worst headache of life', neck stiffness
cases.diagnosis: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage
cases.context: 45-year-old man with a sudden 'thunderclap' headache maximal within seconds ('worst of my life'), brief loss of consciousness, vomiting, photophobia, and neck stiffness. True diagnosis is aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage. CORRECT PATH: emergent NONCONTRAST HEAD CT (highly sensitive within 6 hours of onset); if CT is negative but suspicion remains, LUMBAR PUNCTURE looking for xanthochromia/RBCs (or CT angiography per pathway); once confirmed, control BP to a moderate target, provide analgesia and antiemetics, give NIMODIPINE (reduces vasospasm-related poor outcomes), reverse any coagulopathy, prevent rebleeding, and consult NEUROSURGERY/interventional neuroradiology urgently for aneurysm securing (clip/coil). RED HERRING: dismissing it as migraine/tension headache or treating and discharging without imaging — the classic miss. DETERIORATION: missed diagnosis → catastrophic rebleed; untreated hydrocephalus → decline. Prompt diagnosis and aneurysm securing are key.
criticalActions: Recognize thunderclap headache as SAH until proven otherwise
Obtain an emergent noncontrast head CT
Perform lumbar puncture (xanthochromia) or CTA if CT is negative and suspicion persists
Control blood pressure to a moderate target and provide analgesia/antiemetics
Give nimodipine to reduce vasospasm-related morbidity
Reverse any coagulopathy and prevent rebleeding
Consult neurosurgery / interventional neuroradiology urgently for aneurysm securing
learningPoints: A thunderclap headache maximal at onset is SAH until excluded — image, don't discharge.
Noncontrast CT is highly sensitive early; LP for xanthochromia (or CTA) when CT is negative.
Nimodipine improves outcomes by mitigating vasospasm.
Definitive treatment is securing the aneurysm (clip or coil) — involve neurosurgery early.
cases.intro: A 45-year-old man holds his head in both hands, wincing at the lights, having vomited twice. He says that an hour ago, mid-conversation, he felt an explosive headache slam into the back of his head — 'like being hit with a bat, the worst headache of my life' — and briefly blacked out. His neck is stiff.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It hit me out of nowhere — worst headache of my life, all at once. The light is killing me.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's photophobic with a stiff neck, pressure's up at 184 over 102. He vomited on arrival.

cases.title: Bacterial meningitis
cases.chiefComplaint: 22M — fever, headache, neck stiffness, altered, petechiae
cases.diagnosis: Bacterial meningitis (possible meningococcal)
cases.context: 22-year-old college student (dorm resident) with fever, severe headache, photophobia, neck stiffness, altered mental status, and a spreading petechial/purpuric rash. True diagnosis is bacterial meningitis, possibly meningococcal with early meningococcemia. CORRECT PATH: DO NOT DELAY ANTIBIOTICS for the LP or CT — draw blood cultures and give empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin, add ampicillin if >50 or immunocompromised for Listeria) plus DEXAMETHASONE (ideally with/just before the first antibiotic dose for pneumococcal) as early as possible; obtain a head CT before LP only if indicated (focal deficits, papilledema, immunocompromise, seizure, altered) but antibiotics come FIRST; then LP; droplet isolation and public-health notification with post-exposure prophylaxis for close contacts. RED HERRING: waiting for imaging/LP before antibiotics — every hour of delay increases mortality. DETERIORATION: delayed antibiotics, missed septic shock (meningococcemia) → rapid decline. Early antibiotics + steroids are the priority.
criticalActions: Give empiric IV antibiotics immediately (ceftriaxone + vancomycin; add ampicillin if at Listeria risk) — do not delay for CT/LP
Draw blood cultures before/with the first antibiotic dose
Administer dexamethasone with or just before the first antibiotic dose
Obtain head CT before LP only if specific indications are present
Perform lumbar puncture after antibiotics are given
Institute droplet isolation
Notify public health and arrange prophylaxis for close contacts
learningPoints: Never delay antibiotics for imaging or LP in suspected bacterial meningitis.
Empiric ceftriaxone + vancomycin (+ ampicillin for Listeria risk) plus early dexamethasone.
CT before LP only for focal deficits, papilledema, immunocompromise, seizure, or altered mental status.
A purpuric rash suggests meningococcemia — droplet precautions and contact prophylaxis.
cases.intro: A 22-year-old college student is brought in by roommates, feverish and confused, shielding his eyes from the light. He's had a pounding headache and stiff neck since morning and is now hard to rouse. As you examine him you notice scattered purple spots on his legs that seem to be spreading.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He was fine yesterday. Today he's burning up, his neck is stiff, and he's barely making sense.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Temp 39.6, he's altered, and there's a petechial rash on his legs that's spreading.

cases.title: Status epilepticus
cases.chiefComplaint: 37M — continuous convulsions >5 min, not recovering
cases.diagnosis: Generalized convulsive status epilepticus
cases.context: 37-year-old man with a known seizure disorder (ran out of medication) brought in actively convulsing for more than 5 minutes without recovery. True diagnosis is generalized convulsive status epilepticus — a time-critical emergency. CORRECT PATH: protect the airway/position, high-flow oxygen, IV access, immediate fingerstick GLUCOSE (treat hypoglycemia), first-line BENZODIAZEPINE promptly and adequately dosed (IV lorazepam, or IM midazolam if no IV) — under-dosing benzodiazepines is a common error; if seizures persist, load a second-line agent (levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, or valproate); if still refractory, proceed to anesthetic infusion (propofol/midazolam) and intubation. Search for causes (missed meds, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, infection/meningitis, eclampsia, toxins, intracranial process); check labs, consider CT and LP. RED HERRING: prolonged searching for the cause before adequately treating the seizure; timid benzodiazepine dosing. DETERIORATION: delayed/under-dosed benzodiazepines → refractory status, neuronal injury. Prompt adequate benzodiazepines then second-line loading control it.
criticalActions: Protect the airway, position the patient, and give high-flow oxygen
Check a fingerstick glucose and treat hypoglycemia
Give an adequately dosed first-line benzodiazepine promptly (IV lorazepam or IM midazolam)
Load a second-line agent if seizures persist (levetiracetam/fosphenytoin/valproate)
Escalate to an anesthetic infusion and intubation for refractory status
Investigate causes (electrolytes, infection, toxins, intracranial process; consider CT/LP)
Avoid under-dosing benzodiazepines
learningPoints: Treat convulsive status early with adequately dosed benzodiazepines — under-dosing is a common failure.
Check glucose immediately; hypoglycemia is a reversible cause.
If benzodiazepines fail, promptly load a second-line agent, then escalate to anesthesia.
Simultaneously search for the precipitant (meds, electrolytes, infection, toxins, eclampsia).
cases.intro: A 37-year-old man is carried in by paramedics, convulsing — rhythmic jerking of all four limbs that has been going on, they say, for at least eight minutes without stopping. He's cyanotic around the lips, frothing slightly, and unresponsive. His family says he has epilepsy and ran out of his pills.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: He's been seizing since before we got there — at least eight minutes. We couldn't get a line en route.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He has seizures, but never this long. He ran out of his medication last week.

cases.title: Spinal epidural abscess / cord compression
cases.chiefComplaint: 54M — back pain, fever, leg weakness, urinary retention; IVDU
cases.diagnosis: Spinal epidural abscess with early cord compression
cases.context: 54-year-old man with diabetes and IV drug use presents with several days of worsening midline back pain, fever, and now bilateral leg weakness, saddle-region numbness, and urinary retention. True diagnosis is spinal epidural abscess causing early cord compression (a can't-miss). Classic triad (back pain, fever, neurologic deficit) is often incomplete. CORRECT PATH: recognize the RED-FLAG back pain, perform a focused neuro exam and check post-void residual/saddle sensation/rectal tone, obtain EMERGENT MRI of the spine (whole spine if skip lesions suspected), send ESR/CRP, blood cultures, start empiric IV antibiotics (vancomycin + antipseudomonal/gram-negative coverage) after cultures, and consult NEUROSURGERY urgently for decompression; add steroids only per specialist for cord compression. RED HERRING: treating as mechanical back pain and discharging with analgesics — the disastrous miss; neurologic deficit is time-sensitive. DETERIORATION: delay to MRI/decompression → irreversible paralysis. Emergent MRI, antibiotics, and surgical decompression preserve function.
criticalActions: Recognize red-flag back pain (fever, neurologic deficit, IVDU/immunocompromise)
Perform a focused neurologic exam and assess saddle sensation, rectal tone, and post-void residual
Obtain emergent MRI of the spine
Send ESR/CRP and blood cultures
Start empiric IV antibiotics (vancomycin + gram-negative/antipseudomonal) after cultures
Consult neurosurgery urgently for decompression
Avoid discharging as mechanical back pain
learningPoints: Spinal epidural abscess: think of it with back pain plus fever and/or neurologic deficit, especially in IVDU/diabetes.
The classic triad is often incomplete — check ESR/CRP and get an MRI.
Neurologic deficits are time-critical: emergent decompression preserves function.
Empiric coverage includes MRSA (vancomycin) plus gram-negatives after cultures.
cases.intro: A 54-year-old man with diabetes shifts uncomfortably, describing several days of worsening pain in the middle of his back, now with fevers and legs that feel heavy and weak. He admits to injecting drugs. He hasn't been able to urinate since last night and reports numbness 'where I sit.'

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: The back pain's been building for days, and now my legs feel weak and I can't pee. I've been running fevers.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Temp's 38.5. His legs are weak bilaterally and his bladder is full on the scan.

cases.title: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy
cases.chiefComplaint: 27F — abdominal pain, syncope, hypotension; missed period
cases.diagnosis: Ruptured ectopic pregnancy with hemoperitoneum
cases.context: 27-year-old woman with lower abdominal pain, a few weeks of amenorrhea, light vaginal bleeding, near-syncope, now hypotensive and tachycardic with peritoneal signs and shoulder-tip pain. True diagnosis is a ruptured ectopic pregnancy with hemoperitoneum (hemorrhagic shock). CORRECT PATH: for ANY woman of reproductive age with abdominal pain/syncope, get a urine/serum PREGNANCY TEST first; two large-bore IVs, type and crossmatch, activate blood products for hypotension; BEDSIDE ULTRASOUND (no intrauterine pregnancy with free fluid in the pelvis = ectopic until proven otherwise); emergent OB/GYN consult for operative management (do NOT wait for a formal quantitative beta-hCG or radiology when unstable). RED HERRING: attributing pain to a UTI, ovarian cyst, or GI cause without a pregnancy test — the classic miss; giving methotrexate is inappropriate for a ruptured/unstable ectopic. DETERIORATION: delay to OR, over-reliance on labs while unstable → exsanguination. Blood products + emergent surgery are definitive.
criticalActions: Obtain a pregnancy test in any reproductive-age woman with abdominal pain or syncope
Place two large-bore IVs and type & crossmatch
Perform a bedside ultrasound (free fluid + no IUP suggests ruptured ectopic)
Resuscitate with blood products for hemorrhagic shock
Consult OB/GYN emergently for operative management
Do not delay surgery for quantitative beta-hCG or formal imaging in the unstable patient
Avoid methotrexate in the ruptured/unstable ectopic
learningPoints: Check a pregnancy test in every reproductive-age woman with abdominal pain or syncope.
Free pelvic fluid with no intrauterine pregnancy on bedside ultrasound = ruptured ectopic until proven otherwise.
An unstable ruptured ectopic goes to the OR — don't wait for labs or formal imaging.
Methotrexate is for stable, unruptured ectopics only.
cases.intro: A 27-year-old woman is pale and clammy, doubled over with lower abdominal pain that started sharply this morning; she nearly fainted getting out of the car. She mentions her period is several weeks late and she's had some light spotting. Her belly is rigid and tender, and she complains of pain in her right shoulder.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: The pain's terrible and low in my belly, and my shoulder hurts too. I almost passed out. My period's late.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's tachy and hypotensive at 84 systolic, belly's rigid. I'll get a pregnancy test going.

cases.title: Acute mesenteric ischemia
cases.chiefComplaint: 74F — severe pain out of proportion to exam; AFib
cases.diagnosis: Acute mesenteric ischemia (SMA embolus)
cases.context: 74-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation (not anticoagulated) with sudden severe diffuse abdominal pain that is OUT OF PROPORTION to a relatively benign abdominal exam, plus vomiting and an urge to defecate. True diagnosis is acute mesenteric ischemia, likely an SMA embolus. Labs may show a high lactate and metabolic acidosis (late); a soft exam early lulls the unwary. CORRECT PATH: high index of suspicion in the elderly/AFib/vascular patient, resuscitate, send lactate/labs, give broad-spectrum antibiotics and heparin (anticoagulation), and obtain URGENT CT ANGIOGRAPHY of the abdomen/pelvis; consult VASCULAR SURGERY (and general surgery) emergently for revascularization/embolectomy or resection of necrotic bowel; avoid vasoconstrictors that worsen splanchnic flow if possible. RED HERRING: pain out of proportion with a normal-feeling belly and normal early labs is falsely reassuring; anchoring on gastroenteritis or constipation. DETERIORATION: delay → bowel infarction, peritonitis, sepsis, death (very high mortality). Early CTA and revascularization save bowel and life.
criticalActions: Suspect mesenteric ischemia with pain out of proportion to exam in an elderly/AFib/vascular patient
Send lactate and laboratory studies and resuscitate
Give broad-spectrum antibiotics
Start anticoagulation (heparin)
Obtain urgent CT angiography of the abdomen/pelvis
Consult vascular and general surgery emergently for revascularization/resection
Avoid falsely reassuring interpretation of a benign early exam and normal early labs
learningPoints: Pain out of proportion to exam in an at-risk patient is mesenteric ischemia until proven otherwise.
Early exam and labs can be deceptively normal; a normal lactate does not exclude it.
CT angiography is the test of choice; involve vascular surgery emergently.
Mortality is very high — the diagnosis hinges on suspicion and speed.
cases.intro: A 74-year-old woman writhes on the stretcher, describing sudden, agonizing pain across her whole belly that came on an hour ago — yet when you press on her abdomen, it feels surprisingly soft and only mildly tender. She has atrial fibrillation and isn't on blood thinners. She's vomited once and feels she needs to move her bowels.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: The pain is unbearable — but you're barely touching me and it doesn't hurt that much when you push. It doesn't make sense.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her belly's soft but she's in agony. She's in AFib and not anticoagulated.

cases.title: Perforated peptic ulcer
cases.chiefComplaint: 49M — sudden severe epigastric pain, rigid abdomen
cases.diagnosis: Perforated peptic ulcer with pneumoperitoneum
cases.context: 49-year-old man (chronic NSAID use for back pain, H. pylori risk) with sudden severe diffuse abdominal pain that began in the epigastrium, now with a board-like rigid abdomen, guarding, and rebound. True diagnosis is a perforated peptic ulcer with pneumoperitoneum and evolving peritonitis. Upright CXR may show free air under the diaphragm; CT is more sensitive. CORRECT PATH: two IVs and fluid resuscitation, keep NPO, place an NG tube, give IV PROTON PUMP INHIBITOR and BROAD-SPECTRUM ANTIBIOTICS, adequate analgesia, obtain upright CXR/CT, and consult SURGERY emergently for operative repair; send labs, type and screen. RED HERRING: attributing rigid abdomen to gastritis/pancreatitis and delaying surgery; a subtle presentation in the elderly/immunosuppressed. DETERIORATION: delay → generalized peritonitis, septic shock. Early antibiotics, resuscitation, and surgery are definitive.
criticalActions: Recognize peritonitis (rigid abdomen, guarding, rebound) as a surgical emergency
Resuscitate with IV fluids and keep the patient NPO
Place a nasogastric tube
Give IV proton pump inhibitor and broad-spectrum antibiotics
Obtain an upright chest x-ray and/or CT to detect free air
Provide adequate analgesia and send labs/type & screen
Consult surgery emergently for operative repair
learningPoints: A rigid, board-like abdomen is peritonitis until proven otherwise — resuscitate and call surgery.
Free air under the diaphragm on upright CXR supports perforation; CT is more sensitive.
Give antibiotics and a PPI, keep NPO, and place an NG tube while arranging the OR.
NSAID use and H. pylori are common risk factors for perforated peptic ulcer.
cases.intro: A 49-year-old man lies very still, afraid to move, after a sudden knife-like pain erupted in his upper abdomen an hour ago and spread everywhere. He takes ibuprofen daily for back pain. His abdomen is rigid as a board, and he winces sharply when you lift your hand off it.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It hit me all at once in my stomach — now it hurts everywhere and I can't move without it stabbing.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: His belly's rigid with rebound, he's febrile and tachycardic. He takes ibuprofen every day.

cases.title: Variceal upper GI hemorrhage
cases.chiefComplaint: 58M — massive hematemesis, cirrhosis, hypotension
cases.diagnosis: Bleeding esophageal varices in cirrhosis
cases.context: 58-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis presents vomiting large volumes of bright red blood with melena, hypotensive and tachycardic. True diagnosis is bleeding esophageal varices (variceal upper GI hemorrhage) in portal hypertension. CORRECT PATH: two large-bore IVs, type and crossmatch, activate MASSIVE TRANSFUSION with a RESTRICTIVE transfusion strategy (target hemoglobin ~7 g/dL), correct coagulopathy/thrombocytopenia as needed; give a VASOACTIVE agent (octreotide) and PROPHYLACTIC ANTIBIOTICS (ceftriaxone — reduces mortality in cirrhotic GI bleed), plus a PPI; protect the airway for massive hematemesis/altered mental status (aspiration risk) and arrange EMERGENT ENDOSCOPY for banding; balloon tamponade or TIPS as rescue. RED HERRING: over-transfusing to a high hemoglobin (worsens portal pressure and rebleeding); forgetting antibiotics. DETERIORATION: airway soiling/aspiration, under-resuscitation, or delayed endoscopy → exsanguination. Octreotide + antibiotics + endoscopy control it.
criticalActions: Place two large-bore IVs and type & crossmatch
Transfuse with a restrictive strategy (target hemoglobin ~7 g/dL) and correct coagulopathy
Start a vasoactive agent (octreotide)
Give prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone)
Give a proton pump inhibitor
Protect the airway in massive hematemesis / altered patients
Arrange emergent endoscopy for band ligation (TIPS/balloon tamponade as rescue)
learningPoints: In cirrhotic GI bleeds, prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone) reduce mortality — don't forget them.
Use a restrictive transfusion target (~7 g/dL); over-transfusion raises portal pressure and rebleeding.
Octreotide reduces splanchnic/portal flow; endoscopy provides definitive band ligation.
Protect the airway early in massive hematemesis to prevent aspiration.
cases.intro: A 58-year-old man with known liver cirrhosis is retching up large amounts of bright red blood into a basin, pale and sweaty. He's passed black tarry stools all day. His abdomen is distended with fluid and his skin is jaundiced. His blood pressure is low and he's beginning to look drowsy.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: There's so much blood… I can't stop throwing it up…

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Big volume hematemesis, pressure 86 over 54, heart rate 124. He's a known cirrhotic. He's getting sleepy.

cases.title: Testicular torsion
cases.chiefComplaint: 16M — sudden severe testicular pain, high-riding testis
cases.diagnosis: Testicular torsion
cases.context: 16-year-old boy with sudden severe left testicular pain waking him from sleep, nausea, and vomiting; exam shows a swollen, high-riding testis with a transverse lie and an ABSENT cremasteric reflex. True diagnosis is testicular torsion — a time-critical surgical emergency (salvage rate falls sharply after 6 hours). CORRECT PATH: recognize it CLINICALLY, provide analgesia, and consult UROLOGY IMMEDIATELY for emergent surgical exploration and detorsion/orchiopexy; obtain a Doppler ultrasound only if it will NOT delay surgery (absent flow supports it, but imaging must not postpone the OR when suspicion is high); attempt manual detorsion ('open book') as a temporizing measure if urology is delayed. RED HERRING: diagnosing epididymitis and treating with antibiotics/discharge — the disastrous miss; a normal cremasteric reflex is reassuring but not definitive. DETERIORATION: delay to OR → testicular infarction and loss. Immediate urology and surgery preserve the testis.
criticalActions: Recognize testicular torsion clinically (sudden pain, high-riding testis, absent cremasteric reflex)
Consult urology immediately for emergent surgical exploration
Provide analgesia
Obtain Doppler ultrasound only if it does not delay surgery
Attempt manual detorsion as a temporizing measure if surgery is delayed
Avoid misdiagnosing as epididymitis and discharging
Recognize the time-sensitive salvage window (<6 hours)
learningPoints: Testicular torsion is a clinical, time-critical diagnosis — call urology; don't let imaging delay the OR.
Absent cremasteric reflex and a high-riding, transverse testis support torsion.
Salvage rates fall sharply after ~6 hours from onset.
Don't anchor on epididymitis — a missed torsion loses the testis.
cases.intro: A 16-year-old boy is pale and nauseated, holding himself gingerly, after being woken two hours ago by sudden severe pain in his left testicle. He's vomited twice. The left testicle is swollen, sitting high and sideways, and exquisitely tender.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It woke me up out of a dead sleep — the pain's unbearable and I keep throwing up.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Left testicle's high-riding and very tender, no cremasteric reflex on that side.

cases.title: Ovarian torsion
cases.chiefComplaint: 23F — sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, vomiting, adnexal mass
cases.diagnosis: Ovarian (adnexal) torsion
cases.context: 23-year-old woman with sudden severe intermittent right lower quadrant/pelvic pain, nausea and vomiting, and a tender adnexal fullness; often associated with an ovarian cyst/mass or ovulation induction. True diagnosis is ovarian torsion. Doppler ultrasound may show an enlarged ovary with abnormal/absent flow and 'whirlpool' sign — but flow can be PRESENT and does not exclude torsion (the ovary has dual blood supply). CORRECT PATH: pregnancy test, analgesia/antiemetics, pelvic ULTRASOUND WITH DOPPLER, and emergent GYNECOLOGY consult for laparoscopy and DETORSION (ovary-sparing) — do not rely on preserved Doppler flow to rule it out when clinical suspicion is high. RED HERRING: normal Doppler flow falsely reassuring; anchoring on ovarian cyst, appendicitis, or renal colic. DETERIORATION: delay → ovarian necrosis and loss of fertility. Emergent surgery preserves the ovary.
criticalActions: Obtain a pregnancy test
Provide analgesia and antiemetics
Obtain pelvic ultrasound with Doppler
Recognize that preserved Doppler flow does NOT exclude ovarian torsion
Consult gynecology emergently for laparoscopy and ovary-sparing detorsion
Avoid anchoring on ovarian cyst/appendicitis/renal colic
Expedite surgery when clinical suspicion is high
learningPoints: Ovarian torsion can have preserved Doppler flow (dual blood supply) — don't rule it out on flow alone.
Sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain with an adnexal mass and vomiting suggests torsion.
Emergent gynecologic surgery with detorsion preserves the ovary and fertility.
Always check a pregnancy test in reproductive-age women with pelvic pain.
cases.intro: A 23-year-old woman clutches her right lower abdomen, pale and vomiting, describing waves of severe pelvic pain that started abruptly a few hours ago. She has a known ovarian cyst. The right side of her pelvis is tender with a fullness, and the pain comes and goes in intense surges.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It comes in waves and takes my breath away — I've thrown up twice. My doctor said I had a cyst on that side.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's tender in the right adnexa with a palpable fullness. Pregnancy test is pending.

cases.title: Thyroid storm
cases.chiefComplaint: 42F — fever, tachycardia/AFib, agitation, known hyperthyroid
cases.diagnosis: Thyroid storm (thyrotoxic crisis)
cases.context: 42-year-old woman with Graves disease (stopped her methimazole) with high fever, tachyarrhythmia (AFib with RVR), agitation/delirium, tremor, vomiting/diarrhea, and heart-failure signs, precipitated by an infection. True diagnosis is thyroid storm.
