CORRECT PATH and ORDER matter: (1) BETA-BLOCKADE with propranolol (controls adrenergic symptoms and blocks T4→T3 conversion); (2) a THIONAMIDE (PTU preferred in storm — blocks synthesis and peripheral conversion) BEFORE iodine; (3) IODINE (e.g., SSKI/Lugol) given at least ~1 hour AFTER the thionamide to block hormone release (giving iodine first can fuel synthesis); (4) HYDROCORTISONE (reduces T4→T3 conversion and treats relative adrenal insufficiency); plus aggressive cooling (acetaminophen, NOT aspirin — salicylates displace thyroid hormone), fluids, and treat the precipitant (infection). RED HERRING: giving iodine before the thionamide; using aspirin for fever; anchoring on sepsis alone. DETERIORATION: untreated storm → high-output failure, arrhythmia, hyperthermia, death. The four-pronged regimen in order controls it.
criticalActions: Give beta-blockade (propranolol) for adrenergic symptoms
Give a thionamide (PTU preferred) to block synthesis and peripheral conversion
Give iodine (SSKI/Lugol) at least ~1 hour AFTER the thionamide
Give hydrocortisone (stress-dose steroids)
Cool aggressively with acetaminophen and external measures (avoid aspirin)
Resuscitate with IV fluids and identify/treat the precipitant (e.g., infection)
Manage the tachyarrhythmia and heart failure
learningPoints: Order matters: beta-blocker + thionamide first, then iodine at least an hour later, plus steroids.
PTU is preferred in storm because it also blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion.
Avoid aspirin for fever — salicylates displace thyroid hormone from binding proteins.
Always look for and treat the precipitant (commonly infection).
cases.intro: A 42-year-old woman is agitated and drenched in sweat, her heart galloping irregularly on the monitor. Her family says she has an overactive thyroid but stopped her pills, and she's had a cough and fever for days. She's tremulous, confused, burning up at 40.1°C, and her heart rate is over 150 and irregular.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She has a thyroid problem and ran out of her medicine. She's been feverish, and today she got confused and her heart is pounding.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Temp 40.1, AFib with a rate over 150, she's agitated and diaphoretic.

cases.title: Adrenal (Addisonian) crisis
cases.chiefComplaint: 38F — hypotension refractory to fluids, on chronic steroids, vomiting
cases.diagnosis: Acute adrenal crisis
cases.context: 38-year-old woman on chronic prednisone (autoimmune disease) who stopped steroids during a vomiting illness presents with profound weakness, vomiting, abdominal pain, and hypotension that is REFRACTORY to fluids and pressors, with hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypoglycemia. True diagnosis is acute adrenal crisis (secondary adrenal insufficiency from abrupt steroid cessation plus a stressor; primary/Addison also possible with hyperpigmentation). CORRECT PATH: recognize refractory shock in a steroid-dependent patient, give IMMEDIATE HYDROCORTISONE (do not delay for cortisol/ACTH testing — draw them if quick but treat first), aggressive IV fluids with DEXTROSE (treat hypoglycemia) and isotonic saline (treat hyponatremia/volume), treat hyperkalemia if severe, and identify/treat the precipitant (infection, missed steroids). RED HERRING: treating as sepsis alone and escalating pressors while omitting steroids — hypotension won't resolve without cortisol. DETERIORATION: delayed steroids → persistent shock, arrest. Hydrocortisone plus fluids/glucose reverse it.
criticalActions: Recognize adrenal crisis (refractory hypotension in a steroid-dependent patient, hyponatremia/hyperkalemia/hypoglycemia)
Give IV hydrocortisone immediately (do not delay for cortisol/ACTH testing)
Resuscitate with isotonic fluids and add dextrose for hypoglycemia
Correct hyponatremia/volume with isotonic saline
Treat significant hyperkalemia
Identify and treat the precipitant (infection, missed steroid doses)
Draw a cortisol level if it does not delay treatment
learningPoints: In refractory shock in a steroid-dependent patient, give hydrocortisone before it resolves — don't wait for labs.
Classic labs: hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, and hypoglycemia.
Abrupt cessation of chronic steroids plus a stressor precipitates crisis.
Fluids and pressors alone won't fix the hypotension without cortisol.
cases.intro: A 38-year-old woman lies limp and weak on the stretcher, having vomited repeatedly over two days. She takes prednisone daily for lupus but stopped it when she couldn't keep anything down. Her blood pressure is dangerously low and barely nudges upward despite the fluids already running.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've been throwing up for two days and I feel like I'm fading… I couldn't keep my steroid pills down.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Pressure's 78 over 46 and not really responding to the liter she's had. She's on chronic prednisone.

cases.title: Severe hyperkalemia
cases.chiefComplaint: 63M — weakness, missed dialysis, peaked T waves / wide QRS
cases.diagnosis: Severe hyperkalemia with cardiac toxicity
cases.context: 63-year-old man with end-stage renal disease who missed dialysis presents with weakness and palpitations; ECG shows peaked T waves progressing to a widened QRS and a sine-wave pattern. True diagnosis is severe hyperkalemia (K ~7.5) with cardiac membrane instability. CORRECT PATH and ORDER: (1) IV CALCIUM (gluconate or chloride) FIRST to STABILIZE THE MYOCARDIUM — this is the immediate priority with ECG changes; (2) SHIFT potassium intracellularly with INSULIN + DEXTROSE and nebulized albuterol (and bicarbonate if acidotic); (3) ELIMINATE potassium from the body — the definitive treatment in ESRD is EMERGENT DIALYSIS (loop diuretics if making urine; GI binders act slowly); continuous cardiac monitoring; recheck potassium and glucose. RED HERRING: reaching for a binder or diuretic while ignoring the ECG changes that demand immediate calcium; forgetting that shifting is temporary and dialysis is definitive. DETERIORATION: without calcium and elimination → sine wave, VF, asystole. Calcium then shift then dialysis.
criticalActions: Obtain an ECG and recognize hyperkalemic changes (peaked T waves, wide QRS, sine wave)
Give IV calcium first to stabilize the myocardium
Shift potassium with insulin plus dextrose and nebulized albuterol (bicarbonate if acidotic)
Arrange emergent dialysis as the definitive treatment in ESRD
Use loop diuretics if the patient makes urine; GI binders as adjuncts
Provide continuous cardiac monitoring
Recheck potassium and glucose after treatment
learningPoints: With hyperkalemic ECG changes, give calcium first to stabilize the membrane.
Insulin/dextrose and albuterol only SHIFT potassium temporarily — arrange definitive removal.
In ESRD, emergent dialysis is the definitive treatment.
Monitor glucose after insulin, and keep the patient on continuous cardiac monitoring.
cases.intro: A 63-year-old man on dialysis feels weak and 'off'; he missed his last two sessions. On the monitor his heart rate is slow and the T waves are tall and peaked, with QRS complexes that look increasingly wide. He says his legs feel heavy and his heart is skipping.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I missed dialysis this week — now I'm so weak and my heart feels funny.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Rate's in the 40s with big peaked T waves and the QRS is widening. He's an ESRD patient.

cases.title: Sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia
cases.chiefComplaint: 76F — altered/diaphoretic, glucose 32, on glipizide
cases.diagnosis: Sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia
cases.context: 76-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes on glipizide, found confused and diaphoretic by family; fingerstick glucose is 32. True diagnosis is sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia — importantly, this is PROLONGED and RECURRENT because sulfonylureas keep stimulating insulin release. CORRECT PATH: give IV DEXTROSE immediately (D50 if IV; IM glucagon if no access), recheck glucose and give repeat dextrose/continuous D10 infusion as needed, and then FEED the patient; because sulfonylurea hypoglycemia RECURS, do NOT discharge after a single correction — admit/observe, and consider OCTREOTIDE (suppresses insulin secretion) for refractory or recurrent hypoglycemia. Investigate contributors (renal impairment, missed meals, drug interactions). RED HERRING: giving one amp of D50, seeing the patient wake up, and discharging — she will crash again hours later. DETERIORATION: premature discharge → recurrent severe hypoglycemia, seizure, brain injury. Dextrose + feeding + octreotide + observation are the safe path.
criticalActions: Check the fingerstick glucose and give IV dextrose immediately (glucagon IM if no IV access)
Recheck glucose and repeat dextrose / start a D10 infusion as needed
Feed the patient once alert
Recognize that sulfonylurea hypoglycemia recurs and admit/observe (do not discharge after one correction)
Consider octreotide for recurrent or refractory hypoglycemia
Investigate contributors (renal impairment, missed meals, drug interactions)
learningPoints: Sulfonylurea hypoglycemia is prolonged and recurrent — a single amp of D50 is not enough.
Octreotide suppresses insulin release and is the antidote for refractory sulfonylurea hypoglycemia.
Feed the patient and admit/observe rather than discharging after correction.
Glucagon works if there is no IV access but is less reliable in the glycogen-depleted patient.
cases.intro: A 76-year-old woman is brought in confused and sweating, unable to answer questions clearly. Her daughter says she takes a pill for diabetes — glipizide — and skipped lunch today. The paramedics' fingerstick glucose reads 32.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She got all confused and clammy this afternoon. She takes glipizide for her sugar and didn't eat much today.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Glucose is 32. She's diaphoretic and altered. IV is in.

cases.title: Acetaminophen (paracetamol) overdose
cases.chiefComplaint: 19F — intentional overdose, initially well, high APAP level
cases.diagnosis: Acetaminophen overdose (risk of hepatotoxicity)
cases.context: 19-year-old woman brought in after an intentional ingestion of a large amount of acetaminophen ~4 hours ago; she currently feels only mildly nauseated and looks deceptively WELL (the dangerous latent phase). True diagnosis is acetaminophen overdose with impending hepatotoxicity if untreated. CORRECT PATH: obtain a 4-HOUR (or later) acetaminophen LEVEL and plot on the RUMACK-MATTHEW NOMOGRAM; check LFTs, INR, renal function, and co-ingestants (salicylate level, ECG); give activated charcoal if within ~1-2 hours of ingestion and airway safe; start N-ACETYLCYSTEINE (NAC) if the level is above the treatment line, if timing is unknown/late, if there is evidence of hepatotoxicity, or for staggered/large ingestions — when in doubt, treat, because NAC is nearly fully protective when started early (<8 hours); psychiatric evaluation for the intentional ingestion. RED HERRING: a well-appearing patient with normal early labs is falsely reassuring — hepatotoxicity is delayed 24-72 hours. DETERIORATION: withholding NAC → fulminant hepatic failure. Timely NAC prevents injury.
criticalActions: Obtain a 4-hour (or timed) acetaminophen level and apply the Rumack-Matthew nomogram
Check LFTs, INR, renal function, and screen for co-ingestants (salicylate level, ECG)
Give activated charcoal if within ~1-2 hours and the airway is protected
Start N-acetylcysteine if above the treatment line, timing unknown/late, or evidence of hepatotoxicity
Treat empirically when in doubt (NAC is highly effective when started early)
Arrange psychiatric evaluation for the intentional overdose
Recognize the deceptively well-appearing latent phase
learningPoints: Use a 4-hour-or-later level on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram to guide NAC.
NAC is nearly fully hepatoprotective when started within 8 hours — when in doubt, treat.
A well-appearing patient with normal early labs does not exclude toxicity; injury is delayed.
Always screen for co-ingestants and arrange psychiatric evaluation after intentional overdose.
cases.intro: A 19-year-old woman is brought in by a friend who found empty acetaminophen bottles and a note; the ingestion was about four hours ago. She's tearful but physically feels only a little queasy and otherwise looks well, which is deceptive for what may be coming.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I took a lot of Tylenol a few hours ago… I feel okay, just a little sick to my stomach.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: I found the empty bottles and a note. She won't tell me exactly how many.

cases.title: Salicylate (aspirin) toxicity
cases.chiefComplaint: 52M — tinnitus, hyperventilation, mixed acid-base, confusion
cases.diagnosis: Salicylate (aspirin) toxicity
cases.context: 52-year-old man with tinnitus, nausea/vomiting, hyperventilation (tachypnea), diaphoresis, low-grade fever, and confusion after a large aspirin ingestion. True diagnosis is salicylate toxicity with the classic MIXED PICTURE: primary RESPIRATORY ALKALOSIS plus a primary ANION-GAP METABOLIC ACIDOSIS. CORRECT PATH: check salicylate level (trend serially — levels can rise, especially with enteric-coated), VBG/ABG, electrolytes, glucose, and an acetaminophen level; give activated charcoal if appropriate; the cornerstone is SERUM AND URINE ALKALINIZATION with a sodium BICARBONATE infusion (target urine pH 7.5-8) to enhance elimination and reduce CNS penetration; aggressively REPLETE POTASSIUM (needed for effective alkalinization); glucose for neuroglycopenia even if serum glucose is normal; and arrange HEMODIALYSIS for severe toxicity (altered mental status, high/rising levels, renal failure, pulmonary edema, refractory acidosis). AVOID INTUBATION if possible — mechanical ventilation often cannot match the patient's high minute ventilation and a drop in pH causes rapid deterioration; if intubation is unavoidable, match the high respiratory rate and give bicarbonate. RED HERRING: sedating/intubating for agitation without appreciating the ventilation trap; a single 'normal-ish' level missing a rising trend. DETERIORATION: peri-intubation acidosis, under-alkalinization → CNS/cardiac collapse. Alkalinization, potassium, and dialysis are key.
criticalActions: Check serial salicylate levels, VBG/ABG, electrolytes, glucose, and an acetaminophen level
Recognize the mixed respiratory alkalosis + anion-gap metabolic acidosis
Start a sodium bicarbonate infusion for serum/urine alkalinization (urine pH 7.5-8)
Aggressively replete potassium to enable effective alkalinization
Give glucose for neuroglycopenia even with a normal serum glucose
Avoid intubation if possible; if unavoidable, match the high minute ventilation and give bicarbonate
Arrange hemodialysis for severe toxicity (altered mentation, high/rising level, renal failure, refractory acidosis)
learningPoints: Salicylate toxicity = mixed respiratory alkalosis and anion-gap metabolic acidosis; trend levels serially.
Alkalinization with bicarbonate (and potassium repletion) enhances elimination and protects the CNS.
Avoid intubation when possible — the ventilator often can't match the patient's compensatory hyperventilation.
Hemodialysis for altered mental status, very high/rising levels, renal failure, or refractory acidosis.
cases.intro: A 52-year-old man is breathing fast and deep, sweating, and complaining that his ears won't stop ringing. He's nauseated and increasingly confused. His family found an empty bottle of aspirin. He's febrile without an obvious infection and his respiratory rate is in the mid-30s.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My ears are ringing so loud… I feel sick and I can't stop breathing hard.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: We found an empty aspirin bottle. He's been getting more confused in the last hour.

cases.title: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose
cases.chiefComplaint: 31F — altered, seizure, wide QRS after amitriptyline OD
cases.diagnosis: Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose
cases.context: 31-year-old woman with depression brought in after an amitriptyline overdose, now with altered mental status, a seizure, hypotension, anticholinergic signs, and a WIDE QRS with a terminal R wave in aVR on ECG. True diagnosis is TCA overdose with sodium-channel blockade (cardiotoxicity) and CNS toxicity. CORRECT PATH: the key intervention for QRS widening (>100 ms), ventricular dysrhythmia, or hypotension is SODIUM BICARBONATE boluses (narrows the QRS by overcoming sodium-channel blockade and alkalinizing) — titrate to QRS narrowing; secure the airway for altered mental status/seizure; treat seizures with BENZODIAZEPINES (avoid phenytoin); treat hypotension with fluids and norepinephrine; AVOID class IA/IC and III antiarrhythmics; activated charcoal only if early and airway protected; consider lipid emulsion/ECMO for refractory cardiotoxicity. RED HERRING: giving physostigmine (contraindicated — can precipitate asystole/seizure) or a sodium-channel-blocking antiarrhythmic. DETERIORATION: without bicarbonate, the QRS widens to VT/VF and refractory hypotension. Bicarbonate is the antidote for cardiotoxicity.
criticalActions: Obtain an ECG and recognize sodium-channel blockade (wide QRS, terminal R in aVR)
Give sodium bicarbonate boluses for QRS >100 ms, dysrhythmia, or hypotension; titrate to QRS narrowing
Secure the airway for altered mental status/seizure
Treat seizures with benzodiazepines (avoid phenytoin)
Treat hypotension with fluids and norepinephrine
Avoid class IA/IC/III antiarrhythmics and physostigmine
Consider lipid emulsion/ECMO for refractory cardiotoxicity
learningPoints: Sodium bicarbonate is the antidote for TCA cardiotoxicity — titrate to QRS narrowing.
A wide QRS with a terminal R wave in aVR signals sodium-channel blockade.
Treat seizures with benzodiazepines; avoid phenytoin and physostigmine.
Avoid sodium-channel-blocking antiarrhythmics; consider lipid emulsion for refractory toxicity.
cases.intro: A 31-year-old woman is brought in drowsy and confused after taking a handful of her amitriptyline. In the room she has a brief generalized seizure. Her skin is flushed and dry, her pupils dilated, and the monitor shows a fast rhythm with wide, bizarre QRS complexes. Her blood pressure is low.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She takes amitriptyline for depression and I think she took a lot. She just had a seizure in the hallway.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: QRS looks wide on the monitor, she's hypotensive at 88, and she's barely responsive.

cases.title: Beta-blocker / calcium-channel-blocker overdose
cases.chiefComplaint: 68M — bradycardia and hypotension after cardiac meds OD
cases.diagnosis: Beta-blocker / calcium-channel blocker overdose
cases.context: 68-year-old man who intentionally overdosed on his metoprolol and diltiazem presents with bradycardia and refractory hypotension. True diagnosis is beta-blocker/CCB toxicity (both cause bradycardia and hypotension; CCB, especially dihydropyridines, may spare heart rate; verapamil/diltiazem cause profound brady-hypotension; CCB toxicity classically causes HYPERGLYCEMIA). CORRECT PATH: IV fluids, IV CALCIUM (especially for CCB), GLUCAGON (for beta-blocker — bypasses the beta receptor to raise cAMP), ATROPINE/pacing for bradycardia, vasopressors (norepinephrine/epinephrine), and the key advanced therapy HIGH-DOSE INSULIN EUGLYCEMIA THERAPY (HIET — high-dose insulin with dextrose and potassium monitoring, an inotrope in toxin-induced cardiogenic shock); consider lipid emulsion and ECMO for refractory shock; whole-bowel irrigation for sustained-release ingestions. RED HERRING: treating as simple bradycardia with atropine alone (often ineffective) and under-utilizing calcium, glucagon, and HIET. DETERIORATION: inadequate therapy → refractory cardiogenic shock, arrest. Calcium, glucagon, HIET, and pressors are the toolkit.
criticalActions: Recognize toxin-induced bradycardia/hypotension and place on a monitor with pads
Give IV fluids and IV calcium (especially for calcium-channel-blocker toxicity)
Give glucagon (particularly for beta-blocker toxicity)
Give atropine and use pacing for bradycardia as needed
Start vasopressors (norepinephrine/epinephrine)
Initiate high-dose insulin euglycemia therapy (HIET) with dextrose and potassium monitoring
Consider lipid emulsion and ECMO for refractory shock; whole-bowel irrigation for sustained-release products
learningPoints: High-dose insulin euglycemia therapy is a key inotrope in beta-blocker/CCB cardiogenic shock.
Calcium and glucagon are important early antidotes; atropine alone is often insufficient.
Calcium-channel-blocker toxicity classically causes hyperglycemia.
Consider lipid emulsion and ECMO for refractory toxin-induced shock.
cases.intro: A 68-year-old man is found by his wife barely responsive after swallowing 'a lot' of his heart pills — metoprolol and diltiazem — following an argument. His heart rate is in the 30s and his blood pressure is critically low. He's cool and mottled but still breathing on his own.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He took a bunch of his blood-pressure and heart pills after we fought. I don't know how many.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Rate's 38 and pressure's 72 over 44. Atropine's drawn up — what else do you want?

cases.title: Carbon monoxide poisoning
cases.chiefComplaint: 44M — headache, confusion, whole family sick; faulty furnace
cases.diagnosis: Carbon monoxide poisoning
cases.context: 44-year-old man with headache, dizziness, nauseous, and confusion; his whole family became ill over the same evening at home with a malfunctioning furnace in winter. True diagnosis is carbon monoxide poisoning. KEY PITFALL: the standard PULSE OXIMETER READS FALSELY NORMAL (it can't distinguish carboxyhemoglobin from oxyhemoglobin), so a 'normal' SpO2 is falsely reassuring. CORRECT PATH: remove from exposure, give HIGH-FLOW 100% OXYGEN via non-rebreather immediately (shortens CO half-life), measure a CARBOXYHEMOGLOBIN level via co-oximetry (venous or arterial), obtain ECG/troponin (myocardial ischemia), consider cyanide co-exposure in smoke/fire victims, and consider HYPERBARIC OXYGEN for severe poisoning (loss of consciousness, neurologic deficits, cardiac ischemia, pregnancy, very high COHb) to reduce delayed neurologic sequelae. Ask about and warn other household members. RED HERRING: a normal pulse-ox and vague flu-like symptoms in winter — the classic miss; sending the family home to the same environment. DETERIORATION: continued exposure, missed cardiac ischemia → collapse, delayed neuropsychiatric injury. Oxygen (± hyperbaric) is the treatment.
criticalActions: Recognize CO poisoning (clustered symptoms, faulty heating source) and remove from exposure
Give high-flow 100% oxygen via non-rebreather immediately
Measure a carboxyhemoglobin level by co-oximetry (do not trust the pulse oximeter)
Obtain ECG and troponin to evaluate for myocardial ischemia
Consider concurrent cyanide toxicity in fire/smoke victims
Consider hyperbaric oxygen for severe poisoning (syncope, neuro deficits, ischemia, pregnancy, high COHb)
Warn/evaluate other household members and address the source
learningPoints: Pulse oximetry is falsely normal in CO poisoning — measure carboxyhemoglobin by co-oximetry.
High-flow 100% oxygen shortens the CO half-life; start it immediately.
Consider hyperbaric oxygen for severe poisoning to reduce delayed neurologic sequelae.
Think of CO when multiple household members are simultaneously ill in winter.
cases.intro: A 44-year-old man arrives with a pounding headache, dizziness, and trouble concentrating. He mentions that his wife and kids all got headaches and nausea the same evening, at home, where the furnace has been acting up in the cold snap. His pulse oximeter reads a reassuring 99% — but something doesn't add up.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My head is splitting and I feel foggy. The weird thing is my whole family got sick tonight at the same time.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: His sat reads 99% on room air, but he's confused and the story sounds like the furnace.

cases.title: Serotonin syndrome
cases.chiefComplaint: 29M — agitation, clonus, hyperthermia after new SSRI + tramadol
cases.diagnosis: Serotonin syndrome
cases.context: 29-year-old man on an SSRI who was recently started on tramadol (and used an over-the-counter dextromethorphan cold medicine) presents with agitation, diaphoresis, tremor, and — the KEY DISTINGUISHING FEATURE — NEUROMUSCULAR HYPEREXCITABILITY that is greater in the LOWER extremities: inducible/spontaneous CLONUS, hyperreflexia, and rigidity, plus hyperthermia. True diagnosis is serotonin syndrome (Hunter criteria: clonus + agitation/diaphoresis/tremor/hyperreflexia in the setting of a serotonergic agent). CORRECT PATH: STOP all serotonergic agents; aggressive supportive care — BENZODIAZEPINES for agitation/rigidity, IV fluids, and active COOLING for hyperthermia; consider CYPROHEPTADINE (serotonin antagonist) for moderate-severe cases; for severe hyperthermia/rigidity with respiratory compromise, intubate with NON-DEPOLARIZING paralysis (avoid succinylcholine if hyperkalemia risk) and cool aggressively; treat with benzodiazepines rather than antipyretics (fever is from muscle activity). RED HERRING: mistaking it for neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS is slower onset, lead-pipe rigidity, bradyreflexia, dopamine antagonist) or anticholinergic toxidrome — the clonus/hyperreflexia and serotonergic drug history distinguish it. DETERIORATION: continued serotonergic agents, uncontrolled hyperthermia → rhabdomyolysis, DIC, death. Stopping the agent, benzodiazepines, cooling, ± cyproheptadine control it.
criticalActions: Recognize serotonin syndrome (clonus/hyperreflexia > lower extremities, agitation, hyperthermia with serotonergic drugs)
Stop all serotonergic agents
Give benzodiazepines for agitation and neuromuscular hyperactivity
Provide IV fluids and active cooling for hyperthermia
Consider cyproheptadine for moderate-to-severe cases
For severe cases, intubate and use non-depolarizing paralysis with aggressive cooling
Distinguish it from neuroleptic malignant syndrome and anticholinergic toxidrome
learningPoints: Clonus and hyperreflexia (lower-extremity predominant) plus a serotonergic agent define serotonin syndrome.
Treat with benzodiazepines, cooling, and cessation of serotonergic drugs; cyproheptadine for moderate/severe cases.
Common culprits/combinations: SSRIs with tramadol, dextromethorphan, linezolid, MAOIs.
Distinguish from NMS (lead-pipe rigidity, bradyreflexia, slower onset, dopamine antagonist).
cases.intro: A 29-year-old man is agitated and drenched in sweat, trembling, his legs jerking with sustained clonus when you flex his ankles. He was started on tramadol for a back injury a few days ago and is already taking an SSRI; he's also been taking a cough syrup. He's hyperthermic at 39.4°C and his reflexes are brisk.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I feel wired and shaky, like I can't hold still, and I'm burning up.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: His doctor added tramadol a few days ago on top of his antidepressant, and he's had a cold so he's been taking cough medicine too.

cases.title: Toxic alcohol ingestion (ethylene glycol)
cases.chiefComplaint: 47M — intoxicated-appearing, high anion gap, osmolar gap, AKI
cases.diagnosis: Ethylene glycol poisoning
cases.context: 47-year-old man with alcohol use disorder brought in appearing intoxicated but with no ethanol smell, now with an elevated ANION-GAP metabolic acidosis, an elevated OSMOLAR GAP, and evolving acute kidney injury with calcium oxalate crystals in the urine. True diagnosis is ethylene glycol (antifreeze) poisoning (methanol is the analogous toxic alcohol → visual symptoms). CORRECT PATH: recognize the high anion-gap acidosis with an osmolar gap; give the antidote FOMEPIZOLE (blocks alcohol dehydrogenase, preventing formation of toxic metabolites — give early, before metabolism produces the acid) — ethanol is an alternative if fomepizole is unavailable; sodium bicarbonate for severe acidemia; thiamine and pyridomine (ethylene glycol) / folate (methanol) as cofactors; and arrange HEMODIALYSIS for severe acidosis, high levels, renal failure, or (methanol) visual symptoms. Send toxic alcohol levels, calculate gaps, and check co-ingestants. RED HERRING: dismissing as simple alcohol intoxication because he 'looks drunk'; a normal osmolar gap late (after metabolism) does not exclude it. DETERIORATION: delayed antidote → worsening acidosis, renal failure, death. Fomepizole early plus dialysis are definitive.
criticalActions: Recognize a high anion-gap metabolic acidosis with an elevated osmolar gap
Send toxic alcohol levels and calculate anion and osmolar gaps
Give fomepizole early (or ethanol if fomepizole unavailable) to block alcohol dehydrogenase
Give sodium bicarbonate for severe acidemia
Give cofactors (thiamine/pyridoxine for ethylene glycol; folate for methanol)
Arrange hemodialysis for severe acidosis, high levels, renal failure, or visual symptoms
Avoid anchoring on simple ethanol intoxication
learningPoints: Toxic alcohols cause a high anion-gap acidosis with an osmolar gap — fomepizole blocks the toxic metabolism.
Give the antidote early, before alcohol dehydrogenase generates the toxic acids.
Hemodialysis for severe acidosis, high levels, renal failure, or methanol visual symptoms.
A 'drunk-appearing' patient without ethanol odor and an anion/osmolar gap should raise suspicion.
cases.intro: A 47-year-old man is brought in appearing drunk — slurred and unsteady — but there's no smell of alcohol on his breath and he's breathing fast and deep. He has a history of heavy drinking and was recently despondent. His initial labs show a wide anion gap and his kidneys look impaired.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's been drinking whatever he can find since he lost his job. He seems drunk but there's no booze smell this time.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's breathing fast, anion gap is wide, and there's no ethanol on his breath.

cases.title: Exertional heat stroke
cases.chiefComplaint: 25M — collapsed running a race, hyperthermic, altered
cases.diagnosis: Exertional heat stroke
cases.context: 25-year-old man collapsed near the end of a summer road race, now with altered mental status (confusion, ataxia), a core temperature of 41.5°C, tachycardia, and hypotension; skin may be sweaty (exertional) rather than dry. True diagnosis is exertional heat stroke — hyperthermia with CNS dysfunction. The single most important determinant of outcome is the SPEED OF COOLING. CORRECT PATH: begin IMMEDIATE aggressive cooling — COLD-WATER / ICE-WATER IMMERSION is the most effective method (or evaporative cooling with misting and fans, ice packs to groin/axillae/neck); target core temp ~38.5-39°C then stop to avoid overshoot; measure CORE temperature (rectal/esophageal), give IV fluids, protect the airway, treat seizures with benzodiazepines; check for and treat complications (rhabdomyolysis/CK, hyperkalemia, AKI, coagulopathy/DIC, transaminitis, hypoglycemia). ANTIPYRETICS (acetaminophen/NSAIDs) DO NOT WORK (this is not a hypothalamic set-point problem) and can harm the liver/kidneys. RED HERRING: relying on antipyretics or slow cooling; assuming skin must be dry. DETERIORATION: delayed cooling → multi-organ failure, death (time-at-temperature dependent). Rapid cooling saves the patient.
criticalActions: Measure a core (rectal/esophageal) temperature
Begin immediate aggressive cooling — cold/ice-water immersion (or evaporative + ice packs)
Stop active cooling around 38.5-39°C to avoid overshoot
Give IV fluids and protect the airway; treat seizures with benzodiazepines
Do NOT rely on antipyretics (ineffective and potentially harmful in heat stroke)
Check for and treat complications (rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, AKI, DIC, transaminitis, hypoglycemia)
Admit for ongoing monitoring
learningPoints: Cooling speed determines outcome — cold-water immersion is the most effective method.
Antipyretics don't work in heat stroke; it's not a set-point problem.
Measure core temperature and stop cooling near 38.5-39°C to avoid overshoot.
Screen for rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, AKI, DIC, and hypoglycemia.
cases.intro: A 25-year-old runner is carried in after collapsing in the final mile of a race on a sweltering day. He's confused, unsteady, and combative, his skin hot and flushed and slick with sweat. His rectal temperature reads 41.5°C and his heart is racing.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: He went down at mile 12 in the heat, confused and staggering. Rectal temp on scene was over 41.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's altered and combative, core temp 41.5, heart rate 138, pressure's soft.

cases.title: Severe accidental hypothermia
cases.chiefComplaint: 60M — found outdoors, core 27°C, bradycardic, obtunded
cases.diagnosis: Severe accidental hypothermia
cases.context: 60-year-old man with alcohol use found down outdoors overnight in winter, obtunded, with a core temperature of 27°C, bradycardia, hypotension, and an ECG showing Osborn (J) waves. True diagnosis is severe accidental hypothermia. KEY PRINCIPLES: handle gently (a cold, irritable myocardium can fibrillate with rough movement), 'the patient is not dead until warm and dead' (continue resuscitation and rewarming even in cardiac arrest until core temp is raised), and prolonged pulse checks. CORRECT PATH: remove wet clothing, prevent further heat loss, ACTIVE REWARMING — external (forced warm air) plus internal (warm IV fluids, warm humidified oxygen) — escalating to extracorporeal rewarming (ECMO/bypass) for severe/arrest; for cardiac arrest in severe hypothermia, limit defibrillation attempts and vasoactive drugs until core temp rises (~30°C) because they are often ineffective and can accumulate; treat gently, monitor for arrhythmia, check glucose/electrolytes, and look for a cause (tox, sepsis, hypothyroid, trauma). RED HERRING: aggressive handling or premature termination of resuscitation; treating bradycardia with pacing/atropine (ineffective and unnecessary if perfusing). DETERIORATION: rough handling → VF; premature cessation → preventable death. Gentle handling + rewarming (± ECMO) are key.
criticalActions: Measure a core temperature and place on a monitor (recognize Osborn/J waves)
Handle the patient gently to avoid precipitating ventricular fibrillation
Remove wet clothing and prevent further heat loss
Begin active rewarming: forced warm air, warm IV fluids, warm humidified oxygen
Escalate to extracorporeal rewarming (ECMO/bypass) for severe hypothermia or arrest
In arrest, limit repeated defibrillation and vasoactive drugs until core temp rises (~30°C); continue prolonged resuscitation ('not dead until warm and dead')
Check glucose/electrolytes and evaluate for a precipitating cause
learningPoints: 'Not dead until warm and dead' — continue resuscitation and rewarming in hypothermic arrest.
Handle gently; the cold myocardium is irritable and prone to ventricular fibrillation.
In severe hypothermic arrest, limit shocks and drugs until the core temperature rises (~30°C).
Osborn (J) waves are a classic ECG finding; escalate to extracorporeal rewarming when severe.
cases.intro: A 60-year-old man is brought in after being found down outside overnight in freezing weather, smelling of alcohol. He's barely responsive, rigid and ice-cold, breathing slowly. His core temperature reads 27°C, his heart rate is in the high 30s, and the monitor shows distinctive J waves at the end of each QRS.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Found him in an alley, down all night in the cold. He's barely breathing — we moved him as gently as we could.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Core temp is 27, rate's 38, and there are J waves on the monitor.

cases.title: Preeclampsia with eclamptic seizure
cases.chiefComplaint: 31F, 34 weeks pregnant — headache, BP 190/120, seizure
cases.diagnosis: Eclampsia (severe preeclampsia with seizure)
cases.context: 31-year-old woman at 34 weeks gestation with severe headache, visual changes, right upper quadrant pain, and edema, with BP 190/120, then a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. True diagnosis is eclampsia (severe preeclampsia with seizure). CORRECT PATH: the anticonvulsant of choice is IV MAGNESIUM SULFATE (loading dose then infusion) — NOT standard antiepileptics; treat SEVERE HYPERTENSION (SBP ≥160 or DBP ≥110) with IV labetalol, hydralazine, or nifedipine to prevent maternal stroke; left lateral positioning, airway/oxygen, monitor for magnesium toxicity (loss of reflexes, respiratory depression — have calcium gluconate ready as the antidote); the DEFINITIVE treatment is DELIVERY — call OBSTETRICS emergently and monitor the fetus; evaluate for HELLP (labs: platelets, LFTs, hemolysis). RED HERRING: treating the seizure with benzodiazepines/phenytoin alone and neglecting magnesium and blood-pressure control; forgetting that delivery is the cure. DETERIORATION: uncontrolled hypertension → maternal intracranial hemorrhage; recurrent seizures; abruption. Magnesium + BP control + delivery are the answer.
criticalActions: Give IV magnesium sulfate for seizure control and prophylaxis
Treat severe hypertension (SBP >=160 / DBP >=110) with IV labetalol, hydralazine, or nifedipine
Protect the airway, give oxygen, and position left lateral
Monitor for magnesium toxicity and keep calcium gluconate available as the antidote
Consult obstetrics emergently — delivery is the definitive treatment
Monitor the fetus
Evaluate for HELLP syndrome (platelets, LFTs, hemolysis)
learningPoints: Magnesium sulfate — not standard antiepileptics — is the treatment/prevention for eclamptic seizures.
Control severe hypertension promptly to prevent maternal intracranial hemorrhage.
Delivery is the definitive cure; involve obstetrics emergently.
Watch for magnesium toxicity (areflexia, respiratory depression); calcium gluconate is the antidote.
cases.intro: A 31-year-old woman, 34 weeks pregnant, is rushed in after a witnessed seizure at home. Beforehand she'd had a pounding headache, blurry vision, and pain under her right ribs. Her blood pressure is 192/120 and her legs and hands are swollen. As you approach, her arms and legs begin to jerk again.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's 34 weeks along. She had a terrible headache and saw spots, then she started convulsing.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Pressure's 192 over 120 and she's seizing again. Fetal monitor's being placed.

cases.title: Postpartum hemorrhage
cases.chiefComplaint: 29F — heavy vaginal bleeding after home delivery, hypotension
cases.diagnosis: Postpartum hemorrhage (uterine atony)
cases.context: 29-year-old woman brought in bleeding heavily after a precipitous delivery at home an hour ago, now pale, tachycardic, and hypotensive with a boggy, poorly contracted uterus. True diagnosis is postpartum hemorrhage, most commonly from UTERINE ATONY (the leading cause; remember the '4 Ts': Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin). CORRECT PATH: two large-bore IVs, type and crossmatch, activate MASSIVE TRANSFUSION as needed; FIRST-LINE for atony is UTERINE FUNDAL MASSAGE plus UTEROTONICS — oxytocin, then add methylergonovine (avoid in hypertension), carboprost (avoid in asthma), and misoprostol; give TRANEXAMIC ACID (reduces mortality in PPH); examine for retained placenta/products and genital tract lacerations (Trauma/Tissue); check coagulation (Thrombin); escalate to balloon tamponade (Bakri), and OB for uterine artery embolization/surgery if refractory. RED HERRING: giving fluids alone without addressing tone (massage + uterotonics) or missing a laceration/retained tissue. DETERIORATION: delayed uterotonics/blood → hemorrhagic shock, DIC. Massage, uterotonics, TXA, and blood control it.
criticalActions: Place two large-bore IVs and type & crossmatch; activate massive transfusion as needed
Perform uterine fundal massage
Give uterotonics (oxytocin first; add methylergonovine/carboprost/misoprostol with attention to contraindications)
Give tranexamic acid
Examine for and address the 4 Ts: retained tissue, genital tract trauma/lacerations, and coagulopathy
Escalate to balloon tamponade (Bakri) if refractory
Consult obstetrics for embolization/surgery in refractory hemorrhage
learningPoints: Uterine atony is the leading cause of PPH — treat with fundal massage plus uterotonics first.
Remember the 4 Ts: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin.
Tranexamic acid reduces mortality in postpartum hemorrhage.
Mind uterotonic contraindications: methylergonovine in hypertension, carboprost in asthma.
cases.intro: A 29-year-old woman who delivered at home an hour ago is brought in soaked in blood, pale and dizzy, still bleeding heavily from the vagina. Her uterus feels soft and boggy above the pubic bone. Her blood pressure is low and her heart is racing.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: The baby came fast at home and she just kept bleeding and bleeding. She's soaked through everything.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's tachy at 126, pressure 84 over 52, and her uterus feels boggy. Bleeding is heavy.

cases.title: Sickle cell acute chest syndrome
cases.chiefComplaint: 20M — sickle cell, chest pain, fever, hypoxia, new infiltrate
cases.diagnosis: Acute chest syndrome in sickle cell disease
cases.context: 20-year-old man with sickle cell disease admitted for a pain crisis now with chest pain, fever, cough, worsening hypoxia, and a NEW pulmonary infiltrate on chest x-ray. True diagnosis is acute chest syndrome (a leading cause of death in sickle cell disease). CORRECT PATH: supplemental OXYGEN to correct hypoxia, adequate ANALGESIA (but avoid over-sedation/hypoventilation that worsens atelectasis), INCENTIVE SPIROMETRY, broad-spectrum ANTIBIOTICS covering typical and ATYPICAL organisms (a cephalosporin plus a macrolide), judicious IV fluids (avoid overload → pulmonary edema), bronchodilators if wheezing, and — the key disease-specific therapy — TRANSFUSION: simple transfusion for moderate disease and EXCHANGE TRANSFUSION for severe/rapidly progressive hypoxia; involve hematology and monitor closely for deterioration to respiratory failure. RED HERRING: treating as simple pneumonia or a routine pain crisis and under-recognizing the need for transfusion; over-hydration and opioid-induced hypoventilation worsen it. DETERIORATION: progressive hypoxia → respiratory failure, multi-organ involvement, death. Oxygen, antibiotics, incentive spirometry, and transfusion control it.
criticalActions: Recognize acute chest syndrome (new infiltrate + respiratory symptoms in sickle cell disease)
Give supplemental oxygen to correct hypoxia
Provide adequate analgesia while avoiding over-sedation/hypoventilation
Encourage incentive spirometry
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics including atypical coverage (cephalosporin + macrolide)
Give judicious IV fluids (avoid overload)
Arrange transfusion — simple for moderate disease, exchange transfusion for severe/progressive hypoxia; involve hematology
learningPoints: Acute chest syndrome = new infiltrate plus respiratory symptoms in sickle cell disease — a leading cause of death.
Cover atypical organisms; incentive spirometry helps prevent progression.
Exchange transfusion is indicated for severe or rapidly progressive disease.
Avoid over-hydration and opioid-induced hypoventilation, which worsen it.
cases.intro: A 20-year-old man with sickle cell disease, in the hospital for a pain crisis, now has sharp chest pain, a cough, and a rising fever. His oxygen level has been drifting down into the high 80s and a chest x-ray shows a new patch of infiltrate in the lower lobe. He's breathing quickly and splinting from the pain.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My chest hurts bad now, worse than the crisis, and I can't catch my breath like before.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Sat's dropped to 88, he's febrile, and the new film shows an infiltrate that wasn't there yesterday.

cases.title: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP)
cases.chiefComplaint: 40F — anemia, thrombocytopenia, confusion, schistocytes
cases.diagnosis: Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura
cases.context: 40-year-old woman with fatigue, bruising, confusion, and low-grade fever; labs show microangiopathic hemolytic anemia (anemia + SCHISTOCYTES on smear), profound THROMBOCYTOPENIA, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin, and a NORMAL coagulation profile (PT/PTT normal, distinguishing it from DIC), with mild renal involvement. True diagnosis is TTP (from ADAMTS13 deficiency). The classic pentad (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, neurologic changes, renal dysfunction, fever) is often INCOMPLETE — the combination of MAHA + thrombocytopenia without another explanation is enough to act. CORRECT PATH: recognize MAHA + thrombocytopenia, send ADAMTS13 activity, and arrange URGENT PLASMA EXCHANGE (plasmapheresis) — the life-saving treatment — with hematology; give corticosteroids; consider rituximab/caplacizumab per hematology. CRITICALLY, do NOT give PLATELET TRANSFUSIONS except for life-threatening bleeding — they can fuel microthrombosis and worsen outcomes. RED HERRING: transfusing platelets for the low count (harmful); mistaking it for DIC (coags are normal in TTP) or ITP. DETERIORATION: delay to plasma exchange → stroke, cardiac ischemia, death. Urgent plasma exchange is definitive.
criticalActions: Recognize microangiopathic hemolytic anemia plus thrombocytopenia without another cause
Confirm hemolysis (schistocytes, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin) with a normal coagulation profile
Send ADAMTS13 activity
Arrange urgent plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) with hematology
Give corticosteroids (and consider rituximab/caplacizumab per hematology)
AVOID platelet transfusion except for life-threatening bleeding
Distinguish TTP from DIC (normal coags) and ITP
learningPoints: MAHA + thrombocytopenia without another cause = treat as TTP; the classic pentad is usually incomplete.
Urgent plasma exchange is life-saving — involve hematology immediately.
Avoid platelet transfusions in TTP; they can worsen microthrombosis.
Normal PT/PTT distinguishes TTP from DIC.
cases.intro: A 40-year-old woman comes in confused and covered in scattered bruises, saying she's felt exhausted for days. Her gums are bleeding and she has a low fever. Initial labs show a low hemoglobin, a strikingly low platelet count, and the lab pathologist notes schistocytes on the smear with a normal clotting profile.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've been so tired and I'm bruising everywhere… and I feel foggy, like I can't think straight.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Platelets are very low, she's anemic with schistocytes on the smear, but her PT and PTT are normal.

cases.title: Febrile neutropenia
cases.chiefComplaint: 55F — fever during chemo, looks well but neutropenic
cases.diagnosis: Febrile neutropenia
cases.context: 55-year-old woman on chemotherapy for breast cancer (nadir ~10 days post-cycle) presents with a fever to 38.5°C; she looks relatively well. True diagnosis is febrile neutropenia (ANC < 500, or < 1000 and falling) — an ONCOLOGIC EMERGENCY because a well-appearing neutropenic patient can decompensate into septic shock rapidly. CORRECT PATH: recognize it as an emergency, obtain blood cultures (peripheral AND from any indwelling port/line) and a focused workup (urinalysis/culture, CXR, exam of skin/perirectal area/mucositis/line sites — but AVOID a digital rectal exam to prevent bacteremia), and give EMPIRIC BROAD-SPECTRUM ANTIBIOTICS with ANTIPSEUDOMONAL coverage (e.g., cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam) WITHIN ONE HOUR — do not wait for the ANC to result; add vancomycin for suspected line infection/skin/soft tissue/hemodynamic instability; resuscitate if septic; risk-stratify (MASCC) for admission vs outpatient. RED HERRING: a normal-appearing patient tempting you to delay antibiotics or discharge; withholding antibiotics pending culture/ANC results. DETERIORATION: any delay in antibiotics → rapid progression to septic shock. Door-to-antibiotic time within 1 hour is the metric.
criticalActions: Recognize febrile neutropenia as an oncologic emergency in the chemotherapy patient
Obtain blood cultures (peripheral and from any indwelling line/port) and a focused infectious workup
Give empiric broad-spectrum antipseudomonal antibiotics within 1 hour (cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam)
Do not wait for the ANC result to give antibiotics
Add vancomycin for line infection, skin/soft tissue source, mucositis, or instability
Avoid a digital rectal exam (bacteremia risk)
Resuscitate if septic and risk-stratify for disposition
learningPoints: Give empiric antipseudomonal antibiotics within 1 hour — don't wait for the ANC or for the patient to look sick.
Culture peripherally and from indwelling lines; avoid a digital rectal exam.
A well-appearing neutropenic patient can crash into septic shock quickly.
Add vancomycin for line/skin sources or hemodynamic instability.
cases.intro: A 55-year-old woman on chemotherapy for breast cancer comes in because her home thermometer read 38.6°C. She actually feels only a little tired and looks reasonably well, with a chemo port in her chest. She's about ten days out from her last cycle.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I just have a fever — I honestly feel okay otherwise. I'm on chemo and they told me to come in if I got a temperature.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Temp's 38.6, she has a port. She looks well, but she's mid-chemo.

cases.title: Necrotizing fasciitis
cases.chiefComplaint: 58M diabetic — leg pain out of proportion, systemic toxicity
cases.diagnosis: Necrotizing soft tissue infection (necrotizing fasciitis)
cases.context: 58-year-old man with diabetes presents with a rapidly worsening, exquisitely painful leg over 24 hours — PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION to the modest skin findings — now with systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, hypotension), tense edema extending beyond the erythema, and areas of dusky skin, bullae, and crepitus. True diagnosis is necrotizing fasciitis (a surgical emergency). CORRECT PATH: recognize the RED FLAGS (pain out of proportion, rapid progression, systemic toxicity, crepitus, bullae, skin necrosis), resuscitate aggressively for sepsis, obtain labs (LRINEC supports but does NOT exclude), give BROAD-SPECTRUM ANTIBIOTICS including MRSA coverage plus CLINDAMYCIN (to suppress toxin production), and — most importantly — obtain EMERGENT SURGICAL CONSULTATION for immediate DEBRIDEMENT, which is the definitive and life-saving treatment; do NOT delay surgery for imaging when suspicion is high. RED HERRING: treating as simple cellulitis with antibiotics alone and observing; a benign-looking skin exam early. DETERIORATION: any delay to the OR → progression, septic shock, limb/life loss (mortality is high). Early surgical debridement is the key intervention.
criticalActions: Recognize necrotizing fasciitis red flags (pain out of proportion, rapid progression, systemic toxicity, crepitus, bullae, necrosis)
Resuscitate aggressively for sepsis
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics with MRSA coverage plus clindamycin (toxin suppression)
Obtain emergent surgical consultation for immediate debridement
Do not delay surgery for imaging when clinical suspicion is high
Send supportive labs (understanding LRINEC does not exclude the diagnosis)
Recognize surgical debridement as the definitive, life-saving treatment
learningPoints: Pain out of proportion with systemic toxicity is necrotizing fasciitis until proven otherwise.
Definitive treatment is emergent surgical debridement — antibiotics alone are not enough.
Add clindamycin to suppress toxin production alongside broad-spectrum + MRSA coverage.
A reassuring early skin exam and a low LRINEC do not exclude it — don't delay surgery.
cases.intro: A 58-year-old man with diabetes is writhing with pain in his left leg that has exploded over the last day. The skin looks only mildly red and swollen — far less dramatic than the agony he's in — but the area is tensely swollen well beyond the redness, with a couple of dusky blisters, and you feel crackling under the skin. He's febrile and his pressure is dropping.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: The pain is unbelievable — way worse than it looks. It came on so fast, and now I feel like I'm going to be sick.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's septic — pressure 92 over 56, fever 39. The leg has blisters and I can feel crepitus, but the redness is unimpressive.

cases.title: Toxic shock syndrome
cases.chiefComplaint: 23F — fever, rash, hypotension, tampon use
cases.diagnosis: Toxic shock syndrome (staphylococcal)
cases.context: 23-year-old woman with abrupt high fever, diffuse macular ('sunburn') ERYTHRODERMA, vomiting/diarrhea, myalgias, and hypotension; she has been using tampons and forgot to remove one. True diagnosis is staphylococcal toxic shock syndrome (toxin-mediated). Features: fever, diffuse erythroderma with later desquamation, hypotension, and multi-organ involvement (GI, renal, hepatic, muscular, mucous membranes, CNS). CORRECT PATH: identify and REMOVE the source (retained tampon/packing/wound), aggressive IV FLUID resuscitation and vasopressors for shock, obtain cultures, and give ANTIBIOTICS combining an antistaphylococcal agent (including MRSA coverage — vancomycin) PLUS CLINDAMYCIN (a protein-synthesis inhibitor that suppresses toxin production); consider IVIG for severe/refractory cases; supportive multi-organ care. RED HERRING: mistaking it for a simple viral illness or sepsis without seeking the toxin source; failing to remove the retained tampon. DETERIORATION: source not removed, under-resuscitation → refractory shock, multi-organ failure. Source removal + fluids + clindamycin-containing antibiotics are key.
criticalActions: Recognize toxic shock syndrome (fever, diffuse erythroderma, hypotension, multi-organ involvement)
Identify and remove the source (retained tampon, packing, or wound focus)
Resuscitate aggressively with IV fluids and add vasopressors for refractory shock
Obtain cultures
Give antibiotics combining anti-staphylococcal/MRSA coverage (vancomycin) plus clindamycin (toxin suppression)
Consider IVIG for severe or refractory cases
Provide supportive multi-organ care
learningPoints: Look for and remove the toxin source (e.g., retained tampon) in toxic shock syndrome.
Add clindamycin to suppress toxin production alongside MRSA coverage.
Diffuse 'sunburn' erythroderma with later desquamation and multi-organ involvement is characteristic.
Consider IVIG in severe or refractory cases.
cases.intro: A 23-year-old woman is brought in feverish and flushed, with a diffuse red rash over her whole body that looks like a bad sunburn. She's been vomiting and has diarrhea, aches all over, and her blood pressure is dangerously low. On questioning she realizes she's had a tampon in far longer than she should have.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I feel awful all over and I'm burning up… and I think I left a tampon in way too long.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Diffuse red rash, temp 39.8, pressure's down to 80 over 48. She's toxic-appearing.

cases.title: Pediatric intussusception
cases.chiefComplaint: 1yo — intermittent colic, drawing up legs, currant-jelly stool
cases.diagnosis: Ileocolic intussusception
cases.context: 11-month-old previously healthy infant with episodes of sudden inconsolable crying with drawing up of the legs every 15-20 minutes, lethargy between episodes, vomiting, and a 'currant-jelly' (blood and mucus) stool; a sausage-shaped mass may be palpable in the right abdomen. True diagnosis is ileocolic intussusception (the most common cause of bowel obstruction in this age group). CORRECT PATH: recognize the classic intermittent colicky pattern with lethargy between spells, resuscitate/hydrate and keep NPO, obtain ABDOMINAL ULTRASOUND (the diagnostic test of choice — 'target'/'donut' sign), consult surgery, and for the stable child without perforation/peritonitis perform an AIR (or contrast) ENEMA, which is both DIAGNOSTIC and THERAPEUTIC (reduces the intussusception); surgery is required for failed reduction, perforation, peritonitis, or an unstable child. RED HERRING: attributing lethargy/altered mental status to sepsis or a neurologic cause (intussusception can present with lethargy alone); assuming currant-jelly stool must be present (it's a late finding). DETERIORATION: delay → bowel ischemia, perforation, shock. Ultrasound + air-enema reduction (or surgery) is the path.
criticalActions: Recognize the intermittent colicky pattern with lethargy between episodes (and consider it in unexplained infant lethargy)
Resuscitate/hydrate and keep the child NPO
Obtain abdominal ultrasound (target/donut sign) as the diagnostic test of choice
Consult pediatric surgery
Perform an air (or contrast) enema for reduction in the stable child
Proceed to surgery for failed reduction, perforation, peritonitis, or instability
Do not require 'currant-jelly' stool to make the diagnosis (a late finding)
learningPoints: Intussusception: intermittent colic with legs drawn up and lethargy between episodes — a leading cause of infant bowel obstruction.
Ultrasound is the diagnostic test of choice; an air/contrast enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic.
Lethargy can be the presenting feature and mimic sepsis or a neurologic cause.
Surgery is for failed reduction, perforation, peritonitis, or an unstable child.
cases.intro: An 11-month-old boy is brought in by frantic parents. Every fifteen minutes or so he suddenly shrieks, pulls his knees to his chest, and turns pale — then goes limp and drowsy in between. He's vomited a few times, and the last diaper had dark blood and mucus mixed in it that a nurse describes as looking like currant jelly.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He screams and pulls his legs up, then goes all floppy and sleepy in between. And there was blood and slime in his diaper.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's tachy at 160, drawing up his legs in waves, and there's a currant-jelly stool in the diaper.

cases.title: Kawasaki disease
cases.chiefComplaint: 3yo — 5 days high fever, rash, red eyes, cracked lips
cases.diagnosis: Kawasaki disease
cases.context: 3-year-old with 5 days of high, unremitting fever plus bilateral non-exudative CONJUNCTIVITIS, cracked red lips and 'strawberry' tongue, a polymorphous RASH, swollen/erythematous hands and feet, and a unilateral enlarged cervical lymph node. True diagnosis is Kawasaki disease (medium-vessel vasculitis; classic criteria: fever >=5 days plus 4 of 5 — conjunctivitis, oral changes, rash, extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy). The reason it's a can't-miss is the risk of CORONARY ARTERY ANEURYSMS if untreated. CORRECT PATH: recognize the clinical criteria, obtain inflammatory markers (elevated ESR/CRP), consult PEDIATRICS/CARDIOLOGY and arrange ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY for coronary assessment, and treat with IVIG plus ASPIRIN (a specific exception to the general avoidance of aspirin in children — the benefit in Kawasaki outweighs Reye concerns) to reduce coronary aneurysm risk; treatment is most effective within 10 days of fever onset. RED HERRING: dismissing it as a viral exanthem or scarlet fever and discharging without treatment, missing the window to prevent coronary aneurysms; incomplete/atypical Kawasaki (fewer criteria) in infants. DETERIORATION: untreated → coronary artery aneurysms, myocardial ischemia. Timely IVIG + aspirin prevent the cardiac complications.
criticalActions: Recognize Kawasaki disease (fever >=5 days plus conjunctivitis, oral changes, rash, extremity changes, cervical lymphadenopathy)
Obtain inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) and supportive labs
Consult pediatrics/cardiology and arrange echocardiography for coronary assessment
Treat with IVIG plus aspirin
Treat within 10 days of fever onset to reduce coronary aneurysm risk
Consider incomplete/atypical Kawasaki, especially in infants
Recognize coronary artery aneurysm as the feared complication
learningPoints: Kawasaki disease: fever >=5 days plus 4 of 5 clinical criteria; the danger is coronary artery aneurysms.
Treat with IVIG and aspirin — a specific exception to avoiding aspirin in children.
Treatment within 10 days of fever onset best prevents coronary complications; get an echocardiogram.
Consider incomplete Kawasaki in infants with prolonged fever and fewer criteria.
cases.intro: A 3-year-old girl has had a high fever for five days that won't break. Her eyes are red without any discharge, her lips are cracked and beefy-red with a strawberry-looking tongue, she has a blotchy rash over her trunk, and her hands and feet are swollen and red. Her parents are exhausted and frightened. There's a swollen gland on one side of her neck.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: Five days of fever and nothing brings it down. Her eyes are red, her lips are all cracked, and now her hands and feet are swollen.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Temp 39.5 again. Red eyes, strawberry tongue, rash, swollen hands and feet, and a node on her neck.

cases.title: Pediatric epiglottitis (airway emergency)
cases.chiefComplaint: 4yo — sudden high fever, drooling, tripod, muffled voice
cases.diagnosis: Acute epiglottitis
cases.context: 4-year-old (incompletely vaccinated) with abrupt high fever, severe sore throat, DROOLING, refusal to swallow, a muffled 'hot potato' voice, and anxious TRIPOD positioning; there is soft stridor and the child looks toxic. True diagnosis is acute epiglottitis (supraglottitis) — a true airway emergency (Haemophilus influenzae type b classically; also strep/staph). The CARDINAL RULE: KEEP THE CHILD CALM — any agitation (IV attempts, throat exam with a tongue depressor, lying the child flat, blood draws) can precipitate complete AIRWAY OBSTRUCTION. CORRECT PATH: minimize interventions, keep the child upright in the parent's lap and calm, give humidified oxygen (blow-by), and mobilize the AIRWAY TEAM — the most experienced anesthesiologist/ENT for CONTROLLED airway management in the OPERATING ROOM with a surgical-airway backup; only after the airway is secured give IV ANTIBIOTICS (ceftriaxone) and consider steroids. Do NOT examine the throat or attempt an IV in the ED, and do NOT lay the child flat. RED HERRING: treating as croup/pharyngitis, ordering agitating tests, or examining the airway in the ED. DETERIORATION: agitation/supine positioning/instrumentation → complete obstruction and arrest. Calm, upright, and controlled OR airway management are the priorities.
criticalActions: Recognize epiglottitis as an airway emergency (fever, drooling, tripod, muffled voice, toxic appearance)
Keep the child calm and upright (in the parent's lap); avoid agitation
Do NOT examine the throat, attempt an IV, draw blood, or lay the child flat in the ED
Provide humidified/blow-by oxygen
Mobilize the airway team (ENT/anesthesia) for controlled intubation in the OR with a surgical-airway backup
Give IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone) only after the airway is secured
Consider steroids after airway control
learningPoints: Keep the child calm — agitation or instrumentation can cause complete airway obstruction.
Definitive airway management is controlled intubation in the OR with ENT/anesthesia and a surgical backup.
Defer IVs, bloodwork, throat exam, and supine positioning until the airway is secured.
Classic features: rapid onset, drooling, tripod position, muffled voice, toxic appearance.
cases.intro: A 4-year-old boy sits rigidly upright on his mother's lap, leaning forward with his chin out, drooling and refusing to swallow. He came on fast with a high fever and now speaks in a muffled, hot-potato voice with soft, high stridor on each breath. He looks frightened and ill, and any attempt to move him makes him more distressed.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: It came on so fast — this morning he was fine, now he won't swallow and he's drooling and his voice sounds strange.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's tripoding and drooling, sat's 93, and he gets very upset if we come near his throat.

cases.title: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
cases.chiefComplaint: 70M — palpitations, dyspnea, irregular tachycardia at 150
cases.diagnosis: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
cases.context: 70-year-old man with palpitations, dyspnea, and mild chest discomfort; ECG shows atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response ~150 (irregularly irregular, no discrete P waves). He is currently STABLE (adequate perfusion). True diagnosis is new/uncontrolled AF with RVR. CORRECT PATH: obtain a 12-lead, determine STABILITY — if UNSTABLE (hypotension, ischemic chest pain, pulmonary edema, altered) perform synchronized cardioversion; if STABLE, RATE CONTROL with an IV beta-blocker or non-dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker (diltiazem) — but if there is known reduced EF/decompensated heart failure, prefer amiodarone or digoxin and use caution with AV-nodal blockers. Critically, determine the DURATION: cardioversion of AF present >48 hours (or unknown duration) without adequate anticoagulation or TEE risks thromboembolic stroke. Assess thromboembolic risk (CHA2DS2-VASc) and start/plan ANTICOAGULATION; look for and treat precipitants (sepsis, PE, thyrotoxicosis, ischemia, electrolytes). RED HERRING: a WIDE, irregular, very fast (>200) tachycardia may be AF with WPW pre-excitation — AV-nodal blockers (adenosine, calcium-channel blockers, beta-blockers, digoxin) are CONTRAINDICATED (use procainamide or cardiovert). Also, rate-controlling AF that is compensating for an underlying illness (sepsis/PE) can be harmful — treat the cause. DETERIORATION: cardioverting long-duration AF without anticoagulation → embolic stroke; AV-nodal blockade in WPW → VF. Appropriate rate/rhythm control with attention to duration and preexcitation stabilizes him.
criticalActions: Obtain a 12-lead ECG and confirm AF with RVR
Assess hemodynamic stability; synchronized cardioversion if unstable
Rate-control the stable patient (IV beta-blocker or diltiazem; amiodarone/digoxin if reduced EF/heart failure)
Determine AF duration; avoid cardioversion of AF >48h/unknown duration without anticoagulation or TEE
Assess thromboembolic risk (CHA2DS2-VASc) and address anticoagulation
Recognize AF with WPW pre-excitation (wide, very fast, irregular) and AVOID AV-nodal blockers there
Identify and treat precipitants (sepsis, PE, thyrotoxicosis, ischemia, electrolytes)
learningPoints: Determine stability, duration, and preexcitation before choosing rate vs rhythm control.
Cardioverting AF of >48h/unknown duration without anticoagulation risks embolic stroke.
In AF with WPW (wide, irregular, very fast), AV-nodal blockers are contraindicated — use procainamide or cardiovert.
AF may be compensatory for sepsis/PE/thyrotoxicosis — treat the underlying cause, don't just slow the rate.
cases.intro: A 70-year-old man feels his heart 'fluttering' and can't catch his breath after it started abruptly a few hours ago. He's mildly uncomfortable but perfusing well. The monitor shows an irregularly irregular narrow-complex tachycardia around 150 with no clear P waves.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My heart just started racing and skipping a few hours ago and I got winded. It won't settle down.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Irregularly irregular at about 150, no P waves. Pressure's holding at 126 systolic.

cases.title: Acute liver failure with hepatic encephalopathy
cases.chiefComplaint: 34F — jaundice, confusion, coagulopathy after acetaminophen
cases.diagnosis: Acute liver failure (acetaminophen-induced) with encephalopathy
cases.context: 34-year-old woman with days of malaise, nausea, jaundice, now confused (asterixis) with an elevated INR; she took supratherapeutic acetaminophen over several days for pain. True diagnosis is acute liver failure — coagulopathy (INR >=1.5) plus encephalopathy in a patient without prior cirrhosis. CORRECT PATH: identify the etiology (acetaminophen is the leading cause — send a level and start N-ACETYLCYSTEINE, which benefits ALF even from non-acetaminophen causes and at late presentation); check ammonia, glucose (treat HYPOGLYCEMIA — common and dangerous), electrolytes, lactate, coagulation, renal function, and a viral/toxin workup; manage complications — CEREBRAL EDEMA/raised ICP is the leading cause of death (elevate head of bed, avoid stimulation, treat with hypertonic saline/mannitol for signs of herniation, avoid hypercapnia), treat hypoglycemia, correct coagulopathy ONLY for active bleeding or procedures (don't blindly correct INR — it's a prognostic marker); early consultation and transfer to a LIVER TRANSPLANT CENTER (apply King's College criteria). RED HERRING: reflexively giving FFP to 'fix' the INR (masks prognosis, volume overload) or missing hypoglycemia. DETERIORATION: cerebral edema, hypoglycemia, sepsis. NAC, glucose, ICP management, and transplant referral are the priorities.
criticalActions: Recognize acute liver failure (coagulopathy + encephalopathy without prior cirrhosis)
Send an acetaminophen level and start N-acetylcysteine (beneficial in ALF broadly)
Check and treat hypoglycemia; monitor glucose closely
Manage cerebral edema/raised ICP (head elevation, minimize stimulation, hypertonic saline/mannitol for herniation)
Avoid empiric FFP for the INR alone (correct only for active bleeding/procedures)
Work up the etiology and check ammonia, lactate, renal function, and infection
Consult and transfer early to a liver transplant center (King's College criteria)
learningPoints: Give N-acetylcysteine in acute liver failure — it helps even non-acetaminophen and late presentations.
Cerebral edema is the leading cause of death; treat hypoglycemia aggressively.
Don't reflexively correct the INR with FFP — it's a key prognostic marker.
Refer early to a transplant center using King's College criteria.
cases.intro: A 34-year-old woman is brought in yellow-eyed and confused, her family alarmed by days of vomiting and worsening drowsiness. She'd been taking extra acetaminophen around the clock for a dental abscess. She's jaundiced, her hands flap when she extends them, and her initial INR is markedly elevated.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's been so sick and yellow, and today she got confused. She was taking a lot of Tylenol for a bad tooth all week.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's jaundiced with asterixis and her INR is way up. Fingerstick glucose is on the way.

cases.title: Pediatric dangerous back pain (discitis/osteomyelitis)
cases.chiefComplaint: 7yo — refusal to walk, back pain, fever, night pain
cases.diagnosis: Discitis / vertebral osteomyelitis (dangerous pediatric back pain)
cases.context: 7-year-old with a week of worsening back pain, now refusing to walk or bear weight, with low-grade fever, night pain, and pain with spinal flexion. True diagnosis is discitis/vertebral osteomyelitis — a dangerous cause of pediatric back pain. KEY TEACHING: back pain in a child is UNCOMMON and a RED FLAG; concerning features include young age, constant/night pain, fever, neurologic signs, refusal to ambulate, systemic symptoms, and duration >4 weeks. CORRECT PATH: take the complaint seriously, perform a careful neurologic exam (and assess for red flags — the differential includes discitis/osteomyelitis, spinal epidural abscess, malignancy/tumor, and non-accidental trauma), obtain inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP), and pursue MRI (the imaging of choice for infection/cord pathology); send blood cultures and start empiric IV ANTIBIOTICS covering Staphylococcus aureus after cultures for suspected infection; consult pediatric orthopedics/neurosurgery and admit. RED HERRING: dismissing it as musculoskeletal strain and discharging without a workup — the dangerous miss; a normal plain film does not exclude discitis. DETERIORATION: missed infection → abscess, cord compromise, sepsis. Recognizing red flags, MRI, and antibiotics are the path.
criticalActions: Treat pediatric back pain as a red flag and take a careful history for concerning features
Perform a focused neurologic exam and assess gait/ability to bear weight
Obtain inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) and blood cultures
Obtain MRI as the imaging of choice for infection/cord pathology
Start empiric IV antibiotics covering Staphylococcus aureus after cultures for suspected infection
Consider the dangerous differential (discitis/osteomyelitis, epidural abscess, malignancy, non-accidental trauma)
Consult orthopedics/neurosurgery and admit
learningPoints: Back pain in a child is uncommon and a red flag — don't reflexively call it a strain.
Red flags: young age, night/constant pain, fever, refusal to bear weight, neurologic signs, duration >4 weeks.
MRI (not plain films) is the study of choice for discitis/osteomyelitis and cord pathology.
Consider the dangerous differential including tumor and non-accidental trauma.
cases.intro: A 7-year-old boy is carried in by his mother because he now refuses to stand or walk. He's complained of back pain for a week that's worse at night and keeps him from sleeping, and he's had low fevers. He cries when he bends forward and won't let you flex his spine.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He won't walk anymore and the back pain wakes him up at night. He's had little fevers all week.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He refuses to bear weight, temp's 38.2, and he guards his back when you try to flex it.

cases.title: Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure)
cases.chiefComplaint: 48F — worsening weakness, dyspnea, weak cough; myasthenia gravis
cases.diagnosis: Myasthenic crisis with impending respiratory failure
cases.context: 48-year-old woman with myasthenia gravis, recently started on a new medication and now with a respiratory infection, presents with a few days of worsening bulbar and generalized weakness — difficulty swallowing, nasal speech, weak neck flexion, and now progressive dyspnea with a weak cough. True diagnosis is myasthenic crisis — neuromuscular respiratory failure. KEY TEACHING: the danger is RESPIRATORY COMPROMISE, and standard pulse oximetry and ABG are LATE, unreliable indicators — monitor with SERIAL BEDSIDE SPIROMETRY (forced vital capacity and negative inspiratory force); declining FVC (<15-20 mL/kg or ~1 L), weak cough, and bulbar weakness herald the need for airway protection. CORRECT PATH: assess and continuously monitor respiratory mechanics (FVC/NIF), support with NIPPV early and prepare for intubation for declining mechanics/secretions (avoid depolarizing/long-acting paralytics complications — MG patients have unpredictable responses to neuromuscular blockers); identify and remove precipitants (infection, and MEDICATIONS that worsen MG — e.g., certain antibiotics like aminoglycosides/fluoroquinolones/macrolides, beta-blockers, magnesium); treat with IVIG or PLASMAPHERESIS and steroids (with neurology — steroids can transiently worsen weakness); treat infection. RED HERRING: relying on a 'normal' oxygen saturation while the patient's ventilation fails, or giving a contraindicated medication (magnesium, certain antibiotics). DETERIORATION: unrecognized declining mechanics → sudden respiratory arrest. Serial spirometry, early ventilatory support, IVIG/plasmapheresis, and removing offending drugs control it.
criticalActions: Recognize myasthenic crisis (worsening bulbar/generalized weakness with respiratory compromise)
Monitor respiratory mechanics with serial bedside spirometry (FVC/NIF) — do not rely on pulse oximetry/ABG
Support ventilation early (NIPPV) and prepare for intubation for declining mechanics or secretion burden
Identify and remove precipitants and MG-exacerbating medications (aminoglycosides/fluoroquinolones/macrolides, beta-blockers, magnesium)
Treat the precipitating infection
Arrange IVIG or plasmapheresis and involve neurology (steroids can transiently worsen weakness)
Anticipate unpredictable responses to neuromuscular blockers
learningPoints: In myasthenic crisis, monitor FVC/NIF — oxygen saturation and ABG are late, unreliable signs of failure.
Avoid MG-worsening drugs: certain antibiotics (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides), beta-blockers, and magnesium.
IVIG or plasmapheresis is the definitive therapy; steroids may transiently worsen weakness.
Support ventilation early and anticipate difficult neuromuscular blockade.
cases.intro: A 48-year-old woman with myasthenia gravis has grown weaker over three days, her voice increasingly nasal, struggling to swallow and now to breathe. She has a chest cold and was recently prescribed a new antibiotic. Her cough is weak and she can barely lift her head off the bed, though her oxygen saturation still reads 95%.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I can barely swallow or hold my head up, and now it's getting hard to breathe. I have myasthenia.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her cough is weak and her voice is nasal. Sat's 95 but she looks like she's tiring — want bedside spirometry?

cases.title: Myxedema coma (decompensated hypothyroidism)
cases.chiefComplaint: 74F — hypothermia, bradycardia, altered, hyponatremia
cases.diagnosis: Myxedema coma (severe decompensated hypothyroidism)
cases.context: 74-year-old woman with known hypothyroidism (ran out of levothyroxine) found lethargic and cold in winter, with HYPOTHERMIA, BRADYCARDIA, hypoventilation, hypotension, altered mental status, non-pitting edema, and HYPONATREMIA and HYPOGLYCEMIA; precipitated by cold exposure and infection. True diagnosis is myxedema coma — severe decompensated hypothyroidism (high mortality). CORRECT PATH: recognize the syndrome (hypothermia + altered mental status + hyponatremia in a hypothyroid patient), send TSH/free T4 and cortisol; give IV THYROID HORMONE (levothyroxine T4, +/- liothyronine T3 per guidance); give STRESS-DOSE HYDROCORTISONE BEFORE/with thyroid hormone (concurrent adrenal insufficiency is common, and thyroid hormone alone can precipitate adrenal crisis); support ventilation, PASSIVE REWARMING (avoid aggressive active external rewarming → vasodilation/hypotension), cautious fluids with dextrose for hypoglycemia and careful correction of hyponatremia, and identify/treat the precipitant (infection — have a low threshold for antibiotics). RED HERRING: attributing it to simple hypothermia or sepsis and missing the thyroid cause; giving thyroid hormone without steroids first. DETERIORATION: without hormone + steroids → cardiovascular collapse. Thyroid hormone plus hydrocortisone plus supportive care are key.
criticalActions: Recognize myxedema coma (hypothermia, bradycardia, altered mentation, hyponatremia in a hypothyroid patient)
Send TSH, free T4, and a cortisol level
Give stress-dose hydrocortisone before/with thyroid hormone (cover concurrent adrenal insufficiency)
Give IV thyroid hormone (levothyroxine, +/- liothyronine)
Support ventilation and use passive rewarming (avoid aggressive active external rewarming)
Treat hypoglycemia and correct hyponatremia carefully
Identify and treat the precipitant (low threshold for antibiotics)
learningPoints: Give hydrocortisone before/with thyroid hormone — thyroid hormone alone can precipitate adrenal crisis.
Myxedema coma: hypothermia, altered mental status, hyponatremia, and hypoglycemia in a hypothyroid patient.
Use passive rewarming; aggressive active external rewarming can cause vasodilatory hypotension.
Always look for and treat the precipitant, commonly infection or cold exposure.
cases.intro: A 74-year-old woman is brought in from a cold apartment, barely rousable and icy to the touch. Her family says she has a thyroid problem and stopped taking her pills months ago.
