She's bradycardic, breathing slowly, her face is puffy, and her core temperature is 34.2°C. Early labs show a low sodium and low glucose.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She has a slow thyroid and stopped her medicine a while ago. The apartment was freezing and she just got slower and slower today.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Temp's 34.2, heart rate in the 40s, she's barely responsive. Sodium and glucose are both low.

cases.title: Severe alcohol withdrawal / delirium tremens
cases.chiefComplaint: 52M — tremor, agitation, hallucinations, tachycardia; last drink 3 days ago
cases.diagnosis: Delirium tremens (severe alcohol withdrawal syndrome)
cases.context: 52-year-old man with heavy daily alcohol use, now ~3 days since his last drink, with severe tremor, diaphoresis, tachycardia, hypertension, agitation, confusion, disorientation, and visual hallucinations. True diagnosis is delirium tremens — the most severe form of alcohol withdrawal (autonomic hyperactivity plus altered sensorium), which can be fatal. Timeline: minor withdrawal 6-24h, withdrawal seizures 12-48h, DTs typically 48-96h after the last drink. CORRECT PATH: the cornerstone is escalating, SYMPTOM-TRIGGERED BENZODIAZEPINES (e.g., diazepam/lorazepam titrated to a scale such as CIWA/RASS) — often large cumulative doses; for benzodiazepine-refractory DTs, add PHENOBARBITAL and/or consider dexmedetomidine/propofol (with airway control) as adjuncts; give IV fluids, THIAMINE (before glucose to prevent Wernicke), and correct electrolytes (magnesium, potassium, phosphate); check glucose; evaluate for and treat concurrent illness/injury (occult infection, trauma, GI bleed, pancreatitis) and admit to a monitored/ICU setting. RED HERRING: attributing agitation/altered mentation solely to intoxication or a psychiatric cause and missing DTs or a concurrent condition; giving glucose before thiamine. DETERIORATION: under-treated DTs → seizures, hyperthermia, arrhythmia, death. Aggressive symptom-triggered benzodiazepines (+/- phenobarbital), thiamine, and supportive care control it.
criticalActions: Recognize delirium tremens (autonomic hyperactivity plus altered sensorium ~48-96h after last drink)
Give escalating symptom-triggered benzodiazepines titrated to a validated scale
Add phenobarbital (and/or dexmedetomidine/propofol with airway control) for benzodiazepine-refractory DTs
Give thiamine before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy
Give IV fluids and correct magnesium, potassium, and phosphate
Evaluate for concurrent illness/injury (infection, trauma, GI bleed, pancreatitis)
Admit to a monitored/ICU setting
learningPoints: Symptom-triggered escalating benzodiazepines are the cornerstone of DT management; phenobarbital for refractory cases.
Give thiamine before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.
DTs typically occur 48-96 hours after the last drink and can be fatal.
Always search for a concurrent, precipitating illness or injury.
cases.intro: A 52-year-old man is brought in tremulous, drenched in sweat, and agitated, swatting at things only he can see. His brother says he drinks heavily every day but has been in jail for three days and hasn't had a drink. He's confused about where he is, his heart is racing, and his blood pressure is high.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He drinks a lot every day and hasn't had any for about three days. Now he's shaking, seeing things, and doesn't know where he is.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Get them off me — there's bugs everywhere… where am I?

cases.title: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis
cases.chiefComplaint: 29F — progressive headache, seizure, on OCPs, postpartum-like risk
cases.diagnosis: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis
cases.context: 29-year-old woman (on combined oral contraceptives, recently postpartum-range risk/prothrombotic) with several days of progressively worsening, unusual headache, now with a seizure and blurred vision. True diagnosis is cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST) — it predominantly affects women of reproductive age and those with prothrombotic states, and presents diversely (headache, seizures, focal deficits, encephalopathy, signs of raised intracranial pressure/papilledema). CORRECT PATH: maintain a HIGH INDEX OF SUSPICION (a noncontrast CT is often normal or shows only subtle signs — a 'dense sinus' or, later, a venous infarct that doesn't respect arterial territories, sometimes hemorrhagic); the diagnostic test is CT VENOGRAPHY or MR VENOGRAPHY; once confirmed, start ANTICOAGULATION (heparin) EVEN IF there is associated venous hemorrhage (anticoagulation is still indicated in CVST), manage raised ICP and seizures (benzodiazepines then a maintenance agent), and consult neurology/neurosurgery/neuro-interventional (endovascular thrombectomy for deterioration despite anticoagulation); investigate the prothrombotic cause. RED HERRING: a normal noncontrast CT falsely reassuring; treating as migraine and discharging; withholding anticoagulation because of venous hemorrhage. DETERIORATION: delayed diagnosis → venous infarction, raised ICP, herniation (10-15% death/dependence). Venography, anticoagulation, and ICP/seizure control are the path.
criticalActions: Maintain a high index of suspicion for CVST (young woman, prothrombotic risk, progressive headache with seizure/focal signs)
Recognize that a noncontrast CT is often normal or only subtly abnormal
Obtain CT venography or MR venography to confirm
Start anticoagulation (heparin) even in the presence of associated venous hemorrhage
Control seizures and manage raised intracranial pressure
Consult neurology/neurosurgery/neuro-interventional (endovascular therapy for deterioration)
Investigate the underlying prothrombotic cause
learningPoints: CVST predominantly affects young women and prothrombotic patients — keep a high index of suspicion.
Noncontrast CT is often normal; CT/MR venography makes the diagnosis.
Anticoagulate CVST even when there is associated venous hemorrhage.
Think of it with a venous infarct that doesn't respect arterial territories.
cases.intro: A 29-year-old woman has had a steadily worsening headache for several days — different from her usual headaches and worse when lying down. In the department she has a brief seizure. She takes oral contraceptives. Her initial noncontrast head CT is read as essentially normal, but something about the story doesn't fit a benign headache.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: This headache has been building for days and it's not like my usual ones — worse when I lie down.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She just seized briefly. She's on the pill. The plain CT looked basically normal.

cases.title: Fever in a returning traveler (falciparum malaria)
cases.chiefComplaint: 35M — fever/rigors after West Africa trip, no prophylaxis
cases.diagnosis: Plasmodium falciparum malaria (severe)
cases.context: 35-year-old man with high fevers, rigors, headache, myalgias, and now confusion and jaundice one week after returning from West Africa; he did not take malaria prophylaxis. True diagnosis is Plasmodium falciparum malaria, showing features of SEVERE malaria (impaired consciousness, jaundice, and risk of high parasitemia, acidosis, AKI, hypoglycemia, ARDS). KEY TEACHING: MALARIA must be RULED OUT in every febrile patient returning from an endemic region and is the most common life-threatening tropical cause of fever. CORRECT PATH: send THICK AND THIN BLOOD SMEARS (and/or rapid antigen test) urgently and REPEAT if negative (repeat smears q12-24h x3 before excluding); check glucose (hypoglycemia), lactate, CBC (thrombocytopenia, hemolysis), renal/hepatic function; treat SEVERE malaria with IV ARTESUNATE (first-line) and provide supportive care; isolate/consider other travel infections on the differential (dengue, enteric/typhoid fever, viral hemorrhagic fever, rickettsia) and take a detailed exposure history; involve infectious disease/public health. RED HERRING: attributing fever to a viral syndrome and discharging without a malaria smear; a single negative smear does not exclude malaria. DETERIORATION: untreated falciparum → cerebral malaria, multi-organ failure, death. Prompt smears and IV artesunate for severe disease are the priorities.
criticalActions: Take a detailed travel/exposure history and rule out malaria in the febrile returning traveler
Send thick and thin blood smears (and/or rapid antigen test) urgently
Repeat smears if initially negative before excluding malaria
Recognize features of severe malaria (impaired consciousness, jaundice, high parasitemia, acidosis)
Check glucose, lactate, CBC, and renal/hepatic function
Treat severe malaria with IV artesunate and provide supportive care
Consider other travel infections (dengue, typhoid, viral hemorrhagic fever, rickettsia) and involve ID/public health
learningPoints: Rule out malaria in every febrile patient returning from an endemic region — it's the most common life-threatening tropical fever.
A single negative smear doesn't exclude malaria; repeat thick/thin smears.
Severe falciparum malaria is treated with IV artesunate plus supportive care.
Screen for hypoglycemia and keep dengue, typhoid, and viral hemorrhagic fever on the differential.
cases.intro: A 35-year-old man is shaking with rigors and burning up a week after returning from a business trip to West Africa. He admits he never took the malaria pills he was prescribed. He's got a pounding headache, aches all over, looks slightly jaundiced, and is becoming confused.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: The fevers and chills hit a few days after I got back from Nigeria… I didn't take the malaria pills. My head is killing me.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Temp 39.8 with rigors, he's a bit jaundiced and getting confused. Recent West Africa travel.

cases.title: Acute symptomatic hyponatremia
cases.chiefComplaint: 26F — seizure at an endurance event; Na 116
cases.diagnosis: Acute severe symptomatic hyponatremia
cases.context: 26-year-old woman who drank large volumes of water during a marathon presents with headache, nausea, confusion, and a witnessed seizure; serum sodium is 116 (acute, exercise-associated/dilutional). True diagnosis is acute severe SYMPTOMATIC hyponatremia — a medical emergency because rapidly falling sodium causes cerebral edema. CORRECT PATH: for SEVERE SYMPTOMS (seizure, coma, severe confusion), give HYPERTONIC (3%) SALINE as a bolus (e.g., 100-150 mL boluses, may repeat) to acutely raise sodium ~4-6 mEq/L and reverse cerebral edema — this is the priority regardless of chronicity when symptoms are severe; then correct SLOWLY, limiting the total rise to about 6-8 mEq/L per 24 hours (no more than ~8/day) to avoid OSMOTIC DEMYELINATION SYNDROME; identify the cause (psychogenic/exercise-associated water intake, SIADH, thiazides, adrenal/thyroid), monitor sodium frequently, and treat overcorrection with free water/DDAVP if sodium rises too fast. RED HERRING: correcting too rapidly (osmotic demyelination) or withholding hypertonic saline in a seizing patient for fear of overcorrection. DETERIORATION: cerebral edema → herniation if untreated; overcorrection → osmotic demyelination. Hypertonic saline for severe symptoms, then controlled correction.
criticalActions: Recognize acute severe symptomatic hyponatremia as an emergency (seizure/coma/severe confusion with low sodium)
Give hypertonic (3%) saline in boluses for severe neurologic symptoms to raise sodium ~4-6 mEq/L
Limit total correction to about 6-8 mEq/L per 24 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination
Monitor serum sodium frequently during correction
Identify the underlying cause (water intoxication/exercise-associated, SIADH, thiazides, endocrine)
Treat overcorrection with free water and/or DDAVP if sodium rises too quickly
Admit for close monitoring
learningPoints: Severe symptomatic hyponatremia: give 3% hypertonic saline to raise sodium ~4-6 mEq/L and reverse cerebral edema.
Limit correction to ~6-8 mEq/L/24h to avoid osmotic demyelination syndrome.
Don't withhold hypertonic saline in a seizing patient for fear of overcorrection.
If you overcorrect, re-lower with free water and/or DDAVP.
cases.intro: A 26-year-old woman collapsed with a seizure near the finish line of a marathon. Bystanders say she'd been drinking bottle after bottle of water all day. She's now postictal and confused, and the point-of-care chemistry shows a sodium of 116.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: She seized at the finish line. People said she was chugging water all race. She's confused now.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Point-of-care sodium is 116. She's postictal. What do you want to give?

cases.title: Febrile young infant (<60 days)
cases.chiefComplaint: 3-week-old — fever 38.4°C, otherwise looks okay
cases.diagnosis: Febrile infant (rule out invasive bacterial infection)
cases.context: 3-week-old, previously well, brought in for a rectal temperature of 38.4°C; the baby looks relatively well. True concern is INVASIVE BACTERIAL INFECTION (bacteremia, urinary tract infection, and meningitis) — in infants aged <=60 days, fever may be the ONLY sign, and a well appearance does NOT exclude serious infection. KEY TEACHING: management follows RISK STRATIFICATION (e.g., the 2021 AAP clinical practice guideline for well-appearing 8-60 day infants, and more conservative management for neonates 0-28 days). CORRECT PATH: full evaluation guided by age — for the youngest (<=28 days) and any ILL-appearing infant, obtain CBC, blood culture, URINALYSIS/urine culture (catheterized), inflammatory markers (procalcitonin/CRP), and LUMBAR PUNCTURE (CSF studies), and give EMPIRIC ANTIBIOTICS (e.g., ampicillin + ceftazidime/cefotaxime or gentamicin) — add ACYCLOVIR if HSV is a concern (ill-appearing, seizures, vesicles, maternal HSV, hepatitis, CSF pleocytosis); admit. For 29-60 days, apply risk stratification (biomarkers) to guide LP/antibiotics/disposition. RED HERRING: being reassured by a well-appearing baby and discharging without a workup; missing HSV. DETERIORATION: untreated invasive bacterial infection or HSV → sepsis, meningitis, death. Age-based full workup and empiric antibiotics (+/- acyclovir) are the standard.
criticalActions: Recognize fever in an infant <=60 days as high-risk for invasive bacterial infection despite a well appearance
Obtain blood culture, CBC, and inflammatory markers (procalcitonin/CRP)
Obtain a catheterized urinalysis and urine culture
Perform lumbar puncture with CSF studies per age/risk (mandatory in neonates <=28 days and ill-appearing infants)
Give empiric antibiotics (e.g., ampicillin plus a cephalosporin/gentamicin)
Add acyclovir when HSV is a concern
Apply age-based risk stratification (e.g., 2021 AAP guideline) and admit young/ill infants
learningPoints: In infants <=60 days, fever may be the only sign of invasive bacterial infection — a well appearance doesn't exclude it.
Neonates <=28 days and ill-appearing infants get a full workup including LP and empiric antibiotics.
Add acyclovir for HSV concern (ill appearance, seizures, vesicles, maternal history, hepatitis, CSF pleocytosis).
Use validated risk stratification (2021 AAP guideline, biomarkers) for the 29-60 day group.
cases.intro: First-time parents bring in their 3-week-old daughter because she felt warm; a rectal temperature reads 38.4°C. She's feeding a bit less but otherwise looks okay — pink, moving well, consolable. The parents are anxious and hoping it's 'just a cold.'

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She just felt hot and isn't eating quite as much. She seems okay otherwise — is it just a cold?

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Three weeks old, rectal temp 38.4. She looks well, but she's tiny.

cases.title: Acute bronchiolitis
cases.chiefComplaint: 5-month-old — cough, wheeze, retractions, poor feeding in winter
cases.diagnosis: Acute bronchiolitis (RSV)
cases.context: 5-month-old in winter with several days of upper respiratory symptoms progressing to cough, wheeze, tachypnea, retractions, nasal flaring, and decreased feeding. True diagnosis is acute bronchiolitis (commonly RSV). KEY TEACHING: bronchiolitis is a CLINICAL diagnosis; routine laboratory and radiographic testing play a LIMITED role, and studies show bronchodilators and corticosteroids LACK efficacy. CORRECT PATH: assessment centers on WORK OF BREATHING, hydration, and oxygenation with frequent reassessment; supportive care is the mainstay — NASAL SUCTIONING/saline, maintain hydration (oral or IV/NG if feeding poorly), and supplemental oxygen for hypoxia with escalation to HIGH-FLOW NASAL CANNULA for respiratory distress; do NOT routinely give albuterol, steroids, antibiotics, or a chest x-ray; identify high-risk infants (young age/prematurity, apnea, congenital heart/lung disease, toxic appearance) for admission; provide clear return precautions if discharged. RED HERRING: reflexively treating the wheeze with albuterol/steroids (ineffective) or getting an unnecessary CXR/antibiotics; missing apnea in the very young. DETERIORATION: progressive respiratory failure/apnea in high-risk infants. Supportive care, suctioning, hydration, oxygen/HFNC, and appropriate disposition are the path.
criticalActions: Diagnose bronchiolitis clinically and assess work of breathing, hydration, and oxygenation with frequent reassessment
Provide nasal suctioning and maintain hydration (oral, or IV/NG if feeding poorly)
Give supplemental oxygen for hypoxia and escalate to high-flow nasal cannula for distress
Avoid routine bronchodilators, corticosteroids, antibiotics, and chest x-rays
Identify high-risk infants (young/premature, apnea, cardiopulmonary disease, toxic appearance) for admission
Provide clear return precautions if discharged
learningPoints: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis — labs, x-rays, bronchodilators, and steroids are not routinely helpful.
Care is supportive: suctioning, hydration, and oxygen/high-flow nasal cannula.
Watch for apnea and respiratory failure in young/premature and high-risk infants.
Reserve testing and admission for high-risk or clinically deteriorating infants.
cases.intro: A 5-month-old boy in the thick of winter has had a runny nose for a few days that's turned into a wet cough and fast, labored breathing. His parents say he's barely taking his bottle. He's breathing rapidly with belly retractions and nasal flaring, wheezing and crackling throughout, with a saturation of 89%.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: It started like a cold, but now he's breathing so fast and hardly eating. You can see his belly sucking in.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: RR in the 60s with retractions and flaring, sat's 89, diffuse wheeze. He's five months old.

cases.title: Stercoral colitis (complication of constipation)
cases.chiefComplaint: 81F — chronic constipation, now abdominal pain, distension, toxicity
cases.diagnosis: Stercoral colitis with impending perforation
cases.context: 81-year-old bedbound woman on chronic opioids with long-standing constipation, now with several days of abdominal pain, distension, and a hard stool mass, becoming febrile and tachycardic with focal tenderness. True diagnosis is STERCORAL COLITIS — a dangerous complication of constipation (fecal impaction causing colonic wall ischemia/inflammation, with risk of stercoral ulcer and PERFORATION). KEY TEACHING: constipation is a clinical diagnosis, but serious complications such as fecal impaction and stercoral colitis MUST be ruled out; it is easy to dismiss 'just constipation' in an elderly patient. CORRECT PATH: recognize red flags (older/bedbound/opioids, peritoneal signs, systemic toxicity), resuscitate, obtain CT of the abdomen/pelvis (colonic distension with stool burden, wall thickening, and signs of ischemia/perforation such as free air), send labs/lactate, keep NPO, give analgesia and broad-spectrum antibiotics if colitis/perforation is suspected, and consult SURGERY urgently (perforation is a surgical emergency; high mortality); careful disimpaction/decompression for uncomplicated impaction. RED HERRING: treating as simple constipation with an enema and discharge while a stercoral perforation is developing. DETERIORATION: perforation → feculent peritonitis, septic shock, death. Imaging, antibiotics, and surgical consultation are the path.
criticalActions: Recognize stercoral colitis as a dangerous complication of constipation (don't dismiss 'just constipation')
Identify red flags (elderly/bedbound/opioids, peritoneal signs, systemic toxicity)
Obtain CT of the abdomen/pelvis to assess for stercoral colitis, ischemia, and perforation
Send labs and lactate and resuscitate
Keep NPO and give broad-spectrum antibiotics if colitis/perforation is suspected
Consult surgery urgently for peritonitis/perforation
Perform careful disimpaction/decompression for uncomplicated impaction
learningPoints: Constipation is common, but rule out fecal impaction and stercoral colitis before calling it benign.
Stercoral colitis can progress to ulceration and perforation with high mortality.
CT of the abdomen/pelvis identifies stool burden, wall changes, ischemia, and free air.
Peritoneal signs or perforation demand urgent surgical consultation.
cases.intro: An 81-year-old bedbound woman is brought from her nursing home with days of worsening belly pain and swelling; she hasn't had a proper bowel movement in over a week and takes opioids for chronic pain. Her abdomen is distended and tender in the left lower quadrant with a palpable firm mass of stool, and today she's febrile and her heart is racing.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She hasn't gone in over a week and now her belly is swollen and she's in pain. She's on morphine for her back.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's febrile and tachycardic with a tender, distended belly and a hard stool mass on the left.

cases.title: Acute coronary occlusion MI (OMI without STEMI criteria)
cases.chiefComplaint: 56M — ongoing chest pain, non-diagnostic ST, but occlusion pattern
cases.diagnosis: Acute coronary occlusion myocardial infarction (OMI)
cases.context: 56-year-old man with ongoing, severe chest pain and diaphoresis for an hour; the initial 12-lead does NOT meet classic STEMI millimeter criteria, but shows an OCCLUSION pattern — hyperacute (tall, broad) T waves, subtle ST elevation with reciprocal change, or a suspicious pattern (e.g., de Winter T waves, Wellens, or ST elevation in aVR with diffuse depression). True diagnosis is an ACUTE CORONARY OCCLUSION MI (OMI) that will be MISSED if you rely only on rigid STEMI criteria. KEY TEACHING: the goal is recognizing ECG patterns of acute coronary occlusion EVEN IN THE ABSENCE of traditional STEMI criteria and coordinating rapid reperfusion. CORRECT PATH: give aspirin, obtain SERIAL/repeat ECGs (dynamic changes) and compare to prior, obtain a posterior/right-sided ECG when indicated, use high-sensitivity troponin as adjunct (but don't wait for it to act on a clear occlusion pattern), and ACTIVATE THE CATH LAB / involve cardiology emergently for primary PCI in the patient with refractory symptoms and an occlusion pattern; treat per ACS protocol. RED HERRING: labeling a non-STEMI-criteria ECG as 'NSTEMI, admit and trend troponins' while an artery is occluding — the missed occlusion. DETERIORATION: delayed reperfusion → larger infarct, arrest, cardiogenic shock. Pattern recognition, serial ECGs, and emergent PCI activation are the path.
criticalActions: Recognize acute coronary occlusion ECG patterns even without classic STEMI criteria (hyperacute T waves, de Winter, Wellens, aVR elevation with diffuse depression, reciprocal change)
Give aspirin
Obtain serial/repeat ECGs and compare with prior tracings
Obtain posterior/right-sided leads when indicated
Use high-sensitivity troponin as an adjunct but do not delay reperfusion for it when the pattern is clear
Activate the cath lab / involve cardiology emergently for primary PCI
Treat per ACS protocol
learningPoints: Acute coronary occlusion can occur without meeting STEMI millimeter criteria — learn the occlusion patterns.
Serial ECGs and comparison to prior tracings reveal dynamic occlusion changes.
Don't wait for troponin to act on a clear occlusion pattern with refractory symptoms.
de Winter T waves, Wellens, and aVR elevation with diffuse depression are high-risk patterns.
cases.intro: A 56-year-old man clutches his chest, pale and sweating, with an hour of relentless pressure radiating to his arm. The first 12-lead doesn't show the textbook ST elevations the triage nurse was looking for, but the T waves in the precordial leads look tall and bulky, and there's subtle depression elsewhere. He's still in severe pain.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: The pressure won't let up — it's been an hour and it's going into my arm. I feel like something's really wrong.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: The computer didn't call a STEMI, but his pain is ongoing and those T waves look big to me. Aspirin's given.

cases.title: Caustic (alkali) ingestion
cases.chiefComplaint: 3yo — drank drain cleaner; drooling, oral burns, stridor
cases.diagnosis: Caustic alkali ingestion with airway/esophageal injury
cases.context: 3-year-old who drank from an unlabeled bottle of alkaline drain cleaner, now drooling, refusing to drink, with oral burns, vomiting, and early stridor/voice change. True diagnosis is caustic ALKALI ingestion — alkalis cause LIQUEFACTIVE necrosis with deep, penetrating esophageal injury (risk of perforation and later strictures); the airway is threatened. CORRECT PATH: assess and protect the AIRWAY (early controlled airway management for stridor/respiratory distress/significant edema — anticipate a difficult airway); do NOT induce vomiting, do NOT give activated charcoal (obscures endoscopy, no benefit), do NOT attempt neutralization or 'dilution' with large volumes, and do NOT blindly pass an NG tube; keep NPO, give IV fluids and analgesia; obtain imaging (upright CXR/CT) to evaluate for perforation/mediastinitis, consult GI for early ENDOSCOPY (typically within 12-24 hours) to grade injury and guide management, consult surgery for perforation, and involve TOXICOLOGY/poison control; steroids/antibiotics are selective per specialist. RED HERRING: inducing emesis, giving charcoal, or 'neutralizing' — all harmful; underestimating airway compromise. DETERIORATION: airway obstruction, esophageal/gastric perforation, mediastinitis. Airway protection, NPO, imaging, and early endoscopy are the path.
criticalActions: Assess and protect the airway early (controlled management for stridor/edema; anticipate difficulty)
Do NOT induce vomiting, give activated charcoal, attempt neutralization, or blindly pass an NG tube
Keep the patient NPO and give IV fluids and analgesia
Obtain imaging (upright CXR/CT) to evaluate for perforation/mediastinitis
Consult GI for early endoscopy to grade the injury
Consult surgery for suspected perforation and involve toxicology/poison control
Reserve steroids/antibiotics for specialist guidance
learningPoints: Alkali ingestions cause deep liquefactive esophageal injury — protect the airway and keep NPO.
Do not induce emesis, give charcoal, or try to neutralize/dilute — these are harmful.
Early endoscopy (12-24h) grades the injury and guides management.
Watch for perforation and mediastinitis; involve toxicology/poison control.
cases.intro: A 3-year-old boy is rushed in by panicked parents after they found him with an open bottle of drain cleaner and liquid around his mouth. He's drooling, crying, refusing to swallow, and has white burns on his lips and tongue. His voice sounds hoarse and there's a soft stridor developing.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He got into the drain cleaner under the sink — there was liquid on his mouth. He won't stop drooling and won't drink anything.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Burns on the lips and tongue, he's drooling and starting to sound stridorous. Sat's 95.

cases.title: Ischemic priapism
cases.chiefComplaint: 34M — painful erection >4 hours; sickle cell / medication
cases.diagnosis: Ischemic (low-flow) priapism
cases.context: 34-year-old man with a painful, rigid erection lasting more than 4 hours (associated with sickle cell disease, or a medication such as trazodone/PDE5 inhibitor/intracavernosal injection). True diagnosis is ISCHEMIC (low-flow) priapism — a compartment-syndrome-like urologic EMERGENCY (a form of tissue ischemia; prolonged episodes cause corporal fibrosis and permanent erectile dysfunction). Ischemic priapism is painful with rigid corpora; non-ischemic (high-flow, from trauma) is typically non-painful and not an emergency. CORRECT PATH: recognize and treat promptly (time-sensitive — irreversible damage after prolonged ischemia), provide ANALGESIA, obtain a CORPORAL BLOOD GAS to confirm ischemia (dark, hypoxic, acidotic blood), and perform CORPORAL ASPIRATION with irrigation and INTRACAVERNOSAL PHENYLEPHRINE injections (with cardiac monitoring); consult UROLOGY urgently for refractory cases (may need a shunt); treat the underlying cause (for sickle cell: hydration, oxygen, analgesia, and hematology involvement — exchange transfusion for refractory cases). RED HERRING: dismissing it as embarrassing/self-limited, delaying intervention; confusing high-flow (non-emergent) with ischemic priapism. DETERIORATION: prolonged ischemia → corporal fibrosis and permanent erectile dysfunction. Prompt aspiration/phenylephrine and urology are the path.
criticalActions: Recognize ischemic priapism as a time-sensitive urologic emergency (painful, rigid, >4 hours)
Provide analgesia
Obtain a corporal blood gas to confirm ischemia
Perform corporal aspiration with irrigation and intracavernosal phenylephrine (with cardiac monitoring)
Consult urology urgently for refractory cases
Treat the underlying cause (for sickle cell: hydration, oxygen, analgesia, hematology; exchange transfusion if refractory)
Distinguish ischemic from non-ischemic (high-flow, traumatic, non-painful) priapism
learningPoints: Ischemic priapism is an emergency — prolonged ischemia causes corporal fibrosis and permanent ED.
Confirm with a corporal blood gas; treat with aspiration and intracavernosal phenylephrine.
Ischemic (painful, rigid) differs from high-flow/traumatic (painless) priapism, which is not an emergency.
Treat underlying causes (sickle cell, medications) and involve urology early.
cases.intro: A 34-year-old man comes in embarrassed and in significant pain with a rigid erection that has persisted for over five hours and won't subside. He has sickle cell trait and started a new medication for sleep this week. The shaft is hard and tender.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It won't go down and it really hurts — it's been like this for over five hours. I was too embarrassed to come in sooner.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's clearly in pain, it's rigid and tender. He has sickle cell trait and just started trazodone.

cases.article: 2016-10_Optimizing Survival Outcomes For Adult Patients With Nontraumatic Cardiac Arrest.pdf
cases.title: Nontraumatic cardiac arrest (VF)
cases.chiefComplaint: 62M — witnessed collapse, VF arrest, CPR in progress
cases.diagnosis: Nontraumatic cardiac arrest (ventricular fibrillation)
cases.context: 62-year-old man with a witnessed collapse; bystander CPR was started and the monitor shows VENTRICULAR FIBRILLATION. True scenario is nontraumatic cardiac arrest — survival hinges on HIGH-QUALITY CPR and EARLY DEFIBRILLATION. CORRECT PATH: minimize interruptions in high-quality compressions (adequate rate/depth, full recoil, limit pauses/pre- and post-shock pauses), DEFIBRILLATE the shockable rhythm immediately and every 2 minutes as indicated, give EPINEPHRINE every 3-5 minutes, and an ANTIARRHYTHMIC (amiodarone or lidocaine) for refractory VF/pVT; secure the airway without interrupting compressions and use waveform CAPNOGRAPHY (to confirm tube placement, gauge CPR quality, and detect ROSC — a sudden rise in ETCO2); systematically search for and treat REVERSIBLE CAUSES (the Hs and Ts — hypoxia, hypovolemia, hydrogen ion/acidosis, hypo/hyperkalemia, hypothermia, tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis [coronary/pulmonary]); consider point-of-care ultrasound during rhythm checks. After ROSC: obtain a 12-lead (activate cath lab for STEMI/occlusion), support hemodynamics, and provide TARGETED TEMPERATURE MANAGEMENT for comatose survivors. RED HERRING: frequent, prolonged interruptions in compressions; forgetting the reversible causes. DETERIORATION: poor CPR quality and delayed defibrillation reduce survival. High-quality CPR, early defibrillation, epinephrine, reversible-cause search, and post-ROSC care are the path.
criticalActions: Provide high-quality CPR with minimal interruptions (rate/depth, full recoil, short pauses)
Defibrillate the shockable rhythm immediately and every 2 minutes as indicated
Give epinephrine every 3-5 minutes and an antiarrhythmic (amiodarone/lidocaine) for refractory VF/pVT
Use waveform capnography to guide CPR quality, confirm the airway, and detect ROSC
Systematically search for and treat reversible causes (Hs and Ts)
After ROSC, obtain a 12-lead ECG and activate the cath lab for occlusion/STEMI
Provide targeted temperature management for comatose post-arrest survivors
learningPoints: Survival hinges on high-quality CPR with minimal interruptions and early defibrillation.
Waveform capnography guides CPR quality and heralds ROSC (a sudden ETCO2 rise).
Systematically treat the reversible causes (Hs and Ts).
Post-ROSC: 12-lead ECG, coronary reperfusion when indicated, and targeted temperature management.
cases.intro: A 62-year-old man collapsed at the gym; a bystander started CPR immediately and medics continued it en route. He arrives pulseless with compressions ongoing, and the monitor shows a coarse ventricular fibrillation. The team looks to you to run the code.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Witnessed arrest at the gym, immediate bystander CPR. He's been in VF the whole time — two shocks so far, one round of epi.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Compressions are going, he's in VF. Defibrillator's charged. What do you want?

cases.title: Bleeding dialysis access (AV fistula hemorrhage)
cases.chiefComplaint: 59F — pulsatile bleeding from AV fistula, hypotension
cases.diagnosis: Hemorrhage from arteriovenous dialysis access
cases.context: 59-year-old woman on hemodialysis via an arteriovenous fistula presents with brisk, pulsatile bleeding from the access site after a scab dislodged at home; she is pale and hypotensive from blood loss. True diagnosis is hemorrhage from vascular dialysis ACCESS — a rapidly fatal complication (a fistula/graft carries arterial-pressure flow, and aneurysmal/degenerated access can bleed catastrophically). CORRECT PATH: apply IMMEDIATE FOCAL DIGITAL PRESSURE directly over the bleeding point (precise, firm, sustained manual pressure controls most access bleeds without occluding the whole fistula) — if uncontrolled, escalate to a tourniquet proximal to the site as a temporizing measure while preparing definitive control; resuscitate (two IVs elsewhere, type and cross, blood products for hemorrhagic shock), reverse/consider the uremic-platelet-dysfunction contribution (DDAVP) and any anticoagulation; consult VASCULAR SURGERY urgently for definitive repair; monitor the access for thrombosis after control. Also keep the broader dialysis differential in mind (missed dialysis → hyperkalemia/volume overload/pericarditis-tamponade; access infection; dialysis disequilibrium). RED HERRING: loosely dressing the site or under-appreciating how fast an access can exsanguinate; fully clamping/occluding and forgetting definitive repair. DETERIORATION: continued bleeding → hemorrhagic shock, death within minutes. Focal pressure, resuscitation, and vascular surgery are the path.
criticalActions: Apply immediate, focal, sustained digital pressure over the bleeding point
Escalate to a proximal tourniquet as a temporizing measure if pressure fails
Resuscitate for hemorrhagic shock (IV access elsewhere, type & cross, blood products)
Address uremic platelet dysfunction (DDAVP) and reverse anticoagulation as appropriate
Consult vascular surgery urgently for definitive repair
Monitor the access for thrombosis after hemostasis
Keep the broader dialysis differential in mind (hyperkalemia, volume overload, tamponade, access infection)
learningPoints: A bleeding AV fistula/graft can exsanguinate rapidly — control it with immediate focal digital pressure.
Escalate to a proximal tourniquet if focal pressure fails, and involve vascular surgery.
Uremic platelet dysfunction contributes to bleeding — consider DDAVP.
Remember the wider dialysis differential: hyperkalemia, volume overload, tamponade, and access infection.
cases.intro: A 59-year-old dialysis patient is rushed in with blood-soaked towels pressed to her left forearm, where her fistula is spurting with each heartbeat after a scab came off at home. She's pale and lightheaded, her blood pressure is low, and the towels are soaking through fast.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: The scab on my fistula came off and it just started shooting blood — I couldn't get it to stop.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: It's pulsatile from the fistula and she's already hypotensive at 86. I'm holding pressure now.

cases.title: Opioid overdose
cases.chiefComplaint: 27M — unresponsive, pinpoint pupils, RR 4, found with needle
cases.diagnosis: Opioid overdose (respiratory depression)
cases.context: 27-year-old man found unresponsive with pinpoint (miotic) pupils, a respiratory rate of 4, and shallow breathing; drug paraphernalia was nearby (possible fentanyl-adulterated supply). True diagnosis is opioid overdose with life-threatening RESPIRATORY DEPRESSION (the cause of death in opioid toxicity). CORRECT PATH: the immediate priority is VENTILATION/OXYGENATION (bag-valve-mask support) and NALOXONE — titrate naloxone to restore ADEQUATE BREATHING, not necessarily full arousal (over-reversal can precipitate acute withdrawal, agitation, and — with certain patients — vomiting/aspiration or flash pulmonary edema); may require repeat doses or an INFUSION because many opioids (and fentanyl) outlast a single naloxone dose; check glucose and consider co-ingestants; monitor for RE-SEDATION after naloxone wears off (observe adequately before discharge); evaluate for complications (aspiration, rhabdomyolysis, compartment syndrome from prolonged down-time). Provide harm-reduction: take-home naloxone, and initiate/refer for MEDICATION FOR OPIOID USE DISORDER (buprenorphine) and treatment. RED HERRING: giving a large naloxone bolus and discharging quickly — risks precipitated withdrawal and, worse, re-sedation when naloxone wears off; missing a co-ingestant or hypoglycemia. DETERIORATION: unrecognized re-sedation/apnea after naloxone wears off. Ventilation, titrated naloxone, observation, and linkage to treatment are the path.
criticalActions: Support ventilation/oxygenation immediately (bag-valve-mask)
Give naloxone titrated to restore adequate breathing (avoid over-reversal)
Anticipate the need for repeat naloxone doses or an infusion (opioids/fentanyl outlast a single dose)
Check glucose and consider co-ingestants
Observe adequately for re-sedation after naloxone wears off before discharge
Evaluate for complications (aspiration, rhabdomyolysis, compartment syndrome)
Provide take-home naloxone and initiate/refer for medication for opioid use disorder (buprenorphine)
learningPoints: Titrate naloxone to adequate breathing, not full arousal — over-reversal precipitates withdrawal.
Fentanyl and long-acting opioids outlast naloxone — anticipate re-sedation and observe or infuse.
Support ventilation first; respiratory depression is what kills.
Offer take-home naloxone and start/refer for buprenorphine and opioid use disorder treatment.
cases.intro: A 27-year-old man is carried in unresponsive after being found slumped in a bathroom with a needle beside him. His pupils are pinpoint, his breathing is slow and shallow at about 4 breaths a minute, and his lips are dusky. His oxygen saturation is critically low.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Found him barely breathing with a needle next to him. Pinpoint pupils. We bagged him on the way in but haven't given naloxone yet.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: RR is about 4, sat's 78, pupils pinpoint. Bag-valve-mask is out. What do you want?

cases.title: Methamphetamine (sympathomimetic) toxicity
cases.chiefComplaint: 30M — severe agitation, hyperthermia, tachycardia, chest pain
cases.diagnosis: Methamphetamine toxicity (sympathomimetic crisis with agitation and hyperthermia)
cases.context: 30-year-old man brought in violently agitated, diaphoretic, tachycardic, hypertensive, hyperthermic, with dilated pupils and chest pain after methamphetamine use. True diagnosis is methamphetamine (sympathomimetic) toxicity with severe agitation and HYPERTHERMIA — the combination of extreme agitation and hyperthermia drives complications (rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, AKI, DIC, seizures, death). CORRECT PATH: the cornerstone of managing severe agitation/hyperthermia is aggressive SEDATION with BENZODIAZEPINES (titrated, often large doses; consider a dissociative/antipsychotic adjunct) to control agitation, reduce catecholamine surge, and lower temperature; ACTIVE COOLING for hyperthermia; IV fluids; check glucose; obtain an ECG (tachydysrhythmia, ischemia — treat cocaine/meth-associated chest pain/ischemia with benzodiazepines, nitrates, aspirin; AVOID beta-blockers due to unopposed alpha concern); screen for and treat complications (rhabdomyolysis/CK, hyperkalemia, hyperthermia, seizures, intracranial hemorrhage from hypertension); evaluate for trauma and co-ingestants. RED HERRING: physically restraining without adequate chemical sedation (worsens hyperthermia/rhabdomyolysis/acidosis — a lethal spiral), or reaching for a beta-blocker. DETERIORATION: uncontrolled agitation/hyperthermia → rhabdomyolysis, multi-organ failure, arrest. Benzodiazepine sedation, cooling, and fluids control it.
criticalActions: Control severe agitation with aggressive titrated benzodiazepine sedation
Actively cool the hyperthermic patient
Give IV fluids and check glucose
Obtain an ECG and treat ischemia with benzodiazepines/nitrates/aspirin (avoid beta-blockers)
Screen for and treat complications (rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, seizures, intracranial hemorrhage)
Avoid relying on physical restraint without chemical sedation (worsens hyperthermia/acidosis)
Evaluate for trauma and co-ingestants
learningPoints: Benzodiazepine sedation is the cornerstone for sympathomimetic agitation and hyperthermia.
Physical restraint without chemical sedation worsens hyperthermia, rhabdomyolysis, and acidosis.
Treat stimulant-associated chest pain with benzodiazepines/nitrates; avoid beta-blockers.
Screen for rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, seizures, and hypertensive intracranial hemorrhage.
cases.intro: A 30-year-old man is wheeled in thrashing and screaming, soaked in sweat, taking several staff to keep on the stretcher. His pupils are wide, his heart is racing at nearly 150, his blood pressure is very high, and he's burning up at 40°C. Between shouts he grabs his chest.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Family called — he'd been up for days using meth, then went berserk. Took four of us to get him in.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's combative, temp 40, heart rate 148, pressure 196 over 108. Restraints are on but he's fighting hard.

cases.title: Cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome
cases.chiefComplaint: 24M — cyclic intractable vomiting relieved by hot showers
cases.diagnosis: Cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome
cases.context: 24-year-old man with recurrent episodes of severe, intractable vomiting and diffuse abdominal pain, a long history of daily cannabis use, and the classic pattern of COMPULSIVE HOT SHOWERS/BATHS that relieve the symptoms. True diagnosis is cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome (CHS) — cyclic vomiting in the setting of chronic cannabinoid use, relieved by hot bathing. KEY TEACHING: it is frequently MISDIAGNOSED and over-investigated; recognizing the pattern (chronic use + cyclic vomiting + hot-water bathing relief) avoids unnecessary testing, though a reasonable workup excludes dangerous mimics on first/atypical presentations. CORRECT PATH: rehydrate with IV fluids and correct electrolytes; for symptom control, TOPICAL CAPSAICIN cream (to the abdomen/back) and DOPAMINE ANTAGONISTS such as HALOPERIDOL or DROPERIDOL are effective (more so than conventional antiemetics like ondansetron); benzodiazepines are an adjunct; the DEFINITIVE treatment is CANNABIS CESSATION, so counsel accordingly and arrange follow-up. On atypical/first presentations, exclude dangerous causes (obstruction, pancreatitis, DKA, pregnancy, CNS causes). RED HERRING: repeatedly working up 'cyclic vomiting' without recognizing the CHS pattern; relying on ondansetron alone. DETERIORATION: dehydration, electrolyte derangement; missing a dangerous mimic on atypical presentations. Fluids, capsaicin, haloperidol/droperidol, and cessation counseling are the path.
criticalActions: Recognize cannabinoid hyperemesis syndrome (chronic cannabis use, cyclic vomiting, relief with hot bathing)
Rehydrate with IV fluids and correct electrolytes
Apply topical capsaicin cream for symptom control
Give a dopamine antagonist (haloperidol or droperidol) for refractory symptoms
Counsel on cannabis cessation as the definitive treatment and arrange follow-up
Exclude dangerous mimics on atypical/first presentations (obstruction, pancreatitis, DKA, pregnancy, CNS causes)
Avoid unnecessary repeat imaging/testing once the pattern is recognized
learningPoints: CHS: chronic cannabis use + cyclic vomiting + relief with hot showers/baths.
Topical capsaicin and haloperidol/droperidol outperform conventional antiemetics.
Definitive treatment is cannabis cessation — counsel and arrange follow-up.
Exclude dangerous mimics on first or atypical presentations before anchoring.
cases.intro: A 24-year-old man doubles over retching, having vomited more times than he can count over the last day. He says this keeps happening every few weeks, and the only thing that helps is standing in a scalding hot shower for hours. He smokes cannabis heavily every day and has had multiple prior ED visits and 'normal' workups for the same thing.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's happening again — I can't stop throwing up. The only thing that helps is a really hot shower. I use weed every day.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: This is his third visit this year for the same cyclic vomiting. He's mildly dry but stable.

cases.title: Pit viper (crotaline) envenomation
cases.chiefComplaint: 41M — rattlesnake bite, progressive swelling, coagulopathy
cases.diagnosis: Crotaline (pit viper) snake envenomation
cases.context: 41-year-old man bitten on the leg by a rattlesnake while hiking, with progressive local swelling/pain spreading proximally, ecchymosis, and lab evidence of coagulopathy (thrombocytopenia, low fibrinogen, elevated INR). True diagnosis is North American PIT VIPER (Crotalinae) envenomation, causing progressive local tissue injury, hematologic venom effects (coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia), and possible systemic toxicity. CORRECT PATH: supportive care and analgesia; do NOT use harmful prehospital measures — AVOID tourniquets, incision/suction, and ice (these worsen local injury); mark and serially monitor the leading edge of SWELLING and the limb, elevate the limb; obtain labs including CBC, COAGULATION studies and FIBRINOGEN and repeat them (venom effects evolve); give ANTIVENOM (CroFab/Anavip) for indicated cases — progressive swelling, coagulopathy/hematologic venom effects, or systemic toxicity — titrated to control; update tetanus; consult TOXICOLOGY/poison control; watch for recurrent coagulopathy and delayed effects, and for true compartment syndrome (measure pressures — most swelling is subcutaneous and NOT compartment syndrome, so fasciotomy is rarely needed and controversial). RED HERRING: applying a tourniquet or cutting/sucking the wound (harmful); rushing to fasciotomy for tense swelling that is not true compartment syndrome. DETERIORATION: untreated progressive envenomation → severe coagulopathy/bleeding, tissue loss. Antivenom for indicated cases, serial monitoring, and toxicology involvement are the path.
criticalActions: Provide supportive care and analgesia; avoid tourniquets, incision/suction, and ice
Mark and serially monitor the leading edge of swelling; elevate the limb
Obtain and repeat CBC, coagulation studies, and fibrinogen (venom effects evolve)
Give antivenom for indicated cases (progressive swelling, coagulopathy, systemic toxicity), titrated to control
Update tetanus and consult toxicology/poison control
Distinguish subcutaneous swelling from true compartment syndrome (measure pressures; fasciotomy is rarely needed)
Monitor for recurrent/delayed coagulopathy
learningPoints: Avoid tourniquets, incision/suction, and ice in pit viper bites — they worsen injury.
Antivenom is indicated for progressive swelling, hematologic venom effects, or systemic toxicity.
Serially monitor swelling and repeat coagulation studies — venom effects evolve and can recur.
Tense swelling is usually subcutaneous, not true compartment syndrome — measure pressures before fasciotomy.
cases.intro: A 41-year-old hiker is brought in an hour after a rattlesnake struck his lower leg. The bite site has two fang marks with rapidly spreading swelling, bruising, and severe pain now creeping up toward his knee. Early labs show a dropping platelet count and abnormal clotting.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: A rattlesnake got me on the trail — the swelling's climbing up my leg and it's throbbing. Somebody wanted to put a tourniquet on it.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Swelling's advancing up the calf, and his platelets and fibrinogen are already off. No tourniquet on.

cases.title: Open globe (ruptured globe) injury
cases.chiefComplaint: 38M — eye trauma with a metal fragment, vision loss, teardrop pupil
cases.diagnosis: Open globe (ruptured globe) injury
cases.context: 38-year-old man struck the eye while hammering metal without protection; he has severe eye pain, marked vision loss, and exam shows a peaked/TEARDROP PUPIL, a shallow anterior chamber, and possible extrusion of ocular contents (a positive Seidel sign would confirm leaking aqueous). True diagnosis is an OPEN GLOBE (ruptured globe) — a sight-threatening ocular emergency, with a likely intraocular foreign body given the metal-on-metal mechanism. CORRECT PATH: STOP examining/manipulating the eye once an open globe is suspected — do NOT apply pressure, do NOT check intraocular pressure, do NOT instill medications or attempt to remove a foreign body; place a RIGID EYE SHIELD (not a patch); keep the patient calm, elevate the head, control PAIN and NAUSEA/VOMITING (prevent Valsalva/pressure spikes that extrude contents); make the patient NPO; give BROAD-SPECTRUM IV ANTIBIOTICS (endophthalmitis prophylaxis) and update tetanus; obtain CT orbits (avoid MRI if a metallic foreign body is possible); and consult OPHTHALMOLOGY EMERGENTLY for operative repair. RED HERRING: measuring intraocular pressure or manipulating the globe (extrudes contents), or patching instead of shielding. DETERIORATION: pressure on the globe or Valsalva → extrusion of intraocular contents and permanent vision loss. Rigid shield, antibiotics, antiemetics, CT, and emergent ophthalmology are the path.
criticalActions: Recognize an open globe (teardrop pupil, shallow chamber, extruded contents/positive Seidel) and stop manipulating the eye
Do NOT apply pressure, check intraocular pressure, instill medications, or remove a foreign body
Place a rigid eye shield (not a patch) and elevate the head; keep the patient calm
Control pain and nausea/vomiting to prevent pressure spikes; keep NPO
Give broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for endophthalmitis prophylaxis and update tetanus
Obtain CT of the orbits (avoid MRI if a metallic foreign body is possible)
Consult ophthalmology emergently for operative repair
learningPoints: Suspected open globe: rigid shield, no pressure, no IOP check, no eye manipulation.
Control pain and vomiting to prevent Valsalva-driven extrusion of intraocular contents.
Give IV antibiotics for endophthalmitis prophylaxis and get CT orbits (not MRI with metallic foreign body).
Consult ophthalmology emergently — an open globe is a surgical emergency.
cases.intro: A 38-year-old man was hammering a metal stake without eye protection when something struck his right eye. He's in severe pain and can barely see out of it. The pupil is peaked into a teardrop shape pointing toward a small laceration, and there's fluid on the surface of the eye.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Something flew off the metal and hit my eye — the pain is awful and everything's blurry and dark on that side.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: The pupil's teardrop-shaped and there's fluid leaking. He was hammering metal with no goggles.

cases.article: 2015-12_Intracerebral Hemorrhage In Anticoagulated Patients, Evidence-Based Emergency Department Management.pdf
cases.title: Intracerebral hemorrhage in an anticoagulated patient
cases.chiefComplaint: 77M on warfarin — sudden hemiparesis, headache, decreased consciousness
cases.diagnosis: Intracerebral hemorrhage on anticoagulation
cases.context: 77-year-old man on warfarin (for atrial fibrillation) with sudden severe headache, left hemiparesis, vomiting, and declining consciousness; noncontrast CT shows an intracerebral hemorrhage and his INR is elevated. True diagnosis is anticoagulation-associated intracerebral hemorrhage — where hematoma EXPANSION drives deterioration, so RAPID REVERSAL is the priority. CORRECT PATH: obtain emergent noncontrast head CT and coagulation studies; REVERSE the anticoagulant IMMEDIATELY and agent-specifically — for WARFARIN give PROTHROMBIN COMPLEX CONCENTRATE (PCC, faster/preferred over FFP) PLUS IV VITAMIN K; for DABIGATRAN give IDARUCIZUMAB; for factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban/rivaroxaban) give ANDEXANET ALFA (or PCC if unavailable); manage BLOOD PRESSURE (lower markedly elevated systolic per guideline targets to reduce expansion, avoiding excessive drops); reverse/withhold antiplatelet contribution as indicated; manage raised ICP and airway for declining GCS; and consult NEUROSURGERY and neurology/neurocritical care urgently. RED HERRING: using FFP alone for warfarin (slow, volume) or delaying reversal to 'wait for repeat imaging'; forgetting agent-specific reversal for DOACs. DETERIORATION: hematoma expansion → herniation and death. Immediate agent-specific reversal, BP control, and neurosurgical involvement are the path.
criticalActions: Obtain emergent noncontrast head CT and coagulation studies
Reverse the anticoagulant immediately and agent-specifically
For warfarin: give prothrombin complex concentrate (preferred over FFP) plus IV vitamin K
For dabigatran: give idarucizumab; for factor Xa inhibitors: give andexanet alfa (or PCC if unavailable)
Control markedly elevated blood pressure to reduce hematoma expansion (avoid excessive drops)
Manage raised ICP and protect the airway for declining consciousness
Consult neurosurgery and neurocritical care urgently
learningPoints: In anticoagulated ICH, rapid agent-specific reversal limits hematoma expansion.
Warfarin: PCC plus IV vitamin K (PCC is faster and preferred over FFP).
Dabigatran: idarucizumab; factor Xa inhibitors: andexanet alfa (or PCC).
Control blood pressure and involve neurosurgery emergently.
cases.intro: A 77-year-old man on warfarin for atrial fibrillation is brought in after a sudden severe headache dropped him to the floor with a weak left side; he's vomited and is becoming harder to rouse. His blood pressure is very high, and the noncontrast head CT shows a bleed in the right hemisphere. His INR comes back elevated.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He grabbed his head, said it was the worst pain, and his left side went weak. He's on warfarin for his heart.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: CT shows an intracerebral bleed, INR's up, pressure's 208 over 112, and his level of consciousness is dropping.

cases.article: 2016-05_Congenital Heart Disease In Pediatric Patients - Recognizing The Undiagnosed And Managing Complications In The Emergency Department.pdf
cases.title: Ductal-dependent congenital heart disease (neonatal shock)
cases.chiefComplaint: 10-day-old — sudden shock/cyanosis, poor feeding, weak pulses
cases.diagnosis: Ductal-dependent congenital heart lesion presenting in shock
cases.context: 10-day-old, previously thought well, presents with sudden lethargy, poor feeding, gray/cyanotic color, tachypnea, and shock with WEAK/ABSENT femoral pulses (or marked pre/post-ductal saturation difference). True diagnosis is a previously undiagnosed DUCTAL-DEPENDENT congenital heart lesion (e.g., critical left-heart obstruction like coarctation/hypoplastic left heart, or a ductal-dependent cyanotic lesion) presenting as the DUCTUS ARTERIOSUS CLOSES around 1-2 weeks of life. KEY TEACHING: a neonate in shock or with cyanosis in the first weeks of life must be considered for ductal-dependent CHD, and the life-saving intervention is PROSTAGLANDIN E1 to reopen/maintain the ductus. CORRECT PATH: recognize neonatal shock/cyanosis and consider CHD (check 4-extremity blood pressures and pre/post-ductal saturations, examine femoral pulses); start PROSTAGLANDIN E1 (PGE1) infusion (be prepared for apnea — a known PGE1 side effect — and support the airway); judicious fluids (small aliquots — the failing heart tolerates overload poorly), treat metabolic acidosis, obtain CXR, ECG, blood gas, glucose, and cultures (cover for sepsis empirically, as neonatal sepsis mimics this); consult PEDIATRIC CARDIOLOGY and arrange echocardiography and transfer. RED HERRING: diagnosing 'sepsis' or 'bronchiolitis' alone and missing ductal-dependent CHD; large fluid boluses worsening the failing heart. DETERIORATION: as the duct closes, cardiovascular collapse. PGE1, cautious resuscitation, and cardiology are the path.
criticalActions: Consider ductal-dependent CHD in any neonate with shock or cyanosis in the first weeks of life
Check 4-extremity blood pressures, pre/post-ductal saturations, and femoral pulses
Start a prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) infusion to reopen/maintain the ductus arteriosus
Anticipate PGE1-induced apnea and be prepared to support the airway
Give cautious fluids in small aliquots (the failing heart tolerates overload poorly) and treat acidosis
Cover empirically for neonatal sepsis (a mimic) and obtain CXR/ECG/gas/glucose/cultures
Consult pediatric cardiology, arrange echocardiography, and transfer
learningPoints: A neonate in shock or cyanosis in the first weeks of life may have ductal-dependent CHD as the duct closes.
Prostaglandin E1 reopens the ductus and is life-saving — anticipate apnea and support the airway.
Check femoral pulses and pre/post-ductal saturations; give fluids cautiously.
Neonatal sepsis mimics this — cover empirically while pursuing the cardiac diagnosis.
cases.intro: A 10-day-old boy is rushed in gray, mottled, and breathing fast, having fed poorly and become floppy over the last day. He was born at term and seemed fine at discharge. His hands and feet are dusky, his femoral pulses are barely palpable, and his oxygen saturation is low and doesn't improve much with oxygen.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He was fine when we took him home. Today he wouldn't eat and went gray and floppy — something's really wrong.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Ten days old, mottled and shocky, sat's 82 and barely moves with oxygen. I can hardly feel femoral pulses.

cases.title: Multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C)
cases.chiefComplaint: 8yo — days of high fever, rash, red eyes, abdominal pain, now shock
cases.diagnosis: Multisystem inflammatory syndrome in children (MIS-C)
cases.context: 8-year-old with several days of high fever, rash, conjunctival injection, abdominal pain/vomiting, and now hypotension and poor perfusion, a few weeks after a COVID-19 infection. True diagnosis is MIS-C — a post-infectious hyperinflammatory syndrome with Kawasaki-like features PLUS prominent GI symptoms and a tendency to SHOCK and CARDIAC dysfunction. KEY TEACHING: MIS-C emerged as a distinct entity with significant morbidity/mortality; recognize the combination of persistent fever, multisystem involvement (mucocutaneous, GI, cardiac), elevated inflammatory markers, and recent SARS-CoV-2 exposure. CORRECT PATH: recognize MIS-C and evaluate for SHOCK and cardiac involvement — obtain inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR, ferritin, D-dimer), troponin and BNP, ECG and ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY (for coronary aneurysms and ventricular dysfunction), CBC, blood cultures (exclude bacterial sepsis, which is on the differential); RESUSCITATE septic/cardiogenic shock CAUTIOUSLY (myocardial dysfunction means large fluid boluses can worsen it — titrate fluids and start inotropes/vasopressors early), give empiric ANTIBIOTICS until bacterial infection is excluded, and treat with IVIG (and corticosteroids) per protocol with PEDIATRIC CARDIOLOGY/RHEUMATOLOGY/CRITICAL CARE and consider aspirin/anticoagulation; admit to an appropriate level of care. RED HERRING: anchoring on gastroenteritis/appendicitis for the GI symptoms, or bolusing large volumes into a dysfunctional heart. DETERIORATION: unrecognized cardiac dysfunction/shock. Recognition, cautious resuscitation, IVIG/steroids, and multidisciplinary care are the path.
criticalActions: Recognize MIS-C (persistent fever, multisystem involvement, GI/mucocutaneous/cardiac features, recent SARS-CoV-2)
Obtain inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR/ferritin/D-dimer), troponin, BNP, ECG, and echocardiography
Obtain CBC and blood cultures and give empiric antibiotics until bacterial sepsis is excluded
Resuscitate shock cautiously — titrate fluids and start inotropes/vasopressors early for cardiac dysfunction
Treat with IVIG and corticosteroids per protocol; consider aspirin/anticoagulation
Involve pediatric cardiology, rheumatology, and critical care
Admit to an appropriate level of care
learningPoints: MIS-C: post-COVID hyperinflammation with Kawasaki-like plus GI features and a tendency to shock/cardiac dysfunction.
Get an echocardiogram and troponin/BNP — myocardial dysfunction changes resuscitation.
Resuscitate cautiously with early inotropes; large fluid boluses can worsen cardiac dysfunction.
Treat with IVIG and steroids, cover for bacterial sepsis until excluded, and involve subspecialists.
cases.intro: An 8-year-old girl has had high fevers for four days with a blotchy rash, red eyes, and bad belly pain with vomiting. Her parents mention the whole family had COVID about a month ago. Today she's listless with cold hands, a racing heart, and a blood pressure that's dropping.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's had a high fever for days, a rash, red eyes, and terrible stomach pain. We all had COVID a few weeks ago.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's tachy at 150 and her pressure's soft at 82. Cold extremities, fever 39.5, rash and red eyes.

cases.article: 2015-05_Drowning In The Adult Population Emergency Department Resuscitation And Treatment.pdf
cases.title: Drowning (submersion injury)
cases.chiefComplaint: 22M — pulled from lake, hypoxic, coughing frothy sputum
cases.diagnosis: Drowning (submersion injury) with hypoxemia
cases.context: 22-year-old man pulled from a lake after a submersion event, now with severe hypoxia, coughing frothy sputum, respiratory distress, and altered mental status. True diagnosis is drowning (submersion injury) — the core insult is HYPOXEMIA from surfactant washout, atelectasis, and pulmonary edema/ARDS. KEY TEACHING: OXYGENATION AND VENTILATION are the priorities; there is no role for routine abdominal thrusts to 'remove water' (delays ventilation and risks aspiration), and the wet/dry distinction is not clinically useful. CORRECT PATH: prioritize the AIRWAY and OXYGENATION — supplemental oxygen, escalate to NIPPV/positive-pressure ventilation and intubation with PEEP for significant hypoxia/ARDS; for arrest, rescue breathing/ventilation is emphasized (hypoxic arrest) with standard CPR; consider concomitant HYPOTHERMIA (rewarm; 'not dead until warm and dead' in cold-water immersion) and TRAUMA (C-SPINE precautions if diving/trauma mechanism); monitor for delayed pulmonary deterioration and observe symptomatic patients; there is NO role for prophylactic antibiotics or steroids routinely. Treat complications (arrhythmia, cerebral hypoxic injury). RED HERRING: attempting to expel water with abdominal thrusts; discharging a symptomatic patient too early; forgetting hypothermia/trauma. DETERIORATION: progressive hypoxia/ARDS, hypothermic arrhythmia. Oxygenation/ventilation with PEEP, rewarming, and observation are the path.
criticalActions: Prioritize the airway and oxygenation (supplemental oxygen, escalate to NIPPV/positive-pressure with PEEP)
Intubate with PEEP for significant hypoxia/ARDS
Emphasize ventilation/rescue breathing in submersion arrest (hypoxic arrest)
Do NOT perform abdominal thrusts to 'remove water'
Consider and manage concomitant hypothermia (rewarm) and trauma (C-spine precautions if indicated)
Observe symptomatic patients for delayed pulmonary deterioration
Avoid routine prophylactic antibiotics/steroids
learningPoints: Drowning is fundamentally a hypoxic insult — oxygenation and ventilation (with PEEP) are the priorities.
Don't attempt to 'remove water' with abdominal thrusts; it delays ventilation and risks aspiration.
Consider concurrent hypothermia and trauma; observe symptomatic patients for delayed deterioration.
Prophylactic antibiotics and steroids are not routinely indicated.
cases.intro: A 22-year-old man is rushed in after being pulled from a lake, where witnesses say he was submerged for a couple of minutes before being dragged out. He's coughing up frothy pink sputum, gasping, confused, and cold to the touch, with an oxygen saturation in the low 80s despite a mask.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Submersion in the lake for a couple of minutes, pulled out and coughing. He's hypoxic and cold — water temp was frigid.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Sat's 82 on a mask, he's coughing frothy sputum and confused. Core temp is 35.4.

cases.title: Infective endocarditis (with septic embolization)
cases.chiefComplaint: 36F, IVDU — fever, new murmur, focal neuro deficit
cases.diagnosis: Infective endocarditis with septic embolic stroke
cases.context: 36-year-old woman who injects drugs presents with weeks of fevers, malaise, and night sweats, a new heart murmur, peripheral stigmata (Janeway lesions/splinter hemorrhages), and now an acute focal neurologic deficit from a SEPTIC EMBOLUS. True diagnosis is infective endocarditis (likely right- and/or left-sided, S. aureus common in IVDU) complicated by embolic stroke. KEY TEACHING: consider endocarditis in a febrile IVDU or a patient with fever plus a new murmur/embolic phenomena; endocarditis-associated stroke has special management nuances (thrombolysis is generally CONTRAINDICATED for endocarditis-associated ischemic stroke due to high hemorrhagic transformation risk from mycotic aneurysms). CORRECT PATH: obtain multiple sets of BLOOD CULTURES (before antibiotics when feasible), start EMPIRIC ANTIBIOTICS (vancomycin + coverage per risk) promptly for the septic patient, obtain ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY (TTE then TEE for vegetations), evaluate the stroke with neuroimaging (CT/CTA — look for mycotic aneurysm) but AVOID thrombolytics, apply the Duke criteria, and consult CARDIOLOGY, INFECTIOUS DISEASE, and CARDIOTHORACIC SURGERY (surgical indications: heart failure, uncontrolled infection, recurrent emboli, large mobile vegetations). RED HERRING: giving tPA for the acute stroke without recognizing endocarditis (dangerous), or missing endocarditis in a febrile injection-drug user. DETERIORATION: ongoing embolization, valve failure, sepsis. Cultures, empiric antibiotics, echo, avoiding thrombolysis, and multidisciplinary care are the path.
criticalActions: Consider infective endocarditis in a febrile IVDU or fever with a new murmur/embolic phenomena
Obtain multiple blood cultures (before antibiotics when feasible)
Start empiric antibiotics promptly for the septic patient (vancomycin + coverage per risk)
Obtain echocardiography (TTE then TEE) to identify vegetations
Image the stroke (CT/CTA) but AVOID thrombolytics in endocarditis-associated stroke
Apply the Duke criteria and evaluate for complications (heart failure, emboli, mycotic aneurysm)
Consult cardiology, infectious disease, and cardiothoracic surgery
learningPoints: Suspect endocarditis with fever plus a new murmur or embolic phenomena, especially in injection drug users.
Avoid thrombolysis in endocarditis-associated ischemic stroke — high hemorrhagic transformation risk.
Get blood cultures before antibiotics when feasible, then treat empirically; obtain echocardiography.
Surgical indications include heart failure, uncontrolled infection, recurrent emboli, and large vegetations.
cases.intro: A 36-year-old woman who injects drugs comes in with weeks of fevers, drenching night sweats, and exhaustion — and this morning her right arm suddenly went weak and clumsy. She has a new heart murmur, tender spots on her fingertips, and small splinter-like marks under her nails. She's febrile at 38.9°C.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've had fevers and night sweats for weeks, and this morning my arm just stopped working right. I use needles, I won't lie.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's febrile with a new murmur and a focal arm weakness. There are splinter hemorrhages under her nails.

cases.title: Acute appendicitis
cases.chiefComplaint: 22M — periumbilical pain migrating to RLQ, anorexia, fever
cases.diagnosis: Acute appendicitis
cases.context: 22-year-old man with ~18 hours of pain that began periumbilically and MIGRATED to the right lower quadrant, with anorexia, nausea, low-grade fever, and localized tenderness at McBurney point with guarding/rebound. True diagnosis is acute appendicitis. CORRECT PATH: focused history/exam (migratory pain, anorexia, peritoneal signs; consider Alvarado-type scoring), obtain labs (leukocytosis) and a PREGNANCY TEST in women; IMAGING — ULTRASOUND first in children and pregnancy (avoid radiation), CT abdomen/pelvis in adults when the diagnosis is uncertain; provide ANALGESIA (does not obscure the surgical exam), IV fluids, keep NPO, give ANTIBIOTICS, and consult SURGERY for appendectomy; recognize complicated appendicitis (perforation, abscess) and atypical presentations (retrocecal, pelvic, pregnancy with displaced appendix, extremes of age). RED HERRING: attributing pain to gastroenteritis and discharging; a normal WBC does not exclude it; withholding analgesia for fear of masking the exam. DETERIORATION: missed appendicitis → perforation, peritonitis, abscess, sepsis. History/exam, judicious imaging, antibiotics, and surgery are the path.
criticalActions: Recognize the appendicitis pattern (migratory RLQ pain, anorexia, peritoneal signs)
Obtain labs and a pregnancy test in women of reproductive age
Choose imaging appropriately (ultrasound first in children/pregnancy; CT in uncertain adults)
Provide analgesia, IV fluids, and keep the patient NPO
Give antibiotics
Consult surgery for appendectomy
Recognize complicated disease (perforation/abscess) and atypical presentations
learningPoints: Migratory RLQ pain with anorexia and peritoneal signs suggests appendicitis; a normal WBC doesn't exclude it.
Ultrasound first in children and pregnancy; CT when the diagnosis is uncertain in adults.
Analgesia does not obscure the surgical exam — treat pain.
Watch for atypical presentations (retrocecal, pelvic, pregnancy, extremes of age).
cases.intro: A 22-year-old man describes a dull ache that started around his navel yesterday and has since settled into a sharp pain in the right lower part of his belly. He has no appetite and feels nauseated. He's tender over the right lower quadrant and winces when you release your hand.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It started around my belly button and moved down to the right — now it really hurts there, and I can't eat anything.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's tender at McBurney's with some guarding, low-grade fever. Want labs and imaging?

cases.title: Button battery ingestion (esophageal)
cases.chiefComplaint: 2yo — witnessed battery ingestion, drooling, refusing food
cases.diagnosis: Esophageal button battery impaction
cases.context: 2-year-old with a witnessed (or suspected) button/disc battery ingestion, now drooling and refusing to eat. True diagnosis is an ESOPHAGEAL BUTTON BATTERY — a TIME-CRITICAL emergency because a battery lodged in the esophagus causes rapid caustic/electrical mucosal injury within HOURS (necrosis, and catastrophic complications like tracheoesophageal fistula or aorto-esophageal fistula/exsanguination). KEY TEACHING: this is one of the most time-sensitive foreign-body emergencies. CORRECT PATH: obtain IMMEDIATE X-RAYS (AP and lateral neck/chest/abdomen) — a battery shows a 'double-ring/halo' sign distinguishing it from a coin; if the battery is in the ESOPHAGUS, it must be REMOVED EMERGENTLY by endoscopy (do not delay); as a mitigating measure BEFORE removal in a child >12 months who can swallow, give HONEY (or sucralfate) every 10 minutes to reduce mucosal injury (do NOT give if perforation/mediastinitis suspected or unable to swallow); keep NPO, consult GI/ENT/surgery emergently, and involve poison control; batteries beyond the esophagus in an asymptomatic child are usually managed expectantly. RED HERRING: assuming it's a swallowed coin and observing, or delaying removal — esophageal batteries injure within hours. DETERIORATION: esophageal necrosis, fistula formation, massive hemorrhage. Immediate imaging, honey, and emergent endoscopic removal are the path.
criticalActions: Recognize esophageal button battery ingestion as a time-critical emergency
Obtain immediate AP and lateral x-rays and identify the battery 'halo/double-ring' sign (vs coin)
For an esophageal battery, arrange emergent endoscopic removal without delay
Give honey (or sucralfate) every 10 minutes before removal in an appropriate child who can swallow (not if perforation suspected)
Keep the patient NPO
Consult GI/ENT/surgery emergently and involve poison control
Monitor for and recognize complications (fistula, mediastinitis, hemorrhage)
learningPoints: An esophageal button battery injures within hours — emergent endoscopic removal, don't observe.
The 'double-ring/halo' sign on x-ray distinguishes a battery from a coin.
Honey (or sucralfate) before removal mitigates injury in an appropriate child who can swallow.
Beware delayed catastrophic complications: tracheoesophageal and aorto-esophageal fistula.
cases.intro: A 2-year-old girl is brought in after her parents found the back of a remote open and a small disc battery missing; shortly after, she began drooling and refusing to eat or drink. She's fussy and keeps spitting up saliva.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: The battery from the remote is gone and she was playing with it. Now she's drooling and won't eat or drink anything.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's drooling and refusing to swallow. If that's a battery in the esophagus, we need to move fast, right?

cases.title: Orbital cellulitis (sinusitis complication)
cases.chiefComplaint: 9yo — after sinusitis, red swollen eye, proptosis, painful eye movement
cases.diagnosis: Orbital cellulitis complicating acute bacterial sinusitis
cases.context: 9-year-old with a week of upper respiratory/sinus symptoms now with a red, swollen, painful eye, PROPTOSIS, PAIN WITH and RESTRICTION OF EYE MOVEMENT, and early diplopia, with fever. True diagnosis is ORBITAL (postseptal) CELLULITIS — a complication of acute bacterial sinusitis (ethmoid) that is an emergency, distinguished from the benign PRESEPTAL cellulitis by the presence of proptosis, painful/limited extraocular movements, and vision changes. KEY TEACHING: acute bacterial sinusitis can be complicated by ORBITAL, INTRACRANIAL, and osseous extension. CORRECT PATH: distinguish orbital from preseptal cellulitis (check extraocular movements, proptosis, vision/color vision, and pupillary response for optic nerve compromise); obtain CONTRAST CT of the orbits and sinuses (assess for subperiosteal/orbital abscess and intracranial extension); start EMPIRIC IV ANTIBIOTICS covering typical sinus organisms and MRSA; consult OPHTHALMOLOGY and ENT urgently (abscess or vision compromise may need surgical drainage); admit; watch for INTRACRANIAL complications (cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, abscess). RED HERRING: treating orbital cellulitis as simple preseptal cellulitis with oral antibiotics and discharge — the dangerous miss; vision loss and intracranial spread are the risks. DETERIORATION: vision loss, cavernous sinus thrombosis, intracranial abscess. CT, IV antibiotics, and urgent ophthalmology/ENT are the path.
criticalActions: Distinguish orbital (postseptal) from preseptal cellulitis (proptosis, painful/limited EOM, vision changes)
Assess visual acuity, color vision, and pupillary response for optic nerve compromise
Obtain contrast CT of the orbits and sinuses (assess for abscess and intracranial extension)
Start empiric IV antibiotics covering sinus organisms and MRSA
Consult ophthalmology and ENT urgently
Admit and monitor for intracranial complications (cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, abscess)
Arrange surgical drainage for abscess or vision compromise
learningPoints: Orbital cellulitis (proptosis, painful/limited eye movements, vision change) is an emergency — not preseptal cellulitis.
It complicates ethmoid sinusitis and can extend to abscess or intracranial infection.
Get contrast CT of orbits/sinuses and start IV antibiotics with MRSA coverage.
Watch for optic nerve compromise and cavernous sinus thrombosis.
cases.intro: A 9-year-old boy who's had a stuffy nose and sinus congestion for a week now has a red, swollen, painful right eye that's bulging forward. He says it hurts to look around and he's started seeing double. He's febrile and the eyelid is tense and warm.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's had a sinus thing all week, and now his eye is swollen shut and bulging, and he says it hurts to move it.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: The eye's proptotic and his eye movements are painful and limited. Temp's 38.7.

cases.title: Sentinel injury / suspected child physical abuse
cases.chiefComplaint: 4-month-old — bruise on the cheek, 'rolled off couch'
cases.diagnosis: Sentinel injury concerning for child physical abuse
cases.context: 4-month-old, not yet independently mobile, brought in for a bruise on the cheek with a vague/inconsistent history ('rolled off the couch'); on exam there is also a torn frenulum. True concern is a SENTINEL INJURY of child physical abuse — a minor, often-overlooked injury (bruise, subconjunctival hemorrhage, oral injury/torn frenulum, burn) in a young, PRE-MOBILE infant that can precede severe abuse. KEY TEACHING: 'those who don't cruise rarely bruise' — bruising in a non-ambulatory infant is a RED FLAG; failure to recognize sentinel injuries can result in devastating outcomes including death.
