CORRECT PATH: recognize the red flags (injury inconsistent with developmental stage/mechanism, changing/implausible history, delayed presentation, patterned injuries), perform a thorough head-to-toe exam, and pursue an OCCULT-INJURY WORKUP appropriate to age — SKELETAL SURVEY (for children <2 years), NEUROIMAGING (CT/MRI head for concern of abusive head trauma), fundoscopic exam (retinal hemorrhages), LFTs/lipase and urinalysis for occult abdominal trauma, and coagulation studies to evaluate mimics; ensure the child's SAFETY, make a MANDATED REPORT to child protective services, consult the CHILD ABUSE/protection team and admit as needed; document objectively. RED HERRING: accepting the caregiver's explanation and discharging without a workup or report — the catastrophic miss; attributing bruising to normal infant activity. DETERIORATION: return to an unsafe environment → escalating abuse, death. Recognition, occult-injury workup, mandated reporting, and ensuring safety are the path.
criticalActions: Recognize a sentinel injury in a pre-mobile infant as a red flag for physical abuse
Identify concerning features (injury inconsistent with development/history, changing story, delayed presentation, patterned injuries)
Perform a thorough head-to-toe examination
Pursue an age-appropriate occult-injury workup (skeletal survey <2y, head neuroimaging, fundoscopy, LFTs/lipase, coagulation studies)
Ensure the child's immediate safety
Make a mandated report to child protective services and consult the child protection team
Document findings objectively and admit as needed
learningPoints: Bruising in a non-ambulatory infant is a red flag — 'those who don't cruise rarely bruise.'
Sentinel injuries (bruises, torn frenulum, subconjunctival hemorrhage, burns) can precede severe abuse.
Work up occult injury: skeletal survey, head imaging, fundoscopy, and screen for mimics.
Ensure safety and make a mandated report — missing it can be fatal.
cases.intro: A 4-month-old boy is brought in by a caregiver for a bruise on his cheek, explained as 'he rolled off the couch.' The baby isn't rolling or crawling yet. On exam you also notice a small tear of the tissue connecting his upper lip to his gum. The caregiver's account of what happened shifts as you ask more questions.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He rolled off the couch, that's all. Well — maybe his sibling bumped him. I wasn't right there.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's four months, not mobile yet, with a cheek bruise and a torn frenulum. The story keeps changing.

cases.title: Neonatal cholestatic jaundice (biliary atresia)
cases.chiefComplaint: 5-week-old — persistent jaundice, pale stools, dark urine
cases.diagnosis: Biliary atresia (conjugated hyperbilirubinemia)
cases.context: 5-week-old, breastfed, with PERSISTENT jaundice that hasn't resolved, now with ACHOLIC (pale/clay-colored) STOOLS and dark urine; the infant is otherwise fairly well-appearing. True diagnosis is BILIARY ATRESIA causing CONJUGATED (direct) HYPERBILIRUBINEMIA — a time-critical can't-miss because the Kasai portoenterostomy has far better outcomes when performed EARLY (ideally before ~8 weeks of age). KEY TEACHING: any jaundice persisting beyond ~2 weeks of age must be FRACTIONATED — a CONJUGATED (direct) bilirubin elevation is ALWAYS PATHOLOGIC and points to cholestasis (biliary atresia, choledochal cyst, neonatal hepatitis, metabolic/infectious causes), whereas benign physiologic/breast-milk jaundice is UNCONJUGATED. CORRECT PATH: recognize that prolonged jaundice + pale stools requires evaluation; obtain a FRACTIONATED (total AND direct) bilirubin — a conjugated elevation mandates urgent workup; check LFTs, coagulation (vitamin K-dependent factors — risk of bleeding), and consult PEDIATRIC GI/hepatology and surgery urgently for evaluation (ultrasound, further imaging) and timely Kasai if biliary atresia; give vitamin K; do not attribute cholestatic jaundice to breast-milk jaundice. RED HERRING: reassuring the family that it's 'breast-milk jaundice' without fractionating the bilirubin or noting the pale stools — the disastrous, time-losing miss. DETERIORATION: delayed diagnosis past the surgical window → biliary cirrhosis and need for transplant. Fractionation, urgent GI/surgery referral, and timely Kasai are the path.
criticalActions: Recognize that jaundice persisting beyond ~2 weeks with pale stools requires evaluation
Obtain a fractionated (total and direct/conjugated) bilirubin
Recognize that a conjugated (direct) bilirubin elevation is always pathologic (cholestasis)
Check LFTs and coagulation studies (risk of vitamin K-dependent bleeding) and give vitamin K
Do not attribute cholestatic jaundice to physiologic/breast-milk jaundice
Consult pediatric GI/hepatology and surgery urgently and arrange imaging (ultrasound)
Expedite evaluation to allow timely Kasai portoenterostomy (ideally before ~8 weeks)
learningPoints: Any jaundice beyond ~2 weeks of age must be fractionated — a direct/conjugated elevation is always pathologic.
Pale stools plus prolonged jaundice suggests biliary atresia — a time-critical surgical diagnosis.
The Kasai procedure has far better outcomes when done early (before ~8 weeks).
Don't dismiss cholestatic jaundice as breast-milk jaundice.
cases.intro: First-time parents bring in their 5-week-old daughter because she still looks yellow — it never went away after the newborn period. When you ask, they mention her stools have turned pale, almost gray, and her urine looks dark. She's feeding and otherwise seems well, and they've been told before it's 'just breast-milk jaundice.'

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's been yellow since birth and it hasn't gone away. Lately her poop looks pale, almost gray. Someone said it's just from breastfeeding.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Five weeks old, still jaundiced with pale stools and dark urine. Do you want the bilirubin fractionated?

cases.title: Severe posterior epistaxis
cases.chiefComplaint: 70M on anticoagulation — profuse bleeding from both nostrils and throat
cases.diagnosis: Posterior epistaxis (severe)
cases.context: 70-year-old man on apixaban with heavy nosebleeding that won't stop with pinching, now bleeding from BOTH nostrils and down the THROAT, feeling lightheaded. True diagnosis is a POSTERIOR epistaxis — less common but more DANGEROUS than anterior bleeds (harder to control, associated with airway/aspiration risk and significant blood loss, more frequent in older/anticoagulated patients). CORRECT PATH: ABC first — protect the AIRWAY (blood in the posterior pharynx, aspiration risk), sit the patient upright leaning forward, and resuscitate as needed (IV access, type and screen for significant loss); apply a topical VASOCONSTRICTOR (oxymetazoline) and attempt firm sustained ANTERIOR PRESSURE first; if an anterior source isn't controlled, suspect a posterior bleed and place a POSTERIOR PACK / dual-balloon device (or layered anterior-posterior packing) to tamponade, ensuring the airway is protected; reverse/consider the anticoagulation contribution (check coags; reversal agents for significant hemorrhage); after posterior packing, ADMIT/observe with ENT consultation (risk of rebleeding, hypoxia/bradycardia, and the need for definitive control such as arterial ligation/embolization). RED HERRING: treating a life-threatening posterior bleed like a minor anterior nosebleed and discharging; neglecting the airway/aspiration risk and the anticoagulation. DETERIORATION: airway compromise, hemorrhagic shock. Airway protection, packing/tamponade, anticoagulation management, and ENT involvement are the path.
criticalActions: Protect the airway (aspiration risk from posterior pharyngeal blood) and position upright leaning forward
Establish IV access and resuscitate; type and screen for significant blood loss
Apply a topical vasoconstrictor and attempt firm sustained anterior pressure first
Recognize a posterior source and place a posterior pack / dual-balloon device (or layered packing) to tamponade
Assess and manage the anticoagulation contribution (check coagulation; reverse for significant hemorrhage)
Admit/observe after posterior packing and consult ENT
Arrange definitive control (arterial ligation/embolization) for refractory bleeding
learningPoints: Posterior epistaxis is more dangerous than anterior — airway risk, significant blood loss, harder to control.
Protect the airway and resuscitate; place a posterior pack/balloon when anterior measures fail.
Address anticoagulation and observe/admit after posterior packing (rebleed and hypoxia risk).
Involve ENT; refractory bleeds may need arterial ligation or embolization.
cases.intro: A 70-year-old man on a blood thinner arrives with a towel pressed to his face, having bled from his nose for over an hour despite pinching it. Now blood is running from both nostrils and he keeps coughing and spitting it up from the back of his throat. He feels lightheaded and there's blood pooling in the basin.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've been bleeding for over an hour and pinching doesn't help — now it's running down my throat and I feel woozy.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Bleeding from both sides and down the posterior pharynx, he's on apixaban. He keeps having to spit out blood.

cases.title: Hypertensive emergency (hypertensive encephalopathy)
cases.chiefComplaint: 55F — BP 230/130 with headache, confusion, visual changes
cases.diagnosis: Hypertensive emergency with encephalopathy (end-organ damage)
cases.context: 55-year-old woman with a severe headache, confusion, nausea, and visual disturbances, with a blood pressure of 230/130 and papilledema. True diagnosis is a HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY — severely elevated blood pressure WITH ACUTE TARGET-ORGAN DAMAGE (here, hypertensive encephalopathy) — as distinguished from asymptomatic severe hypertension ('hypertensive urgency') that does NOT need acute IV lowering. KEY TEACHING: the diagnosis hinges on evidence of END-ORGAN damage (brain, heart, kidneys, eyes, aorta), and the treatment principle is CONTROLLED, GRADUAL blood pressure reduction with titratable IV agents. CORRECT PATH: identify the affected organ (neuro exam, ECG/troponin, renal function/urinalysis, fundoscopy, consider CT head to exclude stroke/ICH), and lower the blood pressure in a CONTROLLED fashion with a TITRATABLE IV agent (e.g., nicardipine, clevidipine, or labetalol) — for hypertensive encephalopathy, reduce mean arterial pressure by roughly 10-20% (no more than ~25% in the first hour) to avoid cerebral hypoperfusion; the TARGET and agent depend on the syndrome (special cases: aortic dissection needs rapid rate+pressure control; acute ischemic stroke has specific permissive thresholds; avoid overly rapid drops). RED HERRING: rapidly normalizing the blood pressure (causing watershed ischemia/stroke), or treating asymptomatic severe hypertension aggressively; failing to identify the specific end-organ syndrome. DETERIORATION: uncontrolled hypertension → worsening encephalopathy/stroke; overcorrection → cerebral hypoperfusion. Identify end-organ damage and controlled titratable IV reduction are the path.
criticalActions: Confirm hypertensive emergency by identifying acute target-organ damage (brain/heart/kidney/eye/aorta)
Perform a neuro exam, fundoscopy, ECG/troponin, and renal function/urinalysis; consider CT head to exclude stroke/ICH
Lower blood pressure in a controlled, gradual fashion with a titratable IV agent (nicardipine/clevidipine/labetalol)
For hypertensive encephalopathy, reduce MAP ~10-20% (no more than ~25%) in the first hour
Tailor the target and agent to the specific syndrome (dissection, stroke, etc.)
Avoid rapid normalization that causes cerebral hypoperfusion
Do not aggressively treat asymptomatic severe hypertension (hypertensive urgency)
learningPoints: Hypertensive emergency = severe hypertension WITH acute end-organ damage; asymptomatic severe hypertension is not.
Use titratable IV agents and lower BP gradually (MAP ~10-20% in the first hour for encephalopathy).
The target and agent depend on the syndrome (dissection, stroke, pulmonary edema, eclampsia).
Rapid normalization can cause watershed cerebral ischemia.
cases.intro: A 55-year-old woman comes in with a pounding headache, nausea, and blurry vision, and she's increasingly confused about the date and where she is. Her blood pressure reads 232/130 in both arms, and on fundoscopy her optic discs look swollen.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My head is splitting and my vision's blurry… I feel foggy, I can't think straight.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Pressure's 232 over 130 in both arms, she's confused, and her optic discs look swollen.

cases.title: Transient ischemic attack
cases.chiefComplaint: 68M — resolved 20-min episode of arm weakness and slurred speech
cases.diagnosis: Transient ischemic attack (high-risk)
cases.context: 68-year-old man with hypertension, diabetes, and known atrial fibrillation, who had a 20-minute episode of right arm weakness and slurred speech that has now COMPLETELY RESOLVED. True diagnosis is a transient ischemic attack — critically, a WARNING sign of high short-term stroke risk (highest in the first days). KEY TEACHING: a TIA is a medical urgency, not a 'false alarm' — the goal is rapid risk stratification and initiation of secondary prevention to prevent an impending stroke. CORRECT PATH: confirm symptoms have resolved and there's no acute deficit, check GLUCOSE and exclude mimics (hypoglycemia, seizure, migraine), obtain NEUROIMAGING (noncontrast CT and vascular imaging — CTA of head/neck to find a treatable CAROTID STENOSIS or occlusion), ECG/monitoring (detect atrial fibrillation — a treatable cause), and labs; RISK-STRATIFY (e.g., ABCD2) and start SECONDARY PREVENTION — ANTIPLATELET therapy (or ANTICOAGULATION if AF is the source), statin, and blood-pressure/glucose management; arrange urgent evaluation for CAROTID REVASCULARIZATION if significant symptomatic stenosis; disposition (admission or a rapid TIA pathway) per risk. RED HERRING: reassuring the patient that 'it went away' and discharging without a workup or prevention — missing the chance to prevent the stroke that follows. DETERIORATION: an untreated high-risk TIA → completed stroke within days. Risk stratification, vascular imaging, AF detection, and prompt secondary prevention are the path.
criticalActions: Confirm symptom resolution and check glucose; exclude mimics (hypoglycemia, seizure, migraine)
Obtain neuroimaging including vascular imaging (CTA head/neck) to find carotid stenosis/occlusion
Obtain an ECG and cardiac monitoring to detect atrial fibrillation
Risk-stratify (e.g., ABCD2) for short-term stroke risk
Start secondary prevention: antiplatelet therapy (or anticoagulation for AF), a statin, and BP/glucose control
Arrange urgent evaluation for carotid revascularization if significant symptomatic stenosis
Determine disposition (admission or rapid TIA pathway) by risk
learningPoints: A TIA is a warning of high short-term stroke risk — act urgently, don't reassure and discharge.
Find treatable causes: carotid stenosis (vascular imaging) and atrial fibrillation (ECG/monitoring).
Start secondary prevention promptly (antiplatelet or anticoagulation, statin, risk-factor control).
Risk-stratify and arrange timely carotid revascularization when indicated.
cases.intro: A 68-year-old man comes in a bit shaken: an hour ago, for about 20 minutes, his right arm went weak and his speech slurred, then it fully returned to normal. He feels fine now and wonders if he even needs to be here. He has high blood pressure, diabetes, and atrial fibrillation.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My arm went weak and my words came out slurred for a bit, but it's completely gone now. Do I really need to stay?

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Symptoms resolved, exam looks normal now. He's got AFib on the monitor and a history of hypertension and diabetes.

cases.title: Acute myopericarditis
cases.chiefComplaint: 26M — pleuritic chest pain after viral illness, friction rub
cases.diagnosis: Acute myopericarditis
cases.context: 26-year-old previously healthy man with sharp, PLEURITIC chest pain that is WORSE LYING FLAT and RELIEVED BY SITTING FORWARD, a few weeks after a viral illness; exam reveals a pericardial FRICTION RUB, ECG shows DIFFUSE ST elevation with PR depression, and troponin is mildly elevated (myocardial involvement = myopericarditis). True diagnosis is acute myopericarditis. KEY TEACHING: these diseases typically present with chest pain/dyspnea in young people after a viral illness; the emergency clinician must recognize them, gauge myocardial involvement, and identify dangerous complications. CORRECT PATH: obtain ECG (classic diffuse ST elevation/PR depression, distinguish from STEMI — no reciprocal changes, PR depression, diffuse distribution), troponin (myocarditis component), inflammatory markers, and ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY to assess for EFFUSION/TAMPONADE and ventricular function; treat pericarditis with NSAIDs plus COLCHICINE (but if there is significant MYOCARDITIS/reduced EF, AVOID/limit high-dose NSAIDs and admit); recognize and manage complications — pericardial effusion/TAMPONADE (echo, pericardiocentesis if unstable) and myocarditis with heart failure/arrhythmia (admit, cardiology, monitor); advise activity restriction. RED HERRING: mistaking the diffuse ST elevation for a STEMI (or vice versa — anchoring on 'young, must be pericarditis' and missing an actual MI); missing tamponade or significant myocarditis. DETERIORATION: tamponade, malignant arrhythmia, cardiogenic shock from fulminant myocarditis. ECG/echo, NSAIDs+colchicine (pericarditis) with caution for myocarditis, and recognizing tamponade are the path.
criticalActions: Recognize pericarditis features (pleuritic, positional pain, friction rub, diffuse ST elevation with PR depression)
Obtain a troponin to assess myocardial involvement and distinguish from STEMI
Obtain echocardiography to assess for effusion/tamponade and ventricular function
Treat pericarditis with NSAIDs plus colchicine
For significant myocarditis/reduced EF, limit high-dose NSAIDs, admit, and involve cardiology
Recognize and manage tamponade (pericardiocentesis if unstable) and myocarditis complications (heart failure, arrhythmia)
Advise activity restriction
learningPoints: Pericarditis: positional pleuritic pain, friction rub, diffuse ST elevation with PR depression (no reciprocal change).
A raised troponin signals myocardial involvement (myopericarditis) — limit high-dose NSAIDs and admit.
Get an echocardiogram to look for effusion/tamponade and assess ventricular function.
Treat pericarditis with NSAIDs plus colchicine; watch for tamponade and fulminant myocarditis.
cases.intro: A 26-year-old man describes sharp chest pain that's been building for two days — it's worse when he lies back and eases when he leans forward, and it stabs when he takes a deep breath. He got over a cold last week. You hear a scratchy rub over his heart, and his ECG shows ST elevation across many leads.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's sharp and it's worse when I lie down — sitting up and leaning forward helps. It hurts to breathe in. I just had a cold.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: There's a friction rub, and the ECG shows ST elevation in a lot of leads with PR depression. Troponin's mildly up.

cases.title: Severe traumatic brain injury with herniation
cases.chiefComplaint: 40M — fall, GCS 6, blown pupil, Cushing response
cases.diagnosis: Severe traumatic brain injury with impending herniation
cases.context: 40-year-old man after a fall from height, unconscious with a GCS of 6, a fixed and dilated (blown) LEFT pupil, and signs of raised intracranial pressure (hypertension, bradycardia — the Cushing response, and irregular respirations). True diagnosis is severe TBI with a likely expanding intracranial hematoma and IMPENDING HERNIATION. CORRECT PATH: the priorities are preventing SECONDARY BRAIN INJURY — AIRWAY with RSI (protect the airway for GCS <=8; avoid hypoxia and hypotension, which markedly worsen outcomes), maintain OXYGENATION and a normal-to-slightly-elevated blood pressure (treat hypotension aggressively — even a single episode worsens outcome), maintain NORMOCAPNIA (avoid routine/prophylactic hyperventilation; use brief mild hyperventilation ONLY as a temporizing bridge for acute herniation), elevate the head of bed ~30 degrees and keep the neck midline; for signs of HERNIATION give HYPEROSMOLAR THERAPY (hypertonic saline or mannitol); obtain emergent noncontrast HEAD CT and consult NEUROSURGERY emergently for operative evacuation; reverse anticoagulation, treat/prevent seizures, and manage as polytrauma with C-spine precautions. RED HERRING: prophylactic hyperventilation (causes ischemia), permitting hypoxia/hypotension during intubation, or delaying neurosurgery/CT. DETERIORATION: herniation, secondary injury from hypoxia/hypotension. Airway with avoidance of hypoxia/hypotension, hyperosmolar therapy, CT, and emergent neurosurgery are the path.
criticalActions: Protect the airway with RSI for GCS <=8, avoiding hypoxia and hypotension
Maintain oxygenation and blood pressure (treat hypotension aggressively — it worsens outcomes)
Maintain normocapnia; avoid prophylactic hyperventilation (use brief mild hyperventilation only as a herniation bridge)
Elevate the head of bed ~30 degrees and keep the neck midline
Give hyperosmolar therapy (hypertonic saline or mannitol) for signs of herniation
Obtain emergent noncontrast head CT and consult neurosurgery emergently
Reverse anticoagulation, prevent/treat seizures, and manage as polytrauma with C-spine precautions
learningPoints: Preventing secondary injury (avoiding hypoxia and hypotension) is the core of severe TBI care.
Avoid prophylactic hyperventilation; reserve brief mild hyperventilation for acute herniation as a bridge.
Hyperosmolar therapy (hypertonic saline/mannitol) for herniation; emergent CT and neurosurgery.
A single episode of hypoxia or hypotension markedly worsens TBI outcomes.
cases.intro: A 40-year-old man is brought in unconscious after a fall from a roof. He doesn't open his eyes, withdraws minimally, and makes no words — a GCS around 6. His left pupil is dilated and doesn't react. His blood pressure is very high, his heart rate is slow, and his breathing is irregular.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Fall from a roof, unresponsive at the scene, GCS 6. His left pupil blew on the way in and his heart rate's been dropping.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Left pupil's fixed and dilated, pressure 196 over 104, heart rate 52, breathing irregular. He's not protecting his airway.

cases.title: Traumatic hemorrhagic shock
cases.chiefComplaint: 28M — high-speed MVC, hypotension, distended abdomen
cases.diagnosis: Traumatic hemorrhagic shock
cases.context: 28-year-old man after a high-speed motor vehicle collision, hypotensive and tachycardic with a distended, tender abdomen, pale and cool (Class III/IV hemorrhagic shock). True diagnosis is traumatic hemorrhagic shock from intra-abdominal (and/or pelvic/thoracic) bleeding. CORRECT PATH: follow ATLS — control obvious external hemorrhage, secure the airway as needed, identify the source (FAST exam positive for free fluid; check chest, pelvis, long bones — the 'blood on the floor and four more' sources), and resuscitate with BALANCED BLOOD PRODUCT resuscitation (activate MASSIVE TRANSFUSION with a balanced ratio of PRBC:plasma:platelets ~1:1:1) rather than large-volume crystalloid; apply PERMISSIVE HYPOTENSION (limit crystalloid, target a lower SBP while mentating until bleeding is controlled — except in TBI); give TRANEXAMIC ACID early (within 3 hours); prevent/treat the LETHAL TRIAD (hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy) — keep the patient warm, use calcium; bind an unstable PELVIS; and get the patient to DEFINITIVE HEMORRHAGE CONTROL (OR/angioembolization) emergently with SURGERY. RED HERRING: large-volume crystalloid resuscitation (dilutional coagulopathy, worsened bleeding, hypothermia) and delaying definitive control. DETERIORATION: ongoing hemorrhage and the lethal triad → death. Blood-product resuscitation, TXA, permissive hypotension, warming, and rapid surgical control are the path.
criticalActions: Control obvious external hemorrhage and manage the airway per ATLS
Identify the bleeding source (FAST, chest, pelvis, long bones)
Activate massive transfusion with balanced blood products (~1:1:1) rather than large crystalloid volumes
Apply permissive hypotension until hemorrhage is controlled (except in TBI)
Give tranexamic acid early (within 3 hours)
Prevent/treat the lethal triad (keep warm, treat acidosis, give calcium)
Bind an unstable pelvis and expedite definitive control (OR/angioembolization) with surgery
learningPoints: Resuscitate hemorrhagic shock with balanced blood products (~1:1:1), not large-volume crystalloid.
Permissive hypotension until bleeding is controlled (except in traumatic brain injury).
Give tranexamic acid early and prevent the lethal triad (hypothermia, acidosis, coagulopathy).
Definitive hemorrhage control (OR/embolization) is the priority — resuscitate on the way.
cases.intro: A 28-year-old man is brought in after a high-speed head-on collision, pale, cold, and confused. His abdomen is distended and tender, his pelvis feels unstable, and his blood pressure is dangerously low with a thready, fast pulse. The trauma bay springs into action.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: High-speed head-on, prolonged extrication. He's been hypotensive since the scene — belly's distended and his pelvis feels unstable.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Pressure 78 over 48, heart rate 132, he's cold and pale. FAST is being done now.

cases.article: 2016-11_Evidence-Based Management Of Potassium Disorders In The Emergency Department.pdf
cases.title: Severe hypokalemia
cases.chiefComplaint: 44F — weakness, palpitations, vomiting; K 2.1 with U waves
cases.diagnosis: Severe hypokalemia with cardiac irritability
cases.context: 44-year-old woman with prolonged vomiting (and thiazide/laxative use) presents with profound weakness, cramps, and palpitations; ECG shows flattened T waves, U waves, and a prolonged QT, with a potassium of 2.1. True diagnosis is severe HYPOKALEMIA, dangerous because of arrhythmia risk (including torsades) and muscle weakness. CORRECT PATH: obtain an ECG (U waves, flat T waves, QT prolongation, ectopy) and continuous monitoring; REPLACE POTASSIUM (oral for mild; IV for severe/symptomatic/arrhythmia — via a controlled rate with cardiac monitoring, e.g., 10 mEq/hour peripherally, faster only via central access with close monitoring); crucially, CHECK AND REPLACE MAGNESIUM — hypomagnesemia causes REFRACTORY hypokalemia (potassium won't correct until magnesium is repleted); identify and treat the cause (GI losses, diuretics, renal losses, shift); recheck levels. RED HERRING: repleting potassium without repleting magnesium (refractory hypokalemia) or replacing IV potassium too rapidly (dangerous). DETERIORATION: severe hypokalemia → ventricular arrhythmia (torsades), respiratory muscle weakness. Cardiac monitoring, careful potassium AND magnesium repletion, and treating the cause are the path.
criticalActions: Obtain an ECG (U waves, flat T waves, QT prolongation, ectopy) and continuous cardiac monitoring
Replace potassium (oral for mild; controlled-rate IV for severe/symptomatic with cardiac monitoring)
Check and replace magnesium (hypomagnesemia causes refractory hypokalemia)
Avoid overly rapid IV potassium administration
Identify and treat the underlying cause (GI/renal losses, diuretics, transcellular shift)
Recheck potassium and magnesium after repletion
learningPoints: Replace magnesium along with potassium — hypomagnesemia makes hypokalemia refractory.
Severe hypokalemia causes U waves, QT prolongation, and arrhythmia risk (torsades).
Use controlled-rate IV potassium with cardiac monitoring for severe/symptomatic cases.
Identify and treat the cause (GI losses, diuretics, renal losses, shift).
cases.intro: A 44-year-old woman feels weak all over with muscle cramps and a fluttering heartbeat after days of vomiting; she also takes a water pill and laxatives. On the monitor her T waves are flat with prominent U waves and a long QT, and her potassium comes back at 2.1.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've been throwing up for days and now I'm so weak I can barely lift my arms, and my heart keeps fluttering.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Potassium's 2.1 with U waves and a long QT on the monitor. She's on a thiazide and laxatives.

cases.article: 2006-12_Acutely Decompensated Heart Failure Diagnostic and Therapeutic Strategies.pdf
cases.title: The CHF that does not look like CHF (cold, not warm-and-wet)
cases.chiefComplaint: 71M — worsening shortness of breath and leg swelling, now confused and drowsy
cases.diagnosis: Cold-and-wet cardiogenic shock from acutely decompensated ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF ~20%) with cardiorenal syndrome, requiring inotropic support
cases.context: This is Walter Higbee, 71, with known ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF 20% on echo eight months ago, prior anterior MI) who has decompensated into a low-output, hypoperfused ('cold and wet') state rather than the classic hypertensive, vasodilator-responsive flash pulmonary edema most ED clinicians reflexively treat. He is on carvedilol, lisinopril, furosemide, and spironolactone; the beta-blocker blunts the compensatory tachycardia one would expect in shock, so his heart rate looks deceptively unimpressive (90s-100s) despite true hypoperfusion — a classic trap. Three days of progressive dyspnea and orthopnea preceded a two-day decline in oral intake and today's new confusion, both signs of falling cardiac output rather than simple volume overload alone. Exam should show cool, mottled, diaphoretic extremities, delayed capillary refill, narrow pulse pressure, elevated JVP, bibasilar crackles, and 2+ pitting edema — congestion (wet) coexisting with hypoperfusion (cold), the combination the article's opening vignette warns is easy to mismanage with a 'one-size-fits-all' furosemide/nitrate/morphine cocktail. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively exclude: (1) septic shock — but he is afebrile (36.1C), skin is cool not warm, and there is no clear infectious source, arguing against sepsis; (2) COPD exacerbation — he has mild wheeze and a distant smoking history that could distract, but orthopnea, JVD, and bilateral (not just hyperinflated) findings favor cardiac; (3) acute PE — tachypnea and hypoxia could mimic PE, but there is no unilateral leg swelling, pleuritic pain, or risk factor beyond immobility, and the symmetric edema plus JVD point to a cardiac cause; (4) an evolving STEMI as the decompensation's trigger must be ruled out with an early 12-lead ECG (expect old anterior Q waves, no acute ST elevation, but demand-ischemia-pattern T-wave changes given the shock state) — silent/atypical MI is a recognized ADHF precipitant per the article and a can't-miss here. Labs should show BNP markedly elevated (~1800 pg/mL, reinforcing rather than replacing clinical judgment), creatinine rising from a baseline of 1.4 to 2.6 mg/dL (cardiorenal syndrome from poor renal perfusion), mild lactate elevation (~3.2), and a mildly positive troponin from demand ischemia rather than plaque rupture. CXR shows bilateral pulmonary edema. The critical management pitfall: aggressive nitrates, morphine, and high-dose diuresis (appropriate for the warm-and-wet phenotype) will drop preload further in this preload-dependent, hypotensive, low-output patient and can precipitate cardiac arrest — the article's central teaching point that ADHF is heterogeneous and demands phenotype-specific therapy. If mismanaged with vasodilators/morphine, expect worsening hypotension, escalating confusion, oliguria progressing to anuria, and cardiac arrest. If managed correctly — cautious/no preload reduction, inotropic support (dobutamine or milrinone) to augment cardiac output and perfusion, judicious rather than aggressive diuresis once perfusion improves, early cardiology involvement, ICU-level monitoring, and treatment of the underlying precipitant — expect improving mentation, rising blood pressure, warming extremities, and increasing urine output over the following hour.
criticalActions: Recognize the cold-and-wet (low-output) phenotype and avoid reflexive nitrate/morphine/aggressive-diuretic therapy meant for warm-and-wet flash pulmonary edema
Obtain a 12-lead ECG promptly to identify or exclude an acute coronary precipitant
Initiate inotropic support (e.g., dobutamine or milrinone) to augment cardiac output in the hypoperfused, hypotensive patient
Send BNP, troponin, lactate, and renal function to characterize the ADHF phenotype and cardiorenal syndrome
Obtain chest x-ray to confirm pulmonary congestion and help exclude COPD/pneumonia mimics
Arrange ICU-level disposition with continuous hemodynamic monitoring and early cardiology involvement
learningPoints: ADHF is a heterogeneous syndrome, not a single 'wet CHF' cookbook diagnosis — a one-size-fits-all approach can harm patients whose physiology differs from the classic hypertensive flash pulmonary edema case
A low-output, cold-and-wet or cold-and-dry patient can present with a deceptively normal heart rate if on a home beta-blocker, masking the severity of hypoperfusion
Nitrates, morphine, and aggressive diuresis are appropriate for warm-and-wet ADHF but can worsen hypotension and organ hypoperfusion in cold, low-output patients who instead need inotropic support
Worsening renal function in ADHF (cardiorenal syndrome) reflects poor renal perfusion from low cardiac output, not simply volume status, and should prompt reassessment of the hemodynamic profile
Because ADHF can be precipitated by silent or atypical myocardial infarction, a 12-lead ECG should be obtained early even when heart failure seems like the obvious primary diagnosis
cases.intro: Walter is slumped in the stretcher, skin cool and mottled, breathing fast and shallow while his daughter hovers anxiously at the bedside. The triage nurse reports three days of worsening shortness of breath and leg swelling, but today he's suddenly hard to rouse and has barely urinated since yesterday. His pulse doesn't look nearly as fast as you'd expect for how sick he looks, his lungs crackle at both bases, and there's a faint wheeze that makes you wonder about his lungs versus his heart — or something else entirely.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Seventy-one-year-old male, three days of progressive shortness of breath and leg swelling, family says he's gotten confused and much sleepier since this morning. Sat's 89 percent on room air, pressure's soft at 84 over 58.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's been so tired lately, barely eating, and this morning he wasn't making sense when I talked to him. His feet look huge, but he hasn't run a fever or anything.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I'm... just so tired... can't catch my breath... my legs feel so heavy...

cases.article: 2016-08_Acute Rheumatic Fever An Evidence-Based Approach To Diagnosis And Initial Management PDF.pdf
cases.title: The knee that got better, the ankle that got worse (peds joint pain)
cases.chiefComplaint: 9M — migratory joint pain, low-grade fever x4 days, new breathlessness
cases.diagnosis: Acute rheumatic fever (Jones criteria: migratory polyarthritis + carditis with mitral regurgitation and early heart failure) following unrecognized, untreated group A streptococcal pharyngitis
cases.context: A previously healthy 9-year-old boy of Tongan descent whose family recently emigrated from the Pacific Islands presents with four days of intermittent fever and joint pain that keeps changing location, plus new breathlessness. Three weeks ago he had a sore throat and mild fever that resolved on its own without antibiotics or a clinic visit; nobody thought much of it. Two days ago his left knee was hot, swollen, and so painful he refused to walk on it; today that knee has quietly improved on its own, but his right knee is now swollen and tender and his left ankle has joined in, both warm and puffy. He looks tired, breathes fast, and complains that lying flat makes him feel short of breath. He denies trauma, rash, or travel beyond the move. On exam his right knee is warm, swollen, and exquisitely tender with limited range of motion; his left ankle is similarly swollen and painful; there is no single fixed hot joint pointing toward septic arthritis, and the pattern of one joint calming as another flares is the migratory large-joint polyarthritis classic for acute rheumatic fever (ARF), distinct from the additive, persistent, large-and-small-joint pattern of poststreptococcal reactive arthritis (PSRA), which has a shorter 7-10 day latency after GAS pharyngitis versus ARF's typical 10-28 days. His tachycardia, heart rate in the 150s-160s, is out of proportion to his modest fever, and auscultation reveals a new holosystolic murmur at the apex radiating toward the axilla, consistent with mitral regurgitation from rheumatic carditis, the manifestation most likely to be missed and the one carrying real long-term risk. He is mildly tachypneic with subcostal retractions and orthopnea, and his liver edge is palpable several centimeters below the costal margin, early findings of evolving heart failure from carditis rather than an incidental finding. Labs show elevated ESR and CRP and an elevated ASO titer supporting recent GAS exposure, though throat culture and rapid strep antigen are now negative, since GAS is usually cleared by the time ARF symptoms appear. ECG shows sinus tachycardia with a PR interval prolonged for age. Differentials to actively exclude: septic arthritis (single fixed joint, high fever, ill-appearing — argues against this case), transient synovitis (typically afebrile with no antecedent pharyngitis or murmur), juvenile idiopathic arthritis (chronic and non-migratory), viral polyarthralgia, and PSRA (shorter latency, additive rather than migratory, less NSAID-responsive). Starting NSAIDs before the exam and workup are complete can prematurely resolve the arthritis and obscure the diagnostic picture, so cardiac auscultation, ECG, and echocardiogram should happen before or alongside empiric analgesia. Populations of Pacific Islander, Maori, or Aboriginal Australian descent, and children in Utah or Hawaii, carry markedly higher ARF incidence and should lower the threshold to test. Left untreated, this progresses to worsening carditis, heart failure, and permanent valvular damage; the correct approach is NSAID or aspirin therapy for arthritis and carditis, penicillin to eradicate any residual GAS, cardiology consultation with echocardiography, admission for monitoring, and initiation of long-term secondary penicillin prophylaxis to prevent recurrent attacks and progressive rheumatic heart disease.
criticalActions: Keep acute rheumatic fever on the differential for migratory polyarthritis with antecedent pharyngitis, especially in high-risk populations (Pacific Islander, Maori, Aboriginal Australian, Utah/Hawaii)
Obtain evidence of antecedent GAS infection (ASO titer, anti-DNase B, throat culture/rapid antigen) rather than anchoring on a single negative throat swab
Perform a careful cardiac exam and obtain an ECG and echocardiogram to evaluate for carditis before or alongside starting NSAIDs
Apply the Jones criteria (major/minor findings plus evidence of GAS) instead of settling for septic arthritis, transient synovitis, or JIA on pattern recognition alone
Start NSAID/aspirin therapy for arthritis and carditis plus penicillin to eradicate residual GAS
Admit for cardiology consultation and monitoring, and arrange long-term secondary penicillin prophylaxis
learningPoints: Acute rheumatic fever causes migratory large-joint polyarthritis — one joint resolves as the next flares — unlike the additive, persistent pattern of poststreptococcal reactive arthritis
Tachycardia disproportionate to fever plus a new or evolving murmur should raise concern for rheumatic carditis, the manifestation with the greatest long-term risk
Pacific Islander, Maori, and Aboriginal Australian children, and those in Utah or Hawaii, have markedly higher ARF incidence and warrant a lower testing threshold
Diagnosis requires evidence of antecedent GAS infection (ASO or anti-DNase B titers) since throat culture is often negative by the time arthritis or carditis appears

Untreated or undertreated strep pharyngitis can trigger ARF two to three weeks later; missed diagnosis risks progression to valvular damage, underscoring the need for antibiotics plus secondary prophylaxis
cases.intro: Triage note: 9-year-old boy, four days of intermittent fever, brought in because his right knee is now swollen and he can barely bear weight — though his mom says his left knee, which hurt just as badly two days ago, has already gotten better on its own. He's breathing a little fast and says lying flat makes him feel like he can't get enough air. Mom mentions he had a sore throat about three weeks ago that seemed to clear up on its own without ever seeing a doctor.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Vitals just came back — heart rate's up around 155 to 160, and he's satting 94% on room air. Room's ready whenever you are.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He was totally fine for weeks after that sore throat, I promise. It's just his knee — well, now it's his ankle too. I don't understand why it keeps moving around.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My chest feels kind of tight when I lay all the way down, and my ankle hurts way more than my knee did yesterday.

cases.title: Cauda equina syndrome after a warehouse lifting injury (the symptom he won't mention)
cases.chiefComplaint: 39M - low back pain radiating down both legs after a lifting injury
cases.diagnosis: Cauda equina syndrome from a large central L4-L5 disc herniation causing acute urinary retention and saddle anesthesia
cases.context: Marcus Webb is a 39-year-old warehouse worker who presents with 3 days of low back pain that began after lifting a heavy pallet, now radiating into both posterior thighs and calves - a presentation that, at first glance, looks exactly like the classic mechanical/radicular low back pain textbook teaches (young laborer, recent heavy lifting, leg radiation, brief improvement with NSAIDs). The true diagnosis is cauda equina syndrome from a large central L4-L5 disc herniation compressing multiple lumbosacral nerve roots below the conus medullaris. The cauda equina carries the sympathetic and parasympathetic fibers that govern bladder filling and voiding, plus S2-S4 somatic fibers that supply perineal sensation and voluntary control of the anal and urethral sphincters; because the parasympathetic fibers are the smallest and unmyelinated, they are the most vulnerable to compression, so bladder dysfunction (painless urinary retention with overflow incontinence) is often the earliest sign, appearing before weakness the patient would notice. Marcus will not volunteer that he has been unable to fully empty his bladder since this morning, or that he has numbness 'down there' - he is embarrassed and assumes it is unrelated or too personal to mention; the learner must specifically and directly ask about urinary retention/incontinence, bowel incontinence, saddle/perineal numbness, and bilateral leg symptoms, and must perform a rectal exam, to elicit these findings. Competing diagnoses the learner may anchor on: (1) simple mechanical/musculoskeletal lumbar strain from the lifting mechanism, plausible given the benign-appearing vitals and antalgic gait; (2) uncomplicated lumbar radiculopathy/sciatica, plausible given the bilateral positive straight-leg raise and leg pain, but classic sciatica has normal perineal sensation and strength, and this patient does not; (3) simple urinary retention or constipation from pain, dehydration, or an anticholinergic effect - a chart-closing red herring that must be excluded once the neuro exam is positive. On exam: bilateral positive straight-leg raise, symmetric 4/5 hip flexor and ankle dorsiflexion weakness, decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick over the perianal saddle region and posteromedial thighs bilaterally, markedly decreased anal sphincter tone on rectal exam, and a post-void residual bladder scan of 450 mL (abnormal above roughly 100-200 mL). Vitals are normal and afebrile - this is a neurologic and urologic surgical emergency, not a hemodynamic one, and reassuring vitals must not be allowed to falsely reassure the team. If mismanaged (discharged with NSAIDs and outpatient MRI, as would be reasonable for simple radiculopathy), the compression progresses over hours and Marcus will suffer permanent bladder atony, bowel incontinence, saddle anesthesia, and sexual dysfunction - the denervation syndrome of fasciculations and atrophy that follows unrelieved compression. The correct evidence-based approach is emergent STAT MRI of the lumbar spine (overriding the usual guidance that imaging is not needed for acute uncomplicated low back pain, because red flags are present), immediate post-void residual measurement, and emergent neurosurgical or spine surgery consultation for decompressive laminectomy/discectomy - decompression within roughly 24-48 hours of symptom onset is associated with meaningfully better neurologic, bladder, and bowel recovery than delayed surgery.
criticalActions: Directly ask about urinary retention/incontinence and saddle or perineal numbness rather than waiting for the patient to volunteer them
Perform a rectal exam to assess anal sphincter tone and perianal sensation
Obtain a post-void residual bladder volume
Order emergent/STAT MRI of the lumbar spine rather than routine outpatient imaging
Obtain emergent neurosurgical or spine surgery consultation for decompression
Do not discharge with a diagnosis of simple radiculopathy or mechanical strain despite reassuring vital signs
learningPoints: Cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency where the time-critical threat is neurologic and urinary/bowel disability, not hemodynamic instability - vital signs are often completely normal.

The cauda equina carries sympathetic and parasympathetic fibers controlling the bladder plus S2-S4 somatic fibers for perineal sensation and sphincter control; because the parasympathetic fibers are the smallest and unmyelinated, urinary retention is often the earliest sign of compression.

Patients rarely volunteer bladder, bowel, or saddle-numbness symptoms out of embarrassment - clinicians must specifically ask about them and perform a rectal exam in any back pain patient with bilateral leg symptoms.
A benign-appearing radicular back pain presentation, such as a positive straight-leg raise after a lifting injury, can look identical to cauda equina syndrome until red flags are actively sought.
Delayed decompression risks permanent denervation with fasciculations and atrophy, while urgent MRI and emergent surgical decompression improve the odds of neurologic and bladder recovery.
cases.intro: Marcus Webb is a 39-year-old warehouse worker triaged as a straightforward lift-and-twist back injury - the nurse hands you a chart that reads like a dozen others you've seen this shift. He's grimacing on the stretcher, shifting to find a comfortable position, and says the pain has been building since he threw out his back hauling pallets three days ago. His vitals are unremarkable, he's alert and cooperative, and he mostly just wants something for the pain and a note to go back to light duty.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Room 4 is a back pain, sounds like a lifting injury at work, vitals are all normal, he's just uncomfortable.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's my back, doc. I lifted some heavy pallets a few days ago and now the pain shoots down both my legs, it's honestly the worst it's ever been.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I've thrown my back out before and it always worked itself out with some ibuprofen and rest, I just need something stronger for the pain today.

cases.article: 2016-06_Emergency Department Evaluation And Management Of Blunt Chest And Lung Trauma PDF.pdf
cases.title: Flail chest after the steering wheel hit (the contusion that gets worse tomorrow)
cases.chiefComplaint: 54M, chest wall pain and shortness of breath after high-speed MVC, unrestrained driver with steering wheel deformity
cases.diagnosis: Flail chest (right anterolateral, ribs 4-8) with underlying moderate-to-severe right pulmonary contusion
cases.context: Gary Whitfield, 54, was the unrestrained driver in a high-speed (~55 mph) motor vehicle collision when his sedan struck a guardrail head-on; EMS found the steering column crushed and deformed against his chest. The true diagnosis is flail chest (three or more contiguous rib fractures, right anterolateral, ribs 4-8, producing a free-floating segment with paradoxical inward movement on inspiration) with an underlying moderate-to-severe pulmonary contusion of the right lung, classic sequelae of direct steering-wheel impact and rapid deceleration in an unbelted driver, matching the mechanism this EBMedicine review identifies as the dominant cause (70-80%) of blunt chest trauma. Pathophysiologically, the flail segment causes mechanical splinting and hypoventilation while the underlying contused lung parenchyma progressively hemorrhages and edematizes over the first 24-48 hours, worsening shunt physiology and hypoxia independent of the chest wall injury itself. On arrival he is tachypneic, tachycardic, and hypoxic (RR 32, HR 118, SpO2 89% RA, BP 108/68), splinting his right chest, with visible bruising over the right anterolateral ribs and sternum and a segment that appears to move paradoxically with respiration, though this can be subtle early before fatigue sets in - examiners must watch a full respiratory cycle, not glance and move on. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively exclude: (1) simple/isolated rib fractures with purely musculoskeletal pain (up to two-thirds of blunt chest trauma patients have rib fractures, but this patient's degree of hypoxia and paradoxical movement are out of proportion to simple fractures); (2) acute anxiety/hyperventilation from the crash (tachypnea alone could be mistaken for panic, but true hypoxemia on pulse oximetry excludes this); (3) tension pneumothorax (also causes tachycardia, hypoxia, and respiratory distress, but this patient has bilaterally equal breath sounds, no tracheal deviation, and no jugular venous distension - findings that should redirect away from needle decompression). The can't-miss trap this article stresses: up to 50% of rib fractures are radiographically occult on plain chest x-ray, so an initial film read as only a couple of rib fractures or deceptively unremarkable does NOT exclude flail chest or a large contusion - CT chest is required to fully characterize the fracture pattern and contusion burden. Sternal tenderness is also present, and because sternal fractures carry a 20-40% association with blunt cardiac injury, ECG and troponin screening are indicated. If mismanaged (opioid-sparing due to fear of respiratory depression, aggressive crystalloid resuscitation, or reliance on a reassuring initial CXR), expect progressive splinting, worsening contusion-related shunt, rising RR, falling SpO2 into the 80s, and eventual respiratory failure requiring intubation over the following hours. Correct management - aggressive multimodal analgesia (regional nerve block/epidural preferred over systemic opioids alone) to preserve tidal volume and cough, judicious fluid administration, chest CT, ICU-level monitoring with serial oxygenation checks, and early escalation to noninvasive or invasive ventilation if trending down - prevents this deterioration.
criticalActions: Diagnose flail chest clinically by observing a full respiratory cycle for paradoxical chest wall movement, not by chest x-ray alone
Obtain chest CT to fully characterize the rib fracture pattern and extent of pulmonary contusion, since up to 50% of rib fractures are missed on plain film
Provide aggressive multimodal analgesia (regional nerve block/epidural or scheduled multimodal IV agents) rather than minimal opioids, to preserve tidal volume and prevent splinting
Avoid aggressive crystalloid resuscitation that can worsen the underlying pulmonary contusion
Obtain ECG and troponin to screen for concomitant blunt cardiac injury given sternal wall trauma
Admit to ICU/trauma service with serial oxygenation monitoring and a low threshold for noninvasive or invasive ventilatory support as the contusion evolves
learningPoints: Flail chest is defined as 3 or more contiguous rib fractures causing a free-floating segment with paradoxical movement; mortality is as high as 16%
Up to 50% of rib fractures are radiographically occult on initial plain chest x-ray, so a reassuring film does not exclude flail chest or significant contusion
Pulmonary contusion accompanies 30-50% of severe blunt chest trauma and classically worsens over 24-48 hours, producing delayed hypoxia even when initial imaging looks mild
Pain control is central to management: poor analgesia causes splinting, atelectasis, and progression to respiratory failure
Sternal fractures are associated with cardiac contusion (blunt cardiac injury) in 20-40% of cases and warrant ECG screening
cases.intro: EMS wheels in a disheveled construction supervisor pulled from a sedan that struck a guardrail at highway speed - the steering column bent nearly in half against his chest. He's talking in short, clipped sentences, wincing with every breath, one hand pressed hard against his ribs. The monitor shows him tachycardic and breathing fast and shallow, and the triage nurse can't yet tell if it's pain, panic, or something worse.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Unrestrained driver, thrown into the wheel on impact. Steering column's crushed. He's been getting more short of breath the whole ride in.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It hurts so bad right here every time I breathe in. I feel like I can't get enough air.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Doctor, look at his right chest wall when he breathes - it looks like it's caving in instead of going out.

cases.title: Recurrent blue spells that only happen with Mom (the fabricated-apnea one)
cases.chiefComplaint: 14mo M — third episode this month of witnessed cyanosis and limpness, now alert and playful
cases.diagnosis: Medical child abuse (pediatric condition falsification / Munchausen syndrome by proxy) — caregiver-induced suffocation producing recurrent apparent life-threatening events (ALTEs)
cases.context: Eli is a 14-month-old boy brought in by his mother, a certified nursing assistant, for his third 'blue and floppy' cyanotic episode this month. The true diagnosis is medical child abuse (pediatric condition falsification, historically termed Munchausen syndrome by proxy): the mother is intermittently and covertly suffocating Eli with a blanket or her hand when alone with him, producing transient hypoxia and cyanosis that mimic an apparent life-threatening event, and has also caused a small nosebleed by pinching or picking at his nares to fabricate additional 'proof' something is wrong. Every prior and current episode has occurred only when Eli was alone with his mother — never witnessed by staff, other family, or camera — and each resolves within seconds once he is picked up, stimulated, or a stranger enters the room. On arrival Eli is alert, pink, playful, and interactive; SpO2 is 100% on room air and the exam is entirely normal aside from a thin crust of dried blood at the right naris and no bruising elsewhere. Per the (embellished) history, prior workups including CBC, CMP, chest x-ray, blood/urine/CSF cultures, and a 5-channel pneumogram were all normal or unrevealing except for a mild microcytic anemia (Hgb 10.2). The mother is composed, articulate, and fluent in medical terminology; she has stayed at the bedside continuously, resists letting staff examine or observe Eli without her present, and becomes conspicuously harder to reach by phone whenever discharge is being arranged. Staff who have cared for the family describe her as devoted, pleasant, and remarkably calm during 'emergencies' that alarm everyone else in the room. She mentions, almost in passing, that Eli's older sibling died unexpectedly in infancy of 'unexplained SIDS,' and that the father is rarely involved and unaware of most visits. Competing organic diagnoses the team may reasonably chase early on include gastroesophageal reflux with aspiration, a seizure disorder, an arrhythmia such as prolonged QT syndrome, occult sepsis, or central/obstructive apnea — but none explain why events are witnessed only by the mother, why the desaturation is an abrupt drop that reverses within moments of stimulation rather than a true physiologic apnea pattern, or why repeated invasive testing keeps returning normal despite a story of severe, recurrent, life-threatening spells. If the team continues down the organic pathway, Eli will be re-admitted for another septic workup, empiric antibiotics, and monitoring, then discharged home with his mother again, restarting the cycle and risking a fatal event. The correct approach is to recognize the pattern: obtain history and part of the exam with the caregiver separated from the child, request records from any other EDs or hospitals the family has used, correlate reported spells against continuous cardiorespiratory monitor tracings and direct nursing observation, involve social work and the hospital's child abuse pediatrics/child protection team, and file a mandated report to Child Protective Services rather than clearing the family for discharge.
criticalActions: Obtain history and perform part of the exam with the caregiver separated from the child
Request and review records from other EDs/hospitals the family has used for a pattern of recurrent, doctor-shopping visits
Correlate reported spells with continuous cardiorespiratory monitor data and direct nursing observation rather than caregiver report alone
Recognize that findings physiologically inconsistent with the reported severity argue against further invasive testing, not for it
Consult social work and the child abuse pediatrics/child protection team and file a mandated report to Child Protective Services
Do not discharge the child back to unsupervised caregiver custody until a safety plan with CPS is in place
learningPoints: Medical child abuse should enter the differential when a caregiver's reported symptoms are out of proportion to exam findings, follow an atypical course, or fail to respond to standard treatment.
Perpetrators are the biological mother in about 90% of cases, often with a medical background, and are frequently described by staff as devoted, calm, and likeable even during dramatic 'emergencies.'
A hallmark clue is that events are witnessed only when the caregiver is alone with the child and resolve rapidly once the child is separated, stimulated, or observed by others.
Laboratory or clinical findings that are physiologically implausible or mismatched with the history should prompt suspicion of fabrication rather than escalation of workup.
Emergency clinicians are mandated reporters; suspected medical child abuse requires involving social work and child protection teams and reporting to CPS, not just ruling out organic disease.
cases.intro: Eli, a 14-month-old boy, arrives cradled in his mother's arms after she reports finding him 'blue and floppy' in his crib for the third time this month. He is alert and pink now, babbling and reaching for the otoscope light, while his mother recounts the episode in careful, almost rehearsed detail. Triage shows an oxygen saturation of 98% and a reassuring exam, but she is adamant it happened 'exactly like the last two times' and wants him admitted again for monitoring.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He just went completely limp and turned blue right there in his crib. By the time I got him to the hallway he'd already come back around. This is the third time this month and nobody can tell me what's wrong with him.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: His vitals look great right now and he's acting totally normal. Mom mentioned it always happens when it's just the two of them, and she really doesn't want to step away from his bedside.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: I know how this looks, but I've worked as a CNA for years — I know a real emergency when I see one, and I am not leaving his side until someone figures out what's causing this.

cases.article: 2009-04_Cervical Spine Injury An Evidence-Based Evaluation Of The Patient With Blunt Cervical Trauma.pdf
cases.title: Unstable C-spine fracture in the drunk driver with 'no complaints'
cases.chiefComplaint: 40M s/p MVC (car into house), intoxicated & combative, denies neck pain
cases.diagnosis: Unstable C5-C6 bilateral facet dislocation with combined disruption of the anterior column (vertebral body/disk, anterior and posterior longitudinal ligaments) and posterior column (facet capsules, ligamentum flavum) from a high-energy flexion-distraction frontal MVC, clinically masked by acute alcohol intoxication
cases.context: This is a 40-year-old man, legally intoxicated, who drove his sedan into the side of a house at an estimated 35-40 mph (steering wheel cracked on scene). EMS found him combative, refusing evaluation, and he remains that way in your bay: thrashing against a rigid collar and backboard, shouting that he just needs to urinate and wants to leave AMA. True diagnosis: an unstable C5-C6 bilateral facet dislocation, a flexion-distraction injury that has torn through BOTH the anterior column (disk, anterior/posterior longitudinal ligaments) and the posterior column (facet capsules, ligamentum flavum, nuchal ligament complex). Per the two-column concept, when both columns fail the segment moves as two independent pieces on any further neck motion, placing the cord at imminent risk of injury it has so far escaped. He currently localizes to pain in all four limbs and EMS logged no gross deficit, but this exam is unreliable: he is dysarthric, tangential, and will not cooperate with a real strength/sensory exam, and his only stated complaint is urinary urgency (relative retention from alcohol and a distended bladder) — not his neck. Competing explanations the team will be tempted to anchor on: (1) simple alcohol intoxication/agitation as the whole story, (2) acute urinary retention as the sole source of his distress, (3) a minor cervical strain/whiplash given 'no complaints,' and (4) a mild concomitant head injury (small forehead laceration, cracked windshield) drawing attention away from the neck. The can't-miss trap, straight from this article's own teaching case, is that clinical clearance rules require a reliable patient — alert, sober, no distracting injury, no midline tenderness, no focal deficit — and he fails on intoxication and distraction alone, so the absence of a voiced neck complaint is meaningless; imaging must be driven by mechanism, not by what he says. A second trap: the literature on prehospital immobilization is weak (no RCT proves it prevents secondary injury, and healthy-volunteer trials show collars/boards barely limit head motion anyway), which the team may misuse to justify letting him yank the collar off to 'calm him down' — but rare, catastrophic cord injuries have occurred in exactly this circumstance, immobilization removed and the neck allowed to move, and this patient is not yet clinically or radiographically cleared. Deterioration trajectory if mismanaged: he fights free of the collar, flexes/rotates his neck against staff, the dislocated facets displace further, and he acutely develops bilateral upper-extremity weakness and paresthesias progressing to quadriparesis, loss of rectal tone, priapism, and neurogenic shock (bradycardia with hypotension); if the level ascends he develops respiratory compromise. Correct pathway: maintain rigid immobilization, use pharmacologic sedation rather than removing precautions to manage combativeness, obtain CT of the cervical spine given the high-risk mechanism and unreliable exam, obtain early spine/neurosurgical consultation, and reassess neurologically once he sobers enough for a valid exam, discontinuing precautions only after clearance or definitive fixation.
criticalActions: Maintain rigid cervical collar and spinal precautions despite the patient's agitation and demands to remove them
Recognize that alcohol intoxication invalidates clinical clearance criteria — a 'no complaints' history is not reassuring in an altered patient
Obtain CT of the cervical spine based on the high-risk mechanism, not on the patient's stated complaints
Use pharmacologic sedation/chemical restraint for the combative patient rather than discontinuing immobilization to placate him
Obtain early spine/neurosurgical consultation for an unstable two-column injury
Reassess neurologic status serially as intoxication clears before ever discontinuing precautions
learningPoints: Altered mental status from intoxication invalidates clinical c-spine clearance regardless of whether the patient voices neck pain

The cervical spine has an anterior column (body, disks, longitudinal ligaments) and a posterior column (facets, laminae, ligamentum flavum); injury to only one column is usually stable, but disruption of both lets the spine move as two separate pieces and sharply raises spinal cord injury risk
A distracting complaint (here, urinary urgency) or intoxication can mask significant neck pain and produce a falsely reassuring exam

Evidence for prehospital spinal immobilization preventing secondary injury is weak, but rare catastrophic cord injuries have followed premature removal of immobilization in patients who were not yet clinically cleared
Spinal cord injury and cervical fracture do not always coexist — imaging and immobilization decisions must be driven by mechanism and exam reliability, not by the presence of a specific complaint
cases.intro: It's 2:30 AM and the bay reeks of beer and radiator fluid. EMS wheels back a thrashing 40-year-old man, strapped to a backboard in a cervical collar, who drove his car into the side of a house; he's shouting that he's fine, he just needs to use the bathroom, and he wants this collar off so he can sign out AMA. A shallow laceration beads over one eyebrow, his speech is slurred, and he keeps yanking at his own IV line while the nurse mutters that she doesn't understand why anyone's ordering neck films on a guy with no complaints.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's pulled his IV twice already, doctor. Says there's nothing wrong with him, he just has to pee and he wants to leave. Vitals are up but he won't sit still long enough for me to reassess him.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Car versus house, maybe thirty-five, forty miles an hour, steering wheel's cracked. He was swinging at us on scene too, wouldn't let us get a good look at him before we collared and boarded him.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Get this thing off my neck, I told you, I'm fine. I just gotta piss and then I'm going home, I don't need any of this.

cases.title: Epidural hematoma in a skateboarder who 'feels totally fine' (the lucid interval trap)
cases.chiefComplaint: 22M head strike, brief LOC, now alert and ambulatory with mild headache
cases.diagnosis: Acute right temporoparietal epidural (extradural) hematoma from a middle meningeal artery injury underlying a temporal bone fracture, currently in the classic lucid interval before herniation
cases.context: Ethan is a 22-year-old male with an acute epidural (extradural) hematoma sustained roughly 45-60 minutes before ED arrival when he fell off his skateboard and struck the right temporoparietal skull region on a curb. True pathophysiology: a linear fracture through the right squamous temporal bone lacerated the underlying middle meningeal artery, producing a high-pressure arterial bleed stripping dura from bone into a biconvex, lens-shaped extra-axial hematoma that does not cross suture lines. Per the Monro-Kellie hypothesis (fixed skull volume of blood, brain, and CSF), this rapidly expanding arterial clot is an 'excess' volume that will progressively raise intracranial pressure and lower cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP = MAP minus ICP, normally kept above 60 mmHg) until compensatory displacement of CSF and venous blood is exhausted, at which point deterioration becomes abrupt rather than gradual. THE TRAP: witnesses describe a brief loss of consciousness (about 20-30 seconds) at the scene, but Ethan 'woke up talking' and has since been conversant, joking, and ambulatory — the classic deceptive lucid interval of epidural hematoma, during which the patient looks and feels reassuringly normal while blood is already accumulating beneath an intact-appearing exam. Competing impressions the team may reach for: (1) 'just a scalp laceration/hematoma, GCS 15, can go home with head injury precautions'; (2) attributing any later drowsiness to the two beers he had earlier rather than to intracranial pathology; (3) false reassurance from a single normal neuro check with no plan to reassess. The can't-miss teaching point: roughly half of patients with GCS 14 or less after TBI harbor a traumatic ICH, so a normal initial GCS of 15 does not exclude a surgical bleed — his mechanism (temporal impact with brief LOC), one scene vomiting episode, and a boggy temporal scalp hematoma are indications for an urgent non-contrast head CT even though he 'looks fine.' EXPECTED DETERIORATION IF CT/REASSESSMENT IS DELAYED: by T+60-90 minutes post-injury, headache worsens from 3/10 to 7/10, a second vomiting episode occurs, and GCS softens to 14 (E4V4M6) with slowed responses; by T+90-120 minutes, GCS falls to 11-12 (E3V4M5) with increasing somnolence, new right pupillary anisocoria (6mm sluggish vs left 3mm reactive, from ipsilateral CN III compression by uncal herniation), and emerging left-sided weakness (contralateral peduncle compression), while BP creeps to about 148/85 and HR drifts toward 58. By T+120-150 minutes, if still unrecognized, GCS drops below 8, the right pupil becomes fixed and fully dilated (9mm), and Cushing's triad fully manifests — hypertension 170-190/100+, bradycardia 40-50, and irregular/agonal respirations — signaling impending brainstem herniation and death without emergent decompression. If CT is obtained during the lucid window, it shows a classic biconvex right temporoparietal hyperdensity with an associated temporal skull fracture and only mild mass effect; emergent neurosurgical consultation and burr hole/craniotomy evacuation before herniation carries an excellent prognosis, since epidural hematomas typically cause little primary brain injury — the danger is purely the expanding extrinsic mass.
criticalActions: Obtain a non-contrast head CT despite a reassuring GCS 15 exam, based on mechanism, brief LOC, and vomiting risk factors
Perform serial neurologic reassessments (GCS and pupils) at frequent intervals rather than discharging after a single normal exam
Recognize the lucid interval and do not let interval clinical improvement falsely reassure against an evolving epidural hematoma
Emergently consult neurosurgery and arrange surgical evacuation as soon as an epidural hematoma is identified, without waiting for further deterioration
Treat early signs of rising ICP (head-of-bed elevation, hyperosmolar therapy) while awaiting the OR if anisocoria or GCS decline appears
Avoid corticosteroids, which have no proven benefit in traumatic intracranial hemorrhage
learningPoints: A lucid interval — transient normal or near-normal neurologic status after head trauma — is a classic and dangerous feature of epidural hematoma and must never be mistaken for a benign injury
Roughly half of patients with a GCS of 14 or less after head trauma have a traumatic intracranial hemorrhage, so a reassuring initial exam does not exclude a surgical bleed
An expanding epidural hematoma raises intracranial pressure per the Monro-Kellie hypothesis, and once compensatory mechanisms are exhausted, deterioration to herniation can be abrupt and rapid
A unilaterally blown pupil and Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) are late signs of uncal herniation, not early warnings — do not wait for them before acting
Because epidural hematomas cause mass effect with relatively little underlying primary brain injury, prompt neurosurgical evacuation before herniation offers an excellent prognosis
cases.intro: Ethan, 22, sits upright on the stretcher trading jokes with his roommate, a small dressing taped over a graze on his right temple. He says he 'blacked out for like a few seconds' when his skateboard slid out from under him, but insists he feels basically fine now, just a dull headache. His vitals on the monitor look unremarkable, and the triage nurse has already started wondering aloud whether he can just be sent home.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: GCS is 15, vitals are stable, he's ambulatory and oriented, this looks like a pretty minor bump.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Honestly I feel fine, my head just hurts a little. Can I get some ibuprofen and go? I've got a shift tonight.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He was out for like ten seconds tops, then he got right back up talking normal. I really don't think it's a big deal.

cases.article: 2016-02_The HIV-Infected Adult Patient In The Emergency Department, The Changing Landscape Of Disease.pdf
cases.title: The winded warehouse worker (the one nobody tested)
cases.chiefComplaint: 38M, 3 weeks progressive shortness of breath and dry cough
cases.diagnosis: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP) as the AIDS-defining presentation of previously undiagnosed HIV infection
cases.context: Marcus, 38, has undiagnosed HIV acquired an estimated 8-10 years ago through unprotected sex with men, a history he has never disclosed to any clinician out of fear of stigma and because he has avoided primary care and never been tested. He now has an AIDS-defining CD4 count of 94 cells/mm3 (revealed only if a CD4 panel is sent after HIV diagnosis) and presents with Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP): the classic opportunistic infection of undiagnosed or poorly-engaged HIV, per the article's point that 'OIs are still seen in patients with undiagnosed infection presenting with advanced disease.' The pathophysiology is subacute alveolar inflammation from Pneumocystis overwhelming a CD4-depleted immune system, producing hypoxia that is dramatically out of proportion to an often near-normal lung exam and initially subtle chest imaging (bilateral diffuse interstitial/ground-glass infiltrates, not lobar consolidation). He has had 3 weeks of progressive dyspnea on exertion, dry nonproductive cough, low-grade fevers, drenching night sweats, and 15 lb unintentional weight loss; exam shows oral thrush-like white plaques on the tongue (a key exam clue toward immunosuppression). Competing diagnoses the learner must actively work through: (1) community-acquired/atypical bacterial pneumonia — treated empirically, standard short-course antibiotics will fail because they do not cover Pneumocystis, and the patient will deteriorate over 24-48h if this anchor is not questioned; (2) active pulmonary tuberculosis — must be ruled out with respiratory isolation and AFB smears given the subacute course, night sweats, and weight loss, and shares risk-factor overlap with HIV; (3) anxiety/panic attack or deconditioning — tempting given his young age and vague-sounding dyspnea, but his resting hypoxia (SpO2 87% RA) makes this a dangerous miss. Labs: LDH markedly elevated (>400), A-a gradient widened, ABG showing hypoxemia with hypocapnia from tachypnea. If mismanaged (discharged with reassurance, or treated for CAP alone), he returns in respiratory failure requiring intubation within days. The correct evidence-based approach, consistent with the article's emphasis on maintaining a high index of suspicion for HIV in patients with typical OI features and risk factors even absent disclosed history: obtain CXR and ABG, recognize hypoxia out of proportion to exam, send a fourth-generation HIV Ag/Ab combination test (detects infection earlier than antibody-only assays), start empiric TMP-SMX, add adjunctive corticosteroids if PaO2 <70 mmHg or A-a gradient >35, isolate for TB pending AFB results, and obtain emergent ID consultation and CD4/viral load once HIV is confirmed. This links him to HAART, which the article notes has made life expectancy on treatment comparable to the general population.
criticalActions: Recognize hypoxia (SpO2 87% RA) markedly out of proportion to a benign lung exam as a red flag for PJP, not anxiety or a minor viral illness
Obtain CXR and ABG/A-a gradient rather than treating empirically for typical CAP alone
Send a fourth-generation HIV Ag/Ab combination test given the undiagnosed-risk-factor pattern, even without the patient volunteering history
Initiate respiratory isolation and send AFB smears to actively rule out active tuberculosis before/while treating
Start empiric TMP-SMX and add adjunctive corticosteroids if PaO2 <70 mmHg or A-a gradient >35
Obtain emergent ID consultation and CD4 count/viral load once HIV is confirmed, and arrange linkage to HAART
learningPoints: Opportunistic infections like PJP still occur in patients with undiagnosed HIV or barriers to care, not just in known noncompliant patients
The average time from HIV acquisition to an AIDS-defining CD4 count under 200 cells/mm3 is 8-10 years, often with a clinically silent chronic phase
Emergency clinicians must maintain a high index of suspicion for HIV in patients presenting with typical opportunistic-infection features and risk factors, even when status is undisclosed or unknown
Fourth-generation Ag/Ab combination testing is the ED standard for HIV detection and identifies infection earlier than antibody-only assays
Untreated chronic HIV infection drives a chronic inflammatory state; timely diagnosis and HAART initiation reverses much of that risk
cases.intro: Marcus is triaged for '3 weeks of feeling winded and a cough that won't quit,' initially chalked up to a lingering cold. He's sitting upright on the stretcher, breathing fast but not obviously distressed, and insists he's 'basically fine, just needs an inhaler or something.' The nurse notes he looks thinner than his driver's license photo and flags that his oxygen saturation won't come up above the high 80s no matter how he's positioned. He denies chest pain, denies recent travel, and seems mildly embarrassed when asked routine questions about his health history.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Sats are eighty-seven on room air, heart rate's up around one-eighteen, and he's breathing twenty-eight. Lungs are surprisingly clear when I listened, honestly didn't expect the number to be that low.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I just figured it was a bad cold that wouldn't go away. I've been coughing for like three weeks, sweating through my sheets at night, and I've lost some weight without trying. I probably should've come in sooner but I don't really have a regular doctor.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He says walking from the parking lot left him more short of breath than it should have. He's a little cagey about his personal history but he's willing to answer if you ask him directly.

cases.title: Syncope and 'ACS' in a cancer survivor (the right-heart-strain one)
