cases.chiefComplaint: 52M syncope, chest pain, and shortness of breath
cases.diagnosis: Submassive (intermediate-risk) pulmonary embolism with right ventricular strain, initially mistaken for acute coronary syndrome
cases.context: Marcus is a 52-year-old man eight months out from surgical resection of colon cancer, six months post-chemotherapy, who eleven months ago had a right lower-extremity DVT treated with a completed course of anticoagulation that was stopped once imaging showed resolution. His malignancy history, recent major abdominal surgery, chemotherapy-related hypercoagulability, and now-discontinued anticoagulation together make him high pretest probability for recurrent venous thromboembolism. For 3-4 days he has felt generally weak with mild exertional dyspnea; today, climbing stairs at home, he had a brief syncopal episode followed by midsternal chest pain and shortness of breath, prompting EMS transport. This is a submassive PE: clot burden has acutely raised right ventricular afterload, producing RV wall stress, dilation, and early dysfunction, but the RV is still compensating, so systemic blood pressure remains preserved (SBP 100s) rather than collapsing into obstructive shock — the key distinction from massive/high-risk PE. His ECG shows sinus tachycardia, rightward axis deviation, diffuse ST depressions, and deep T-wave inversions across the anterior/anterolateral precordial leads — a right-heart-strain pattern that is easy to misread as ischemia; the natural anchor is 'ACS,' especially with his chest pain and diaphoresis, and a mildly positive first troponin (0.08 ng/mL, trending to 0.14) will tempt the team to keep chasing an MI workup while the real driver — RV strain from clot burden, not coronary occlusion — goes unaddressed. Other plausible-but-wrong paths include community-acquired pneumonia (tachypneic, hypoxic) and an anxiety/panic attack (he minimizes symptoms after a hard year), both of which must be actively excluded rather than defaulted to. Labs: mild thrombocytopenia (128), markedly elevated D-dimer, elevated BNP (410 pg/mL), and the mildly positive troponin above — both cardiac biomarkers positive, consistent with RV strain/submassive classification rather than low-risk PE. Bedside focused cardiac ultrasound shows a dilated, hypokinetic right ventricle with McConnell's sign and a flattened septum; CT pulmonary angiography shows bilateral filling defects with an RV:LV ratio >1. The core teaching point: this patient does not meet criteria for massive/high-risk PE (no hypotension or shock), so guideline-concordant management is therapeutic anticoagulation with close monitoring, reserving systemic thrombolysis for hemodynamic deterioration — a genuinely controversial area, since thrombolysis lowers deterioration risk but carries major bleeding risk including intracranial hemorrhage. If unrecognized or anticoagulation is delayed while the team chases ACS, expect progressive tachycardia (HR climbing to 130s), falling BP (into the 80s), worsening hypoxia, and eventual obstructive shock as the RV decompensates. If recognized and anticoagulated promptly, with thrombolysis held in reserve and reassessed only if he deteriorates, HR and BP should stabilize over the next 30-60 minutes of the encounter.
criticalActions: Recognize the ECG right-heart-strain pattern as a mimic of ACS rather than anchoring on ischemia
Risk-stratify severity using vitals, cardiac troponin, and focused bedside cardiac ultrasonography for RV strain
Obtain CT pulmonary angiography to confirm PE and quantify RV:LV ratio once suspicion is high
Start therapeutic anticoagulation promptly rather than delaying it to complete an ACS workup
Classify as submassive/intermediate-risk (normotensive with RV strain) and weigh anticoagulation alone versus thrombolysis rather than defaulting to either
Reassess vitals frequently and escalate to thrombolysis or catheter-directed therapy if hemodynamic deterioration occurs
learningPoints: The classic PE triad of pleuritic pain, dyspnea, and hemoptysis is rarely all present; hemoptysis occurs in under 3% of PE patients
Right ventricular outflow obstruction from PE raises RV afterload, causing wall stress, dilation, and dysfunction that can progress to hypotension and hemodynamic collapse if untreated
A normal or only mildly elevated troponin and a preserved blood pressure do not exclude submassive PE with RV strain
Severity assessment with clinical findings, cardiac troponin, and focused cardiac ultrasonography guides the anticoagulation-versus-thrombolysis decision in intermediate-risk PE
Recent malignancy, major surgery, and a prior VTE history should raise pretest probability for PE even when the presentation mimics ACS
cases.intro: Marcus, 52, is sitting bolt upright on the gurney, pale, diaphoretic, and pressing a fist to his sternum. His wife tells the triage nurse he briefly passed out on the stairs at home, then came around clutching his chest and gasping for air. Records show he finished treatment for colon cancer eight months ago and had a leg clot last year that was treated and resolved; he shrugs this off and says he's just been 'wiped out and achy' for the past few days. The monitor is running fast, and it isn't obvious yet whether this is his heart, his lungs, or nerves finally catching up with him after a hard year.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's tachycardic in the 100s, pressure's holding around 106 over 70, and his sat dropped to 93 percent on room air when we walked him back from triage.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's like an elephant sitting on my chest, and I got so dizzy on the stairs I just went out for a second. I figured it was stress, but this feels different.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He finished his cancer treatment back in the winter and had that clot in his leg last year, but the doctor said it was all cleared up. He's just been dragging himself around all week saying he's tired.

cases.title: Just dizzy? (the posterior circulation stroke mistaken for vertigo)
cases.chiefComplaint: 67M - dizziness, vomiting, and unsteady gait x4h
cases.diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke of the posterior (vertebrobasilar) circulation - atherosclerotic thrombosis of the dominant right vertebral artery causing a right PICA-territory cerebellar infarct with early basilar artery hypoperfusion
cases.context: Raymond, 67, developed abrupt vertigo, nausea, and vomiting around 0400 while turning over in bed, roughly 4 hours before ED arrival - squarely within the extended IV rt-PA window this issue discusses. The true diagnosis is an acute ischemic stroke in the posterior circulation: atherosclerotic thrombosis at the origin of his dominant right vertebral artery has produced a right PICA-territory cerebellar infarct, and CTA shows sluggish, reduced flow into the basilar artery, raising concern for propagation to frank basilar occlusion. His risk factors are textbook for large-artery posterior circulation disease: 15 years of poorly controlled hypertension (home readings often over 160/95), type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.9%), hyperlipidemia, and a 40-pack-year smoking history; he has no atrial fibrillation. This case is built directly around this article's own risk-management pitfall: 'vertigo can be very difficult to differentiate in its etiology; when in doubt, consider posterior circulation ischemia, especially when additional symptoms (double vision, coordination problems, difficulty walking) are present.' Raymond has exactly those associated findings on exam - intermittent binocular diplopia on right gaze, mildly slurred ('thick') speech, right-sided dysmetria on finger-to-nose testing, and a wide-based ataxic gait that pulls right - findings a rushed exam anchored on 'inner ear vertigo' will miss. The tempting distractors are benign peripheral vertigo/BPPV (no clear positional trigger; symptoms are constant, not episodic with head turns), vestibular neuritis (no preceding viral illness, and neuritis does not cause diplopia or limb ataxia), and migraine with aura (his wife raises this because he has an old migraine history, but there is no headache today and the exam findings are new). His initial noncontrast CT head is read as grossly unremarkable by the covering resident - posterior fossa infarcts are notoriously easy to miss on early plain CT - and only directed CT angiography reveals the vertebral artery cutoff and basilar hypoperfusion. If anchored as benign vertigo and discharged, the expected trajectory is progressive cerebellar edema causing fourth-ventricle compression and obstructive hydrocephalus, or frank basilar occlusion causing brainstem infarction, quadriparesis, cranial nerve palsies, respiratory failure, and locked-in syndrome over the following hours. The correct pathway mirrors this article's core guidance for any acute ischemic stroke: pin down the last-known-well time and calculate rt-PA eligibility within the 3-hour and extended 3-to-4.5-hour windows, screen thrombolytic contraindications (no anticoagulants, no recent GI bleed, no hemorrhage on CT), obtain emergent CTA/MRI rather than accepting a falsely reassuring plain CT, engage neurology/stroke and neurosurgery immediately, and arrange urgent transfer to an endovascular-capable comprehensive stroke center without delay if this community ED lacks thrombectomy capability - since delayed transfer within the endovascular therapeutic window is an explicitly named pitfall in this issue.
criticalActions: Perform a focused exam beyond 'dizzy' - check for diplopia, dysarthria, and limb/gait ataxia before anchoring on benign vertigo
Do not be reassured by a normal initial noncontrast CT - obtain emergent CT angiography or MRI of the posterior circulation
Pin down the exact last-known-well time and calculate eligibility for IV rt-PA within the 3-hour and extended 3-to-4.5-hour windows
Screen for thrombolytic contraindications (anticoagulation, recent GI bleed, hemorrhage on CT) rather than withholding rt-PA out of generalized fear of bleeding
Consult neurology/stroke service immediately and arrange emergent transfer to an endovascular-capable comprehensive stroke center without delay if a vertebrobasilar large-vessel occlusion is found
Watch for signs of expanding cerebellar edema or brainstem compromise (declining consciousness, new cranial nerve findings, respiratory irregularity) that mandate urgent neurosurgical/ICU involvement
learningPoints: Vertigo is one of the hardest symptoms to source in the ED - keep posterior circulation ischemia on the differential, especially with accompanying diplopia, dysarthria, or limb/gait incoordination
A reassuring exam and a normal early noncontrast CT do not exclude posterior circulation stroke; cerebellar and brainstem infarcts are easy to miss on initial imaging
Extended IV rt-PA eligibility (up to 4.5 hours from last-known-well) applies to posterior circulation strokes just as it does to anterior circulation strokes
Vertebrobasilar large-vessel occlusion is a time-critical endovascular thrombectomy target - delayed transfer to a capable center is a preventable, named pitfall
Untreated posterior circulation stroke can progress to cerebellar edema with obstructive hydrocephalus or basilar occlusion with brainstem infarction and locked-in syndrome
cases.intro: Raymond, 67, is wheeled back from triage clutching the stretcher rail, insisting the room 'won't stop spinning.' He vomited twice in the waiting room and blames a greasy breakfast, but his wife mutters that his words have sounded a little thick since they left the house. The triage nurse jotted down 'vertigo, question inner ear' on the chart, and his blood pressure is a bit high but nothing on the monitor screams emergency.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's been dizzy since he got up this morning, threw up twice, and he's walking like he's had a few too many. I put him down as vertigo but wanted you to lay eyes on him first.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: The room just keeps tilting, doc, like I'm standing on a boat. My arms and legs move fine, I just can't walk straight without holding onto something, and things look a little doubled sometimes.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He said his words were coming out funny earlier, kind of slurred, but he's talking okay now. He gets migraines sometimes, so I figured maybe it was one of those.

cases.title: Blast lung after the garage explosion (the one hiding behind the cuts)
cases.chiefComplaint: 45M with facial/arm lacerations and ear pain after a propane cylinder explosion in an enclosed garage
cases.diagnosis: Primary blast lung injury (pulmonary blast injury) from an enclosed-space overpressure wave — occult on initial exam, with an expected delayed-onset respiratory decompensation
cases.context: Marcus Delgado, a 45-year-old auto mechanic, was standing beside a pressurized cylinder when it ruptured and detonated inside his enclosed two-bay garage; the confined structure reflected and amplified the overpressure shock wave, placing him at markedly higher risk for primary blast injury than an equivalent open-air blast. The true diagnosis is primary blast lung injury: the pressure differential across the air-fluid interfaces of the alveoli causes direct pulmonary parenchymal damage — alveolar hemorrhage, contusion, and risk of pneumothorax — that is frequently occult on arrival because clinical and radiographic findings can lag hours behind the insult. Like the 29-year-old woman in the reference case series (lacerations, HR 110, BP 110/80, O2 97% on room air, who said she 'felt better' on re-evaluation even though it remained unclear whether she was safe to discharge or harbored delayed blast sequelae), Marcus looks reassuringly well at triage: talking, ambulatory, and fixated on his visible forearm lacerations (secondary blast injury from flying shrapnel/debris) and a bruised shoulder from being thrown into a workbench by the blast wind (tertiary blast injury). He minimizes his breathing and blames his rapid pulse on being shaken up — an anxiety/panic reaction is a plausible but incorrect explanation the learner must rule out, as is simple musculoskeletal chest-wall pain from the tertiary blunt trauma. The critical exam clue, mirroring both the 10-year-old with bilateral tympanic membrane rupture and tinnitus and the 62-year-old bus driver with bilateral ear bleeding and hypoxia in the enclosed bus, is Marcus's bilateral blood-tinged otorrhea and muffled hearing: tympanic membrane injury is a marker of significant blast overpressure exposure and should raise suspicion for occult primary blast lung even when the chest exam and initial vitals look reassuring. His vitals on arrival are only mildly abnormal (HR 108, BP 126/80, RR 20, O2 96% on room air) — deceptively better than the hypoxic, dyspneic bus driver, but Marcus is simply earlier in the same disease process. If observation is inadequate and he is discharged prematurely, expect progressive tachypnea, oxygen saturation drifting into the 80s, hemoptysis, and eventual pneumothorax or respiratory failure over the following hours — the hallmark delayed-onset trajectory of blast lung. If he later requires intubation, aggressive positive-pressure ventilation risks forcing air from injured alveoli into the pulmonary veins, causing systemic arterial air embolism with stroke- or MI-like presentations; the safer approach uses the lowest effective airway pressures. The correct, evidence-based path is: examine both tympanic membranes, obtain chest imaging even though he looks well, and admit for a period of in-hospital observation to watch for delayed respiratory decompensation before any discharge is considered.
criticalActions: Inspect both tympanic membranes as a marker of blast overpressure exposure
Obtain chest imaging to evaluate for pulmonary contusion/blast lung and pneumothorax despite a reassuring initial exam
Do not discharge based on normal-appearing vitals alone; admit or hold for a period of observation given the risk of delayed-onset respiratory symptoms
Serially reassess respiratory rate, work of breathing, and oxygen saturation for delayed hypoxia or hemoptysis
If ventilatory support becomes necessary, use a cautious low-pressure ventilation strategy to reduce the risk of arterial air embolism
Distinguish the occult primary (pressure-wave) lung injury from the obvious secondary (shrapnel) and tertiary (thrown/blunt) injuries rather than anchoring on the visible wounds
learningPoints: Primary blast injury comes from the pressure/shock wave itself and preferentially damages air-filled organs (lungs, bowel, ears), producing blast lung that can be completely occult on initial exam
Patients injured in an enclosed or confined space at the moment of detonation are at higher risk for primary blast injury because the space reflects and amplifies the overpressure wave
Tympanic membrane rupture or ear bleeding is a marker of significant blast exposure and should heighten suspicion for occult primary blast lung even when the patient looks well
Respiratory symptoms from primary blast lung are frequently delayed in onset, so a period of ED observation is warranted even in patients who appear well after a blast
Positive-pressure ventilation in a patient with primary blast lung risks precipitating arterial air embolism, so ventilation should use the lowest effective pressures
cases.intro: Marcus is sitting up on the stretcher, chatty and a little wired, dried blood streaking one ear and gauze taped over gashes on his forearm from flying debris when a propane cylinder blew in his garage. He keeps craning his neck to look at his cut-up arm and asking when someone can stitch it so he can get back to his shop, brushing off any questions about his breathing. He was thrown a few feet into a workbench by the blast but walked to the ambulance himself and has been talking in full sentences the whole ride in.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: We pulled him out of his garage after a propane cylinder blew — he got knocked into a workbench but he's been walking and talking the whole time, mostly worried about the cuts on his arm.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I'm fine, honestly, just get these cuts stitched up so I can go check on my shop. My ears are ringing like crazy but I can breathe okay.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Heart rate's up a bit and there's some old blood crusted around both ear canals, otherwise he's talking fine and moving all four extremities without any trouble.

cases.title: The seatbelt bruise that isn't 'just a bruise' (peds lap-belt complex)
cases.chiefComplaint: 7F, restrained rear-seat passenger, head-on MVC, abdominal bruising
cases.diagnosis: Lap-belt complex: L2 flexion-distraction (Chance) fracture of the lumbar spine with associated small-bowel/mesenteric injury from an improperly positioned (lap-belt-only, no shoulder harness) restraint in a frontal motor vehicle collision
cases.context: Maya is a 7-year-old girl who was a rear-seat passenger restrained only by a lap belt (no shoulder harness, center rear seating position of an older sedan) when her mother's car was struck head-on by a vehicle that crossed the center line. Maya was thrown forward against the belt; there was no ejection and no loss of consciousness, and she was talking and crying at the scene. EMS found a linear band of erythema/early bruising across her lower abdomen at the belt line ('seatbelt sign') with mild diffuse abdominal tenderness, but she was alert, moving all extremities, and otherwise looked reassuring. The true diagnosis is a lap-belt complex: an L2 flexion-distraction (Chance) fracture with an associated small-bowel/mesenteric injury (early peritoneal findings are minimal, but a mesenteric tear/bowel-wall contusion is present and will progress to perforation and peritonitis over hours if unrecognized). Biomechanics: because the lap belt crosses her soft abdomen rather than her bony pelvis (she has outgrown her booster but is still too small for proper adult-belt fit, and there is no shoulder harness to arrest forward torso flexion), sudden deceleration jackknifes her trunk forward over the belt, which becomes a fulcrum: the anterior spinal column distracts in tension (Chance fracture) while bowel and mesentery are crushed/sheared against the fixed belt and spine. Plausible competing impressions the learner may fall into: (1) 'it's just a seatbelt bruise/abdominal-wall contusion, she looks great, can go home'; (2) false reassurance from a near-normal initial exam and near-normal vitals; (3) attributing mild tenderness to benign soft-tissue contusion and deferring imaging. The can't-miss teaching point: a seatbelt sign is a specific marker for this triad (spine fracture, hollow viscus injury, solid organ/vascular injury), and in young children the initial exam and vitals are notoriously falsely reassuring - just as a normal neuro exam does not exclude a basilar skull fracture or intracranial hemorrhage in this population, a benign-looking abdomen does not exclude Chance fracture or bowel injury under a seatbelt sign. If mismanaged, expect progressive abdominal distension, worsening tenderness/guarding, climbing heart rate (108 toward 130s-140s), falling blood pressure, fever, and eventual instability from missed bowel perforation/peritonitis/sepsis over 6-12 hours, or new leg weakness, numbness, or bowel/bladder dysfunction if an unstable Chance fracture is missed and the spine isn't protected. The evidence-based approach - maintaining high suspicion despite the reassuring exam, obtaining CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast plus dedicated lumbar spine imaging, keeping spinal precautions, serial abdominal exams, and early trauma/pediatric surgery and spine consultation - identifies the injury before deterioration and allows timely operative management.
criticalActions: Maintain high suspicion for intra-abdominal and spinal injury despite a reassuring initial exam and near-normal vitals given the seatbelt sign
Obtain CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast to evaluate for hollow viscus and solid organ/vascular injury
Obtain dedicated lumbar spine imaging to evaluate for a flexion-distraction (Chance) fracture
Maintain spinal precautions until the spine is definitively cleared
Perform serial abdominal examinations and reassessment, since bowel/mesenteric injury can be initially occult
Obtain early trauma/pediatric surgery consultation given the seatbelt-sign triad
learningPoints: A seatbelt sign (bruising across the abdomen) after a pediatric MVC is a marker for the classic triad of Chance fracture, hollow viscus injury, and intra-abdominal solid organ/vascular injury

Lap-belt-only or improperly positioned restraint (common when children outgrow booster seats but are still too small for adult belt fit) transmits flexion-distraction forces across the abdomen and lumbar spine
A reassuring initial exam and near-normal vitals do not rule out serious injury in this population, just as a normal neuro exam does not exclude a basilar skull fracture or intracranial hemorrhage
Restrained children have much lower overall injury risk than unrestrained children, but improper positioning of the restraint itself creates a distinct, easily missed injury pattern
High suspicion plus CT abdomen/pelvis, spine imaging, and early surgical consultation prevent missed bowel perforation/peritonitis and missed unstable spine fracture with neurologic injury
cases.intro: EMS wheels back a 7-year-old girl who's chatting with her mom, complaining that her tummy 'feels kind of sore' after the car she was riding in was hit head-on on the highway. She's alert, making eye contact, and manages a small smile when the nurse asks about her stuffed animal, and the reddish-purple band of bruising across her lower belly looks to the paramedic like 'just a seatbelt mark.' Her mother, shaken but uninjured, insists Maya 'was talking the whole ride here' and seems mostly worried about missing tomorrow's school field trip.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Seven-year-old, restrained in the back with a lap belt, head-on collision at maybe forty miles an hour. She was crying at the scene but never lost consciousness, and vitals have been stable the whole transport.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She kept telling me her stomach hurt a little, but she's been talking and asking for her tablet the whole ride, so I figured she was okay.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My tummy just feels kind of achy where the seatbelt was. I don't want a shot.

cases.title: The fall that doesn't add up (elder abuse, not accident)
cases.chiefComplaint: 84F, unwitnessed fall at home, neck laceration + forearm bruising
cases.diagnosis: Physical elder abuse by caregiver (adult son) — injury pattern and history inconsistent with the reported fall mechanism in a cognitively impaired, dependent older adult
cases.context: Eleanor Whitfield, 84, was brought to the ED by her adult son after what he describes as an unwitnessed mechanical fall at home. She has atrial fibrillation on apixaban 5mg BID and moderate-to-severe dementia, and has lived with her son as her sole caregiver for the past 18 months since her husband died and she could no longer manage independently. She has no other regular visitors, does not attend any community or religious groups, and her son answers 'it's just the two of us now' when asked about other family or support. On exam she has a 4cm jagged, partially healed laceration along the right lateral neck and confluent violet bruising across both medial forearms in a pattern that does not fit a single fall onto an outstretched hand — there are no bruises over the bony prominences (hips, elbows, knees) typically seen with a mechanical fall. The paramedic run sheet, based on the scene, describes finding her on the kitchen floor with no rug nearby, while her son now tells the story of her tripping over a rug in the living room, a discrepancy the triage nurse flagged but has not reconciled. Eleanor is quiet and deferential, answering most questions with 'ask my son' or a small nod toward him, and grows visibly more hesitant whenever he answers for her. The two most tempting alternative explanations are that her apixaban is simply causing exaggerated bruising from a genuinely accidental fall, and that her dementia makes her an unreliable historian whose 'confusion' explains any inconsistency — both are plausible in isolation but neither explains the mismatched fall location or the symmetric medial-forearm bruising, which is more consistent with being grabbed or restrained than with a fall. Vitals show only a mildly elevated heart rate and blood pressure consistent with pain and anxiety, not hemodynamic instability. CBC shows no significant anemia; coagulation studies and CT head/C-spine, obtained given the neck laceration and anticoagulation, are unremarkable with no traumatic brain or spinal injury. If treated as a routine mechanical fall — brief wound closure and reassurance — Eleanor will be discharged back into her son's custody with no intervention, and elder abuse is underreported by at least two orders of magnitude precisely because these encounters are missed; overlapping types of abuse carry markedly higher mortality. The evidence-based approach is to interview her privately without her son present, perform a full skin and body exam for occult injuries, document the exact discrepancy between the EMS report and the caregiver's account, and — because making the diagnosis and reporting to Adult Protective Services is the single most important management step for suspected elder abuse — file an APS report and involve social work/case management before finalizing disposition.
criticalActions: Interview the patient privately, away from her son, before finalizing the history
Perform a complete skin and body exam looking for injuries inconsistent with the stated fall mechanism
Obtain CT head/C-spine and coagulation studies given the neck laceration and apixaban use
Document the discrepancy between the EMS-reported mechanism and the caregiver's account verbatim
Report suspected abuse to Adult Protective Services rather than discharging the patient back into the caregiver's custody
Engage social work/case management to build a multidisciplinary safety plan before disposition
learningPoints: Elder abuse is underreported by at least two orders of magnitude — a fall may be the patient's only opportunity to be identified and helped
Physical abuse is often mistaken for accidental trauma or the normal skin fragility of aging, especially in patients on anticoagulants
Cognitive impairment and dependency on a caregiver are major risk factors, and spouses and adult children are the most common perpetrators
A history that shifts between EMS, caregiver, and patient, or a caregiver who answers for the patient, is itself a red flag
The single most important management step is making the diagnosis and reporting suspicion to Adult Protective Services
cases.intro: Eleanor, 84, arrives by ambulance after 'a fall at home,' her son at the bedside recounting how she tripped over a rug while he was in the next room. She has a jagged laceration along the right side of her neck and deep purple bruising across both inner forearms, which her son attributes to her blood thinner making her 'bruise at the slightest bump.' She answers questions with a small nod toward her son, and the paramedic note clipped to the chart describes a different scene than the one he's telling you now.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: EMS called it an unwitnessed fall, but the run sheet says she was found on the kitchen floor, no rug anywhere near her. That's not quite the story he's telling us now.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's so fragile these days, doctor. She bruises if you just help her out of a chair. I wasn't even in the room when it happened.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I don't... I don't really remember. Ask my son, he takes care of everything for me.

cases.title: The flank GSW that looked fine (until it wasn't)
cases.chiefComplaint: 44F, single gunshot wound to the left flank
cases.diagnosis: Occult splenic laceration with left renal hilar/vascular injury and retroperitoneal hemorrhage from a left flank gunshot wound, producing hemorrhagic shock despite a deceptively reassuring exam and a negative E-FAST
cases.context: This 44-year-old woman was shot once in the left flank while fleeing a robbery. The single entry wound sits over the posterior axillary line at roughly the 10th-11th intercostal level; there is no exit wound, so the bullet is retained. True injury: the round tracked obliquely anteromedial and caudad through the thoracoabdominal junction, lacerating the lower pole of the spleen and injuring the left renal hilum/vascular pedicle before lodging in the retroperitoneum near the psoas. She is bleeding into both the peritoneal cavity and, more dangerously, the retroperitoneal space. Despite this, she looks deceptively okay early on: awake, alert, oriented, conversational, anxious rather than distressed — classic compensated hemorrhagic shock, where tachycardia and peripheral vasoconstriction are masking ongoing blood loss. The wound itself looks unimpressive — a single clean puncture, minimal external bleeding, no bruising yet — and her abdomen is only mildly tender without peritonism on first look. The trap for the learner is twofold: (1) dismissing this as 'just a flank flesh wound' because the patient is talking and the entry hole is small, and (2) treating a negative E-FAST as reassurance that there's no significant bleeding. Per the article's ballistics principles, a bullet's kinetic energy causes damage via permanent cavitation (crush injury along the path) and temporary cavitation (tissue stretch around it) that can extend well beyond the visible track and occurs regardless of caliber or bullet size — wound appearance does not predict internal injury. E-FAST only detects free intraperitoneal fluid; it does not reliably visualize retroperitoneal hemorrhage, which is exactly where this patient's renal/vascular bleeding is occurring, so a negative scan here is falsely reassuring. If the learner anchors on the benign-looking wound and the negative FAST, deprioritizes trauma surgery involvement, and delays further evaluation, she will progressively decompensate: HR climbing from the 100s toward the 130s, systolic BP falling from ~108 into the 80s and then 70s, pulse pressure narrowing further, with progression from anxious to diaphoretic to confused as compensation fails. The correct approach is immediate trauma team activation for any torso gunshot wound regardless of apparent stability, reasoning about the bullet's likely trajectory rather than the cosmetic appearance of the wound, recognizing that a negative FAST cannot exclude retroperitoneal hemorrhage, serial hemodynamic reassessment, and early transition to blood-product resuscitation (not crystalloid alone) the moment a downward trend appears — followed by CT if she remains stable enough to tolerate it, or emergent operative exploration if she decompensates. Start vitals borderline-compensated: HR ~108, BP ~108/86, RR 20, SpO2 97% on room air, afebrile — with clear room to trend toward decompensated shock if the encounter is mismanaged.
criticalActions: Activate the trauma team for any torso gunshot wound regardless of how stable the patient initially appears
Reason about the bullet's likely trajectory rather than relying on wound size or caliber to judge severity
Recognize that a negative E-FAST does not exclude significant retroperitoneal hemorrhage
Reassess vital signs serially and maintain suspicion for occult hemorrhage despite a reassuring first exam
Escalate to blood-product resuscitation rather than crystalloid alone once any downward hemodynamic trend appears
Obtain further imaging (CT) if stable enough, or move to emergent operative exploration if she decompensates
learningPoints: Wound size and bullet caliber do not predict internal injury severity
Tissue damage results from kinetic energy transfer via permanent and temporary cavitation, which can extend far beyond the visible wound track
Flank and torso gunshot wounds carry high risk for occult retroperitoneal injury that FAST does not reliably detect
A negative E-FAST only rules out free intraperitoneal fluid, not retroperitoneal bleeding
Patients can be awake, alert, and conversational while in compensated hemorrhagic shock before rapidly decompensating
cases.intro: EMS wheels in a 44-year-old woman on a backboard, shot once in the left flank while fleeing a robbery. She's awake, talking, and asking to just get patched up and go — the entry wound looks like a small, clean puncture with barely any bleeding, and at first glance she seems remarkably okay for someone who's been shot.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Got a forty-four-year-old female, single gunshot wound to the left flank, one shot, no exit that we found. She's been talking to us this whole ride in, vitals have held steady.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I'm okay, really, it barely even hurts. Can someone just look at it quick? I need to call my kids.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Pressure's one-oh-eight over eighty-six, heart rate's up around one-oh-eight. Entry wound looks pretty clean, belly's soft on my first pass.

cases.article: 2015-09_Hemophilia And Von Willebrand Disease In Children Emergency Department Evaluation And Management PDF.pdf
cases.title: The head bump that looks fine (until it isn't) - severe hemophilia B
cases.chiefComplaint: 7yoM, fall from bed, hit head, mild headache
cases.diagnosis: Occult traumatic intracranial hemorrhage (evolving left frontal subdural hematoma) in a child with severe hemophilia B, requiring emergent empiric factor IX replacement before imaging confirmation
cases.context: Ethan is a 7-year-old boy with severe hemophilia B - congenital factor IX deficiency with baseline factor IX activity under 1 IU/dL - who receives scheduled prophylactic recombinant factor IX infusions three times weekly at home using AlphaNine SD; his last dose was approximately 48 hours ago, and by this point in the dosing cycle his circulating factor IX activity has likely waned well below hemostatic levels. The diagnosis is already well known to the family, who carry his factor dosing card and can state his home product and usual dose without prompting. Roughly 30 minutes ago he rolled off his bed, a fall of about 3 feet, and struck the left side of his head on a hardwood floor; he cried briefly, then went back to playing. At triage he is awake, alert, oriented, GCS 15, with a normal neurologic exam and no palpable scalp hematoma or bruising, and vitals are only mildly abnormal. The true process at work is an evolving, initially clinically silent traumatic intracranial hemorrhage (a thin left frontal subdural hematoma): in severe hemophilia, even minor, low-mechanism head trauma with a reassuring exam can produce a rapidly expanding bleed, because factor levels - not exam findings - determine whether the injury tamponades or propagates. Competing framings the learner must actively reject: (1) a routine 'low-risk' pediatric minor head injury managed with observation alone per standard criteria (normal exam, no hematoma, no vomiting) - those rules assume normal coagulation and do not apply here; (2) false reassurance that because he 'looks fine and is playing,' urgent treatment can wait for CT results; (3) hesitation or delay because the ED stocks a different factor IX brand (BeneFIX) than his home product (AlphaNine SD) - treatment should not be withheld over a product mismatch. The critical, can't-miss teaching point is 'treat first, image second': empiric factor IX replacement (target activity near 100 IU/dL, roughly 50-100 IU/kg IV) must be given as soon as head trauma is recognized in this patient, before and regardless of CT head results, because the foundation of hemophilia management is urgent clotting factor replacement guided by the presenting complaint, not by imaging turnaround. If treatment is delayed pending CT, expect progressive deterioration over the next 30-60 minutes: worsening headache, vomiting, increasing lethargy or irritability, then pupillary asymmetry and eventually bradycardia with hypertension (Cushing triad) as the subdural expands with midline shift, potentially requiring emergent neurosurgical evacuation. If factor IX is given promptly, CT head (once obtained) shows only a small, stable ~4mm subdural hematoma without midline shift, and the child remains neurologically intact and can be admitted for observation rather than taken to the OR.
criticalActions: Treat any head trauma in a child with hemophilia as a potential intracranial hemorrhage regardless of a normal exam
Give empiric factor IX replacement immediately - do not wait for CT head results before treating
Confirm timing of the last prophylactic factor dose to recognize trough vulnerability at time of injury
Do not apply standard low-risk minor head injury observation-only pathways to a patient with an underlying bleeding disorder
Obtain CT head after or alongside (never instead of) empiric factor administration
Reassess frequently for signs of expanding intracranial hemorrhage: worsening headache, vomiting, altered mental status, pupillary asymmetry, Cushing triad
learningPoints: The foundation of hemophilia treatment is urgent clotting factor replacement guided by the presenting complaint, not by diagnostic imaging
Head trauma in a child with hemophilia requires empiric factor replacement even with a normal neurologic exam and no scalp hematoma
Standard 'low-risk' minor head injury observation criteria assume normal coagulation and do not apply to patients with bleeding disorders
Trough factor levels between scheduled prophylactic doses can leave an otherwise well-controlled hemophilia patient transiently vulnerable to bleeding after trauma
Hemophilia classically produces severe joint, intracranial, and intramuscular hemorrhage, distinct from the milder mucocutaneous bleeding pattern of von Willebrand disease
cases.intro: Seven-year-old Ethan is brought back by his mother after rolling off his bed and hitting his head on the floor about half an hour ago. He's awake, alert, and complaining of a mild headache, but otherwise sitting up on the stretcher asking for his tablet back. His mother mentions, almost in passing while filling out the intake form, that he has a bleeding condition and gets factor infusions a few times a week. The triage nurse notes his vitals are only mildly elevated and there's no bump or bruise anywhere on his scalp.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Vitals are stable, he's a GCS 15, and I don't feel any hematoma on his scalp. Mom says he's acting completely normal for him.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He just rolled right off the bed, it wasn't even that high up. He cried for a minute and then wanted his tablet back. He does get his factor shots at home - I think his last one was Tuesday.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My head hurts a little bit. Can I have my tablet back now?

cases.title: The 'just tired and feverish' toddler (meningococcemia before the crash)
cases.chiefComplaint: 4M — fever, lethargy, faint rash
cases.diagnosis: Meningococcemia (Neisseria meningitidis bacteremia) with early septic shock progressing toward respiratory failure requiring prepared, controlled RSI
cases.context: Caleb is a previously healthy 4-year-old boy brought in by his parents for fever and increasing lethargy that began the evening before; the true process is early meningococcemia evolving into septic shock, the same final common pathway toward pediatric respiratory failure this article's own index case describes in its late, fully decompensated form (RR 40, HR 180, BP 50/palp, SpO2 84% on high-flow face mask, weak with increased work of breathing and grayish skin color). At this presentation he is roughly 1-2 hours upstream of that picture: HR 148, RR 34 with mild subcostal retractions, SpO2 93% on room air, BP 92/58, Temp 39.4C, a faint non-blanching petechial rash just emerging on his shins and ankles, cool mottled distal extremities, leg pain out of proportion to exam, and a weak, hoarse cry with reduced eye contact - subtle findings easily reframed as 'just a tired, fussy kid with a fever' unless actively sought. Competing explanations the learner must rule out: viral gastroenteritis/dehydration (lethargy plus fever, but doesn't explain a non-blanching rash or leg pain), a benign viral exanthem or allergic drug rash (blanches, doesn't track with his toxic appearance), and febrile UTI/pyelonephritis (fever plus fussiness, but no rash or shock physiology). The can't-miss trap is anchoring on 'not that sick yet' vitals and deferring escalation. Per the article, respiratory failure in children funnels through only a handful of final common pathways, and pediatric intubation itself carries real risk if rushed or poorly prepared: reported complication rates for pediatric endotracheal intubation include unrecognized esophageal intubation (2%), tube dislodgement (14%), mainstem intubation (18%), and incorrect tube size (24%) - evidence for meticulous preparation (personnel, equipment, positioning), not for a hurried 'crash' attempt. Anatomically, Caleb's proportionally large occiput will passively flex his neck and obstruct both spontaneous ventilation and laryngoscopic view if he is laid flat without a shoulder roll or towel to align his airway axis, and unnecessary agitation (forcing him supine for repeated exams, separating him from his parent) can precipitate abrupt decompensation. If mismanaged - delayed recognition, agitating exams, or an unprepared airway attempt without adequate preoxygenation, suction ready for likely vomiting, and a backup can't-intubate/can't-ventilate plan - he decompensates over 1-2 hours into the article's index presentation: profound shock, SpO2 84% despite high-flow oxygen, and impending arrest. The correct approach - early recognition, IV/IO access, empiric antibiotics, fluid resuscitation, keeping him calm and upright with a parent nearby, proper occiput positioning, and a deliberately prepared RSI (or well-performed bag-valve-mask, which the literature shows performs comparably to ETI and is a reasonable temporizing bridge) with the most experienced available provider present - averts the crash and gets him to a controlled airway and PICU instead of a code.
criticalActions: Recognize subtle signs of impending shock/respiratory failure (grayish/mottled skin, weak cry, leg pain, mild retractions) rather than waiting for hypotension or desaturation
Obtain IV/IO access early and give empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics plus fluid resuscitation without delay
Keep the child calm and upright with a parent nearby; avoid unnecessary agitating exams that can precipitate decompensation
Position for the large pediatric occiput (shoulder roll/towel) to align the airway axis before any laryngoscopy attempt
Prepare thoroughly before any airway attempt: preoxygenation, working suction for anticipated vomiting, correct-size equipment, and a failed-airway backup plan
Call for the most experienced available provider and consider bag-valve-mask as an effective temporizing bridge rather than a rushed intubation
learningPoints: Pediatric respiratory failure funnels through only a handful of final common pathways regardless of the underlying disease - recognize early signs rather than waiting for the classic decompensated picture
Pediatric endotracheal intubation carries substantial reported complication rates: unrecognized esophageal intubation, tube dislodgement, mainstem intubation, and incorrect tube size
The proportionally large pediatric occiput passively flexes the neck in the supine position, obstructing both ventilation and the laryngoscopic view unless properly positioned
Bag-valve-mask ventilation performs comparably to endotracheal intubation in pediatric patients and is a reasonable, safer temporizing strategy when the airway is not yet fully prepared
A deliberately prepared approach - preoxygenation, positioning, personnel, and a backup plan - is essential given the low margin for error in a decompensating child
cases.intro: Caleb is curled against his mother's chest in bay 4, cheeks flushed and eyes half-closed, more interested in dozing than in the beeping monitor. His mom says he's had a fever since last night and just seems 'wiped out' today, quieter than his usual self. The nurse mentions his hands feel a little cool despite how warm his forehead is, and there's a faint spray of tiny red-purple dots starting on his shins that nobody's quite sure about yet.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's been fussy and warm since this morning, mom says he just seems really tired for a fever this low, and he barely fussed when I put the pulse ox on.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He didn't want his juice earlier and he keeps saying his legs hurt, but he won't really look at me when I talk to him.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My legs hurt. I want to sleep.

cases.article: 2017-01_Altered Level Of Consciousness Evidence-Based Management In The Emergency Department.pdf
cases.title: Vomiting for three days, now she won't wake up (the metabolic crisis behind it)
cases.chiefComplaint: 9F lethargy and disorientation after days of vomiting, known metabolic disease
cases.diagnosis: Acute metabolic decompensation (hyperammonemic/organic-acid encephalopathy) in a child with propionic acidemia, precipitated by a catabolic illness with vomiting and poor intake
cases.context: Lily is a 9-year-old girl with a known diagnosis of propionic acidemia, an inborn error of organic acid metabolism, who is brought in after three days of nonbloody, nonbilious vomiting and one day of progressive lethargy and increased work of breathing; she has been unable to tolerate her usual metabolic formula because of the vomiting. Her parents deny fever, diarrhea, or a preceding respiratory illness, and she has no history of seizures. The true diagnosis is an acute metabolic decompensation: the vomiting and poor intake pushed her into a catabolic state, causing toxic organic-acid metabolites (and, via impaired urea-cycle flux, ammonia) to accumulate faster than her body can clear them, producing a hyperammonemic/organic-acid encephalopathy that is now manifesting as her altered level of consciousness. On exam she is disoriented to person, place, and date, with dry mucous membranes and a capillary refill of about two seconds; vitals show a temperature of 37C, heart rate 150, respiratory rate 28 with visibly increased work of breathing (a compensatory response to underlying metabolic acidosis), and a blood pressure of 80/40 reflecting a mix of dehydration and the metabolic crisis itself. Competing diagnoses the team must actively work through include simple dehydration from a garden-variety viral gastroenteritis (her vomiting and tachycardia could easily be explained this way if the underlying metabolic disease is overlooked), an occult infection or sepsis driving the catabolic stress (which must be sought and treated in parallel, since infection is the single most common precipitant of metabolic crises), and a primary CNS process such as meningoencephalitis (her fever-free status and gradual, illness-linked onset argue against it but do not exclude it outright). The case is built around the 'Metabolic: inborn errors of metabolism' entry of the standard ALOC mnemonic (MOVESTUPID), which specifically flags propionic acidemia as a can't-miss cause of altered consciousness in a child already known to carry the diagnosis -- the trap is treating her as routine gastroenteritis-with-dehydration and giving standard maintenance fluids without addressing the underlying catabolic crisis. If unrecognized, ongoing accumulation of ammonia and toxic metabolites drives progressive cerebral edema, deepening obtundation, coma, and risk of irreversible neurologic injury or death. The correct, evidence-based approach -- immediate bedside glucose, ammonia, venous blood gas and lactate to confirm the crisis; prompt dextrose-containing IV fluids to reverse catabolism and halt further metabolite production; correction of dehydration and electrolytes; early involvement of metabolic/genetics specialists; and a parallel search for an infectious precipitant -- reverses the encephalopathy and prevents further neurologic injury.
criticalActions: Recognize that a known inborn error of metabolism (e.g., propionic acidemia) with vomiting and lethargy requires urgent metabolic-specific management, not routine rehydration alone
Obtain immediate glucose, ammonia, venous blood gas, and lactate to assess for a metabolic decompensation/hyperammonemic crisis
Start IV dextrose-containing fluids promptly to reverse the catabolic state driving toxic metabolite accumulation
Actively search for and treat a precipitating infection in parallel, since infection is the most common trigger of metabolic crises
Involve metabolic/genetics specialists early to guide protein restriction and disease-specific therapy
Reassess mental status frequently using standard ALOC terminology (lethargy, obtundation, disorientation) to detect trajectory toward coma requiring escalation
learningPoints: Inborn errors of metabolism are an explicit, distinct category in the standard ALOC differential (MOVESTUPID) and must be considered in any child with a known metabolic disease presenting with vomiting and lethargy
A catabolic trigger such as illness, poor intake, or vomiting can rapidly precipitate metabolic decompensation in patients with organic acidemias, even without a new infection
Precise ALOC terminology (lethargy, obtundation, disorientation) helps track a patient's trajectory and communicate severity between team members
Tachycardia, tachypnea, and hypotension in a metabolically vulnerable child may reflect a systemic metabolic crisis rather than dehydration alone
A broad, systematic differential rather than anchoring on the known underlying diagnosis or on simple dehydration ensures other dangerous causes are not missed
cases.intro: Lily's parents carry her in still in her pajamas, saying she's been throwing up for three days and this morning she just wouldn't wake up all the way. They mention, almost in passing, that she has some kind of rare metabolic condition they manage at home with a special formula, but insist she's never been this out of it before. The triage nurse flags that she's breathing fast and hard, mumbling instead of answering questions, and doesn't seem to know where she is.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's had this rare metabolic thing since she was a baby, we usually manage it fine at home with her special formula, but she hasn't kept anything down in three days.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's tachypneic and working hard to breathe, and when I ask her where she is she just stares at me and mumbles, she doesn't even know her own name today.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: This is so much worse than any of her usual sick days, normally a little extra formula and some fluids fixes it by tomorrow.

cases.article: 2016-09_Toxic Alcohol Ingestion, Prompt Recognition And Management In The Emergency Department.pdf
cases.title: The moonshine that stole his sight
cases.chiefComplaint: 44M — vomiting and blurry vision after a weekend of homemade spirits
cases.diagnosis: Methanol poisoning (toxic alcohol ingestion with formic acid accumulation causing anion-gap metabolic acidosis and optic nerve toxicity)
cases.context: A 44-year-old man presents roughly 18 hours after drinking home-distilled "moonshine" spirits shared at a weekend gathering; he reports several other attendees also became ill. He initially felt merely hungover with nausea and abdominal pain, but now describes his vision as blurry, like looking through fog or a snowstorm, and photophobia. TRUE DIAGNOSIS: methanol poisoning. Methanol itself is only weakly toxic, but hepatic alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) slowly oxidizes it to formaldehyde and then to formic acid; formic acid is the toxic metabolite responsible for both a severe high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis and direct, relatively selective toxicity to the retina and optic nerve (inhibits mitochondrial cytochrome oxidase, producing histotoxic hypoxia in the opsin-rich retina), which is why methanol poisoning classically causes visual blurring, scotomata, and can progress to permanent blindness — this visual toxicity is the key feature separating methanol from ethylene glycol, which instead produces calcium oxalate crystalluria and acute kidney injury without visual symptoms. Because methanol itself must first be metabolized, there is often a latent, deceptively well-appearing period of 12-24 hours (longer if ethanol was co-ingested, since ethanol competitively occupies ADH and delays formic acid formation) before the anion gap and visual symptoms appear — an early normal-appearing anion gap with a normal-seeming exam does NOT exclude methanol poisoning. COMPETING DIAGNOSES to actively rule out: (1) simple ethanol intoxication/hangover — he does have a faint alcohol smell from the tainted moonshine itself, which is a red herring that invites premature discharge; (2) diabetic or alcoholic ketoacidosis, another cause of high-anion-gap acidosis with vomiting and tachypnea, but his glucose and ketones will be unremarkable; (3) other anion-gap acidosis mimics discussed in toxic-alcohol teaching — salicylate toxicity, metformin- or sepsis-related lactic acidosis — none of which explain the new visual complaints. CAN'T-MISS TRAPS: the osmolar gap (measured minus calculated serum osmolality) can support suspicion when elevated but is neither sensitive nor specific and CANNOT be used to rule the diagnosis in or out; the gap also narrows and normalizes over time as methanol is metabolized into formic acid, so a normal osmolar gap late in the course is falsely reassuring while the anion gap is only beginning to widen — labs should be trended, not single-point. CONCRETE NUMBERS for consistency: initial vitals HR 118, BP 138/84, RR 32 (deep, Kussmaul-pattern), T 37.1C, SpO2 97% RA; venous/arterial blood gas pH 7.16, HCO3 9 mEq/L, anion gap 28; measured serum osmolality 320 mOsm/kg vs calculated 295 mOsm/kg (osmolar gap ~25, consistent with unmetabolized methanol still present); visual acuity 20/100 bilaterally with sluggish pupillary response and funduscopic exam showing bilateral optic disc hyperemia/blurred margins; ethanol level low/undetectable on this presentation despite the alcohol odor (the moonshine itself contained methanol as a contaminant). DETERIORATION IF MISMANAGED: if discharged as "just drunk" or the acidosis is undertreated, formic acid continues accumulating — worsening anion-gap acidosis, refractory seizures, cerebral edema (especially putaminal hemorrhagic necrosis), progression from blurred vision to complete irreversible blindness, coma, and death. CORRECT EVIDENCE-BASED MANAGEMENT (per article): early recognition and empiric antidotal therapy with fomepizole (or ethanol infusion if fomepizole is unavailable) to competitively block alcohol dehydrogenase and halt further formic acid production; sodium bicarbonate for severe acidemia (correcting pH also reduces the non-ionized formic acid fraction able to cross into the CNS/retina); folate (folinic acid) administration to accelerate the folate-dependent metabolism of formate to carbon dioxide and water, hastening its clearance; and emergent hemodialysis for severe acidosis, markedly elevated methanol levels, visual symptoms, or renal failure, since dialysis clears both methanol and formic acid. Any history of co-ingested ethanol should be elicited, since large ethanol levels can complicate and prolong antidotal dosing.
criticalActions: Recognize a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis with visual complaints as methanol poisoning, not simple intoxication
Send a toxic alcohol level and calculate both anion gap and osmolar gap, trending them over time rather than trusting one normal value
Start fomepizole early (or ethanol infusion if fomepizole unavailable) to block alcohol dehydrogenase and halt formic acid production
Give sodium bicarbonate for severe acidemia
Administer folate (folinic acid) to accelerate formate clearance
Arrange emergent hemodialysis for severe acidosis, high methanol level, visual symptoms, or renal failure
learningPoints: Methanol is metabolized by alcohol dehydrogenase to formaldehyde then formic acid, which drives both the anion-gap acidosis and direct retinal/optic nerve toxicity.
Visual disturbance progressing toward blindness is the hallmark feature that distinguishes methanol poisoning from ethylene glycol poisoning.

A normal-appearing anion gap or osmolar gap early in the course does not rule out methanol poisoning — there is often a latent period before formic acid accumulates, and the osmolar gap narrows as metabolism proceeds.
Fomepizole (or ethanol) blocks further toxic metabolite formation and should be started as soon as toxic alcohol poisoning is suspected, without waiting for a confirmatory level.
Hemodialysis clears both methanol and formic acid and is indicated for severe acidosis, high levels, visual symptoms, or renal failure.
cases.intro: A 44-year-old man is brought in by his brother, queasy and complaining his vision has gone blurry and foggy since this morning. He smells faintly of alcohol and says he just figured he was hungover from a weekend cookout where a few of them shared some homemade liquor a coworker distilled himself. He is breathing fast and deep, and keeps squinting like the lights are too bright.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My stomach's been killing me since last night, and now everything just looks like static, like a TV with no signal. It's freaking me out.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: We were at a cookout and a guy brought his own batch of moonshine, a few of us drank it. I feel fine but he's been getting worse all morning.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's breathing fast and deep, sats are fine, but his gas shows a pretty wide gap and his vision is off.

cases.title: Thunderclap headache after a deadlift (the one that isn't SAH)
cases.chiefComplaint: 41F - sudden 'worst headache of my life' during exertion, now with blurry vision and near-syncope
cases.diagnosis: Pituitary apoplexy - acute hemorrhage/infarction into a previously undiagnosed nonfunctioning pituitary macroadenoma, causing thunderclap headache, bitemporal visual field deficit, and acute secondary adrenal insufficiency
cases.context: Maria's true diagnosis is pituitary apoplexy: acute hemorrhage/infarction into a previously unrecognized pituitary macroadenoma, incidentally seen on a head CT three years ago after a car accident and never followed up. The apoplexy occurred about 20 minutes before arrival during a heavy deadlift - a Valsalva-type trigger the article specifically flags ('sudden headache with exercise, coughing, straining, or orgasm is concerning for SAH'), which is exactly why this case is dangerous: the trigger and pain quality are indistinguishable from aneurysmal SAH. Pathophysiology: rapid pituitary expansion from hemorrhage stretches the dural sheath (severe headache), compresses the optic chiasm (bitemporal visual field loss), can extend into the cavernous sinus (painful diplopia/ptosis), and destroys functioning gland tissue, producing acute secondary adrenal insufficiency - the article's own red-flag list for pituitary apoplexy is 'hypotension, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, visual field deficit, history of pituitary tumor.' Competing diagnoses to actively exclude: (1) SAH - the leading concern given thunderclap onset with exertion, 'worst headache of my life,' vomiting, and mild neck discomfort; the article notes 93% of SAH patients call it the worst headache of their life, but so do 77% of patients WITHOUT SAH, so the descriptor alone cannot end the workup. (2) Migraine - she has no personal migraine history, but staff may anchor on 'just a bad migraine,' especially if ketorolac or a triptan partially relieves her pain; per the article's ACEP Level C recommendation, response to headache medication must never be used to exclude a dangerous secondary cause, since SAH and structural lesions also respond to these drugs. (3) Tension headache - excluded quickly by the explosive onset and abnormal exam. The critical trap: noncontrast head CT is negative for blood (small intrasellar hemorrhage is often missed on CT), and a subsequent LP shows normal opening pressure with no xanthochromia and few RBCs - appropriately excluding SAH. But a negative CT and LP do NOT end the secondary-headache workup. Only when the visual field deficit and abnormal glucose/sodium/blood pressure are pursued does the team order a pituitary-protocol MRI brain (the article's Table 8 lists MRI/MRV brain as the specific study for pituitary apoplexy, venous thrombosis, and hypertensive encephalopathy), revealing a 2.3 cm hemorrhagic pituitary macroadenoma compressing the optic chiasm. If mismanaged - discharged after negative CT/LP as 'atypical migraine' - she deteriorates over hours into worsening vision loss and hemodynamic collapse from unrecognized adrenal crisis. Correct management - recognizing the endocrine emergency, giving empiric stress-dose IV hydrocortisone, obtaining emergent MRI, and consulting neurosurgery/endocrinology for possible urgent decompression - prevents permanent vision loss and cardiovascular collapse. Vitals/labs to run consistently: HR 110-120s, BP 86-92/50-58 (hypotensive, worsening if untreated), afebrile, fingerstick glucose low-normal to frank hypoglycemia, sodium mildly low (~128-131), cortisol level pending/low, CT head clean, LP clean, MRI diagnostic.
criticalActions: Check fingerstick glucose and a basic metabolic panel early in a thunderclap headache patient with visual changes, screening for adrenal-insufficiency pattern (hypoglycemia, hyponatremia)
Perform a full neuro-ophthalmologic exam including formal visual fields and extraocular movements, not just pupils and gross vision
Obtain noncontrast head CT and, if negative, proceed to LP for suspected SAH per standard pathway
Recognize that a negative CT and negative LP for SAH do NOT end the secondary-headache workup when red flags (visual field deficit, endocrine derangement, hypotension) persist
Order a pituitary-protocol MRI/MRV brain once SAH is excluded but exam/labs still point to a specific secondary cause
Give empiric stress-dose IV hydrocortisone and consult neurosurgery/endocrinology urgently rather than waiting on imaging confirmation once adrenal crisis is suspected
learningPoints: "Worst headache of my life" and thunderclap onset are sensitive but not specific for SAH - 77% of patients WITHOUT SAH also describe it that way

A negative noncontrast CT and negative LP rule out SAH but do not close the secondary-headache workup - MRI/MRV brain is needed for pituitary apoplexy, venous sinus thrombosis, and hypertensive encephalopathy

Pituitary apoplexy classically presents as thunderclap headache plus visual field deficit plus endocrine collapse (hypotension, hypoglycemia, hyponatremia) - treat it as an endocrine emergency, not just a headache
Response to migraine-specific medication (triptans, NSAIDs, neuroleptics) does not exclude a dangerous secondary cause - SAH and structural lesions can also respond to these drugs (ACEP Level C)
Do not anchor on a benign headache history - patients without a migraine history, or with an old 'incidental' pituitary finding, still need a full red-flag-driven workup
cases.intro: Maria is wheeled back clutching her head, moaning that it's the worst headache of her life. It hit instantly, like being struck with a bat, twenty minutes ago mid-deadlift at the gym. She's vomited twice in triage and keeps her eyes shut against the overhead lights, and now says everything looks blurry and doubled. The triage nurse has already flagged her chart as 'rule out bleed.'

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Room four, sudden thunderclap headache during exercise, worst of her life, vomited twice, and her pressure's a little low so I already grabbed a repeat set.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It just exploded in my head, like nothing I've ever felt, right when I was lifting. Everything looks blurry and doubled and I feel like I might pass out.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She never gets headaches like this, ever. They found some little spot on her brain scan a few years back after her car accident, but the doctors said it was nothing to worry about.

cases.article: 2016-08_Transient Global Amnesia, Emergency Department Evaluation And Management.pdf
cases.title: The woman who keeps asking the same question (TGA, not stroke)
cases.chiefComplaint: 67F - sudden repetitive questioning, can't form new memories x30 min
cases.diagnosis: Transient global amnesia (classic, red-flag-negative presentation)
cases.context: Diane, a 67-year-old woman with a history of migraine headaches but no seizures, prior stroke, or psychiatric illness, was arguing with her husband about weekend plans in their backyard when she abruptly stopped mid-sentence and asked him what they were doing outside. Over the next 30 minutes she asked the identical question - 'What are we doing out here?' - at least eight times, each time as though hearing it fresh, unable to retain the answer moments after being told it; this is the classic 'broken-record' phenomenon of transient global amnesia (TGA), the acute, isolated inability to form new declarative memories (facts and events) while implicit and procedural memory (motor tasks, coordination) remain fully intact. TGA has a mean symptom duration of 4-6 hours, resolves in the majority of cases within 8 hours, and resolves gradually and spontaneously within 24 hours without any medical intervention; critically, the diagnosis cannot be confirmed until symptoms have fully resolved. Diane's husband, a reliable witness, reports no preceding head trauma, no convulsive movements, tongue-biting, or incontinence, and no loss of consciousness - only sudden repetitive questioning during an emotionally charged argument, one of TGA's classic precipitants (reported in 18%-29% of cases), alongside intense physical exertion (25%-31%) and Valsalva-associated activities like swimming, sex, or abrupt temperature change. In the ED she is calm and cooperative, oriented to person and place (she knows her name, her husband, and that she is in a hospital) but uncertain of the exact time, and she repeats the same two or three questions every few minutes with no memory of having asked them before or of the physician's prior visits to the bedside. She has no headache, no dysarthria, no facial droop, no limb weakness or sensory change, no ataxia, no visual field deficit, and no aphasia - she speaks fluently and follows complex commands. Vitals and fingerstick glucose are normal. Competing diagnoses the team must actively exclude: posterior circulation stroke/TIA (which can also cause sudden amnesia via thalamic or hippocampal infarction, but should also produce other focal signs such as dysarthria, ataxia, or visual loss); a complex partial seizure with postictal amnesia (favored by a witnessed convulsion, tongue-biting, incontinence, known epilepsy, or a shorter, stuttering recovery); and a toxic-metabolic cause such as hypoglycemia or intoxication (excluded by normal glucose and no substance exposure). By the article's exclusion criteria, TGA is ruled out entirely if a patient has head trauma within the preceding 72 hours, a known seizure disorder, any other neurologic abnormality (motor deficit, aphasia, dysarthria, cognitive impairment, loss of attention span), or a known psychiatric disorder - Diane has none of these. Her amnesia should gradually and fully resolve over the next several hours of observation, at which point the diagnosis of classic, red-flag-negative TGA is confirmed; the evidence-based approach is a minimalist one - thorough witnessed history, serial neurologic exams to confirm no evolving focal deficit, and reassurance with observation - rather than reflexive stroke-alert activation, an emergent thrombolysis-pathway workup, or unnecessary medication, since overtesting is discouraged once red flags have been excluded.
criticalActions: Obtain a witnessed history of the amnesia episode's onset, duration, and any preceding trigger (emotional stress, physical exertion, Valsalva-type activity) from a family member or bystander
Perform a thorough neurologic exam to rule out focal deficits - motor weakness, aphasia, dysarthria, ataxia, visual field loss - since any such finding excludes TGA and mandates urgent stroke workup
Screen for and exclude head trauma within the preceding 72 hours, known epilepsy or seizure history, and psychiatric history, since any of these excludes the diagnosis of TGA
Check fingerstick glucose and consider basic toxic-metabolic screening to exclude alternative causes of acute amnesia
Confirm the patient remains oriented to person and place with preserved procedural memory, and observe for spontaneous, gradual resolution of the anterograde amnesia before finalizing the diagnosis
Avoid reflexive stroke-alert activation or unnecessary emergent intervention when the history and exam are classic and red-flag-negative; favor observation, safety, and reassurance
learningPoints: TGA is defined by acute, profound anterograde amnesia lasting up to 24 hours (mean 4-6 hours, most resolving within 8) with preserved orientation to person and place and no other neurologic deficits
Classic precipitants include emotional stress and intense physical exertion, plus Valsalva-associated activities such as swimming, sex, or sudden temperature change

Head trauma within 72 hours, known epilepsy, any other neurologic abnormality (motor deficit, aphasia, dysarthria, cognitive impairment, loss of attention span), or psychiatric history excludes the diagnosis of TGA and should prompt evaluation for stroke, seizure, or another cause
The diagnosis of TGA cannot be confirmed until symptoms have fully resolved; failure of the amnesia to resolve excludes the diagnosis
Once red flags are excluded, a minimalist approach - observation, ensuring safety, and reassuring the patient and family - is appropriate, avoiding overtesting and inappropriate intervention
cases.intro: Diane's husband flags down the triage nurse, worried - thirty minutes ago, mid-argument in the backyard, his wife suddenly stopped mid-sentence and asked him what they were doing outside. She's asked the same question at least six times since. She knows her name, she knows him, but she keeps asking how she got to the emergency department, as if hearing it for the first time each time.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She keeps asking the same thing over and over - she asked me three times in the waiting room what happened. She knows who I am, she just can't hold onto anything new.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I'm sorry, have we already talked? I don't understand - what am I doing here?

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her vitals have been stable since triage. She's calm, not in any pain, just keeps repeating herself every few minutes.

cases.title: Post-ROSC and unresponsive (the cooling clock already running)
cases.chiefComplaint: 57M - post-ROSC after out-of-hospital VF arrest during exertion, now comatose
cases.diagnosis: Postcardiac arrest syndrome (myocardial stunning, systemic ischemia-reperfusion injury, evolving brain injury) after out-of-hospital VF arrest from a suspected acute coronary occlusion - patient remains comatose, making him a candidate for moderate therapeutic hypothermia (target 32-33.9C)
cases.context: Ian is a 57-year-old man who was moving furniture at home when he developed sudden dyspnea and mild chest tightness, coughed violently, vomited once, gasped, and collapsed. Bystanders started CPR immediately; EMS arrived about 20 minutes later with CPR in progress, intubated him in the field, and found an initial rhythm of PEA. On arrival to the ED the monitor showed ventricular fibrillation; after two defibrillations plus resuscitation medications, organized QRS complexes returned with a palpable pulse - ROSC was achieved roughly 50 minutes after his collapse. He is now minutes post-ROSC: intubated, comatose, making no purposeful movement to voice or noxious stimulus, with a soft blood pressure (86/52), sinus tachycardia at 122, and a temperature already drifting up toward 37.9C. This is postcardiac arrest syndrome, which the resuscitation literature frames as four intertwined problems the team must manage at once: ongoing postcardiac arrest brain injury (ischemia-reperfusion cascades that continue for hours to days and represent the real treatment window, not a one-time event), postcardiac arrest myocardial dysfunction (stunning that produces exactly this kind of transient hypotension and can be mistaken for irreversible cardiogenic shock if not given time and support), a systemic ischemia-reperfusion response (vasoplegia, capillary leak, fever, coagulopathy), and the persistent precipitating pathology that caused the arrest in the first place - here, exertional chest tightness immediately before collapse points toward an acute coronary occlusion that has not yet been investigated. Because he remains comatose after ROSC from an initially shockable rhythm, he is a candidate for moderate therapeutic hypothermia targeting 32-33.9C; temperatures colder than 30C carry substantially more adverse effects without added benefit, so overshooting the cold end of the target is its own hazard, not just failing to cool at all. The correct path: reconfirm he is truly unresponsive, start active cooling toward 32-33.9C without delay since the brain-injury cascade is already running, obtain a 12-lead ECG and pursue urgent evaluation/treatment of an occlusive coronary lesion if found rather than treating ROSC as the finish line, and control shivering with adequate sedation and analgesia (adding neuromuscular blockade if needed) so he does not generate heat and drift off target - while periodically reassessing under that sedation so a silent re-arrest, seizure, or myoclonus is not mistaken for shivering or missed entirely under a uniformly deep sedation blanket. If mismanaged - sedation pushed so deep that a re-arrest or status myoclonus goes unnoticed, the coronary cause never investigated so the culprit lesion re-occludes, or shivering left untreated so core temperature never reaches target and instead climbs - expect recurrent hemodynamic instability, a second arrest, or accelerating secondary brain injury. Managed correctly, with prompt cooling, controlled shivering, the precipitating cause identified and treated, and hemodynamics supported through the myocardial stunning period, he has a real chance at neurologically intact survival.
criticalActions: Confirm candidacy: patient remains comatose (no purposeful response to voice or pain) after ROSC from an initially shockable rhythm
Initiate active cooling promptly toward a target of 32-33.9C rather than leaving him normothermic or hyperthermic
Obtain a 12-lead ECG and evaluate for an acute coronary occlusion as the precipitating cause, pursuing urgent cardiology/cath evaluation if found
Control shivering with adequate sedation and analgesia (adding paralysis if needed) rather than under-treating it and letting core temperature drift upward
Avoid over-sedating to the point that a re-arrest, seizure, or myoclonus cannot be recognized underneath it
Support blood pressure through the myocardial stunning period rather than attributing hypotension solely to sedation
learningPoints: Cardiac arrest survivors face 'postcardiac arrest syndrome' - a combination of brain injury, myocardial dysfunction, systemic ischemia-reperfusion injury, and the persistent underlying disease that caused the arrest.
Moderate therapeutic hypothermia targets 32-33.9C; temperatures below 30C carry substantially more adverse effects without added benefit.
Ischemic brain injury cascades continue for hours to days after ROSC, so the window to intervene with cooling is time-sensitive, not something to defer.
Restoring a pulse is only half the job - the cause of the arrest, often an acute coronary occlusion, must still be actively found and reversed alongside post-arrest stabilization.
Ongoing microvascular ischemia after ROSC reflects real, continuing injury rather than reperfusion noise, which is another reason to search hard for a treatable cardiac cause.
cases.intro: The resus bay is still humming from the code: an intubated man lies motionless on the gurney, wrists slack, pupils sluggish under the exam light, the monitor tracing a fast but organized rhythm. EMS is finishing their handoff while a nurse hangs a second liter and adjusts the ventilator tubing. Nobody in the room has spoken to him and gotten a response yet.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: We got him back after two shocks. Down about fifty minutes total counting our time on scene, initial rhythm was PEA and it converted to V-fib once we got rolling. He hasn't opened his eyes or moved on his own since the pulse came back.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: His pressure's been soft since he rolled in, I've got two lines running and the vent's set. He's not tracking, not moving to voice, and nothing purposeful when I did a sternal rub a minute ago.

speech.speaker: family
