speech.text: He said his chest felt tight right before he started coughing and went down. He was just moving the couch. He's never had any heart trouble before.

cases.article: 2015-10_Managing Delirium In The Emergency Department, Tools For Targeting Underlying Etiology.pdf
cases.title: Not just her dementia getting worse (elderly delirium from a UTI)
cases.chiefComplaint: 82F brought in by nursing home aide for acting confused
cases.diagnosis: Hypoactive delirium secondary to a urinary tract infection with early urosepsis physiology, superimposed on stable baseline mild vascular dementia
cases.context: Eleanor is an 82-year-old woman with a 2-year history of mild, stable vascular dementia (documented MoCA 22/30 at baseline) and hypertension, living with her daughter and attending a senior day program three times weekly. Per her daughter and the day-program aide who brought her in, Eleanor is normally oriented to her routine, recognizes family, converses appropriately, and was doing word-search puzzles and chatting normally as recently as yesterday afternoon. Over the past 18-24 hours she became progressively lethargic, harder to rouse, and tangential, drifting mid-sentence and mistaking the examiner for an old coworker; her attention wanders within seconds of redirection and fluctuates, tracking conversation appropriately one moment and staring blankly or dozing the next. This is the true diagnosis: a new hypoactive delirium precipitated by a urinary tract infection with early urosepsis physiology, not progression of her baseline dementia. Per DSM-5 as cited in the article, delirium is an acute (hours to days) and/or fluctuating disturbance of attention and cognition due to a medical condition — distinct from dementia, which declines gradually over months to years with attention relatively preserved until late stages. This acuity-of-onset and fluctuating-attention distinction, plus a systematic search for the underlying medical cause, is the article's central can't-miss teaching point, and missed-diagnosis rates for delirium in the ED run 54%-89%, largely because it gets misattributed to baseline dementia or psychiatric illness. Concrete findings: HR 104, BP 108/62 (relatively low against her home baseline of 150s/90s on antihypertensives), RR 20, T 38.4C, urine dipstick cloudy with positive leukocyte esterase and nitrites, WBC 15.8 with left shift, lactate mildly elevated at 2.1, creatinine 1.4 (baseline 0.9, prerenal from poor oral intake during the illness). Competing framings the learner must actively rule out: (1) family/staff anchoring on 'her dementia is just getting worse,' contradicted by the acute 24-hour timeline and fluctuating rather than stable attention; (2) primary psychiatric decompensation or 'sundowning' — the nursing home actually gave her an extra as-needed dose of quetiapine overnight for perceived agitation, a red herring that could be blamed for oversedation instead of being recognized as a reaction to an unrecognized underlying illness (toxin/medication effect being one of the article's two named delirium etiology categories, alongside infection-driven aberrant stress response); (3) dismissive 'she's just old' framing. If the UTI is missed and she is simply sedated or admitted for a 'dementia workup,' expect progressive tachycardia and hypotension, evolution to septic shock, and a real risk of permanent step-down in cognitive baseline, since the article emphasizes delirium's significant morbidity and mortality. The correct approach: recognize this as new delirium using a bedside screening tool such as the Confusion Assessment Method, systematically search for the underlying etiology (vitals, exam, UA/culture, CBC, BMP, medication reconciliation), start IV fluids and empiric antibiotics for the UTI, and manage any behavioral symptoms with nonpharmacologic reorientation first-line, reserving low-dose age-appropriate antipsychotics only for severe safety-threatening agitation.
criticalActions: Recognize fluctuating attention and acute onset as delirium, not simply worsening baseline dementia
Obtain collateral history from family/nursing home staff to establish the acute timeline against her known baseline
Apply a validated bedside screening tool such as the Confusion Assessment Method
Systematically work up the underlying etiology: vitals, exam, UA/urine culture, CBC, BMP, medication reconciliation
Treat the identified reversible cause promptly with IV fluids and empiric antibiotics for the UTI
Manage agitation with nonpharmacologic measures first-line, reserving low-dose antipsychotics for severe safety-threatening agitation
learningPoints: Delirium is defined by acute onset and/or fluctuating disturbance in attention and cognition, distinct from the gradual, attention-preserved decline of dementia
Missed diagnosis rates for delirium in the ED run 54%-89%, often because it is misattributed to baseline dementia or psychiatric illness
Delirium is a syndrome, not a diagnosis in itself - it signals an underlying medical condition such as infection, hypotension, hypoxia, or a toxin/medication effect that must be found and treated
Delirium has hypoactive, hyperactive, and mixed psychomotor subtypes; the quiet, lethargic hypoactive form is the one most easily overlooked at the bedside
Delirium carries significant morbidity and mortality - identifying and treating the underlying cause is the emergency, not sedating the behavior
cases.intro: The nursing home aide waves you over before you've even sat down: she's just not herself today, probably her dementia getting worse, but the nurses wanted her checked out. Eleanor sits slumped in the stretcher, eyes at half-mast, plucking at her blanket and answering questions from two sentences ago. Her chart lists hypertension and mild memory loss from a visit two years back, nothing more recent. The aide mentions Eleanor was fine and doing her puzzles at breakfast yesterday, but has been increasingly hard to rouse since this morning.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: You have my sister's eyes, you know. Did you bring the mail? I haven't seen the mail in days.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Vitals just came back, she's tachycardic and low grade febrile, one oh four over sixty two, temp thirty eight point four. Family said she was completely normal yesterday afternoon.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: Her dementia has never been this bad. Usually she knows exactly where she is and who I am. Last week she was doing her word search puzzles just fine.

cases.article: 2016-07_Ebola Virus Disease Epidemiology, Clinical Presentation, And Diagnostic And Therapeutic Modalities.pdf
cases.title: Fever in a returning traveler (the one you isolate before you touch)
cases.chiefComplaint: 29F — fever, myalgia, headache, vomiting and diarrhea x3 days; recent travel to Liberia
cases.diagnosis: Ebola virus disease (EVD) — Filoviridae, acquired during travel to an active West African outbreak region
cases.context: Aisha, 29, returned to the US 9 days ago after a 3-week visit to family outside Monrovia, Liberia, during the height of the West African Ebola virus disease (EVD) outbreak that the WHO declared a global public health emergency in 2014 — the largest, longest EVD epidemic to date, traced to an index case in Guinea. She will not volunteer exposure history unprompted, but on direct questioning she recalls attending a neighbor's funeral and helping care for a cousin who was 'very sick with fever and vomiting' in the days before she flew home. She became unwell 3 days ago with abrupt fever, severe diffuse myalgias/arthralgias, frontal headache, and profound malaise, followed over 24-48 hours by nausea, vomiting, and large-volume watery diarrhea. This morning she noticed blood-tinged saliva while brushing her teeth and dried blood in her right nostril — early, easily overlooked hemorrhagic signs. EVD (Filoviridae, an enveloped negative-sense RNA virus) is a severe multisystem disease with historically extreme mortality — case fatality up to 90% in young children in the 2014 epidemic — transmitted only through direct contact with the blood or body fluids (urine, saliva, vomit, sweat, semen, diarrhea) of a symptomatic person; casual contact does not transmit it, but an unprotected healthcare worker who examines or draws blood from an undiagnosed symptomatic patient is at real risk. Because early EVD is clinically indistinguishable from illnesses endemic to West Africa, the team must actively rule out malaria (thick/thin smear), typhoid (blood culture), and ordinary viral gastroenteritis or influenza — all plausible given her fever, myalgia, and diarrhea — without letting that workup delay isolation. The critical, easily-missed trap: epidemiologic risk-factor screening (recent travel to an affected country, or contact with a person with suspected/confirmed EVD) must happen at first contact/prescreening, before the patient is roomed, examined, or has blood drawn, so isolation and infection-control/public-health notification start immediately if positive — a routine exam and phlebotomy first risks unprotected staff exposure and nosocomial spread. If unrecognized, expect deterioration over hours to a day: worsening tachycardia, hypotension, tachypnea, cool mottled extremities with prolonged capillary refill, oliguria, and mucosal bleeding progressing toward multi-organ dysfunction and shock. Vitals on arrival: HR 122 sinus tachycardia, BP 96/58, RR 26, O2 93% room air, T 39.7C. She is ill-appearing, diaphoretic, lethargic but oriented, with dry mucous membranes and diffuse mild abdominal tenderness, no rash. The correct approach — travel/exposure screening at prescreening, immediate isolation with rigorous infection-control precautions and PPE, minimizing staff contact, activating the EVD evaluation pathway, and notifying public health — followed by supportive care while confirmatory testing is arranged — limits both her mortality risk and transmission risk to staff and other patients.
criticalActions: Ask about recent travel to an Ebola-affected country or contact with a symptomatic EVD patient at first contact, before physical exam
Isolate the patient immediately in a private room with rigorous infection-control precautions once travel/exposure risk factors are identified
Don appropriate PPE and minimize unnecessary staff contact before any hands-on exam or phlebotomy
Notify infection control and the public health department once EVD risk factors are identified
Actively screen for and rule out malaria, typhoid, and other endemic febrile illnesses without delaying isolation
Provide supportive care (fluid/electrolyte repletion, correction of coagulopathy) as the mainstay of EVD management
learningPoints: Early EVD is clinically nonspecific — fever, myalgia, headache, and GI symptoms mimic malaria, typhoid, and viral gastroenteritis.
Epidemiologic risk-factor screening (travel to an affected country or contact with a symptomatic EVD patient) must happen at prescreening, before exam or blood draw.
EVD spreads only through direct contact with the blood or body fluids of a symptomatic person, but unprotected healthcare workers are at high risk once that contact occurs.
Case fatality is extremely high — up to 90% in young children in the 2014 epidemic — making early recognition and supportive care time-critical.
Standardized tools such as WHO case definitions and the CDC clinical evaluation algorithm exist to guide the isolation and public-health notification decision.
cases.intro: The triage nurse waves you toward bay 4: 'Twenty-nine-year-old female, fever and body aches for three days, some vomiting and diarrhea overnight — looks like a bad flu, but she's pretty tachycardic.' Aisha is curled on her side under a blanket, sweaty and shivering, and mentions between breaths that she just got back from visiting her mother outside Monrovia a little over a week ago. Nobody has flagged the travel yet, and the room is set up like any other flu case — no mask, no gown, a family member sitting at the bedside holding her hand.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I feel like I got hit by a truck. My whole body aches and my head is pounding. I've been throwing up and it won't stop, and now it's coming out the other end too.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She was fine when she flew back from Liberia. This all started a few days after she got home. I figured it was just some bug from the water over there.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Temp's 39.7, heart rate's up around 120, pressure's a little soft. She hasn't wanted to eat or drink anything since yesterday.

cases.title: Fight bite over the knuckle (the one patients lie about)
cases.chiefComplaint: 27M — hand pain and swelling after "punching a wall" at a bar
cases.diagnosis: Occult clenched-fist ("fight bite") injury of the right 4th MCP joint — a human-tooth laceration that penetrated the extensor tendon and MCP joint capsule and inoculated it with oral flora, misrepresented by the patient as a wall-punch contusion
cases.context: Marcus, a 27-year-old man, arrives via EMS after a 911 call from a bar; paramedics report he 'drank way too much' and was swinging at people before the fight broke up. He is intoxicated with dysarthria, mild ataxia, and nystagmus, agitated and minimizing, insisting 'I punched a wall' and demanding discharge; hospital security has already been called for his combativeness. His right hand shows swelling over the 4th and 5th MCP joints with a deceptively small 5-mm puncture-like wound directly over the dorsal aspect of the 4th MCP joint, surrounded by only about 1 cm of erythema, minimal bleeding, and no gross deformity. Vitals are near-normal and reassuring — HR 96, BP 132/78, RR 16, O2 98% room air, T 37.0C — reinforcing the false sense that this is a trivial injury. The true diagnosis is an occult clenched-fist injury ('fight bite'): when Marcus struck another patron's mouth with a closed fist, the opponent's tooth lacerated the skin over the MCP joint and, because the fist was clenched at the moment of impact, also sliced through the extensor tendon and punctured the joint capsule, inoculating it with mixed oral flora. Once his hand is examined and imaged in the normal relaxed, open position, the three injured layers — skin, tendon, and joint capsule — retract independently and no longer line up, so the wound looks like an innocuous, shallow puncture rather than a joint-penetrating injury. Competing red herrings the learner must actively rule out: (1) the stated mechanism of 'punched a wall,' suggesting a simple contusion/abrasion needing nothing more than reassurance; (2) a superficial laceration requiring only irrigation, a tetanus update, and discharge; (3) attributing all his findings to alcohol intoxication and treating the hand as an incidental, low-priority finding while the team is preoccupied with his combativeness and neuro exam. Can't-miss teaching points drawn from the ACEP penetrating-extremity-trauma guideline: irrigate the wound under pressure, debride devitalized tissue, do NOT primarily close a bite-mechanism wound, obtain 3-view hand radiographs looking for a retained tooth fragment or fracture, update tetanus/Tdap, consider comorbidities and infection risk, and maintain a low threshold for hand surgery consultation — plus the key exam pearl of inspecting the hand with the fist re-clenched to trace the wound tract through all three tissue planes, since a resting-hand exam hides the joint capsule breach. If unrecognized and the patient is discharged after a tetanus shot and a bandage, expect progressive swelling spreading across the dorsum of the hand, pain out of proportion to exam, decreasing active MCP extension, fever climbing toward 38.3-38.5C and HR rising past 110 within 6-24 hours, purulent drainage, and evolution into septic arthritis and deep space infection that can destroy the joint and lead to permanent stiffness or digit loss. The correct approach — recognizing the fight-bite mechanism despite the patient's denial, examining/imaging in the position of injury, pressure irrigation, leaving the wound open, and urgent hand surgery consultation for possible joint exploration — prevents this cascade.
criticalActions: Actively screen for an occult clenched-fist (fight bite) injury whenever a knuckle wound is explained as 'I punched a wall,' rather than accepting the stated mechanism at face value
Re-examine and image the hand with the fist re-clenched to trace the wound tract, since skin, extensor tendon, and joint capsule injuries retract out of alignment when the hand is relaxed
Obtain 3-view hand radiographs to look for a retained tooth fragment or associated fracture
Irrigate the wound under pressure, debride devitalized tissue, and do not primarily close a bite-mechanism wound
Update tetanus/Tdap status and start antibiotics directed at oral flora
Maintain a low threshold for emergent hand surgery consultation given possible joint capsule and extensor tendon penetration
learningPoints: A knuckle wound after 'punching a wall' should always raise suspicion for an occult clenched-fist (fight bite) injury, even when the patient denies striking a person
Because the MCP joint is examined relaxed and open but injured with the fist clenched, skin, tendon, and joint capsule lacerations retract independently and can look deceptively minor on exam
The ACEP guideline for penetrating extremity trauma recommends pressure irrigation, debridement, individualized wound closure, tetanus updating, and a low threshold for hand surgery consultation
Open finger and hand wounds are among the most common diagnoses leading to medical malpractice litigation, largely driven by missed joint penetration and infection
Intoxication, agitation, and a patient's minimized or false history are classic setups for search-satisfying error, where the team stops looking once a plausible (but wrong) explanation is accepted
cases.intro: EMS wheels in a disheveled young man who reeks of beer, slurring his words as he tries to sit up on the stretcher. 'He was causing a scene at the bar, swinging at everybody, then suddenly he's holding his hand,' the medic reports, nodding at a small, almost unremarkable mark over the knuckle of his right ring finger. The patient waves you off: 'I'm fine, I punched a wall, that's all — I'm outta here.' Security has already been called for his combativeness, and the nurse mutters that this is probably just another drunk with a bruised hand.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I told you already, I punched the wall. I'm fine, just let me sign out.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's pretty agitated and smells strongly of alcohol, but his vitals are stable. There's a small mark over his knuckle he doesn't want me to touch.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's barely even bleeding. Can we hurry this up?

cases.title: Third-trimester RUQ pain that isn't her gallbladder (the one with barely-high blood pressure)
cases.chiefComplaint: 27F G1P0 at 34 weeks — RUQ/epigastric pain, headache, malaise x2 days
cases.diagnosis: HELLP syndrome (Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) — a life-threatening variant of preeclampsia, without seizures
cases.context: Danielle is a 27-year-old primigravida at 34+2 weeks gestation presenting with two days of worsening right-upper-quadrant and epigastric pain, nausea with one episode of vomiting, generalized malaise, and a dull frontal headache she blames on dehydration and poor sleep. Her pregnancy has otherwise been unremarkable with routine prenatal care; she has no known chronic hypertension, diabetes, or prior preeclampsia, but is a primigravida, a recognized risk factor. The true diagnosis is HELLP syndrome — a life-threatening obstetric complication generally considered a variant of preeclampsia, defined by hemolytic anemia, elevated liver enzymes, and low platelet count, and which can occur in late pregnancy or up to four weeks postpartum. Critically, her vitals sit deliberately in the diagnostic gray zone: BP 142/86 (systolic just above the >140 mm Hg threshold sometimes used, but diastolic below the >=90 mm Hg on two occasions or >110 single-reading definition of hypertension), HR 98, RR 18, O2 97% on room air, T 37.2C. Because the pressure is only borderline rather than the dramatic >160/110 of florid severe preeclampsia, this case is designed to be mistaken for gastritis or peptic disease, acute cholecystitis/biliary colic, or a nonspecific viral illness — plausible competing diagnoses the learner must actively work to exclude rather than anchoring on a benign GI cause. The can't-miss teaching point is that severe preeclampsia and its HELLP variant are defined by end-organ findings — right upper quadrant or epigastric pain, headache, visual disturbance, thrombocytopenia under 100,000, and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia — not by how dramatically the blood pressure reads; a mild or borderline pressure is not reassuring. If labs are drawn, they should show platelets 78,000/uL, AST 210 U/L and ALT 185 U/L, LDH 720 U/L with schistocytes on peripheral smear, and urine dipstick +2 protein. If unrecognized and the patient is discharged home or treated only for reflux or gallbladder disease, expect progressive RUQ pain from hepatic involvement, worsening thrombocytopenia toward DIC, and a real risk of rapid, unpredictable progression to an eclamptic seizure, placental abruption, hepatic rupture, or maternal/fetal death. The evidence-based path is early recognition of severe-preeclampsia-spectrum disease regardless of blood pressure severity, immediate CBC with platelets, LFTs, LDH, peripheral smear, and urine protein, emergent obstetric consultation, and expedited delivery planning — the literature notes delivery may be necessary in severe preeclampsia even at the cost of prematurity.
criticalActions: Recognize severe-preeclampsia-spectrum disease (RUQ/epigastric pain, headache, malaise) even when blood pressure is only mildly elevated or borderline
Order CBC with platelets, LFTs, and LDH with peripheral smear to evaluate for hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and thrombocytopenia
Check urine dipstick/protein for proteinuria to support a preeclampsia-spectrum diagnosis
Do not anchor on gastritis, cholecystitis, or viral illness before excluding preeclampsia/HELLP in a late-pregnancy patient
Obtain emergent obstetric consultation for expedited delivery planning
Monitor closely for rapid, unpredictable progression to eclampsia, hepatic complications, or DIC
learningPoints: HELLP syndrome is a life-threatening variant of preeclampsia defined by hemolysis, elevated liver enzymes, and platelets under 100,000 — not by how high the blood pressure reads.
Severe preeclampsia can present with only mild or borderline blood pressure elevation; RUQ/epigastric pain, headache, and visual changes are the red flags, not a dramatic BP number.
RUQ/epigastric pain, nausea, and malaise in the third trimester are easily mistaken for gastritis, gallbladder disease, or a viral syndrome — preeclampsia/HELLP must be actively excluded.
HELLP and preeclampsia can occur late in pregnancy or up to four weeks postpartum, so the diagnosis should stay on the differential even after delivery.
Progression from preeclampsia to eclampsia is unpredictable and can occur rapidly, so recognition and obstetric involvement should not wait for a 'classic' severe BP reading.
cases.intro: The triage nurse waves you over: 'Room 4, 34 weeks pregnant, says her right side has been aching for two days and she just can't shake this headache.' Danielle is curled slightly on her side, one hand pressed under her ribs, insisting it's probably just her gallbladder acting up like her sister's did during pregnancy. Her prenatal chart shows an unremarkable pregnancy so far, and her blood pressure on the triage sheet doesn't look particularly alarming — but she looks more uncomfortable than the numbers would suggest.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's like this burning, achy pain right under my ribs on the right side, it's been two days and now it's kind of crept up into my head too, I just feel wiped out.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her blood pressure's a little up but nothing crazy, one-forty-two over eighty-six, and she says she threw up once this morning.

cases.title: Ear pain that isn't the ear (the commonly-missed one)
cases.chiefComplaint: 71M — persistent right ear pain, unresponsive to two courses of antibiotics
cases.diagnosis: Referred otalgia from an occult head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (ear exam normal; pain referred via cranial nerves IX/X from a base-of-tongue/hypopharyngeal primary)
cases.context: Walter is a 71-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history and daily heavy alcohol use who presents with one month of constant, boring right ear pain that has failed two separate courses of treatment from his primary doctor — first oral antibiotics for presumed otitis media, then antibiotic-steroid otic drops for presumed otitis externa. Neither helped. Over the past week he has also noticed odynophagia (pain swallowing, now avoiding solid food), a 10-pound unintentional weight loss over the month, and a new raspy quality to his voice. The true diagnosis is referred otalgia: the ear is innervated by sensory afferents from cranial nerves V, VII, IX, and X plus the cervical plexus, so disease elsewhere in the head and neck can present as ear pain with a completely normal-looking ear — in one cited series, 28% of otalgia cases in non-ED settings were secondary/referred rather than primary ear disease. In Walter's case the referred pain (via the glossopharyngeal/vagal auricular branches) originates from an occult squamous cell carcinoma of the base of tongue or hypopharynx, which also explains his odynophagia, weight loss, and voice change. COMPETING DIAGNOSES the learner must actively rule out on exam: (1) otitis externa ("swimmer's ear") — but there is no canal erythema, edema, debris, or tenderness with tragal pull, and no swimming/pool exposure; (2) acute otitis media — but the tympanic membrane is intact, translucent, and mobile with no bulging or erythema; (3) TMJ dysfunction — he does have mild jaw clicking with chewing, but the pain is constant and disproportionate to jaw movement, unlike typical TMJ pain. CAN'T-MISS FINDINGS: the otoscopic exam is essentially normal despite severe reported pain (pain out of proportion to ear findings is itself the red flag); a firm, non-tender, roughly 2 cm right level II cervical lymph node is palpable on neck exam; oropharyngeal exam (best with a tongue depressor and good light, sometimes limited by gag) may reveal asymmetry or a subtle base-of-tongue/tonsillar fullness; he has no fever. Vitals: HR 88, BP 136/82, RR 16, room-air O2 95% (long smoking history), T 36.8C — he is hemodynamically stable and afebrile, which itself argues against an acute infectious ear process. DETERIORATION IF MISSED: if the clinician anchors on "another ear infection," prescribes a third round of antibiotics or drops, and discharges him to routine PCP follow-up, the underlying malignancy continues to grow undiagnosed — over subsequent weeks to months this can progress to bulkier nodal disease, local invasion, and eventual airway or swallowing compromise, and the window for a potentially curable early-stage resection or chemoradiation closes. CORRECT PATH: recognize that a normal ear exam does not explain his pain, take a thorough head and neck history (dysphagia, odynophagia, hoarseness, weight loss, tobacco/alcohol use), perform a full head and neck exam including oral cavity/oropharynx inspection and neck palpation for nodes, and arrange urgent otolaryngology referral for flexible laryngoscopy/endoscopy and biopsy rather than another empiric ear treatment course.
criticalActions: Recognize that a normal-appearing ear canal and tympanic membrane do not explain severe otalgia and should prompt a search for referred pain
Take a focused head and neck history: odynophagia, hoarseness, weight loss, tobacco and alcohol use
Perform a full head and neck exam beyond the ear — oral cavity/oropharynx inspection and neck palpation for lymphadenopathy
Work through the referred-otalgia differential (TMJ dysfunction, dental disease, parotitis, pharyngitis/tonsillitis, head and neck cancer) rather than anchoring on otitis externa or otitis media
Avoid prescribing a third empiric course of ear antibiotics/drops when exam findings do not support an ear infection
Arrange urgent otolaryngology referral for endoscopic evaluation and biopsy rather than routine primary care follow-up
learningPoints: Otalgia is often not an ear problem — in one cited study, 28% of ear pain cases in non-ED settings were due to referred (secondary) otalgia.

The ear is innervated by cranial nerves V, VII, IX, and X plus the cervical plexus, so TMJ disease, dental disease, parotitis, pharyngitis/tonsillitis, and head and neck cancer can all present as ear pain.

Persistent unilateral otalgia with an unremarkable otoscopic exam — especially in an older adult with a tobacco/alcohol history — is a red flag for occult head and neck cancer, not just another ear infection.
Pain that is out of proportion to ear exam findings should redirect the workup toward the rest of the head and neck rather than toward a repeat course of otic treatment.
Because significant ear and referred disease can be occult, a thorough exam is warranted in any patient with otalgia, including vulnerable populations who cannot reliably report symptoms.
cases.intro: Room 6, the nurse says, tapping the chart — right ear pain for about a month now, he's already been through a course of antibiotics and then a course of ear drops from his regular doctor, and neither one touched it. Walter sits hunched forward on the stretcher with one hand cupped over his right ear, wincing every time he swallows. His wife stands at the bedside with her arms crossed. He's stopped eating anything but soup and pudding this week, she says. And have you noticed his voice? It didn't used to sound like that.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's this ear, right here — burns all day, and it gets worse whenever I try to swallow, like it shoots right down into my jaw.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's been seen twice this month for the same complaint — different antibiotic, then ear drops — nothing's helped so far.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's lost weight without trying and his voice has gone raspy the last week or so. I keep telling him it's more than just his ear.

cases.title: Slumped with a paced rhythm (the spikes that stop capturing)
cases.chiefComplaint: 74F with a pacemaker — found unresponsive at home, bradycardic and hypotensive
cases.diagnosis: Pacemaker failure to capture — pacing spikes fire on schedule but intermittently fail to depolarize the myocardium, producing dissociated/absent QRS complexes and symptomatic bradycardia
cases.context: Dolores is a 74-year-old woman with a dual-chamber (DDD) pacemaker placed years ago for sinus node dysfunction. EMS broke down her apartment door for a welfare check and found her slumped on the couch, pale, diaphoretic, and intermittently confused. She is bradycardic at HR 42 and hypotensive at 86/40. The true diagnosis is pacemaker failure to capture: the generator is firing regularly (pacing artifacts appear at the expected programmed interval, proving the battery and timing circuit still work), but the myocardium is not reliably depolarizing in response — wide, irregular escape QRS complexes appear dissociated from the pacing spikes, and some spikes are followed by nothing at all (a pause with a pacing artifact alone). A prior ECG on file shows a normally captured paced rhythm with a consistent, narrower post-spike QRS, confirming this is a new problem, not her baseline. Failure to capture occurs when the stimulus reaches the myocardium but does not trigger depolarization; causes include lead dislodgement or fracture, a rising capture threshold from fibrosis at the lead tip (exit block), electrolyte derangement (hyperkalemia raises capture threshold and also distorts paced-complex morphology), or reduced generator output from battery depletion. Competing diagnoses to actively exclude: (1) vasovagal/reflex syncope — tempting given her age, but it does not explain regular pacing spikes dissociated from QRS on the monitor; (2) a rate-slowing medication effect (new beta-blocker, calcium-channel blocker, or digoxin) — this would simply trigger more pacing, not loss of capture, so it does not fit; (3) new device-unrelated native conduction disease — this would not explain a regularly firing, non-capturing spike train, which specifically implicates the pacing hardware itself. The clinching monitor findings are regular pacing artifacts at the programmed rate, pauses where a spike appears with no QRS following it, and wide escape complexes that come and go independently of spike timing — total dissociation between pacer spikes and QRS. Left unrecognized, she remains dependent on an unreliable escape rhythm; expect progressively longer pauses, worsening hypotension and hypoxia, and cardiac arrest. The correct approach mirrors any unstable bradycardia: apply pacer pads immediately and be ready for transcutaneous pacing with sedation as a bridge (the intrinsic device cannot be trusted), send a basic metabolic panel to check potassium and review the medication list, obtain a 12-lead ECG to compare against her prior paced tracing, and emergently involve cardiology/EP for urgent device interrogation (lead impedance, sensing, and capture thresholds) with probable transvenous pacing and lead revision or generator replacement.
criticalActions: Recognize failure to capture on the monitor — pacing spikes present but not consistently followed by a QRS
Treat as unstable bradycardia: apply pacer pads and prepare transcutaneous pacing as a bridge
Compare to a prior ECG to confirm this is a new loss of capture, not baseline
Check a basic metabolic panel for potassium and review medications that could worsen bradycardia or raise capture threshold
Emergently consult cardiology/EP for urgent pacemaker interrogation
Anticipate need for transvenous pacing and lead revision or generator replacement if transcutaneous pacing is inadequate
learningPoints: Failure to capture means the pacing artifact is present but not followed by a QRS — the device is firing but not depolarizing the myocardium.
A prior ECG showing a normally captured paced rhythm is the key comparison for identifying a new loss of capture.

An implanted pacemaker does not guarantee protection from life-threatening bradycardia — a malfunctioning device still needs standard unstable-bradycardia rescue with pacer pads and transcutaneous pacing.
Hyperkalemia can raise the capture threshold and distort paced-complex morphology, so check electrolytes in any patient with a pacing problem.
Causes of failure to capture include lead dislodgement or fracture, rising capture threshold (exit block), and battery depletion — all require device interrogation, not just resuscitation.

vitals.rhythm: Paced rhythm with intermittent failure to capture

cases.intro: EMS broke down Dolores's apartment door for a welfare check and found her slumped on the couch, barely rousable. Her daughter says she's had a pacemaker for years, so nobody worried when she got dizzy and 'not herself' the last couple of days. The cardiac monitor is chirping with an odd, irregular rhythm, and the nurse keeps glancing at it and frowning. Her pressure is low and she's cool and pale, but nothing on exam explains why.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's had that pacemaker for years, so I didn't think this could be her heart — she's just been so tired and dizzy the last couple of days.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Rate's sitting in the low 40s and she's hypotensive at 86 over 40. Something looks off with the spikes on the monitor, but I can't quite tell what.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I feel so tired... and dizzy... my chest feels funny.

cases.title: Grandma's pillbox toddler (the patch that keeps on dosing her)
cases.chiefComplaint: 18-month-old girl — hard to wake from an unusually long nap at grandmother's house
cases.diagnosis: Unintentional pediatric opioid toxicity from a discarded transdermal fentanyl patch (opioid toxidrome with respiratory depression)
cases.context: True diagnosis: unintentional opioid toxicity in an 18-month-old girl who mouthed a discarded, still-potent transdermal fentanyl patch belonging to her grandmother, who wears one on her upper arm for chronic back pain. Grandmother was babysitting and stepped away for roughly 45-60 minutes; only after repeated, specific questioning does she admit she had just changed her patch and, rather than folding the sticky side in and disposing of it as instructed, set the used one down in her purse along with her other pill bottles. Used fentanyl patches retain up to half their original drug load and remain dangerous. The child was found unusually quiet in her grandmother's lap and became progressively harder to rouse over the next 30-45 minutes. The easy-to-miss physical clue is a patch stuck to skin in an axillary, inguinal, or diaper-area fold, or torn adhesive fragments in her mouth. Pathophysiology: fentanyl is an extremely potent, highly lipophilic mu-opioid agonist; buccal or transdermal absorption in a small child produces profound CNS and respiratory depression, miosis, and hypoventilation wildly out of proportion to what looks like a trivial exposure -- the textbook 'one patch (or pill) can kill' pediatric scenario, since prescription opioids are the single drug class most often responsible for pediatric ingestion injury and death, ahead of sedative-hypnotics and cardiovascular drugs. Competing diagnoses to actively exclude: hypoglycemia (grandmother also carries a sulfonylurea for her own diabetes in the same purse -- check a fingerstick glucose immediately; it returns normal at 94), a sedative-hypnotic ingestion from that same purse, and occult trauma/non-accidental injury or an evolving CNS infection (she is afebrile, exam is otherwise unremarkable, no bruising, no bulging fontanelle, arguing against these). The can't-miss trap is the deceptively subtle early picture: vitals and exam can look like 'just a very sleepy toddler' rather than a critically poisoned one, and the caregiver's first history is vague and minimizing ('she wasn't given anything, I don't know what happened') until asked pointedly and repeatedly what medications she carries -- these histories are very often incomplete or withheld at first pass, not because of deception but because the caregiver hasn't connected the dots. Expected trajectory if unrecognized: worsening bradypnea and hypoxia progressing to apnea, hypoxic bradycardia, and cardiac arrest. Correct management reverses this: support ventilation/oxygenation first, give weight-based naloxone titrated to restore adequate breathing (not necessarily full arousal, since over-reversal can precipitate agitation/withdrawal), search the entire body (skin folds, mouth, diaper area) for and remove any adherent patch or pill fragments -- continued transdermal or buccal absorption will cause re-sedation even after a good naloxone response -- and admit for extended monitoring, since fentanyl's effect can outlast a single naloxone dose and repeat dosing or an infusion may be needed. Monitor numbers: HR 84 (mildly low for age), BP 78/48, RR 10 and shallow, SpO2 85% on room air, temp 35.9C, pupils 2mm and sluggish, responds to painful stimulus with a moan and withdrawal but does not open her eyes (roughly GCS 8-9). Fingerstick glucose 94. ECG shows sinus bradycardia without conduction abnormality.
criticalActions: Recognize the opioid toxidrome (miosis, bradypnea, CNS depression) rather than anchoring only on hypoglycemia, sepsis, or trauma
Check a fingerstick glucose immediately in any lethargic toddler with an unclear ingestion
Support airway and ventilation (bag-valve-mask as needed) before and while giving naloxone
Give weight-based naloxone titrated to restore adequate breathing, and anticipate needing repeat doses or an infusion
Search the child's skin, mouth, and diaper area for a retained transdermal patch or pill fragments and remove any source found
Ask the caregiver repeatedly and specifically about her own medications, and admit the child for extended monitoring given the risk of re-sedation
learningPoints: Opioids, sedative-hypnotics, and cardiovascular drugs are the prescription drug classes most responsible for pediatric ingestion injury and death.
A single adult-strength opioid dose -- or even a discarded, used transdermal fentanyl patch -- can be lethal to a toddler: the classic 'one pill can kill' scenario.
Caregiver histories in pediatric ingestions are often vague or incomplete at first; repeated, specific, nonjudgmental questioning is often what uncovers the actual agent.
Check glucose immediately in any lethargic toddler with a possible ingestion, since hypoglycemic agents are an important co-consideration and mimic.
Because opioids can outlast a single naloxone dose, anticipate re-sedation, search for and remove any retained source, and observe closely before discharge.
cases.intro: An 18-month-old girl is carried into the ED by her grandmother, who says she 'just can't wake her up' from what should have been a short nap. A half-open purse sits on the stretcher beside them, and the room smells faintly of adhesive and ointment. The girl moans and pulls away from a sternal rub but doesn't open her eyes, and her breathing looks slow and unusually quiet for a toddler. Her grandmother, visibly rattled, keeps insisting, 'she was fine an hour ago, I only stepped out for a few minutes.'

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She was fine an hour ago, I promise. I only left her with my purse for a few minutes while I answered the phone.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Resps are down around 10 and shallow, sat's mid-80s on room air, and she's barely rousable to a painful stimulus. Pupils look small. What do you want first?

cases.title: Swollen and shocky with clear lungs (right heart failure, not left)
cases.chiefComplaint: 54F — progressive leg swelling and shortness of breath, now dizzy and hypotensive
cases.diagnosis: Acute-on-chronic decompensated right heart failure (cor pulmonale) from abrupt discontinuation of home pulmonary vasodilator therapy for idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension, causing preload-dependent right ventricular failure and cardiogenic shock
cases.context: This is Denise Okafor, 54, with a three-year history of idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension maintained on home tadalafil and a low-dose loop diuretic, who lost pharmacy coverage roughly eight days ago and has been without her pulmonary vasodilator since. Per the article's central pearl, chronic pulmonary vasodilator therapy is the only disease-modifying treatment for pulmonary arterial hypertension, and abrupt discontinuation can rapidly precipitate acute-on-chronic right heart failure — exactly what is happening here. Rising pulmonary vascular resistance has driven right ventricular afterload up, right ventricular dilation, and now overt right ventricular failure with reduced cardiac output: four days of worsening dyspnea and bilateral leg edema culminating today in orthostatic dizziness and frank hypotension (88/58) with sinus tachycardia (118), tachypnea (26), and hypoxia (90% room air). Exam should show jugular venous distension to the angle of the jaw, tender hepatomegaly, 2+ pitting edema to the knees, cool distal extremities with a narrow pulse pressure, and — critically — clear lung fields, a pattern the article notes can suggest isolated right ventricular failure, though it lacks strong sensitivity/specificity since heart failure is often biventricular. This clear-lungs-but-swollen-and-hypotensive picture is the trap: it looks enough like typical "wet" left-sided ADHF/flash pulmonary edema that a reflexive learner reaches for nitroglycerin and aggressive furosemide, and enough like undifferentiated shock that another pushes large-volume IV fluid boluses. Both are dangerous. Because right and left ventricular function are coupled by ventricular interdependence (a shared septum and fixed pericardial space), a large fluid bolus meant to "support the pressure" will further distend an already-failing, preload-dependent right ventricle, bow the septum leftward, and impair left ventricular filling — dropping cardiac output further rather than raising it. Nitrates reflexively used for "CHF" can drop systemic and right coronary perfusion pressure in a patient already hypotensive. Competing diagnoses to actively exclude: septic shock (afebrile at 36.8C, no infectious source, though deranged liver enzymes from hepatic congestion could mimic cholangitis); volume overload from primary renal failure (creatinine is rising, but reflects cardiorenal syndrome from elevated central venous pressure impairing renal perfusion, not primary renal disease — withholding diuretics or giving fluids for presumed prerenal azotemia worsens congestion); and acute pulmonary embolism (tachycardic and hypoxic, but no unilateral leg swelling, pleuritic pain, or recent immobilization, and the gradual four-day course fits medication lapse better than acute clot). If mismanaged with fluid boluses, nitrates, aggressive diuresis, or unnecessary intubation with high PEEP (which the article warns increases right ventricular afterload and decreases preload), expect progressive hypotension, altered mentation, and cardiovascular collapse or arrest. If recognized and managed with cautious volume management, avoidance of nitrates and phenylephrine (raises pulmonary vascular resistance), vasopressor/inotropic support to preserve right coronary perfusion pressure and augment right ventricular contractility, bedside echocardiography confirming a dilated, hypokinetic right ventricle with septal flattening, and urgent pulmonary hypertension/cardiology involvement to restart vasodilator therapy, expect gradual improvement in blood pressure, heart rate, and mentation over the following hour.
criticalActions: Recognize preload-dependent right heart failure physiology and avoid empiric large-volume IV fluid boluses meant to treat the hypotension as undifferentiated shock
Avoid nitroglycerin and other vasodilators reflexively used for 'CHF', which can drop systemic and right coronary perfusion pressure in this hypotensive, preload-dependent patient
Obtain bedside or formal transthoracic echocardiography to assess right ventricular size and function and confirm right-sided rather than left-sided failure
Obtain a 12-lead ECG to evaluate for right ventricular strain and contributing ischemia

Avoid phenylephrine if a vasopressor is needed, since it raises pulmonary vascular resistance and worsens right ventricular afterload; avoid unnecessary intubation and high-PEEP positive pressure ventilation for the same reason

Identify the precipitant (lapse in home pulmonary vasodilator therapy), involve pulmonary hypertension/cardiology specialists urgently, and recognize rising creatinine as cardiorenal congestion rather than prerenal hypovolemia
learningPoints: Right heart failure produces the same congestive signs as left heart failure (JVD, edema, hepatomegaly) but clear lungs and a preload-dependent, afterload-sensitive physiology change the entire treatment calculus

Because the right and left ventricles share the interventricular septum and pericardium (ventricular interdependence), large fluid boluses that overdistend the right ventricle can bow the septum into the left ventricle and paradoxically lower cardiac output
Chronic pulmonary vasodilator therapy is disease-modifying in pulmonary arterial hypertension, and abrupt discontinuation can rapidly precipitate acute-on-chronic right heart failure
Phenylephrine and high-PEEP positive pressure ventilation both raise right ventricular afterload and should be avoided when possible in right heart failure

Rising creatinine in right heart failure often reflects cardiorenal syndrome from venous congestion rather than prerenal hypovolemia, so treatment should target congestion rather than reflexive fluid administration
cases.intro: Denise is sitting bolt upright on the stretcher, gripping the rails, her ankles ballooned to twice their size and spilling over the tops of her sneakers. The triage nurse reports four days of worsening breathlessness and swelling that finally brought her in when she felt lightheaded standing up this morning. Her neck veins look distended even from the doorway, but when you listen to her chest, the lungs sound surprisingly clear — not the crackly, waterlogged breath sounds you'd expect from someone this swollen and short of breath. Her blood pressure on the monitor is lower than her family expected, and they mention she stopped taking one of her pills a while back after a mix-up with her pharmacy.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Fifty-four-year-old female, four days of worsening leg swelling and shortness of breath, today she felt dizzy getting out of bed. Pressure's soft at 88 over 58, heart rate's up in the 110s, sat's 90 percent on room air. Lungs are clear on my listen, which surprised me given how swollen she is.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I just feel so puffed up and winded... standing up made my head spin, that's why we came in.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's got some kind of pressure problem with her lungs, she's had it for years and takes a pill for it, but her insurance dropped her prescription about a week ago and she never got it refilled.

cases.article: 2016-02_Ventriculoperitoneal Shunt Complications In Children An Evidence-Based Approach To Emergency Department Management PDF.pdf
cases.title: VP shunt malfunction in a child (the one everyone calls gastroenteritis)
cases.chiefComplaint: 6M with VP shunt, 2 days headache/vomiting, now lethargic
cases.diagnosis: Mechanical VP shunt malfunction (proximal catheter obstruction) causing acute obstructive hydrocephalus with rising intracranial pressure
cases.context: Eli is a 6-year-old boy with hydrocephalus from congenital aqueductal stenosis, diagnosed in infancy, for which a right parieto-occipital VP shunt (proximal ventricular catheter, one-way medium-pressure valve, reservoir, and distal catheter terminating in the peritoneal cavity) was placed at 4 months of age; he underwent a single shunt revision at 14 months of age, a short insertion-to-first-revision interval, which is a recognized marker of higher future complication risk, along with his young age. He now presents with 2 days of progressively worsening headache, three episodes of nonbloody, nonbilious vomiting overnight, and new lethargy — he is difficult to rouse and keeps his eyes closed in the exam room. The true diagnosis is mechanical VP shunt malfunction (most likely proximal catheter obstruction) causing acute obstructive hydrocephalus: CSF can no longer drain through the failed shunt, the ventricles are re-dilating, and ICP is climbing toward the threshold for herniation. Annual VP shunt malfunction rates run as high as 5%, and if this goes unrecognized, expect the classic evolution of rising ICP — worsening lethargy, a falling GCS, emerging anisocoria with a sluggishly reactive pupil, and eventually bradycardia with widened pulse pressure/hypertension and irregular respirations — progressing to herniation, apnea, and death (malfunction-related mortality has been reported as high as 1% to 2%). Because a viral illness is going around his school and he is afebrile, it is tempting to anchor on viral gastroenteritis; the family may also mention he's been eating less and hasn't stooled today, inviting a constipation work-up, and the headache alone could be framed as migraine. The critical trap the learner must avoid: reading a 'normal' head CT or shunt series in isolation. Ventricular size and shunt appearance can look deceptively unremarkable early in a malfunction, so any imaging obtained must be compared directly to Eli's own prior baseline shunt CT/series rather than judged as normal in a vacuum, and a reassuring-looking scan should never be used to defer neurosurgery consultation. The evidence-based approach is a complete shunt series (skull, chest, and abdominal radiographs to trace the catheter for disconnection, kinking, or migration) plus emergent head CT compared to baseline, with neurosurgery consulted early and in parallel with imaging rather than only after abnormalities are confirmed; lumbar puncture is contraindicated given the risk of herniation from unrecognized elevated ICP. On exam today he is intermittently arousable and mildly photophobic, with his old shunt scalp incision visible; fontanelle is closed at his age; pupils are currently 4mm and reactive bilaterally (not yet anisocoric); mild neck discomfort is positional rather than true meningismus; his abdomen is soft with no distension over the distal catheter tract; and there is no diarrhea, arguing against straightforward gastroenteritis. If the correct workup (imaging with baseline comparison, shunt series, early neurosurgery) is pursued, he goes emergently to the OR for shunt revision and recovers fully; if the encounter anchors on gastroenteritis, constipation, or migraine and he is discharged, expect progressive decline over the following hours to anisocoria, bradycardia, hypertension, decreasing responsiveness, and ultimately respiratory arrest from herniation.
criticalActions: Maintain high suspicion for shunt malfunction in any child with a VP shunt and headache, vomiting, or lethargy, even with a benign-appearing initial exam
Obtain emergent head CT and compare it directly to the patient's own baseline/prior shunt imaging rather than reading it as a stand-alone study
Order a complete shunt series (skull, chest, abdomen radiographs) to evaluate the catheter for disconnection, kinking, or migration
Consult neurosurgery emergently and in parallel with imaging rather than waiting for imaging to 'confirm' malfunction first

Recognize early signs of rising ICP (relative bradycardia, widening pulse pressure, irregular respirations, anisocoria, declining mental status) and prepare for emergent airway/ICP management if the patient deteriorates
Avoid lumbar puncture in a shunted child with signs of possible elevated intracranial pressure
learningPoints: VP shunt malfunction has an annual failure rate as high as 5%, and mechanical failure can present with nonspecific vomiting, headache, and lethargy that mimic viral gastroenteritis, migraine, or constipation
A normal-appearing CT or shunt series does not reliably exclude shunt malfunction; imaging must always be compared to the patient's own prior baseline study, not judged in isolation
Vomiting with a paradoxically normal-to-low heart rate in a shunted child, rather than the tachycardia expected from dehydration, can be an early sign of rising intracranial pressure
Risk factors for shunt complications include younger age, prematurity, more prior revisions, and a shorter interval from placement to first revision

Untreated shunt malfunction can progress to herniation and death, with malfunction-related mortality reported as high as 1% to 2%, so neurosurgery should be involved early rather than only after imaging 'proves' the diagnosis
cases.intro: Eli, a 6-year-old boy, is carried into the family triage bay by his mother, who says he's 'just not himself' — two days of headache, three episodes of vomiting overnight, and now he barely opens his eyes when she calls his name. She mentions, almost as an aside while filling out the intake sheet, that he has 'a shunt in his head' from when he was a baby. The triage nurse notes he's afebrile and not obviously in distress, and a resident wonders aloud whether this is just the stomach bug going around his class.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's been throwing up since yesterday morning, and last night he wouldn't even finish his favorite show, he just wanted to lie there with his eyes closed. Half his class has that stomach thing going around, so I figured it was just that, but he's never this quiet.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: His temp's normal and he's not tachycardic like the other kids we've seen today with the stomach bug, actually his heart rate's a little on the low side for how much he's been throwing up.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My head hurts... I don't want the light on.

cases.title: Fast and wide, but stable (the tempting drug trial)
cases.chiefComplaint: 64F — sudden palpitations while gardening, wide-complex tachycardia on monitor, vitals holding
cases.diagnosis: Monomorphic ventricular tachycardia (VT) arising from re-entry around a prior anterior-MI scar — NOT SVT with aberrant conduction or pre-excitation. Per the article's risk-stratification approach, any regular wide complex tachycardia (QRS >120ms, rate >100) must be treated as VT until proven otherwise, regardless of hemodynamic stability; the presence of structural heart disease (prior MI, reduced EF) makes VT the overwhelmingly likely diagnosis even though this patient looks well and is talking.
cases.context: Sharon is a 64-year-old woman with a prior anterior MI 3 years ago (drug-eluting stents to the LAD) and a known reduced ejection fraction (~35% on last echo) who developed sudden palpitations while gardening. She walked into triage herself, is alert and conversant, and denies chest pain, though she feels 'fluttery' and mildly short of breath. The monitor shows a regular wide complex tachycardia at 172 bpm with QRS ~150ms; BP, oxygenation, and mentation are all preserved — she is hemodynamically STABLE. The true diagnosis is monomorphic VT from a re-entrant circuit around her old infarct scar, the most common substrate for monomorphic VT described in the literature. Because she looks comfortable and is talking normally, the trap is assuming this is 'just SVT with a bundle branch block' or pre-excited SVT and reaching for an AV-nodal blocking agent (verapamil, diltiazem) to 'see if it converts.' The article's core teaching is explicit: VT should be the first rhythm considered in any wide complex tachycardia, since misdiagnosis leads to delayed or inappropriate therapy that can precipitate further hemodynamic compromise — stability at this moment does NOT rule out VT and does not make empiric AV-nodal blockade safe. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively rule out: (1) SVT with aberrant conduction (rate-related bundle branch block) — favored by the patient's calm affect, but her structural heart disease and regular monomorphic morphology argue strongly for VT; (2) SVT with pre-excitation (antidromic AVRT) — also dangerous with AV-nodal blockers; (3) atrial fibrillation/flutter with aberrancy — should be irregular, which this is not. Look for AV dissociation, fusion or capture beats, and a very wide/bizarre QRS with extreme axis — findings that favor VT over aberrancy. If a learner gives verapamil or another AV-nodal blocker, Sharon should deteriorate: BP dropping, worsening lightheadedness, and acceleration toward pulseless VT/arrest within minutes — mirroring exactly the kind of collapse the article warns can follow inappropriate pharmacologic treatment of misdiagnosed WCT. The correct evidence-based path: treat as VT — obtain a 12-lead ECG, place pads, get IV access, and since she remains stable, use a stable-VT antiarrhythmic (procainamide or amiodarone) rather than a nodal blocker, with synchronized cardioversion held in reserve if she decompensates at any point; obtain urgent cardiology consultation given her known ischemic cardiomyopathy and likely need for EP evaluation/ICD assessment.
criticalActions: Recognize regular wide complex tachycardia and treat it as VT until proven otherwise, even though the patient is hemodynamically stable
Obtain a 12-lead ECG and assess for AV dissociation, fusion/capture beats, and monomorphic morphology favoring VT
Ask about cardiac history (prior MI, reduced ejection fraction, structural heart disease) to raise pretest probability of VT
Avoid empiric AV-nodal-blocking agents such as verapamil or diltiazem given the risk of hemodynamic collapse if the rhythm is actually VT
Choose a stable-VT antiarrhythmic (procainamide or amiodarone) over a diagnostic drug trial, with pads placed and cardioversion ready if she decompensates
Consult cardiology given known ischemic cardiomyopathy and need for further arrhythmia workup
learningPoints: VT is the most common cause of regular wide complex tachycardia and should be assumed first, not last.
Hemodynamic stability does not rule out VT — a stable-appearing patient can still be in VT and can still deteriorate.
Misdiagnosing wide complex tachycardia and giving inappropriate therapy can precipitate further hemodynamic compromise.
Monomorphic VT most often arises from re-entry around a myocardial scar in patients with prior MI.
AV-nodal blocking agents like verapamil are dangerous 'diagnostic trials' in a wide complex tachycardia that turns out to be VT.
cases.intro: Sharon was pulling weeds in her garden when her heart suddenly took off, and now she's sitting upright on the stretcher, alert and chatting, one hand pressed to her chest. The monitor above her bed is beeping fast and steady, tracing wide, bizarre-looking complexes across the screen. She looks flushed but not distressed, and asks if this is 'just her heart racing again like it did once before.'

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It just started all of a sudden, like someone flipped a switch. I feel it fluttering in my chest and I'm a little out of breath, but I don't have any pain.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her pressure's holding fine and she's saturating well, but that rhythm on the monitor is definitely not normal. Wanted you to see it before we do anything.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I did have a heart attack a few years back, they put some stents in. Is that why my chest feels weird right now?

cases.title: The nursing home resident who's 'just not herself' (afebrile sepsis in the elderly)
cases.chiefComplaint: 81F — generalized weakness, poor appetite, and mild confusion x2 days, from assisted living
cases.diagnosis: Sepsis from aspiration pneumonia in a frail elderly patient, presenting afebrile without localizing respiratory complaints rather than with classic fever
cases.context: Ruth Whitfield is an 81-year-old woman brought from her assisted-living facility because staff say she is 'just not herself' — weak, barely eating, and mildly confused for the past two days. Her baseline is that she ambulates independently with a cane and is fully oriented. The true diagnosis is sepsis, most likely from an aspiration pneumonia (she has known mild dysphagia and a witnessed coughing fit with a meal three days ago), now progressing toward septic shock if unrecognized. The physiologic trap is that aging blunts the hypothalamic febrile response and cytokine-driven thermogenesis, so elderly and immunocompromised patients frequently mount little or no fever — and can even become hypothermic — despite a dysregulated, life-threatening response to infection; her temperature on arrival is only 36.2C. Her other vitals show the subtler, easy-to-miss indicators of organ dysfunction that this kind of presentation depends on: heart rate 104, blood pressure 98/58 (down from a documented baseline near 130/76), respiratory rate 24 (a compensatory response to evolving metabolic acidosis), and oxygen saturation 90% on room air. Labs show lactate 3.8 mmol/L, WBC 13,600 with 8% bands, and creatinine mildly above her baseline. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively work through and rule out: simple dehydration or urinary tract infection from poor oral intake (plausible given her vague symptoms, but does not explain the tachypnea, hypoxia, and elevated lactate); 'failure to thrive'/generalized weakness of aging or early dementia (a chart-closing label that is dangerously easy to reach for in a frail elderly patient with no fever); and a mild viral illness (undermined by the hypoxia, tachypnea, and lab abnormalities). The can't-miss teaching point is explicit: the absence of fever, or even a low temperature, must never be used to exclude sepsis in an elderly or immunocompromised patient — early recognition instead depends on actively screening for and weighing subtle clinical and laboratory indicators of organ dysfunction (altered mentation, tachypnea, hypotension relative to baseline, rising lactate, leukocytosis with bandemia) using a validated sepsis screening/scoring approach, not on waiting for a temperature spike. If this presentation is anchored on 'no fever, probably just dehydrated' and sent for oral fluids and observation, she is expected to deteriorate over the following hours into frank septic shock with worsening hypotension, rising lactate, and multi-organ dysfunction (renal, respiratory, and further mentation decline). The correct, evidence-based path — prompt recognition despite the absence of fever, obtaining lactate and blood/sputum cultures before antibiotics, hemodynamic optimization with IV crystalloid, early broad-spectrum antibiotics, and identifying and addressing the pulmonary infection source — averts this trajectory and stabilizes her.
criticalActions: Do not exclude sepsis based on a normal or low temperature in an elderly patient
Actively screen for subtle indicators of organ dysfunction (altered mentation, tachypnea, relative hypotension) despite the absence of fever
Obtain lactate and blood/sputum cultures before starting antibiotics
Begin IV fluid resuscitation for hemodynamic optimization and give broad-spectrum antibiotics promptly
Identify the infection source (e.g., chest exam/imaging for pneumonia) and pursue source control
Reassess perfusion and escalate to vasopressors if hypotension persists despite fluids
learningPoints: Elderly and immunocompromised patients often mount a blunted febrile response — a normal or low temperature must never be used to exclude sepsis.
Early sepsis can present with subtle clinical and laboratory indicators of organ dysfunction, such as altered mentation, tachypnea, and relative hypotension, rather than overt fever.
Validated screening and scoring for early recognition of sepsis matter most in patients with vague, nonspecific symptoms of infection.
Hemodynamic optimization, cultures and lactate before antibiotics, prompt broad-spectrum antibiotics, and infection source identification/control remain the core of early sepsis management.
Unrecognized atypical sepsis progresses to septic shock and multi-organ dysfunction, while early recognition and treatment change the outcome.
cases.intro: Ruth Whitfield is wheeled in from her assisted-living facility, slumped and drowsy, with staff saying she's 'just not herself' — barely eating, weak, and a little confused for two days. She has no fever, her vitals look almost unremarkable at a glance, and the aide who brought her in shrugs that she's probably just dehydrated or worn down.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's usually so sharp and gets around fine with her cane. The last couple days she just seems tired and out of it, hasn't wanted to eat much.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: No fever at triage, temp's actually a little low at 36.2. She's breathing a bit fast and her pressure's lower than what's charted from her last visit.

cases.title: The herald bleed after aortic surgery (the one that comes back for real)
cases.chiefComplaint: 74M, one episode of melena now resolved, history of AAA repair
cases.diagnosis: Secondary aortoenteric fistula (aortoduodenal) from prior open AAA graft repair, presenting as a herald bleed preceding impending massive upper GI hemorrhage
cases.context: Walter Higgins is a 74-year-old man who underwent open surgical repair of an infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm with a Dacron tube graft 4 years ago, now presenting after a single episode of dark, tarry, foul-smelling stool at home approximately 8 hours ago that has not recurred. He feels fatigued but denies current hematemesis, ongoing melena, abdominal pain, or lightheadedness, and his vitals on arrival are only mildly abnormal (HR 104, BP 108/68, otherwise unremarkable) - deceptively reassuring. The true diagnosis is a secondary aortoenteric fistula: the aortic graft has slowly eroded through the retroperitoneum into the adjacent third/fourth portion of the duodenum, creating a direct communication between the prosthetic graft and bowel lumen. The episode that brought him in is a classic herald bleed - a self-limited first hemorrhage from transient clot at the fistula tract - that portends a much larger, often fatal, secondary hemorrhage within hours to days if the graft-enteric communication is not identified and repaired before it ruptures catastrophically. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively rule out include peptic ulcer disease (no NSAID, aspirin, or anticoagulant use and no dyspepsia argue against this), a diverticular bleed (stool was melenic and tarry rather than bright-red hematochezia, and he has no history of diverticulosis), and hemorrhoids or another anorectal source (exam should show no hemorrhoidal bleeding, and the stool character points to a proximal, not distal, source). The critical, can't-miss teaching point is that any gastrointestinal bleeding, even a single minor and now-resolved episode, occurring in a patient with a history of aortic graft or endograft repair must be treated as an aortoenteric fistula until proven otherwise, no matter how reassuring the current vitals look, because the herald bleed is deceptive: patients often appear stable and get sent home, then return in extremis with exsanguinating hemorrhage and hemorrhagic shock, sometimes fatally before any diagnosis is made. If unrecognized, expect Walter to look stable for the next 30 to 90 minutes of the encounter, then abruptly develop massive hematemesis, hypotension in the 70s over 40s, reflex tachycardia above 130, and hemorrhagic shock requiring massive transfusion. If recognized early, the correct approach is emergent CT angiography of the abdomen and pelvis, immediate combined vascular surgery and GI or general surgery consultation for likely operative graft excision and bowel repair, two large-bore IVs with crystalloid resuscitation, type and crossmatch with transfusion readiness, and airway precautions given the risk of sudden massive hematemesis, mirroring the ABCs-first, IV-access-first approach to any high-risk upper GI bleed. Vital signs should stay only mildly deranged unless the learner delays escalation, at which point rapid deterioration into hemorrhagic shock should follow.
criticalActions: Specifically ask about any history of aortic aneurysm repair, aortic graft, or endograft in every patient with a GI bleed
Treat any GI bleed in a patient with a prior aortic graft as an aortoenteric fistula until proven otherwise, even if the bleeding has stopped and looks minor
Secure two large-bore IVs and begin early crystalloid resuscitation in anticipation of sudden decompensation
Prepare for airway protection and have suction ready given the risk of sudden massive hematemesis
Obtain emergent CT angiography of the abdomen and pelvis and simultaneously consult vascular surgery and GI or general surgery rather than deferring workup
Type and crossmatch blood and prepare for massive transfusion given the risk of catastrophic secondary hemorrhage
learningPoints: Aortoenteric fistula is an unusual but catastrophic cause of upper GI bleeding that must be immediately considered in any patient with a history of aortic endografting who presents with hematochezia or hematemesis
A herald bleed - a self-limited, deceptively minor bleed - classically precedes massive, often fatal hemorrhage in aortoenteric fistula, so apparent stability does not exclude the diagnosis
A benign-appearing GI bleed in a patient with a history of aortic graft surgery is a distinct high-risk category, not equivalent to a routine peptic ulcer or diverticular bleed

Standard upper GI bleed care - airway protection, large-bore IV access, early crystalloid resuscitation, and transfusion readiness - still applies while the aortoenteric fistula workup proceeds urgently
Delayed recognition converts a survivable herald bleed into fatal exsanguination, so index of suspicion rather than bleed severity should drive urgent imaging and surgical consultation
cases.intro: Walter is sitting up in bed looking mildly pale but conversational, sipping ice chips while his daughter hovers by the rail. The triage note says one episode of dark, tarry stool at home this morning that hasn't recurred, and his vitals look grossly stable. The nurse mentions almost in passing that his old chart shows 'some vascular surgery' a few years back, though the details are vague.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It was just the one bad trip to the bathroom, doc. Everything looks normal now. I feel fine, just a little tired.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He had that big aorta surgery, the belly one, maybe four years ago now. He's been doing great ever since, so we didn't think it could be related.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Vitals are basically stable, heart rate's just a touch fast, but he's talking to us fine. Wanted to flag him before things get busy.

cases.title: Dope sick in bed 12 (the COWS score that unlocks buprenorphine)
cases.chiefComplaint: 34M with 2 days of nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and body aches
cases.diagnosis: Moderate-to-severe opioid withdrawal (COWS approximately 13-14) in a patient with untreated opioid use disorder, a candidate for ED-initiated buprenorphine induction once objective withdrawal severity is confirmed, with warm handoff to outpatient addiction treatment
cases.context: Marcus is a 34-year-old man with 3 years of nonmedical opioid use that began after a workplace back injury when his oxycodone prescription was discontinued; he transitioned to buying pills and, more recently, street fentanyl. He was released from a 5-day county jail stay two mornings ago with no opioid access inside, and his last use before that was roughly 36 hours ago. He now has the classic noradrenergic-rebound picture of opioid withdrawal: once opioid receptors are no longer occupied, unchecked locus coeruleus output drives autonomic hyperactivity and diffuse GI/somatic symptoms that are miserable but not independently life-threatening. Exam findings support a Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale (COWS) score of about 13-14: dilated pupils (5mm), visible piloerection/gooseflesh on the forearms, rhinorrhea and lacrimation, restlessness and pacing, a fine hand tremor, repeated yawning, diffuse myalgias and joint aches, abdominal cramping with 3 episodes of vomiting and watery diarrhea today, and anxiety with irritability when asked to wait. Vitals show mild autonomic hyperactivity consistent with withdrawal (tachycardia, mild hypertension, low-grade temp) rather than sepsis or opioid toxicity. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively exclude: viral gastroenteritis/influenza (no true fever, no sick contacts, symptoms tied precisely to time since last opioid use rather than an infectious prodrome), a primary anxiety/panic disorder (restlessness and tachycardia present, but pupils are dilated with piloerection and rhinorrhea, not the picture of panic alone), and alcohol withdrawal (Marcus denies significant alcohol use, has no history of DTs/seizures, and his tremor/tachycardia resolve with the opioid-specific findings rather than alcohol-withdrawal timing). The two can't-miss traps drawn from this article: first, buprenorphine must not be dosed until withdrawal is objectively confirmed as at least moderate on a COWS-type assessment, because as a high-affinity partial agonist it will displace any residual full agonist still occupying receptors and can precipitate a severe, treatment-resistant withdrawal state if given too early or in a patient who is not yet in sufficient withdrawal; here, Marcus is roughly 36 hours out from his last use and his COWS is comfortably in the moderate-severe range, making him an appropriate induction candidate. Second, simply treating his nausea, diarrhea, and myalgias symptomatically (ondansetron, loperamide, clonidine, NSAIDs) and discharging him once he feels better addresses none of his underlying opioid use disorder; the article's own evidence shows ED-initiated buprenorphine produces meaningfully better engagement in ongoing addiction treatment than referral to treatment alone. If OUD is left unaddressed today, Marcus will return to opioid use with reduced tolerance (from his days in jail and now the ED stay), placing him at markedly elevated overdose risk given an illicit fentanyl supply. The evidence-based approach is COWS-confirmed buprenorphine induction in the ED plus a warm handoff — a direct connection to a bridge clinic, outpatient MOUD prescriber, or opioid treatment program before he leaves, rather than a printed referral sheet.
criticalActions: Recognize opioid withdrawal (not gastroenteritis, panic, or alcohol withdrawal) from the history and exam pattern
Calculate an objective COWS score before considering any buprenorphine dosing
Confirm at least moderate objective withdrawal severity is present before dosing buprenorphine, to avoid precipitating acute withdrawal
Initiate buprenorphine induction in the ED once withdrawal severity is objectively confirmed
Arrange a warm handoff with direct linkage to outpatient MOUD treatment before discharge, not just a referral sheet
Avoid discharging with only symptomatic treatment (antiemetics, antidiarrheals, clonidine) while leaving the underlying opioid use disorder unaddressed
learningPoints: Opioid withdrawal is a noradrenergic rebound phenomenon once opioid receptors are unoccupied, producing autonomic hyperactivity and diffuse GI/somatic symptoms that are miserable but rarely independently life-threatening.
The Clinical Opiate Withdrawal Scale (COWS) objectively grades withdrawal severity and should guide the timing of buprenorphine dosing rather than clinical impression alone.
Dosing buprenorphine before withdrawal is objectively established can precipitate acute, severe withdrawal, because its high receptor affinity displaces any residual full agonist.
ED-initiated buprenorphine for opioid use disorder produces better engagement in ongoing addiction treatment than referral to treatment alone.
Every ED encounter for opioid withdrawal is a chance to treat the underlying opioid use disorder, not just its symptoms; discharge should include a warm handoff to outpatient MOUD care.
cases.intro: Marcus is curled forward on the stretcher hugging his stomach, a bucket by his knee, restless and unable to sit still for more than a few seconds. The nurse notes he's vomited twice since triage and keeps rubbing his arms, which are covered in goosebumps despite the warm room. He tells you he feels like he has the worst flu of his life and just wants something to make it stop, glancing at the clock every few minutes.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's been in here twenty minutes, HR's been sitting in the 110s, and he's already asked me three times how much longer until he sees the doctor.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My stomach's killing me, I can't stop throwing up, and every joint in my body aches. I got out of county two days ago and I just feel like I'm crawling out of my skin.

speech.speaker: patient
