speech.text: I know what this is, I've felt it before. Can you just give me something so I don't have to feel like this anymore?

cases.article: 2016-04_Mammalian Bites In The Emergency Departmetn Recommendations For Wound Closure, Antibiotics, And Postexposure Prophylaxis.pdf
cases.title: The cat bite that 'barely broke skin' (creeping tenosynovitis)
cases.chiefComplaint: 25F — swollen, painful right index finger 2 days after a cat bite
cases.diagnosis: Pasteurella multocida septic flexor tenosynovitis of the right index finger following a cat-bite puncture wound to the hand
cases.context: Jenna is a 25-year-old woman who has been informally feeding a stray cat outside her apartment for several weeks. Two days ago, while offering it food by hand, the cat 'nipped' her right index finger on the volar surface near the proximal phalanx-MCP crease, leaving two tiny puncture marks that barely bled. She washed it with soap and water, put on a bandage, and did not seek care, assuming it was trivial — 'cats bite lightly, it's basically nothing' compared to a dog bite. Within 24 hours the finger became progressively swollen, red, and increasingly painful, and she developed a low-grade fever and malaise; she now presents 48 hours post-bite. The true process is a rapidly progressive Pasteurella multocida (and possibly P. septica) infection that has tracked from the tiny puncture wound into the flexor tendon sheath, producing early septic flexor tenosynovitis with early spread toward the deep palmar space. This is the central teaching point of the source article: cat bites, unlike the crushing/tearing wounds of dogs, are narrow, deep punctures from thin sharp teeth that inoculate oral flora directly into joints, tendon sheaths, and deep tissue planes while leaving a deceptively benign-looking, nearly closed skin entry point — wound size is a poor predictor of infection risk, and cat-bite wounds (concentrated on the hand, more common in women) become infected at a disproportionately high rate relative to how innocuous they look. Competing framings the learner must actively exclude: (1) simple superficial cellulitis that would resolve with oral antibiotics and no imaging or consult — wrong, because Kanavel signs indicate deep space/tendon sheath involvement; (2) a benign local or allergic reaction to the bite itself ('it's just swollen from the bite') — the patient may voice this framing, but fever, tachycardia, and Kanavel signs argue against it; (3) 'the wound looks fine so it must be fine' — the external puncture marks are small and nearly re-epithelialized, falsely reassuring the untrained eye. On exam, the finger is held in a slightly flexed, sausage-like fusiform posture, exquisitely tender along the flexor tendon sheath, with pain on passive extension — early Kanavel signs. Vitals show low-grade fever and mild tachycardia consistent with an early systemic response but not yet frank sepsis. If unrecognized (e.g., discharged with a narrow anti-staphylococcal antibiotic alone, no imaging, no surgical evaluation, and the wound closed with sutures), expect progression over 12-24 hours to worsening tenosynovitis, spread into the deep palmar/thenar space, abscess formation, tendon necrosis, and possible bacteremia requiring urgent operative washout and permanent loss of hand function. Correct management: leave the puncture wound open (do NOT primarily close it), obtain a hand x-ray to exclude retained tooth fragment/fracture/joint penetration, start empiric antibiotics with activity against Pasteurella (amoxicillin-clavulanate, not a narrow anti-staphylococcal agent), consult hand surgery for evaluation of tenosynovitis and possible operative drainage, update tetanus status, and assess the need for rabies PEP given the biting animal is an unobservable stray cat.
criticalActions: Recognize the small, near-closed puncture wound as high-risk despite its benign appearance — do not judge infection risk by wound size
Examine for Kanavel signs of flexor tenosynovitis (fusiform swelling, flexed resting posture, tenderness along the sheath, pain with passive extension)
Leave the puncture wound open — do not primarily close a bite-related puncture wound to the hand
Obtain a hand x-ray to assess for retained tooth fragment, fracture, or joint penetration
Start empiric antibiotics with anti-Pasteurella activity (amoxicillin-clavulanate), not a narrow anti-staphylococcal agent alone
Consult hand surgery emergently for possible operative washout, update tetanus status, and assess rabies PEP given the unobservable stray cat
learningPoints: Cat bites cause narrow, deep puncture wounds that inoculate bacteria into joints and tendon sheaths, so a tiny, nearly closed entry wound can hide a serious deep infection.
Puncture-type bite wounds, especially cat bites to the hand, should generally not be closed primarily because of their disproportionately high infection risk.
Pasteurella multocida (and P. septica) is the classic rapid-onset pathogen in cat-bite infections and requires an antibiotic active against it, such as amoxicillin-clavulanate.
Hand bites demand a deliberate check for Kanavel signs of flexor tenosynovitis, a surgical emergency requiring hand surgery consultation.
Every mammalian bite needs an explicit tetanus and rabies postexposure prophylaxis assessment based on the wound, immunization history, and the biting animal's availability for observation.
cases.intro: Bed 7 wanted to be triaged fast — she says it's silly to even be here. Jenna, 25, is cradling her right hand against her chest, a bandaid peeling off two tiny puncture marks on the palm side of her index finger. She says a stray cat she's been feeding for weeks nipped her two days ago, 'barely broke the skin.' Now the finger is swollen like a sausage and she can barely bend it without wincing, but she keeps insisting it's probably nothing.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's honestly such a small bite, I don't even know why it's this swollen. I already washed it and put a bandaid on, it should be healing by now.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her temp's up a little and that finger looks pretty tense to me — she says it's worse today than yesterday, not better.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I really don't want to make a big deal out of this, I just need something for the swelling so I can get back to work.

cases.title: Fever after Puerto Rico (the defervescence trap)
cases.chiefComplaint: 29F — fever and malaise 4 days after returning from Puerto Rico
cases.diagnosis: Dengue fever with warning signs (impending dengue hemorrhagic fever/dengue shock syndrome) — WHO 'dengue with warning signs' category
cases.context: Marisol is a 29-year-old woman who returned 4 days ago from a week-long family visit to Puerto Rico, where local health authorities are mid-outbreak of dengue transmitted by Aedes aegypti mosquitoes. She developed abrupt high fever, severe retro-orbital headache, diffuse myalgias/arthralgias ('breakbone' pain), and a faint macular rash on day 1 of illness — the classic dengue prodrome of primary or secondary DENV infection. Today, day 4-5 of illness, her fever has just begun to defervesce (down from 39.4C at triage to 38.9C now), but rather than improving she feels worse: increasing fatigue, one episode of vomiting, and vague diffuse abdominal discomfort she is minimizing. This defervescence-with-clinical-worsening pattern is the textbook entry into dengue's critical phase, when increased capillary permeability causes plasma leakage into the pleural and peritoneal spaces, hemoconcentration, and risk of shock; it is easily mistaken for 'the virus finally breaking' and discharged home. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively exclude: influenza/nonspecific viral syndrome (would not produce a rash, positive tourniquet test, or thrombocytopenia), Plasmodium falciparum malaria (also a classic endemic-travel fever, but there is no active malaria transmission in Puerto Rico, and a blood smear/rapid antigen test would be negative), and typhoid/enteric fever (which classically produces sustained fever with relative bradycardia rather than dengue's tachycardia and hemoconcentration). On exam, faint petechiae are visible on the forearm distal to where the blood pressure cuff sat, and a formal tourniquet test (cuff inflated midway between systolic and diastolic pressure for 5 minutes) would be positive with more than 20 petechiae per square inch, reflecting increased capillary fragility. Labs reveal thrombocytopenia (platelets 78,000/uL and trending down) and leukopenia, with hematocrit trending upward from her baseline — hemoconcentration signaling early plasma leakage. The patient, unaware of any of this, is specifically requesting ibuprofen or aspirin for her headache; giving either drug in the setting of dengue-associated thrombocytopenia and capillary fragility risks precipitating major hemorrhage and must be avoided (acetaminophen only). If warning signs (persistent vomiting, worsening abdominal pain, mucosal or gum bleeding, lethargy or restlessness, rapid platelet fall with rising hematocrit) are missed and she is discharged with NSAIDs, expect progression over the next 24-48 hours to overt plasma leakage (pleural effusion, ascites), spontaneous mucosal or GI hemorrhage, narrowing pulse pressure, and dengue shock syndrome. The evidence-based approach — recognizing WHO warning signs, avoiding NSAIDs/aspirin, initiating cautious isotonic crystalloid resuscitation, and admitting for serial hematocrit, platelet, and vital-sign monitoring — prevents this deterioration and reflects the WHO dengue guidance referenced in this review.
criticalActions: Obtain a detailed travel and exposure history including destination, dates, and mosquito exposure
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin for fever or pain control given thrombocytopenia and bleeding risk
Perform a tourniquet test and examine for petechiae or mucosal bleeding
Order CBC with platelet count and trend hematocrit to detect hemoconcentration from plasma leakage
Recognize WHO warning signs (abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, lethargy, bleeding) as mandating admission and close monitoring
Initiate cautious isotonic IV fluid resuscitation and admit for serial hematocrit, platelet, and vital sign monitoring
learningPoints: Dengue is the most rapidly spreading mosquito-borne viral illness worldwide and should be considered in any febrile returning traveler.
The critical phase of dengue often begins as fever defervesces — clinical worsening at this point is a red flag, not reassurance.
NSAIDs and aspirin are contraindicated in suspected dengue because of thrombocytopenia and bleeding risk.
WHO warning signs (abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, rising hematocrit with falling platelets) mandate hospital admission for monitoring.
No pathognomonic findings exist for mosquito-borne illness, so a thorough travel history is essential to differentiate dengue from malaria and other travel-related fevers.
cases.intro: The nurse waves you over before you've even reached the doorway: she's spiked twice since triage, and a bunch of little red dots turned up under the blood pressure cuff on the recheck. In bed 4, a young woman lies curled on her side with a damp washcloth on her forehead, wincing every so often. She says she just got back from visiting family and just wants something for the fever and headache before she has to be back at work Monday.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I got back from Puerto Rico four days ago and I've just felt awful since — fevers, this awful headache behind my eyes, and my whole body aches like I ran a marathon.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her temp's actually down half a degree from triage, but she says she feels worse than before, more tired, and she threw up once out in the hallway.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Can I just get some ibuprofen or aspirin for this headache before I head home? That's usually all I take.

cases.title: Diverticulitis that isn't 'the usual' (the perforated one)
cases.chiefComplaint: 61F — LLQ abdominal pain, 'same as my diverticulitis last year'
cases.diagnosis: Perforated sigmoid diverticulitis with contained pelvic abscess and evolving peritonitis (complicated diverticulitis, modified Hinchey Ib–II trending toward free perforation) — not simple recurrent uncomplicated diverticulitis
cases.context: Denise is a 61-year-old woman with known sigmoid diverticulosis and one prior episode of uncomplicated diverticulitis eight months ago that resolved with a short course of oral antibiotics as an outpatient. Her risk factors are typical for diverticular disease: a low-fiber Western diet, obesity (BMI 33), a sedentary lifestyle, and intermittent NSAID use for chronic knee pain. She now presents with roughly 36 hours of progressively worsening left lower quadrant pain, subjective fevers at home, and mild nausea, and — anchored on her prior benign course — she is convinced this is 'the same thing' and wants a phone-in antibiotic prescription so she can go home. In truth, what began as a recurrent diverticular flare has silently progressed: a diverticulum in the sigmoid colon underwent a walled-off microperforation that has partially failed containment, producing a roughly 5 cm pelvic abscess adjacent to thickened, inflamed sigmoid colon, with a tiny focus of extraluminal free air and a small amount of free pelvic fluid on imaging — complicated diverticulitis trending toward free perforation and diffuse peritonitis if not intercepted. This is fundamentally different from her prior uncomplicated episode, which by current evidence can often be managed with oral or even no antibiotics as an outpatient in reliable, non-toxic patients. Competing framings a learner may be tempted to accept: (1) simple recurrent uncomplicated diverticulitis warranting the same oral-antibiotic-and-discharge plan as last time; (2) constipation with fecal loading, given her low-fiber diet and mild distension; (3) viral gastroenteritis, given the nausea and low-grade fever. Each is plausible on first glance but wrong. Findings that must not be explained away: heart rate persistently 110s–120s despite antipyretics and fluids, temperature 38.9°C, an exam where focal left lower quadrant tenderness has, on careful serial exam, spread toward voluntary guarding and mild rebound rather than staying localized, WBC 19,500/µL with a left shift, markedly elevated CRP, and a lactate of 3.2 mmol/L. These are not the exam and labs of 'the same thing as last time.' The correct pathway is CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast to characterize the abscess and exclude free perforation, IV fluid resuscitation, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam, or ceftriaxone plus metronidazole), urgent surgical consultation, and admission — with interventional radiology drainage likely needed for the abscess, and the surgical team on standby should she develop diffuse peritonitis, free air, or hemodynamic instability, at which point she would need emergent operative source control (Hartmann's procedure or similar). If mismanaged as outpatient uncomplicated disease with oral antibiotics and discharge, expect progressive abdominal pain, spreading peritonitis, worsening tachycardia and hypotension, rising lactate, and septic shock within 24–48 hours — a preventable death from a disease that, correctly triaged, has an excellent prognosis.
criticalActions: Do not anchor on the patient's prior benign episode — reassess with a full exam and vitals rather than accepting 'same as last time'
Recognize peritoneal signs (spreading guarding/rebound), persistent tachycardia, and fever as red flags that mandate escalation beyond the uncomplicated-diverticulitis pathway
Obtain CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast to differentiate uncomplicated from complicated diverticulitis before considering outpatient management
Start IV fluid resuscitation and IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone plus metronidazole) rather than oral antibiotics
Obtain emergent surgical consultation given abscess, extraluminal free air, and evolving peritonitis
Admit the patient and arrange interventional radiology drainage or operative source control rather than discharging home
learningPoints: Diverticulitis most commonly arises in the sigmoid (left) colon in Western patients, from perforation of a diverticulum through the colonic wall.

Uncomplicated diverticulitis in reliable, non-toxic patients can often be managed with oral or even no antibiotics as an outpatient — but that pathway depends entirely on first excluding complicated disease.

Complicated diverticulitis (abscess, free perforation, diffuse peritonitis, obstruction, or fistula) is a different disease requiring CT confirmation, IV antibiotics, and surgical consultation, not the outpatient oral-antibiotic pathway.

A presentation that 'feels like' a prior benign episode can mask silent progression to perforation; persistent tachycardia, spreading peritoneal signs, and an elevated lactate should never be dismissed just because the patient says it's the same as last time.
CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast is the imaging modality of choice to distinguish uncomplicated from complicated diverticulitis and to guide whether percutaneous drainage or surgery is needed.
cases.intro: The nurse waves you into bay 7: '61-year-old, left lower belly pain since yesterday, says she had this exact thing eight months ago and the pills fixed her right up.' The patient is curled on her side, wincing as she shifts, insisting she just needs 'the same antibiotics as last time' so she can get home to her dog. Her forehead is damp, and the monitor is chirping a heart rate that doesn't quite match how calm she's trying to sound.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's just like before, honestly. I had this same pain last year and some pills took care of it in a few days.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Temp's 38.9, heart rate's up in the 110s, and she flinched pretty hard when I pressed on her belly just now, more than I'd expect.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I really don't think I need to be admitted. Can't you just call in a prescription like last time?

cases.title: The 'ear infection' that won't quit (diabetic, 2 weeks of drops)
cases.chiefComplaint: 70M with diabetes, worsening left ear pain and drainage x2 weeks despite ear drops
cases.diagnosis: Necrotizing (malignant) otitis externa — Pseudomonas aeruginosa skull-base osteomyelitis extending from the external auditory canal in a diabetic host
cases.context: Walter is a 70-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (home glucose logs running 250-300s) who presents with 2 weeks of progressively worsening left-sided otalgia and otorrhea despite an appropriate course of ciprofloxacin/dexamethasone otic drops prescribed for what looked like straightforward otitis externa. The true diagnosis is necrotizing (malignant) otitis externa: an invasive Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection that has spread beyond the external auditory canal into adjacent soft tissue, cartilage, and skull-base bone (temporal bone osteomyelitis), driven by his diabetic microvascular disease and impaired local immunity. This is a true emergency distinct from simple swimmer's ear — left untreated, infection tracks along the fissures of Santorini and the tympanomastoid suture into the skull base, threatening the facial nerve as it exits the stylomastoid foramen, the jugular foramen (CN IX-XI), the hypoglossal canal (CN XII), the sigmoid sinus, and ultimately the meninges/brain. Learners must actively exclude three tempting mimics: (1) simple otitis externa — would be expected to improve on topical drops within days, not worsen after two full weeks of therapy; (2) acute otitis media/mastoiditis — the tympanic membrane here is intact, not bulging or perforated, and pain is centered on the canal itself rather than post-auricular; (3) TMJ dysfunction — his pain does radiate toward the jaw/TMJ, a classic red herring, but true TMJ pain is reproducible with jaw movement and palpation of the joint and does not come with fever, otorrhea, or canal findings. The can't-miss exam finding is granulation tissue visible at the osseocartilaginous (bony-cartilaginous) junction of the external auditory canal, with the canal itself macerated, edematous, and exquisitely tender — pain out of proportion to the modest-appearing external findings is the hallmark. He is febrile to 38.3C; other vitals are near-normal but trending toward mild tachycardia from pain and fever. A careful cranial nerve exam is essential: early disease has no cranial neuropathy, but if unrecognized, expect facial nerve (CN VII) palsy first (most common), then lower cranial nerve palsies (IX, X, XI, XII) as osteomyelitis extends toward the jugular foramen, and ultimately signs of intracranial extension (worsening headache, altered mental status, meningismus) if care is delayed — his daughter's report of new overnight confusion should raise concern for exactly this trajectory. The evidence-based approach is CT of the temporal bone to assess bony erosion (MRI if intracranial extension is suspected), inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP, typically markedly elevated and useful for trending response to therapy), tight glucose control, and prompt initiation of IV antipseudomonal antibiotics — not simply more topical drops — plus emergent ENT consultation for admission, possible biopsy/culture to confirm the organism and exclude malignancy, and long-term antibiotic planning.
criticalActions: Recognize necrotizing otitis externa risk in a diabetic patient with otitis externa that fails topical therapy after an appropriate trial
Perform a full cranial nerve exam, specifically screening for facial nerve (CN VII) palsy and lower cranial nerve involvement
Obtain CT temporal bone imaging to evaluate for bony erosion/skull-base osteomyelitis, adding MRI if intracranial extension is suspected
Send inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP), blood glucose, and blood cultures rather than treating as simple otitis externa
Start IV antipseudomonal antibiotics rather than continuing topical otic drops alone
Obtain emergent ENT consultation for admission and definitive management
learningPoints: Suspect necrotizing otitis externa in any diabetic or immunocompromised patient with otitis externa that fails to improve on topical therapy
Granulation tissue at the osseocartilaginous junction of the ear canal, with pain out of proportion to exam, is the classic can't-miss finding
Pseudomonas aeruginosa is the usual causative organism, and management requires IV antipseudomonal antibiotics, not topical drops alone
New cranial neuropathy, especially facial nerve palsy, signals skull-base spread and a worse prognosis
Advanced imaging (CT temporal bone ± MRI) and ENT consultation are indicated whenever this diagnosis is clinically suspected
cases.intro: The nurse waves you toward bed 4 before you're even through the doors: a diabetic gentleman back for the third time this month with what everyone keeps calling 'that same ear infection,' except the drops his primary doctor gave him two weeks ago haven't touched it. He's holding his head tilted, wincing every time anyone gets near his left ear, and his daughter is hovering by the bedside looking more worried than the chief complaint would suggest.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He's diabetic, sugars have been running high at home he says. Temp's up to 38.3, and he would not let me touch the outside of that left ear, guarded it like crazy.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's this same ear thing, doc. I've been putting the drops in just like they told me, but it keeps getting worse, and now the pain shoots right up into my jaw and my head.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He didn't want to come back in, he's stubborn like that, but last night on the phone I could barely understand what he was saying and he never complains about pain this much.

cases.title: The ground-level fall with the deceptively clean FAST
cases.chiefComplaint: 78F found on kitchen floor after a mechanical fall, left hip/pelvic pain
cases.diagnosis: Unstable pelvic ring fracture with ongoing retroperitoneal venous hemorrhage causing hemorrhagic shock, with a falsely reassuring negative FAST exam
cases.context: Doris Kaminski is a 78-year-old woman on chronic metoprolol for hypertension who was found by her daughter on the kitchen floor after what witnesses describe as a simple mechanical slip-and-fall from standing height onto her left hip, striking the hardwood directly. This is a deliberately low-energy mechanism, mirroring the elderly ground-level-fall case in the source article, chosen because pelvic ring disruption and clinically significant hemorrhage are not reserved for high-speed motor vehicle or motorcycle trauma; even standing-height falls in older patients with osteoporotic bone can disrupt the pelvic ring and lacerate the presacral venous plexus, producing large-volume retroperitoneal bleeding. The true diagnosis is an unstable pelvic fracture (lateral-compression or vertical-shear pattern) with ongoing retroperitoneal venous hemorrhage; because the retroperitoneum lies outside the four windows FAST interrogates (perihepatic, perisplenic, pelvic/pouch of Douglas, pericardial), the FAST exam performed in the trauma bay is negative for free fluid. Learners must resist three false-reassurance traps: (1) the negative FAST being read as no significant bleeding rather than as evidence that only excludes a hemoperitoneum source; (2) her borderline blood pressure being attributed to her home metoprolol dose or to a benign geriatric baseline rather than to evolving hemorrhagic shock; and (3) the injury being framed as an isolated orthopedic left hip fracture rather than a pelvic ring injury with major hemorrhage potential. On arrival she is anxious but conversant, with left flank and pelvic ecchymosis, tenderness and gross instability to gentle anteroposterior and lateral pelvic compression, and a shortened, externally rotated left leg. Vitals show early compensated shock: HR 112, BP 92/58, RR 20, O2 95% on room air, low-grade temp 36.2C. Per the article, EAST guidelines make a Level I recommendation that FAST is not sensitive enough to exclude an intraperitoneal or retroperitoneal hemorrhage source, and a Level II recommendation that pelvic fracture pattern alone is unreliable for predicting need for angioembolization, mortality, or hemorrhage - hemodynamic trend and exam findings must drive management, not fracture morphology or a clean FAST. If unrecognized, expect her tachycardia and hypotension to worsen, mental status to decline, and pelvic girth/hematoma to expand as she exsanguinates into the retroperitoneal space over the next 30-60 minutes toward cardiac arrest. The correct evidence-based path: apply a pelvic binder or wrapped sheet at the level of the greater trochanters immediately to reduce pelvic volume and tamponade venous bleeding, obtain a pelvic x-ray and CT (or CT angiography) rather than trusting the negative FAST, secure large-bore IV access with crystalloid and early blood product availability, and involve trauma surgery, orthopedics, and interventional radiology early for possible angioembolization or preperitoneal pelvic packing if she remains unstable.
criticalActions: Apply a pelvic binder or circumferentially wrapped sheet at the level of the greater trochanters immediately given exam findings of pelvic instability
Do not use a negative FAST exam to exclude pelvic or retroperitoneal hemorrhage as the source of hypotension
Obtain pelvic x-ray and CT (or CT angiography) to evaluate for retroperitoneal hematoma and arterial extravasation rather than relying on FAST alone
Treat persistent tachycardia and borderline hypotension as evolving hemorrhagic shock rather than attributing them to home beta-blocker use or baseline geriatric vitals
Activate trauma surgery, orthopedics, and interventional radiology early for a hemodynamically unstable pelvic fracture
Reassess vitals and perfusion serially and escalate to blood products/OR pelvic packing if instability persists despite binder placement
learningPoints: FAST has good specificity for pelvic-fracture patients but EAST gives a Level I recommendation that it is not sensitive enough to exclude an intraperitoneal or retroperitoneal hemorrhage source - a negative FAST cannot rule out life-threatening pelvic bleeding
Retroperitoneal hemorrhage occurs outside the four standard FAST windows, so significant pelvic venous or arterial bleeding can be entirely occult on ultrasound
Clinically significant pelvic ring fractures with major hemorrhage can result from low-energy, standing-height falls in older adults, not only high-energy mechanisms

EAST gives a Level II recommendation that pelvic fracture pattern (e.g., Young-Burgess classification) alone is unreliable for predicting need for angioembolization, mortality, or hemorrhage - hemodynamic status should guide management
Relative hypotension in an elderly patient on a home beta-blocker can mask or be misattributed, delaying recognition of hemorrhagic shock
cases.intro: Doris, 78, is wheeled back on a backboard after her daughter found her on the kitchen floor, unable to get up. She says she just slipped reaching for a mug and landed on her left hip, and insists she feels fine other than the pain, worried mostly about who will feed her cat tonight. The paramedic mentions her pressure has run a little low since pickup, but she takes a pill for her blood pressure every morning, so no one is especially alarmed yet.

speech.speaker: medic
speech.text: Found down about twenty minutes, ground-level fall onto her left side, no loss of consciousness per the daughter. Pressure was ninety over sixty in the truck, heart rate around one-ten, she takes a blood pressure pill so we figured that was just her normal.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It just hurts so much right here in my hip and my backside when you press on it. I heard a bit of a crack when I went down. I do feel a little cold and shaky, but I always feel that way when I get worked up.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her pressure just rechecked ninety-two over fifty-eight, heart rate one-twelve. Pelvis looks bruised on the left and it is tender and a little wobbly when I press in on the hips. FAST looked clean to me, no free fluid anywhere.

cases.title: Renal colic with a fever (the obstructed, infected one)
cases.chiefComplaint: 67F with left flank pain, fever, and hematuria
cases.diagnosis: Obstructing left ureteral stone with an infected, obstructed collecting system (pyonephrosis) - a urologic emergency requiring emergent decompression (ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy) and IV antibiotics, not outpatient management of uncomplicated renal colic
cases.context: The patient is a 67-year-old woman with a history of recurrent urinary tract infections and two prior symptomatic kidney stones (both passed spontaneously) who presents with 8 hours of left flank pain radiating toward the groin, plus two days of fevers, shaking chills, and dysuria she wrote off as 'another bladder infection.' The true diagnosis is an obstructing left ureteral stone causing an infected, obstructed collecting system (pyonephrosis): the stone behaves as a struvite ('infection') calculus, formed from ammonia and alkaline urine generated by urease-splitting bacteria (e.g. Proteus, Klebsiella) that have colonized her upper urinary tract, and those same organisms are now proliferating behind a near-complete obstruction with nowhere to drain, producing infected urine under pressure. This is a true urologic emergency: pus trapped behind an obstructed system can progress to urosepsis and irreversible loss of renal function within hours, and IV antibiotics alone cannot adequately penetrate an obstructed, purulent collecting system - the obstruction must be mechanically relieved. Vitals show fever (39.3C), tachycardia (HR 118) that does not settle with analgesia, and borderline hypotension (96/58); unlike the tachycardia of straightforward colic, which trends down once pain is controlled, these findings must not be dismissed as 'pain plus a viral bug.' Competing diagnoses the learner must actively work through: (1) uncomplicated renal colic - she has a stone history and colicky flank pain, and it is tempting to treat with NSAIDs/opioids and discharge home with a urine strainer and outpatient urology follow-up, which is correct ONLY when there is no fever and the system can still drain; (2) pyelonephritis without obstruction - her UTI history and dysuria make this plausible, and IV antibiotics alone would be correct if imaging showed no obstructing stone or hydronephrosis, but that is not the case here; (3) musculoskeletal back pain - she is older with chronic aches and easy to anchor on a benign explanation, the same trap that led to a missed diagnosis in a similarly vague back-pain patient elsewhere in this literature. Labs should show leukocytosis (WBC about 17,000) with a left shift, elevated lactate (about 2.8), pyuria and bacteriuria on UA, and blood on urine dip - hematuria is nonspecific and its presence or absence neither confirms nor excludes a stone. Noncontrast CT abdomen/pelvis, the diagnostic study of choice, reveals a 7mm obstructing left ureteral stone with moderate-to-severe hydronephrosis and perinephric stranding. If managed like routine colic (analgesia, IV fluids, discharge), she will deteriorate into overt septic shock with permanent nephron loss; the evidence-based path is emergent urology consultation for decompression (stent or percutaneous nephrostomy), blood and urine cultures drawn before antibiotics, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics, and admission, with combined opioid-plus-NSAID analgesia for comfort - recognizing that aggressive IV hydration does not hasten stone passage and is not a substitute for source control. Life-threatening mimics of renal colic (AAA, ovarian torsion, appendicitis) should also be actively considered and excluded.
criticalActions: Obtain emergent noncontrast CT abdomen/pelvis to confirm the obstructing stone and grade hydronephrosis
Recognize fever and persistent tachycardia with an obstructing stone as an infected, obstructed system - not uncomplicated colic
Send urinalysis, urine culture, blood cultures, and lactate, and start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics promptly
Emergently consult urology for decompression (ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy) rather than planning outpatient stone management
Treat pain with combined opioid plus NSAID therapy rather than relying on aggressive IV hydration to pass the stone
Actively exclude AAA, ovarian torsion, and appendicitis as life-threatening mimics of renal colic
learningPoints: Fever with an obstructing ureteral stone signals an infected, obstructed collecting system (pyonephrosis) - a true urologic emergency, not routine renal colic
Noncontrast CT is the imaging modality of choice for suspected urinary stones; ultrasound is preferred in pregnant patients and children
Hematuria has low specificity - many inflammatory and infectious conditions cause it, and its absence does not exclude a stone
Struvite ('infection') stones form from ammonia and alkaline urine generated by urease-producing bacteria colonizing the urinary tract, linking certain stones directly to infection and obstruction
Combining opioids with NSAIDs is the optimal evidence-based analgesic regimen, while aggressive IV hydration has not been shown to speed stone passage
cases.intro: Bed 14, curtain half-drawn. Dorothy, 67, is curled on her side with one hand pressed into her left flank, insisting this happens to her all the time and she just needs something for the pain so she can go home. The triage nurse says her temperature just came back hot and her heart rate hasn't settled despite two rounds of pain medicine. A family member hovers near the doorway, adding that she seemed fine yesterday aside from some burning when she used the bathroom.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I get kidney stones all the time, this is nothing new, just give me something for the pain and let me go home.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her temp just came back at 102.8, and she's still tachycardic even after the morphine.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's had a couple of bladder infections this year too, but she never wants to make a big deal out of anything.

cases.title: New stroke in sickle cell disease (the one hiding inside 'another pain crisis')
cases.chiefComplaint: 7M with known sickle cell disease (HbSS) - 'another pain crisis,' now with slurred speech and a dragging right foot
cases.diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke from sickle cell cerebral vasculopathy in a child with homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS)
cases.context: The true diagnosis is an acute ischemic stroke from sickle cell cerebral vasculopathy, not a routine vaso-occlusive pain crisis. Marcus has homozygous HbSS, the most severe SCD genotype, with a baseline hemoglobin around 7.5 g/dL, two prior admissions for extremity/back pain treated with opioids and IV fluids, no chronic transfusion program, and no transcranial Doppler screening on record. Unlike the microvascular sickling that produces ordinary pain crises, chronic HbS polymerization injures the endothelium of the large intracranial arteries (distal ICA, proximal MCA/ACA), producing progressive intimal hyperplasia and stenosis; today, on top of a viral URI, this produced an acute large-vessel occlusion and cerebral infarction. Two days ago Marcus had a runny nose and low-grade fevers, which his mother blames for today's presentation. This morning he complained of his usual bilateral leg and low back pain, took his home oxycodone, and napped; on waking his speech was slurred and he was dragging his right foot, which the family and triage nurse are framing as 'he's just tired from the crisis and his cold.' Competing framings the learner must actively rule out: (1) a routine vaso-occlusive crisis with opioid sedation explaining the grogginess - wrong, because the facial droop and unilateral weakness are new focal findings, not global sedation; (2) viral illness/dehydration causing lethargy - wrong, because the deficit is lateralized and reproducible on exam, not simply low energy; (3) hypoglycemia from poor oral intake - a fingerstick should be checked but does not explain facial asymmetry. The article's central can't-miss teaching is that any new neurologic symptom in a patient with SCD is stroke until proven otherwise and must trigger emergent evaluation, not be folded into the routine pain-crisis plan. It also stresses that hemoglobin electrophoresis is unreliable for confirming SCD genotype if the patient was transfused within the prior 90 days, so transfusion history must always be obtained, and that transfusion therapy in SCD - used to prevent and treat strokes as well as acute chest syndrome and priapism - targets reducing HbS to below 30%, which is why simple top-up transfusion is not equivalent to definitive treatment. On exam: right facial droop, right arm and leg strength 3/5, mild expressive slurring, alert but frightened, oriented. Vitals: HR 118, BP 102/62, RR 24, T 37.8C, SpO2 96% on room air. Labs pending show Hgb 6.8 g/dL and reticulocytes 9%, close to his hemolytic baseline rather than a dramatic acute drop, which is a trap - normal-ish labs do not rule out stroke. If this is treated only as a pain crisis with opioids and fluids, the infarct territory will progressively expand over hours with worsening hemiparesis and lasting disability; prompt non-contrast CT to exclude hemorrhage, STAT hematology/blood bank activation for emergent exchange transfusion, and neurology involvement halt progression and reflect the evidence-based, age-tailored stroke strategy the article emphasizes.
criticalActions: Recognize a new focal neurologic deficit in an SCD patient as stroke, not sedation or fatigue from a pain crisis
Obtain emergent non-contrast head CT to exclude hemorrhage before other stroke-directed therapy
Send STAT CBC, reticulocyte count, and type & crossmatch, and ask about any transfusion in the last 90 days
Activate hematology/blood bank emergently for exchange transfusion targeting HbS below 30%, not a simple top-up transfusion
Consult neurology and arrange MRI/MRA once stabilized to define the infarct and underlying vasculopathy
Avoid hypoxia, hypotension, and over-sedation that can worsen cerebral ischemia
learningPoints: New neurologic symptoms in a patient with sickle cell disease are stroke until proven otherwise, not just fatigue from a pain crisis.
Stroke in SCD arises from a distinct large-vessel intracranial vasculopathy, different from the microvascular vaso-occlusion that causes ordinary pain crises.
The definitive emergency treatment for acute stroke in SCD is exchange transfusion to reduce HbS below 30%, not a routine simple transfusion.
Hemoglobin electrophoresis cannot reliably confirm SCD genotype if the patient has been transfused within the prior 90 days.
Optimal stroke management strategies in SCD are age-dependent, so care must be tailored rather than applying one protocol to every age group.
cases.intro: The triage nurse waves you over: Room 4, seven-year-old with sickle cell, mom says it's another pain crisis, but something about his face looks off to her. Marcus is curled on the stretcher clutching his legs, groggy from the oxycodone he took at home after his nap, while his mother insists he's just worn out from the same crisis as always on top of the cold he's had all week. When you ask him to smile, one side of his mouth barely moves, and his right foot drags as the tech helps him sit up.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Mom says he's having another pain crisis like usual, but she mentioned his speech has sounded strange since he woke up from his nap, so I wanted you to see him first.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He gets these crises all the time, I gave him his pain medicine at home just like we always do, he's just extra sleepy today because of his cold and the pain, that's all.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My leg... it won't listen to me... and my mouth feels funny when I talk.

cases.title: The 'ankle sprain' that isn't (adolescent growth-plate trap)
cases.chiefComplaint: 13F, twisted ankle playing soccer, refusing to bear weight
cases.diagnosis: Juvenile Tillaux fracture — Salter-Harris III avulsion fracture of the anterolateral distal tibial epiphysis from an external-rotation mechanism, occurring in the narrow adolescent window as the distal tibial growth plate fuses asymmetrically (central and medial physis close first, leaving the anterolateral segment open and vulnerable); requires CT to measure articular displacement and orthopedic evaluation for possible operative fixation.
cases.context: Maya is 13 years old, right at the narrow developmental window when the distal tibial growth plate fuses asymmetrically: the central physis closes first, then the medial side, leaving the anterolateral physeal segment open and mechanically vulnerable for roughly the last 12-18 months before skeletal maturity. She was playing club soccer and planted her right foot to cut around a defender when her cleat caught in the turf; her body rotated externally over a fixed foot, an external-rotation mechanism that avulses the anterolateral distal tibial epiphysis where the anterior inferior tibiofibular ligament attaches, producing a juvenile Tillaux fracture (a Salter-Harris III injury). She heard a pop, could not continue playing, and has limped since. On cursory exam this looks like any other adolescent 'rolled ankle': there is diffuse lateral swelling and ecchymosis, and if you only palpate the anterior talofibular ligament and lateral malleolus she will wince there too, encouraging an anchoring diagnosis of a simple lateral ankle sprain. A distal fibula buckle/torus fracture is another plausible red herring given her age and mechanism, and 'growing pains' should be dismissed outright since this is an acute traumatic injury with focal findings, not a nonspecific nocturnal ache. The finding that should redirect the exam is point tenderness localizing specifically to the anterolateral joint line just above and lateral to the ankle mortise — over the distal tibial physis itself, not over the fibular ligaments — plus pain with passive external rotation of the foot. Standard 3-view ankle radiographs (AP, lateral, mortise) can look deceptively unremarkable or show only a subtle, easily-dismissed fracture line at the anterolateral epiphysis; if plain films are equivocal or show any lucency there, dedicated CT of the ankle is required to fully characterize the fracture plane(s) and measure articular step-off/displacement, since this determines whether the fracture can be treated with strict non-weight-bearing casting (displacement <2mm) or needs open reduction internal fixation (displacement >=2-3mm or articular incongruity). Because this is a growth-plate fracture that crosses the articular surface, it needs orthopedic evaluation regardless of exact management — not the routine sprain algorithm of RICE, an air-splint or ankle brace, and self-directed return to activity. If misread as a sprain and sent home weight-bearing in a walking boot, the fragment can displace further, healing with articular step-off, malunion, and premature post-traumatic ankle arthritis; growth arrest is a lesser risk here than in younger children since the physis is nearly closed, but chronic ankle dysfunction from articular incongruity is a real long-term consequence. Vitals are normal — this is an isolated orthopedic injury, not a systemic process.
criticalActions: Palpate specifically over the anterolateral joint line/distal tibial physis, not just the lateral ligaments, to localize point tenderness
Obtain a dedicated 3-view ankle series (AP, lateral, mortise) rather than assuming a normal-looking film excludes fracture
Order CT ankle when exam or plain films raise concern for a growth-plate/epiphyseal fracture to characterize the fracture plane and measure articular displacement
Splint non-weight-bearing and keep the patient strictly non-weight-bearing rather than dispensing a walking boot or ankle brace
Consult orthopedics for evaluation of possible operative fixation given articular surface involvement
Recognize the adolescent transitional age window (partially closed growth plate) as the risk factor that distinguishes this from a routine sprain or buckle fracture
learningPoints: In younger children growth plates and bones are weaker than ligaments, so ankle trauma more often fractures the physis than sprains it; as adolescents approach skeletal maturity, ligamentous sprains become relatively more common

Juvenile Tillaux and triplane fractures occur only in the narrow adolescent window when the distal tibial physis is fusing asymmetrically, because the anterolateral segment is the last part to close and stays vulnerable to external rotation
These fractures classically mimic a routine ankle sprain and are easy to miss on cursory exam and even on standard plain films
Point tenderness over the anterolateral joint line/distal tibial physis, not the lateral ligaments, is the exam clue that should redirect suspicion away from a simple sprain
Because these fractures involve the articular surface, they need orthopedic evaluation, often CT for surgical planning, and possible operative fixation to avoid malunion and chronic ankle dysfunction
cases.intro: Maya, 13, is wheeled back from triage with her right ankle wrapped around a bag of crushed ice, wincing every time the footrest bumps it. Her mother trails behind with a soccer bag and a worried look, telling the triage nurse she just rolled it going for the ball and it's probably just a sprain like her sister had last year. The ankle is swollen and bruised along the outside, and Maya won't put any weight on it, but nothing looks obviously deformed at first glance.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: She's been non-weight-bearing since it happened about two hours ago, vitals are stable, and she's rating the pain a six out of ten.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I planted my foot to cut around a girl and my cleat just stuck in the grass. I felt something pop and then I couldn't run on it anymore.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: Her older sister sprained her ankle almost the exact same way last season and was fine within a week, so I'm guessing this is the same thing.

cases.title: Ski-crash facial fracture airway (the one you must predict, not react to)
cases.chiefComplaint: 52M — facial trauma after high-speed ski collision, declining mental status, desaturating
cases.diagnosis: Le Fort II/III maxillofacial fracture ('dish-face' deformity) from a high-speed skiing collision, with trismus, mobile/unstable midface, bilateral periorbital hematomas, and positional desaturation — an anatomically and physiologically predicted difficult airway requiring anticipated failed-airway/CICO preparation before the first intubation attempt
cases.context: Dean is a 52-year-old male snowboarder who struck a tree at high speed on a black-diamond run and was found unresponsive for roughly one minute by ski patrol before regaining a GCS of 14, now trending down to 11 over the past 20 minutes. He presents with a classic 'dish-face' deformity — a flattened, retruded midface — with bilateral periorbital ecchymosis and lid edema so severe his eyes are nearly swollen shut, brisk epistaxis with blood and secretions pooling in the oropharynx, and trismus limiting mouth opening to roughly 1.5 cm. On palpation the maxilla is mobile and 'free-floating' relative to the rest of the facial skeleton, consistent with a Le Fort II/III fracture pattern. He also has a short, thick neck (large circumference, BMI approximately 33) and cannot tolerate supine positioning — his oxygen saturation drops into the low 80s within seconds of lying flat and recovers only when propped upright, a positional/physiologically difficult airway layered on top of his anatomic predictors. Vitals: HR 128, BP 108/68, RR 28, SpO2 84% on non-rebreather while upright (drops further supine), T 36.3C. He has no known medical history, takes no medications, and was previously healthy. This is an anatomically AND physiologically difficult airway: trismus, midface instability (poor mask seal, unpredictable anatomy, active bleeding into the airway), and positional desaturation all predict a difficult first attempt and raise the risk of a cannot-intubate-cannot-oxygenate (CICO) event if the team proceeds straight to a routine RSI. Pitfalls to avoid: (1) pushing induction/paralytic before a deliberate difficult-airway assessment (mouth opening, thyromental distance, neck mobility, obstruction) is done and verbalized; (2) attempting the first laryngoscopy without a video laryngoscope, a supraglottic airway, and an opened surgical/cricothyrotomy kit already at the bedside, and without the most experienced operator present; (3) inadequate preoxygenation — forcing the patient supine or skipping apneic oxygenation/upright positioning before the attempt, which will drop his saturation precipitously given his baseline desaturation pattern. Closed-claims and NAP4 data cited in the source guideline show the deadliest failures are not the difficult airway itself but the response to it: failure to plan ahead, perseverating on a failed technique instead of bridging with a supraglottic airway, and delaying the call for a surgical airway once CICO develops — most airway deaths and brain injuries occur outside the OR, in exactly this kind of unanticipated-until-too-late trauma presentation, and notably in patients who otherwise looked low-risk. If the learner anticipates the difficult airway, assembles backup devices and a surgical airway plan, optimizes positioning/preoxygenation, and calls for the most experienced airway operator before the first attempt, the airway can be secured safely with immediate escalation to front-of-neck access if CICO occurs. If unprepared, the team faces a failed first attempt with bleeding obscuring the view, an unrecognized CICO scenario, hypoxic brain injury, and a scramble for a scalpel with no kit open.
criticalActions: Perform a systematic difficult-airway assessment (mouth opening, thyromental distance, neck mobility, obstruction) before pushing induction drugs
Assemble and open backup airway equipment (video laryngoscope, supraglottic airway, surgical/cricothyrotomy kit) at the bedside before the first attempt
Call for the most experienced airway operator / activate a difficult airway response before attempting intubation
Optimize preoxygenation and positioning (upright/ramped, apneic oxygenation) rather than forcing the patient supine
If the first attempt fails, place a supraglottic airway as a bridge rather than repeating the same failed technique
Escalate immediately to front-of-neck surgical airway without delay if a cannot-intubate-cannot-oxygenate scenario develops
learningPoints: Difficult airway predictors (trismus, midface instability, short thick neck, blood/secretions in the airway) must be anticipated, not discovered mid-attempt
Closed-claims data show the most common preventable failures are failure to plan for a difficult airway, perseveration on a failed technique, and delayed calling for a surgical airway
NAP4 found most airway deaths and brain injuries occurred in seemingly low-risk patients — always prepare for difficulty regardless of how healthy the patient looks
A supraglottic airway should be used as a bridge/rescue device after a failed attempt, not skipped in favor of repeated laryngoscopy
Difficult airway management follows an algorithm — intubation, supraglottic airway, fiberoptic/awake intubation, and front-of-neck access — with equipment standardized and ready before induction
cases.intro: EMS wheels in a 52-year-old man strapped to a backboard, his face oddly flattened and swollen, both eyes bruised shut behind ballooning lids. 'He hit a tree at the bottom of the black run — ski patrol says he was out cold for about a minute,' the paramedic reports, while the monitor's oxygen saturation number keeps drifting into the 80s. The nurse can barely get him to open his mouth beyond a finger's width, and every time the head of the bed goes down he starts gurgling and clawing at the mask. His eyes track a beat slower than they did a few minutes ago.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: He desats into the 80s every time we try to lay him flat, so we've been keeping him sitting up as much as we can.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It hurts... I can't open my mouth right... everything's blurry.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He hit that tree going full speed, I saw the whole thing happen — he was out for like a minute before he came to.

cases.title: The intubated patient who isn't really intubated
cases.chiefComplaint: 58M with severe pneumonia/septic shock, emergent RSI in the ED
cases.diagnosis: Unrecognized esophageal intubation during emergent RSI for septic pneumonia with impending respiratory failure — masked by falsely reassuring auscultation and an early colorimetric color change, only revealed by the absence of a sustained continuous waveform capnography trace
cases.context: David is a 58-year-old man with four days of productive cough, fever, and worsening dyspnea from an untreated right lower lobe pneumonia that has progressed to septic shock with impending respiratory failure. On arrival he is tachypneic (RR 34), hypoxemic despite a non-rebreather mask (SpO2 84%), tachycardic (HR 128), and hypotensive (92/54) with a temp of 39.2C; an ABG shows pH 7.28, PaCO2 52, PaO2 58 on 15L NRB, lactate 4.8. He is somnolent and cannot protect his airway, so the team performs emergent RSI. A resident intubates on the first attempt; the nurse auscultates 'breath sounds equal bilaterally,' and the colorimetric end-tidal detector changes color for the first one to two breaths (residual gastric CO2, a classic false-positive) before the team moves on to sedation and a chest x-ray order, believing the airway is secured. In truth the tube is in the esophagus. Colorimetric capnometry and auscultation are each individually unreliable and can both be falsely reassuring immediately after intubation — gastric CO2 can trigger a transient color change, and transmitted upper-airway sounds can mimic bilateral breath sounds. SpO2 also stays falsely near-normal for a couple of minutes post-intubation because of preoxygenation reserve, further delaying recognition. If this is missed, the correct next step — placing continuous waveform capnography — never happens, and SpO2 begins a steep, unrelenting decline toward cardiac arrest from hypoxia; unrecognized esophageal intubation carries roughly 69% mortality overall and approaches 100% if the patient was apneic on arrival. Competing explanations the team may chase instead of rechecking the airway include right mainstem bronchial intubation, tension pneumothorax, or bronchospasm/mucus plug causing post-intubation desaturation — all plausible in a septic pneumonia patient, but each distinguishable from esophageal placement because they still produce a sustained (even if abnormal) CO2 waveform, versus a flat or rapidly extinguishing trace with true esophageal intubation. A second trap: once the tube is confirmed correctly tracheal, this patient's ETCO2 will run persistently low — a widened PaCO2-ETCO2 gradient — not from inadequate ventilation but because septic shock and hypotension reduce pulmonary blood flow and increase alveolar dead space; the fix is aggressive fluid resuscitation, pressors, and source-control antibiotics, not escalating minute ventilation. The correct, evidence-based path is continuous waveform capnography placed immediately after intubation, watched for a sustained rising-and-falling CO2 waveform over several breaths, with the tube pulled and the patient re-bagged and reintubated the instant the waveform is flat or absent — this averts arrest and gets him safely onto the ventilator with pressors and antibiotics for the underlying pneumonia and septic shock.
criticalActions: Place continuous waveform capnography immediately after intubation rather than relying on auscultation or a single colorimetric color change
Recognize that a flat or rapidly extinguishing ETCO2 waveform after the first couple of breaths means esophageal intubation regardless of 'equal' breath sounds on exam
Immediately remove a misplaced tube and re-bag/reintubate rather than treating the desaturation as pneumonia progression, mainstem intubation, or pneumothorax
Do not trust pulse oximetry alone to confirm tube position — SpO2 can remain falsely reassuring for minutes after esophageal intubation due to preoxygenation reserve

Interpret a persistently low ETCO2 after confirmed correct placement as a sign of reduced pulmonary blood flow from shock, and treat with fluids/pressors rather than simply increasing minute ventilation
Treat the underlying septic pneumonia promptly with antibiotics and hemodynamic support once the airway is secured
learningPoints: Continuous waveform capnography is the most reliable bedside confirmation of correct endotracheal tube placement, more reliable than auscultation or a single colorimetric check
Colorimetric capnometry can produce a transient false-positive color change from residual gastric CO2 immediately after an esophageal intubation
Unrecognized esophageal intubation is highly lethal — about 69% mortality overall, approaching 100% if the patient was apneic on arrival
Pulse oximetry reflects oxygenation, not airway position or ventilation, and can stay falsely reassuring for several minutes after a misplaced tube

The PaCO2-ETCO2 gradient widens whenever pulmonary blood flow falls, as in shock, pulmonary embolism, hypovolemia, or cardiac arrest — a falling ETCO2 in a hypotensive patient may reflect shock physiology rather than a ventilation problem
cases.intro: The trauma bay monitor alarms as David, slumped and diaphoretic, is wheeled back from triage — four days of fever and cough, now barely rousable, chest heaving at over 30 breaths a minute despite the non-rebreather hissing at his face. The team calls for emergent airway management; moments after the tube goes in, the nurse announces breath sounds equal bilaterally and thinks they're good, but the numbers on the monitor haven't settled the way anyone expected.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Can't... can't catch my breath... hurts so bad.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Tube's in, breath sounds equal on both sides, sat's holding around ninety for now.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's had this cough for days, we kept telling him to go to the doctor, we thought it was just a bad flu.

cases.article: 2009-02_An Evidence Based Approach To Male Urogenital Emergencies.pdf
cases.title: Scrotal pain 'out of proportion' in a diabetic (the one that needs the OR, not antibiotics alone)
cases.chiefComplaint: 68M ~ 2 days of worsening scrotal/perineal pain and swelling, fever, poorly controlled diabetes
cases.diagnosis: Fournier's gangrene — polymicrobial necrotizing fasciitis of the scrotal, penile, and perineal fascial planes in a diabetic male, requiring emergent surgical debridement in addition to broad-spectrum antibiotics
cases.context: Harold is a 68-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (reports skipping insulin for weeks, home glucose readings 'in the 300s') who presents with two days of perineal and scrotal pain and swelling that began as mild soreness and has rapidly worsened, now with fever, chills, and difficulty walking or sitting because of the pain. The true diagnosis is Fournier's gangrene: a rapidly progressive, polymicrobial necrotizing infection of the superficial and deep fascial planes of the scrotum, penis, and perineum, typically arising from a trivial or occult portal of entry (perianal, urethral, or cutaneous) in a host with diabetes or other immunocompromise, allowing synergistic aerobic-anaerobic organisms to track along fascial planes far beyond what the overlying skin suggests. As the source article emphasizes, this diagnosis must be actively considered in any elderly, diabetic, or immunocompromised male with perineal pain that is 'out of proportion' to the physical findings — the skin can look deceptively unremarkable early while destruction is already extensive underneath. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively rule out: acute epididymitis (gradual onset, dysuria, pyuria, a preserved cremasteric reflex, tenderness localized to the epididymis rather than diffuse) and a simple scrotal abscess/cellulitis (localized fluctuance or erythema without systemic toxicity or pain disproportionate to exam). Both are far more common causes of scrotal swelling and are the easy anchor that leads learners astray. The can't-miss red flags separating Fournier's from these mimics are pain disproportionate to visible skin findings, crepitus on palpation (subcutaneous gas from anaerobic organisms), dusky/violaceous or frankly necrotic skin patches, bullae, malodorous drainage, and systemic toxicity with evolving hypotension. Because this is a surgical emergency and not a medical one, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics alone are necessary but not sufficient — emergent urology/general surgery consultation for operative debridement is the definitive, time-critical intervention. Any delay (awaiting imaging, awaiting a courtesy outpatient/next-day urology evaluation, or discharging home on oral antibiotics after anchoring on epididymitis or cellulitis) allows the infection to spread along fascial planes, precipitating septic shock, extensive tissue loss requiring reconstructive surgery, and death; reported mortality is substantial even with prompt treatment and far higher when recognition is delayed. On presentation: HR 118, BP 100/64 (early compensated septic physiology), RR 22, temp 39.4C, glucose markedly elevated. Scrotal skin is edematous and exquisitely tender out of proportion to the modest erythema visible, with palpable crepitus and a small dusky patch near the perineal raphe on close inspection. If the learner anchors on epididymitis and pursues oral antibiotics for discharge, escalate over the encounter toward worsening tachycardia, hypotension, altered mentation, and visibly expanding skin discoloration.
criticalActions: Recognize perineal/scrotal pain out of proportion to exam findings in a diabetic or immunocompromised male as a red flag for Fournier's gangrene, not simple epididymitis
Perform a full genital and perineal skin exam specifically checking for crepitus, dusky/necrotic discoloration, bullae, and malodor
Do not anchor on epididymitis based on dysuria or a preserved cremasteric reflex alone in a toxic-appearing diabetic patient
Obtain emergent urology or surgery consultation for probable necrotizing infection rather than treating as an outpatient problem
Start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics and begin sepsis resuscitation (IV fluids, labs, lactate) without delay
Do not defer definitive surgical debridement while awaiting imaging or a next-day/courtesy subspecialist evaluation
learningPoints: Fournier's gangrene should be considered in elderly, diabetic, or immunocompromised men with perineal pain 'out of proportion' to physical exam findings.
Fournier's gangrene is a polymicrobial necrotizing infection of the genital and perineal fascia, distinct from and far more dangerous than epididymitis or a simple scrotal abscess.
Fever, dysuria, and a preserved cremasteric reflex favor epididymitis, but none of these findings excludes an early necrotizing infection in a high-risk host.
Antibiotics alone are not definitive treatment — emergent surgical debridement is the time-critical intervention that determines outcome.
As with other true genitourinary emergencies, early identification and aggressive management are critical to maximizing outcomes; deferring to a 'see them in the morning' plan is dangerous.
cases.intro: Harold sits hunched forward on the stretcher, unable to get comfortable, sweat beading on his forehead as his wife hovers nearby. The triage nurse says he's a diabetic who's been feeling worse and worse 'down there' for two days, and that his fingerstick glucose was over 350. He winces every time he shifts his weight, and he seems reluctant to let anyone look too closely.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It started as just some soreness two days ago, but now it hurts so much I can barely sit or walk, and my wife says I've had chills all night.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: His sugar was over 350 at triage, he's spiking a fever, and his heart rate's been sitting in the teens over a hundred since he got here. There's some swelling and redness down there but he really doesn't want it examined.

cases.title: The sore throat that got better, then didn't (the can't-miss one)
cases.chiefComplaint: 19M with sore throat that improved then recurred with neck swelling, fever, and dyspnea
cases.diagnosis: Lemierre syndrome — septic thrombophlebitis of the internal jugular vein (Fusobacterium necrophorum) with septic pulmonary emboli, following an initial oropharyngeal infection
cases.context: Tyler is a previously healthy 19-year-old college student who had a typical viral-appearing sore throat, low-grade fever, and mild tonsillar erythema about nine days ago. He never sought care, assumed it was just a cold, and felt roughly 70 percent better by day four — the timeline you would expect for ordinary pharyngitis, which the literature notes typically resolves within 3 to 5 days. Two days ago, instead of continuing to improve, he developed a new high fever, rigors, and unilateral right-sided neck pain and swelling along the sternocleidomastoid muscle, worse with head turning. Today he is markedly worse: pleuritic chest pain, a new cough, and dyspnea. The true process is Lemierre syndrome: his initial oropharyngeal infection, classically driven by Fusobacterium necrophorum, an anaerobic organism that has been isolated from the throat in cases of recurrent or persistent pharyngitis, seeded the peritonsillar or parapharyngeal space, tracked to the adjacent internal jugular vein, and caused septic thrombophlebitis. The infected clot is now showering septic emboli, classically to the lungs, producing multifocal septic pulmonary emboli that explain his new pleuritic pain, cough, and hypoxia. Left unrecognized, this progresses to worsening septic shock, cavitary lung abscesses, empyema, and potentially death — exactly the kind of life-threatening complication the literature says should broaden the differential whenever ordinary pharyngitis fails to follow its expected 3-to-5-day course. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively rule out: simple viral pharyngitis that just needs more time, which is wrong because he initially improved and then got dramatically worse rather than simply persisting; infectious mononucleosis, plausible in a college-age patient with adenopathy and fatigue but unable to explain unilateral neck vein tenderness or new respiratory findings; and strep throat needing only oral antibiotics, since GABHS-focused algorithms cover only group A strep and would miss Fusobacterium and other suppurative causes such as group B, C, or G strep and peritonsillar abscess entirely. On exam, the right neck is diffusely tender and mildly swollen along the sternocleidomastoid with a palpable cord-like fullness; there is no drooling, mild trismus, a slightly muffled voice, but no stridor. Lungs reveal scattered crackles and right-sided pleuritic pain on deep inspiration. He looks toxic and diaphoretic. Correct management is blood cultures before antibiotics, contrast-enhanced neck imaging to identify internal jugular thrombophlebitis and any drainable collection, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering anaerobes rather than the narrow oral regimen used for isolated GABHS pharyngitis, chest imaging to characterize septic emboli, ICU-level monitoring, ENT or vascular consultation, and consideration of anticoagulation — versus the deterioration trajectory of shock, respiratory failure, and death if this is dismissed as just a sore throat taking a while to clear.
criticalActions: Recognize that a sore throat which improves and then dramatically worsens beyond the typical 3-5 day course is a red flag for a life-threatening complication, not routine pharyngitis
Examine for unilateral neck swelling/tenderness along the sternocleidomastoid and screen for airway red flags (drooling, trismus, voice change, stridor)
Obtain blood cultures before starting antibiotics
Get contrast-enhanced neck imaging (CT) to evaluate for internal jugular vein septic thrombophlebitis or deep neck space infection
Start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics covering anaerobes (e.g., Fusobacterium) rather than narrow oral GABHS-only therapy
Obtain chest imaging and escalate to ICU-level care given respiratory symptoms suggesting septic pulmonary emboli
learningPoints: Acute pharyngitis typically resolves within 3 to 5 days; symptoms that persist beyond this — or that improve and then dramatically worsen — should broaden the differential to life-threatening complications such as Lemierre syndrome.

Fusobacterium has been isolated from the throat in cases of recurrent or persistent pharyngitis and is implicated in its suppurative complications, even though the evidence for routine treatment is still debated.

GABHS-focused strep testing and treatment protocols only address group A strep; other bacterial causes such as group B, C, or G streptococci and Fusobacterium can cause suppurative disease that these protocols miss entirely.

Red flags like drooling, voice change, trismus, stridor, or a toxic appearance signal a deep neck space or airway-threatening process and should prompt urgent escalation beyond symptomatic pharyngitis care.
The primary goal in any patient with sore throat is first to rule out airway compromise and life-threatening disease before defaulting to routine symptomatic management.
cases.intro: Tyler, 19, slouches on the gurney with his hand pressed against the right side of his neck, wincing every time he swallows or turns his head. "My sore throat was basically gone by like day four," he tells the nurse, "and then two days ago it just came roaring back — but now my neck's swollen too, and I can't really catch my breath." The triage nurse notes his temp is spiking, his heart is racing, and pulls you aside: this doesn't look like a normal sore throat anymore.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Triage has him tachycardic at 128, temp 39.6, and sats dipping to 91% on room air — he says his sore throat was almost gone before it came back way worse.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's my neck, right here, it's killing me, and it hurts to turn my head. I've also got this sharp pain in my chest when I breathe in.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He was doing so much better a few days ago, we thought it was just a cold. Then yesterday he spiked a fever and could barely get out of bed.

cases.title: Painless bloody diaper in a toddler (the one that isn't a fissure)
cases.chiefComplaint: 2yo M — sudden painless rectal bleeding, playing and eating normally
cases.diagnosis: Bleeding Meckel's diverticulum (ectopic gastric mucosa ulcerating adjacent ileal mucosa, causing painless lower GI hemorrhage)
cases.context: This 2-year-old previously healthy boy presents with two episodes of sudden, painless rectal bleeding since last evening — a large amount of bright-red to dark-maroon blood mixed with stool, brought to triage in a soiled diaper. He has no fever, no vomiting, no abdominal pain, and continues to tolerate his diet and play normally; his caregivers keep emphasizing how 'fine' he seems, which is precisely the trap. The true diagnosis is a bleeding Meckel's diverticulum: a congenital remnant of the omphalomesenteric duct on the antimesenteric border of the distal ileum, present in roughly 2% of the population, that in about half of symptomatic cases contains ectopic gastric (or occasionally pancreatic) mucosa. Acid secreted by this ectopic gastric tissue ulcerates the adjacent, unprotected ileal mucosa, producing painless, often brisk lower GI hemorrhage — classically bright-red or dark maroon stool without tenesmus, straining, or abdominal tenderness. Follow the 'rule of 2s': ~2% prevalence, roughly 2 feet from the ileocecal valve, about 2 inches long, 2 types of ectopic tissue, most often symptomatic before age 2, and about twice as common in boys — this patient fits nearly every criterion. Vitals show mild tachycardia (HR ~130) from ongoing blood loss with an otherwise reassuring exam — soft nontender abdomen, no masses, normal perianal inspection — and this deceptively benign picture is the central diagnostic trap: learners anchor on the well-appearing, playful child and downgrade the bleeding's significance. Competing diagnoses to actively exclude: an anal fissure (visible mucosal tear, pain with defecation, streaking of blood rather than large-volume mixed blood — absent here on exam), infectious or allergic colitis (typically diarrhea, mucus, possible fever or an atopic/eczema history, smaller-volume bleeding rather than sudden painless hemorrhage), and intussusception (intermittent colicky pain, lethargy between episodes, possibly a palpable sausage-shaped mass — none of which this child has). Labs reveal a falling hemoglobin/hematocrit reflecting ongoing losses even before the visible episodes. If dismissed as a self-limited fissure or colitis, expect recurrent large-volume bleeding, progressive pallor, worsening tachycardia, and evolution toward hemorrhagic shock (rising heart rate, narrowing pulse pressure, delayed capillary refill, eventual hypotension) as the ectopic acid continues to erode ileal mucosa. The correct approach: stabilize first — IV access, type and screen, CBC, coagulation studies, isotonic fluids or blood products for any instability — before pursuing further diagnostics; obtain a technetium-99m pertechnetate (Meckel's) scan, taken up by ectopic gastric mucosa and the diagnostic study of choice for this presentation; and involve pediatric surgery early, since definitive management is surgical resection of the diverticulum. This requires the same multidisciplinary coordination between emergency medicine, pediatric surgery, and gastroenterology that severe pediatric GI bleeding demands, while avoiding both under-triage of painless bleeding as trivial and unnecessary invasive workup once a clear surgical diagnosis is secured.
criticalActions: Recognize painless bright-red/maroon rectal bleeding in a well-appearing toddler as high-risk, not reassuring
Obtain IV access and send CBC, type and screen, and coagulation studies before extensive workup
Resuscitate with isotonic fluids or blood products if any sign of hemodynamic instability rather than waiting for imaging
Actively exclude anal fissure, infectious/allergic colitis, and intussusception on history and exam
Order a technetium-99m pertechnetate (Meckel's) scan rather than discharging as a presumed fissure or colitis
Consult pediatric surgery early once Meckel's diverticulum is suspected, since resection is definitive treatment
learningPoints: Painless rectal bleeding in a toddler is itself a red flag for Meckel's diverticulum, not a reassuring finding
The 'rule of 2s' — ~2% prevalence, ~2 feet from the ileocecal valve, ~2 inches long, 2 types of ectopic tissue, usually symptomatic by age 2, twice as common in boys
Ectopic gastric mucosa within the diverticulum secretes acid that ulcerates adjacent normal ileal mucosa, causing bleeding
Technetium-99m pertechnetate (Meckel's) scan is the diagnostic study of choice, since it is taken up by ectopic gastric mucosa
Patient stabilization always takes precedence over diagnostic workup in pediatric GI bleeding, and severe or surgical causes must be actively ruled out even when the child looks well
cases.intro: A toddler is carried into triage by his parents, a diaper in hand streaked with a surprising amount of bright-red and dark maroon stool. He's babbling, reaching for the pulse-ox clip on his toe, and utterly uninterested in the commotion around him. His parents insist he's been eating and playing like always — 'this just came out of nowhere' — and keep asking if it could just be something he ate. Vitals show him a little fast at the heart, otherwise unremarkable, and his belly is soft when you press on it.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He had two episodes since last night, so much blood in the diaper, but he's acting completely normal, ate his whole breakfast like nothing happened.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Heart rate is 130, blood pressure 95 over 50, temp is normal, sats are 99% on room air. He didn't even flinch when I pressed on his belly.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Truck! Vroom vroom!

cases.title: Pediatric myocarditis after 'just a cold' (the one that isn't gastro)
cases.chiefComplaint: 5M — 3 days vomiting, poor feeding, fast breathing after a cold
cases.diagnosis: Acute viral myocarditis with early cardiogenic (heart failure) shock following a preceding viral prodrome
