cases.context: Miles is a previously healthy 5-year-old boy who had a mild upper-respiratory viral illness (rhinorrhea, low-grade fever) roughly one week ago that seemed to resolve, but three days ago he began vomiting intermittently, stopped eating well, grew progressively more tired, and over the last day has been breathing faster than usual. The true diagnosis is acute viral myocarditis: a systemic viral infection (most commonly coxsackievirus, adenovirus, parvovirus B19, or human herpesvirus 6) directly invaded and damaged his myocytes during an initial injury phase, and ongoing immune-mediated inflammation is now impairing ventricular contractility, pushing him into early, compensated heart failure that can decompensate into cardiogenic shock or a life-threatening ventricular dysrhythmia without warning. The presentation is deliberately non-specific and easy to mis-triage: caregivers and the learner will be tempted toward viral gastroenteritis/dehydration (vomiting, poor oral intake, lethargy), a lingering viral URI or bronchiolitis/reactive airway (tachypnea, mild retractions, effortful breathing), or simple fever-driven tachycardia — none of which fully explain the picture. The can't-miss trap is that his heart rate (170s-180s) is markedly out of proportion to his low-grade temperature (37.9C) and to how calm he appears between cries; on exam he is pale and unusually quiet/lethargic, has faint bibasilar crackles without wheeze, a soft gallop rhythm with somewhat distant heart sounds, a liver edge palpable about 3 cm below the costal margin (hepatomegaly), mildly delayed capillary refill (about 3 seconds), and cool, mildly mottled extremities, but no rash, no diarrhea to explain dehydration, and only mild diffuse abdominal tenderness. If this is misread as gastroenteritis and given a routine large-volume isotonic fluid bolus, or dismissed as a resolving viral illness for outpatient follow-up, he is expected to decompensate over hours into overt cardiogenic shock, pulmonary edema, ventricular dysrhythmia, or cardiac arrest. The evidence-based approach is to recognize the mismatch between fever and tachycardia, obtain a 12-lead ECG early (looking for low voltages, ST-T changes, ectopy, or heart block), get a chest x-ray (cardiomegaly, pulmonary edema), check troponin/BNP, arrange urgent bedside echocardiography to assess ventricular function, and involve pediatric cardiology/PICU early. Fluid management must be cautious — small aliquots with frequent reassessment rather than the large boluses used for presumed dehydration or septic shock, since volume-loading a failing ventricle can precipitate flash pulmonary edema. He needs continuous cardiac/SpO2 monitoring, supplemental oxygen, and admission to an ICU setting where inotropic and antidysrhythmic support can be started if he worsens.
criticalActions: Obtain a 12-lead ECG early in any child with unexplained tachycardia, vomiting, or a vague post-viral illness
Recognize tachycardia out of proportion to the degree of fever as a heart-failure red flag, not simple dehydration or fussiness
Avoid large-volume isotonic fluid boluses; give small cautious aliquots and reassess for worsening respiratory distress or hepatomegaly
Obtain chest x-ray and troponin/BNP, and consult pediatric cardiology/PICU early for suspected myocarditis
Arrange urgent echocardiography to assess ventricular function before committing to a benign disposition
Anticipate rapid deterioration to cardiogenic shock or ventricular dysrhythmia and admit to an ICU-level setting
learningPoints: Pediatric myocarditis often presents with a nonspecific flu-like prodrome — GI symptoms, malaise, poor feeding — rather than obvious cardiac complaints
Tachycardia disproportionate to the degree of fever is a key clue to myocarditis and should not be attributed to fever or agitation alone
Hepatomegaly, a gallop rhythm, and respiratory distress without wheezing point toward heart failure rather than a primary respiratory or GI illness
Untreated myocarditis can progress rapidly to cardiogenic shock, ventricular dysrhythmia, or cardiac arrest
Coxsackievirus, adenovirus, parvovirus B19, and human herpesvirus 6 are among the most commonly implicated pathogens in pediatric myocarditis
cases.intro: Miles is a wilting five-year-old draped across his mother's lap, cheeks flushed, breathing a little too fast for the quiet room he's in. She says he's had 'some kind of stomach bug' since last week — a cold that seemed to clear up, and now he won't eat, keeps throwing up, and just wants to sleep. The triage nurse frowns at the monitor: his heart rate won't come down much even between cries.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He had a cold about a week ago, runny nose, low fever, we thought it was over. Then two days ago he started throwing up and he's just so tired all the time.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My tummy hurts and I don't want to eat anything.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: His heart rate's been sitting in the 170s to 180s since he got here and it doesn't come down much even when he's calm, and his liver's a bit more prominent than I'd expect.

cases.title: Cognitive decline in a 63-year-old (not the dementia you think)
cases.chiefComplaint: 63F — 3 months of progressive personality change, irritability, and memory lapses
cases.diagnosis: Neurosyphilis, parenchymatous (general paresis) form of tertiary syphilis — CNS invasion by Treponema pallidum presenting as progressive cognitive and personality change
cases.context: Diane is a 63-year-old woman brought to the ED by her daughter for three months of progressive personality change, irritability, and memory lapses. The true diagnosis is neurosyphilis, specifically the parenchymatous (general paresis) form of tertiary syphilis. Treponema pallidum spirochetes have no expressed surface-based proteins, which lets them evade early immune clearance and invade the CNS well before any late-stage symptoms appear; when infection goes unrecognized through the primary and secondary stages, the organism can smolder silently for years to decades before manifesting as slowly progressive cortical and meningeal inflammation — mood change, irritability, memory loss, poor judgment, and subtle word-finding difficulty, historically termed general paralysis of the insane. Diane's sexual history must be taken sensitively: she initially denies current sexual activity, but gentle, nonjudgmental questioning reveals she remarried about a year ago after being widowed and has not used condoms with her new husband; further back, she vaguely recalls a painless genital sore in her twenties that resolved on its own and was never evaluated — a self-healing primary chancre she never connected to anything since. She denies any prior rash involving her palms and soles. The learner must actively rule out the more obvious competing diagnoses that anchor a busy clinician on first pass: early-onset Alzheimer disease or another primary dementia, a major depressive episode precipitated by her recent retirement, and hypothyroidism or another reversible metabolic cause of cognitive slowing. The can't-miss trap, exactly as the source article frames it, is that syphilis is the great imitator: a patient with normal vital signs, no focal neurologic deficits, and a firm denial of current sexual risk can still have neurosyphilis, and clinicians who stop at a dementia workup because the patient denies being sexually active will miss it. Findings that should trigger RPR/VDRL testing here are the disproportionate personality and mood change relative to her baseline, slowed responses without a clearer alternative explanation, and any remote history of an unexplained genital sore or rash. If unrecognized, Diane's course will keep worsening — progressive cognitive decline, further personality deterioration, and eventually the other late complications the article describes, including tabes dorsalis (permanent loss of proprioception and ataxia) and cardiovascular syphilis. The correct approach is RPR/VDRL screening despite her denial of current activity, confirmatory treponemal testing with reliable follow-up arranged before discharge, penicillin-based treatment appropriate to neurosyphilis (with awareness that the ongoing penicillin G benzathine shortage has changed some stage-specific regimens), and sensitive discussion of partner notification and testing for her husband. Vitals: HR 78, BP 126/78, RR 16, O2 98% room air, T 36.9C. On exam she is alert and oriented x3 but slow to answer, with mild word-finding pauses and no focal weakness, facial droop, or gait abnormality on cursory exam.
criticalActions: Take a sensitive, nonjudgmental sexual history despite the patient's initial denial and older age
Order RPR/VDRL screening for syphilis given an atypical psychiatric/cognitive presentation, not just an isolated dementia workup
Recognize this as a possible neurosyphilis presentation before anchoring on primary dementia or depression
Arrange confirmatory treponemal testing with reliable follow-up if RPR/VDRL is positive
Initiate penicillin-based treatment appropriate to the stage, accounting for current penicillin G benzathine shortages and alternative regimens
Discuss and facilitate partner notification and testing for her husband
learningPoints: Syphilis is called the great imitator because its presentation varies widely by stage and can mimic dementia, depression, or other psychiatric illness
Treponema pallidum's lack of surface proteins allows early invasion of the CNS, so neurologic and psychiatric involvement can occur even without a recognized prior syphilis history
Denial of current sexual activity should not exclude syphilis testing, since risk can stem from remote or lifetime exposure rather than current behavior
RPR/VDRL is a screening test that requires reliable follow-up for confirmatory treponemal testing when positive
Persistent penicillin G benzathine shortages have driven the need for alternative treatment regimens across stages of syphilis
cases.intro: Diane, 63, is brought to the ED by her daughter, worried about three months of increasingly odd behavior — mood swings, irritability, and forgetting things mid-sentence. The nurse notes she is alert and cooperative but answers slowly, as if hunting for words that used to come easily. Her daughter mentions Diane retired last spring and has just not been herself since, and quietly adds that her mother remarried about a year ago after being widowed. Vitals are unremarkable, and there is nothing dramatic on first glance — just a woman who seems, subtly, not quite present.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I do not know why everyone is so worried. I just get tired and my words get jumbled sometimes. Ask my daughter, she is the one who insisted on this whole hospital thing.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She is not herself, doctor. She forgets things she would never forget before, and she has been snapping at people out of nowhere. I know she is not exactly forthcoming about her personal life since she remarried, but I do not think that has anything to do with this.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Vitals are all normal, she is alert and oriented times three, no focal weakness or facial droop, just seems slow. Family is pushing hard for a dementia workup.

cases.title: Pediatric orbital blowout fracture with entrapment (the 'white-eyed' one)
cases.chiefComplaint: 11M eye pain and nausea after baseball to the face, painful with upward gaze
cases.diagnosis: Left orbital floor 'blowout' fracture with inferior rectus muscle entrapment (pediatric 'white-eyed' trapdoor fracture) — a surgical emergency requiring urgent ophthalmology/oculoplastic consultation to prevent muscle ischemia and permanent diplopia
cases.context: Mason is an 11-year-old boy struck directly in the left eye by a fastball roughly 45 minutes ago; he did not lose consciousness and has no other visible trauma. The ground truth is a left orbital floor 'blowout' fracture with entrapment of the inferior rectus muscle in a linear pediatric 'trapdoor' fracture: the blow raised intra-orbital pressure enough to fracture the weak orbital floor (composed of the maxilla and lamina papyracea, the thinnest and most frequently fractured orbital bones), but because pediatric bone is elastic, the fractured segment sprang back toward its original position and pinched the inferior rectus muscle and surrounding fat within the fracture line. This is a true surgical emergency — an entrapped, ischemic muscle can progress to necrosis and permanent diplopia/restrictive strabismus within roughly 24-48 hours if not surgically released, so emergent (not outpatient) ophthalmology/oculoplastic consultation and repair is required. Classically, these pediatric trapdoor fractures are 'white-eyed': because the tight trapdoor seals the fracture site, there is little to no ecchymosis, proptosis, or subcutaneous emphysema, so the eye can look deceptively benign — a trap for the examiner who anchors on the mild external appearance. On exam Mason has marked restriction of upward gaze in the left eye (roughly 30-40% of normal excursion) with vertical diplopia on attempted upgaze, mild left infraorbital cheek/upper-lip numbness (the infraorbital nerve courses through the orbital floor and is commonly injured with these fractures), and subtle enophthalmos on close inspection. Critically, when the nurse or examiner has him track upward, he becomes nauseated and his heart rate transiently drops from the 90s into the 50s-60s for a few seconds — the oculocardiac reflex, a vagally mediated response to extraocular muscle traction that is a recognized red flag for entrapment, especially in pediatric trapdoor fractures. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively rule out: (1) a simple periorbital contusion/'black eye' with no fracture, needing only ice and reassurance; (2) a non-entrapped orbital floor fracture, which can often be managed with routine outpatient ophthalmology follow-up rather than emergent repair; and (3) an isolated globe injury (hyphema, globe rupture) without fracture — Mason's globe exam, visual acuity, and pupils are normal and there is no hyphema, which argues against isolated globe pathology and should refocus attention on the EOM/entrapment findings. The correct evidence-based path is dedicated CT of the orbits/facial bones to confirm the fracture and demonstrate the entrapped muscle, a carefully documented EOM exam (especially upgaze), and emergent multidisciplinary (ophthalmology/oculoplastic surgery) consultation for surgical release — delay risks permanent muscle ischemia, necrosis, and lifelong diplopia.
criticalActions: Obtain dedicated CT of the orbits/facial bones to identify the orbital floor fracture and entrapped inferior rectus muscle
Perform and document a full extraocular movement exam, specifically testing upward gaze, in every patient with periorbital trauma
Recognize restricted upgaze plus the oculocardiac reflex (bradycardia, nausea/vomiting with attempted eye movement) as signs of entrapment requiring emergent, not outpatient, evaluation
Obtain emergent ophthalmology/oculoplastic surgery consultation for suspected entrapment rather than routine outpatient referral
Screen for concomitant ocular (globe), intracranial, and cervical spine injury as part of the systematic maxillofacial trauma evaluation
Do not anchor on minimal external bruising ('white-eyed' fracture) — absence of ecchymosis does not rule out entrapment
learningPoints: Orbital blowout fractures occur when a direct blow to the globe raises orbital pressure and fractures the weak orbital floor (maxilla and lamina papyracea), which can protect the globe from rupture
Ocular injury occurs in up to 40% of facial trauma, and muscle or nerve entrapment is a recognized vision- and function-threatening complication
Children have more pliable orbital bone that can spring back and trap the inferior rectus in a linear 'trapdoor' fracture with little to no external bruising — the 'white-eyed' blowout fracture
Restricted upward gaze, vertical diplopia, and the oculocardiac reflex (bradycardia, nausea, vomiting with attempted eye movement) signal an entrapped muscle at risk for ischemia and necrosis

The inferior orbital nerve runs through the orbital floor, so infraorbital hypesthesia is an expected associated finding, and multidisciplinary (ophthalmology/oculoplastic) consultation is appropriate emergently for suspected entrapment, distinct from routine follow-up for a simple non-entrapped fracture
cases.intro: Mason, 11, is brought in by his mom straight from Little League practice, holding an ice pack to his left eye after taking a fastball to the face about forty-five minutes ago. His eyelid is a little puffy with just a faint bruise starting to form, but otherwise the eye looks surprisingly okay. He keeps wincing and says it hurts worse when he tries to look up at the ceiling, and he's felt queasy a couple of times since he got here.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It doesn't even look that bad, right? It just really hurts when I try to look up, and I feel like I might throw up.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: His heart rate just dropped from the nineties into the fifties for a few seconds when I had him track my finger upward.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He got hit dead in the eye with the ball maybe forty minutes ago. There wasn't even that much swelling so I almost didn't bring him in.

cases.title: The teen PE nobody suspects (post-op leg pin + the pill)
cases.chiefComplaint: 13F: sudden pleuritic chest pain and shortness of breath, courtside at her brother's game
cases.diagnosis: Acute pulmonary embolism in an adolescent with combined provoked VTE risk factors — six days of post-surgical immobility after open reduction/pinning of a femur fracture plus estrogen-containing oral contraceptive use started three months ago
cases.context: Maya is a 13-year-old girl with a genuine acute pulmonary embolism (PE), most likely originating from a lower-extremity DVT, provoked by two simultaneous risk factors the article identifies as classic for pediatric VTE: prolonged post-surgical immobility (she has been essentially couch-bound for 6 days since orthopedic pinning of a femur fracture) and initiation of an estrogen-containing oral contraceptive 3 months ago (started for menstrual regulation, not sexual activity). Per the source article, estrogen-containing hormonal contraception increases PE risk more than 14-fold via elevated prothrombin, factors VII/VIII/X, von Willebrand factor, and fibrinogen; separately, immobility promotes venous stasis. Adolescents are more likely than younger children to stack two or more risk factors at diagnosis, and that stacking is exactly what happened here — this is not an idiopathic clot the way most adult PEs are; in children, VTE is almost always provoked by an identifiable risk factor. The can't-miss trap: because pediatric PE is rare, clinicians reflexively anchor on more common adolescent chest-pain mimics — musculoskeletal strain from cheering/jumping, an anxiety or panic attack triggered by the excitement of the game, costochondritis, or early pneumonia/pleurisy — and the article warns that no validated clinical decision rule (no pediatric Wells or PERC equivalent) exists to lean on, so risk-factor recognition on history IS the diagnostic tool. Vitals are deceptively reassuring rather than dramatic: HR 112 (sinus tachycardia, easy to blame on pain/anxiety), RR 26 (mild tachypnea), SpO2 97% on room air at rest (desaturates toward 90-92% with ambulation or exertion), BP 108/68, temp 37.1C. Exam: mild right-sided pleuritic chest wall tenderness that is reproducible but not fully so, no calf swelling noted yet (early), well-appearing, anxious-looking, tachypneic but speaking in full sentences, surgical incision on the thigh healing well without infection. The article notes diagnosis is commonly delayed up to 7 days from symptom onset in children, and that delay raises risk of clot propagation, loss of venous access, VTE recurrence, postthrombotic syndrome, and death. If the learner anchors on anxiety or musculoskeletal pain and defers workup, expect progressive deterioration over the encounter: HR climbing into the 130s, SpO2 drifting down into the high 80s/low 90s on room air, new hypotension, jugular venous distension, and signs of right heart strain/obstructive shock as clot burden propagates. The evidence-based path is risk-factor-driven imaging (CT pulmonary angiography for suspected PE given her risk-factor profile) and, once confirmed, prompt anticoagulation per pediatric hematology guidance (heparin-based therapy, transitioning per ASH/ACCP-referenced pediatric protocols), which halts propagation and prevents hemodynamic collapse.
criticalActions: Elicit and recognize combined VTE risk factors on history — recent surgical immobilization and estrogen-containing oral contraceptive use — despite the patient being a healthy-appearing adolescent
Do not anchor on musculoskeletal pain, anxiety/panic attack, or pneumonia as the sole explanation without actively screening for PE given her risk-factor profile
Recognize that reassuring or only mildly abnormal vital signs (SpO2 97% at rest) do not exclude pediatric PE
Obtain risk-factor-driven imaging (CT pulmonary angiography) rather than withholding workup for lack of a validated pediatric clinical decision rule
Initiate anticoagulation promptly per pediatric hematology guidance once VTE is confirmed to prevent clot propagation and hemodynamic collapse
Consult pediatric hematology early given the absence of validated pediatric-specific risk-stratification protocols
learningPoints: Pediatric VTE is almost always provoked by an identifiable risk factor, unlike adult VTE which is frequently idiopathic
Adolescents are more likely than younger children to have two or more simultaneous VTE risk factors present at diagnosis
No validated clinical decision rule (no pediatric Wells or PERC equivalent) exists for pediatric VTE, so risk-factor recognition on history is the primary diagnostic tool
Estrogen-containing hormonal contraceptives raise PE risk more than 14-fold by increasing prothrombin, factors VII/VIII/X, von Willebrand factor, and fibrinogen
Diagnosis of pediatric VTE is commonly delayed up to 7 days from symptom onset, increasing risk of clot propagation, recurrence, postthrombotic syndrome, and death
cases.intro: Maya, a lanky 13-year-old, is curled on her side on the stretcher clutching her right ribcage, wincing with every deep breath. The pain and breathlessness hit suddenly twenty minutes ago while she was sitting courtside cheering at her brother's basketball game. Her mother hovers nearby, mentioning almost as an aside that Maya had her leg pinned by the orthopedic surgeon six days ago and started a birth control pill a few months back for irregular periods. Maya insists she's 'probably just stressed about finals' and asks if she can head back to the game once the pain meds kick in.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It hurts more when I breathe in deep, like a stabbing feeling on my right side. I probably just pulled something cheering too hard.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's been resting a lot since her leg surgery last week, barely moving off the couch. Oh, and she started the birth control pill a few months ago for her periods, if that matters.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her oxygen's reading ninety-seven percent and heart rate's up around one-twelve, doctor. She looks anxious but she's talking in full sentences.

cases.title: Teen headache that's not 'just a migraine' (pseudotumor cerebri)
cases.chiefComplaint: 16F, worsening daily headache with transient vision blackouts
cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri) with papilledema
cases.context: Maya is a 16-year-old girl with obesity (BMI about 34) who presents with three weeks of progressively worsening, holocranial headache, now daily and severe (8/10), accompanied by intermittent blurry vision and a whooshing sound in her right ear. The true diagnosis is idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri): elevated intracranial pressure with no mass lesion or hydrocephalus on imaging, related to her obesity and possibly compounded by the oral doxycycline she started two months ago for acne, since tetracyclines are a recognized precipitant. The pathophysiology is impaired CSF resorption raising intracranial pressure, which transmits along the optic nerve sheath and causes papilledema; if unrecognized, sustained papilledema causes irreversible optic nerve axonal injury and permanent vision loss, making this a vision-threatening emergency masquerading as a benign headache. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively rule out: migraine without aura (she has a maternal history of migraine and the pain is throbbing with photophobia and nausea, tempting an easy 'chronic migraine' label), tension-type headache (bandlike, stress-associated, common at her age), and chronic sinusitis (she has mild nasal congestion and was previously told by her pediatrician this 'might be sinus pressure'). Distinguishing red-flag features to elicit: the headache is markedly worse lying flat and eases somewhat sitting upright; she has had several transient visual obscurations, seconds-long grayouts of vision in both eyes, worse with standing quickly; pulsatile tinnitus (a whooshing sound synchronous with her pulse); and no trauma, fever, or focal weakness. Vitals are near-normal since this is a neuro-ophthalmologic rather than hemodynamic process: HR 78, BP 128/82, RR 16, T 36.8C, O2 99% room air. Exam shows obesity, normal mental status and cranial nerves except blurred, elevated optic disc margins bilaterally on fundoscopic exam (papilledema), the single most important finding and one easily missed if fundoscopy is skipped. No neck stiffness, no focal motor or sensory deficit, gait is normal. If missed and treated only symptomatically as migraine, expect progressive visual field constriction, worsening papilledema, and permanent optic atrophy or blindness over subsequent weeks. The correct evidence-based approach: perform a careful fundoscopic exam on any worsening or atypical pediatric headache; obtain brain MRI with MR venography to exclude a mass lesion or dural venous sinus thrombosis, both of which can also raise ICP and must be excluded before lumbar puncture; if imaging is unremarkable, perform an LP in the lateral decubitus position with manometry documenting elevated opening pressure (this case: 32 cmH2O, with normal CSF cell count, protein, and glucose), which is both diagnostic and can transiently relieve symptoms; involve ophthalmology for formal visual field and acuity testing and neurology for ongoing management, including weight-loss counseling, acetazolamide, and discontinuing the doxycycline.
criticalActions: Perform a fundoscopic exam looking specifically for papilledema
Obtain brain MRI with MR venography before lumbar puncture to exclude a mass lesion or venous sinus thrombosis
Perform a lumbar puncture in the lateral decubitus position with manometry to measure opening pressure
Consult ophthalmology for formal visual field and acuity assessment and consult neurology
Do not anchor on 'chronic migraine' without screening for vision-threatening red flags
Discontinue the doxycycline and counsel on weight management with acetazolamide per neurology
learningPoints: Papilledema is the key exam finding that separates a secondary, vision-threatening headache from a primary headache disorder
Positional headache worse lying flat, transient visual obscurations, and pulsatile tinnitus are hallmark features of raised intracranial pressure
Obesity and certain medications such as tetracyclines are recognized risk factors for pediatric pseudotumor cerebri
Neuroimaging must precede lumbar puncture whenever a secondary headache cause is suspected, to exclude a mass lesion or venous sinus thrombosis first
Untreated papilledema can progress to permanent optic nerve damage and vision loss, unlike primary headache disorders like migraine
cases.intro: Maya, 16, sits hunched on the exam table with the lights dimmed, one hand pressed against her forehead. Her mother explains that these headaches have been building for three weeks now, worse than anything before: 'She gets migraines, but this is different, it just won't quit.' Maya mentions that her vision sometimes goes gray for a few seconds, especially when she stands up fast, and that her right ear has been making a strange whooshing sound. She denies fever, injury, or recent illness, and just wants something to make the pounding stop.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It hurts worse when I lie down, and sometimes my vision just goes gray for a few seconds, like the lights dim, then it comes back.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's had migraines since she was twelve, but they never lasted this long or came with that ringing sound in her ear.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her weight's up about fifteen pounds from her last visit here six months ago, and she started a new acne medication a couple months back.

cases.title: The kidney that looked fine (blunt flank trauma, gross hematuria, and the grade nobody guessed)
cases.chiefComplaint: 24M s/p ATV rollover, right flank pain, bloody urine
cases.diagnosis: High-grade (AAST grade IV) blunt laceration of the right kidney with active contrast extravasation and early perinephric urinoma
cases.context: Tyler is a 24-year-old man who presents after his ATV flipped at moderate speed on a gravel trail and threw him into a downhill embankment; he struck his right flank and lower ribs directly on a rock outcropping and was not wearing a harness. He fits the classic epidemiology of blunt renal trauma described in the literature: young (under 45) and male, with a noniatrogenic blunt mechanism of exactly the kind that accounts for most GU injuries, and the kidney is the GU organ injured most often in blunt trauma. The true diagnosis is a high-grade (AAST grade IV) laceration of the right kidney extending into the collecting system, with active contrast extravasation from a lacerated segmental renal vessel and an early perinephric urinoma forming from urine leak. His initial vitals are deceptively reassuring -- HR 108, BP 118/76, RR 18, O2 97% room air, afebrile at 36.9C -- masking early, compensated hemorrhage into the retroperitoneum. This is the central trap: he 'looks fine,' is alert and joking, minimizing his pain, tempting the team to treat him as a low-acuity soft-tissue flank contusion and defer imaging. His first voided urine is grossly blood-tinged, tea-to-rose colored, tempting a second, opposite error: assuming visible blood tracks with injury severity. It does not -- gross hematuria can occur with low-grade contusions, and severe injuries (including pedicle/vascular injury) can present with minimal or absent hematuria, so hematuria degree alone must never decide who needs imaging or estimate severity. Competing diagnoses the learner should actively work to exclude: (1) a simple musculoskeletal flank or rib contusion / nondisplaced rib fracture from direct impact that would not warrant renal-protocol imaging; (2) a benign, self-limited low-grade renal contusion (AAST grade I) with microscopic hematuria that resolves with observation alone; (3) a lumbar transverse-process fracture causing referred flank pain. The evidence-based approach is a contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis with a delayed excretory-phase sequence to assess for urine extravasation, obtained because of the blunt mechanism, flank ecchymosis, and hematuria (regardless of degree) rather than vital-sign reassurance -- plus early urology and trauma surgery consultation to grade the injury and plan management, since renal and GU injuries are easy to miss in the rush to address more obviously life-threatening problems. Because the patient is currently hemodynamically stable, the correct plan is expectant, nonoperative management with serial hemoglobin/hematocrit and vital-sign monitoring, urology at bedside, and readiness for angioembolization if extravasation persists or he decompensates. If the injury is missed or imaging is delayed because he 'looked fine' and had only modest hematuria, expect ongoing occult retroperitoneal hemorrhage over the next 1-2 hours -- HR climbing into the 130s, systolic BP drifting to 85-90, worsening flank fullness -- followed by an enlarging urinoma, fever, abscess, sepsis, or uncontrolled bleeding requiring delayed nephrectomy, versus early recognition, which typically allows renal salvage via angioembolization/observation and avoids long-term sequelae such as hypertension, chronic kidney disease, or nephrectomy.
criticalActions: Order a contrast-enhanced CT abdomen/pelvis with delayed excretory-phase imaging based on mechanism and any hematuria, not on reassuring vitals
Do not use the degree of gross vs. microscopic hematuria to decide whether imaging or urology consultation is needed
Trend serial vital signs and hemoglobin/hematocrit to detect occult ongoing retroperitoneal hemorrhage despite an initially stable-appearing patient
Consult urology and trauma surgery early to grade the renal injury and plan nonoperative vs. operative/angioembolization management
Recognize active contrast extravasation on CT as an indication for interventional radiology angioembolization or surgical exploration
Pursue nonoperative, expectant management with close monitoring if the patient remains hemodynamically stable, per the modern conservative approach to renal trauma
learningPoints: Renal and other GU injuries are frequently overlooked in trauma patients because clinicians are focused on more immediately life-threatening problems.
The amount of hematuria does not reliably predict renal injury severity -- high-grade and even pedicle injuries can present with minimal or no visible blood in the urine.
CT with IV contrast, including a delayed excretory phase, is the gold-standard imaging test for suspected blunt renal trauma.
A missed or delayed renal injury diagnosis raises the risk of urinoma, abscess, sepsis, delayed nephrectomy, and long-term sequelae like hypertension and chronic kidney disease.
Coordinated care at a trauma center with early urology involvement reduces the risk of nephrectomy and improves outcomes in renal trauma.
cases.intro: EMS wheels in a 24-year-old man strapped to a backboard, wincing but wisecracking, after his ATV flipped on a gravel embankment trail. A spreading bruise blooms over his right flank and lower ribs where he says he 'clipped a rock,' and he keeps insisting he can just walk it off. The triage nurse holds up his first urine sample against the light -- it's tinged pink-to-red -- while the monitor shows his heart rate quietly ticking upward.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Honestly it's just my side, I've walked away from way worse spills than this. Can we hurry this up, I've got work in the morning.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: His pressure's holding okay but his heart rate's crept up into the one-teens since we got him back here, and that urine sample he gave us looks like rose wine.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He didn't even want us to call the ambulance, said he was fine, but he went kind of pale on the ride over and wouldn't stop holding his side.

cases.title: The reaction that came back (just as she was walking out the door)
cases.chiefComplaint: 22F, throat tightness and new hives 3 hours after apparent resolution of an anaphylactic reaction
cases.diagnosis: Biphasic anaphylactic reaction (recurrence of IgE-mediated multisystem anaphylaxis hours after apparent resolution, without re-exposure to the trigger)
cases.context: Maya, a 22-year-old woman with no prior history of severe allergic reactions, is on hour three of ED observation after an initial anaphylactic reaction to peanuts hidden in a pad thai sauce. Roughly forty minutes after eating she developed diffuse urticaria, lip and tongue swelling, throat tightness, expiratory wheezing, and lightheadedness; paramedics gave one dose of IM epinephrine 0.3 mg (1:1000) in the field, and on ED arrival she received a second IM epinephrine dose plus diphenhydramine 50 mg IV and methylprednisolone 125 mg IV -- the same first-line-plus-adjunct regimen this article's own teaching cases use for anaphylaxis. Within thirty minutes her hives faded, airway swelling resolved, and vitals normalized (HR 84, BP 118/74, RR 16, O2 99% RA); she has looked and felt entirely well for over two hours, and her family began packing to leave. The TRUE process unfolding now is a biphasic anaphylactic reaction: a recurrence of the same IgE-mediated, mast-cell/basophil-mediator-driven multisystem process -- no new allergen exposure required -- that classically strikes within roughly 1 to 72 hours of apparent resolution (most within 8-10 hours), consistent with this article's framing of anaphylaxis as a 'continuum' of acute multi-organ symptoms rather than a single discrete event. She now has new-onset urticaria on the neck, throat tightness, wheeze, and objective hemodynamic compromise (HR 128, BP 92/58, RR 26, O2 91% RA, T 37.1C) -- this is NOT just nerves. Plausible distractors the learner must actively exclude: (1) anticipatory anxiety/panic from the trauma of the first reaction, which can produce tachycardia and a subjective 'throat tightness' feeling but should NOT produce new objective urticaria, wheeze, or hypotension; (2) a benign medication effect -- residual tachycardia/tremor from the epinephrine already on board, or drowsiness from the diphenhydramine -- which would not explain new hives or a falling blood pressure; (3) an unrelated new process such as a vasovagal episode from prolonged bed rest or dehydration, which would not produce urticaria or wheeze. The can't-miss trap is discharging a patient too early because she 'looks fine' shortly after epinephrine resolves the first wave of symptoms -- this article poses exactly that tension in its own teaching case ('the patient wants to go home, and you wonder if that's a good idea') for a reaction that had only partially resolved. If this biphasic recurrence is missed or under-treated (reassurance and discharge instead of re-treatment), expect rapid progression over the next 15-30 minutes to worsening stridor, oxygen desaturation below 90%, and refractory hypotension/shock, without the same obvious early warning the family already watched resolve once. The correct approach: recognize the recurrent objective signs as anaphylaxis, re-administer IM epinephrine immediately, secure the airway with supplemental oxygen and IV crystalloid for hypotension, and extend ED observation (not discharge) with admission consideration, plus an epinephrine auto-injector prescription and allergy/immunology follow-up before any eventual discharge.
criticalActions: Recognize recurrent urticaria, wheeze, and hypotension hours after resolution as a biphasic anaphylactic reaction, not anxiety
Re-administer IM epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg (1:1000) to the anterolateral thigh immediately
Reassess and secure the airway; give supplemental oxygen and IV crystalloid for hypotension
Do not discharge -- extend ED observation and consider admission after a biphasic reaction
Prescribe an epinephrine auto-injector and arrange allergy/immunology follow-up before eventual discharge
learningPoints: Biphasic anaphylaxis can recur hours after apparent complete resolution of the first reaction, without any new allergen exposure
Patients who look fully recovered after epinephrine still need an observation period before discharge because recurrence is unpredictable
New objective signs (urticaria, wheeze, hypotension) after anaphylaxis should be treated as a true recurrence and re-dosed with epinephrine, not dismissed as anxiety
Anaphylaxis is defined by multisystem involvement -- skin/mucosal signs plus respiratory, cardiovascular, or GI involvement -- which distinguishes it from an isolated anxiety response
Every anaphylaxis patient should leave the ED with an epinephrine auto-injector prescription and clear return precautions
cases.intro: The discharge paperwork is half-signed and Maya's mother is already zipping up her jacket when the bedside monitor starts chirping. Three hours ago Maya was covered in hives and gasping after a bite of pad thai; now she says her throat 'feels tight again' and the nurse spots new welts blooming across her neck. She insists she's just nervous about going home -- but her heart rate on the monitor just jumped into the 120s.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I feel really anxious all of a sudden, like it's happening again... but I haven't eaten anything, I promise, I've just been sitting here.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Her heart rate just climbed to the 120s and I'm seeing new hives on her neck that weren't there ten minutes ago.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She was doing so well, we were literally about to walk out the door. Is this normal?

cases.title: The 'she stopped breathing' baby who's actually low-risk (BRUE)
cases.chiefComplaint: 10-week-old F — resolved cyanotic/limp episode with panicked bystander CPR
cases.diagnosis: Low-risk Brief Resolved Unexplained Event (BRUE). The infant meets all six 2016 AAP low-risk criteria: age >60 days (10 weeks), born at >=32 weeks' gestation with postconceptional age >=45 weeks (born full-term, now 10 weeks old), a first and isolated event (not recurrent or clustered), event duration <1 minute (~20-30 seconds), no CPR performed by a trained medical provider (the chest compressions/rescue breaths were done by a panicked, untrained parent, which per the guideline does not count against low-risk status), and no concerning historical or physical exam findings (normal growth, immunizations up to date, no fever/illness, no trauma, entirely normal cardiac/neurologic exam and vitals on arrival). The frightening description of 'she stopped breathing and turned blue' and the fact that a parent performed CPR are red herrings that tempt over-classification as higher-risk; correctly parsing 'CPR by a trained medical provider' versus lay bystander CPR is the crux of the case.
cases.context: This is a case built to test correct application of the 2016 AAP BRUE risk-stratification criteria. BRUE (brief resolved unexplained event) replaced the older, vaguer term ALTE; it describes a sudden, brief episode in an infant under 1 year that has FULLY RESOLVED by the time of evaluation, with the infant back to baseline, and for which no explanation is identified after a thorough history and exam. The witnessed episode itself involved a brief color change (perioral/facial cyanosis, though the parent describes it loosely as 'turning blue'), a marked change in tone (the infant went limp), an apparent breathing abnormality (appeared to stop breathing), and a brief period of altered responsiveness — occurring while she was mid-feed. The whole episode lasted roughly 20-30 seconds before she gasped, cried, and returned fully to her normal color, tone, and behavior; by ED arrival she is pink, alert, vigorously rooting for a bottle, and has a completely normal physical exam (normal cardiac auscultation with no murmur, normal neurologic tone and reflexes, soft flat fontanelle, no bruising or marks suggesting trauma, normal growth parameters tracking her curve). She was born at full term (39 weeks' gestation) with an uncomplicated delivery, is now 10 weeks old (postconceptional age well over 45 weeks), is up to date on immunizations, has had no fevers, cough, congestion, or ill contacts, and this is her first-ever such episode — no prior similar spells. In her panic, the infant's mother — who has no medical training — picked her up, patted her back, and gave a few rescue breaths and light chest compressions before the infant gasped and began crying; this is the detail most likely to make a learner reflexively over-classify this infant as higher-risk. The learner must actively rule out competing explanations that would change management: a seizure (no rhythmic jerking, no eye deviation, no post-ictal lethargy was witnessed or found), a primary cardiac arrhythmia such as prolonged QT (no family history of sudden unexplained death or syncope, normal cardiac exam, no murmur or arrhythmia on exam), an occult serious infection such as pertussis, UTI, or sepsis (afebrile, well-appearing, normal exam throughout), and gastroesophageal reflux with a choking/aspiration event during feeding that could fully 'explain' the episode (there was no witnessed regurgitation, choking, or gagging described, keeping it truly 'unexplained' rather than an explained reflux event). The teaching crux: per the AAP guideline, the six explicit low-risk criteria are age >60 days; gestational age >=32 weeks AND postconceptional age >=45 weeks; a first, isolated event (not recurrent, not in clusters); event duration <1 minute; NO CPR performed specifically by a trained medical provider (bystander/parental CPR does not count against low-risk); and no concerning historical or exam findings. This infant meets every single one. If the learner is swayed by the dramatic CPR history into ordering an extensive workup (labs, neuroimaging, EEG) or admitting for monitoring, they have overtested a genuinely low-risk infant, exposing her to unnecessary risk without a high likelihood of finding a treatable cause — exactly the problem the old ALTE label caused. If the learner instead fails to take a complete history/exam and casually reassures without confirming every criterion (missing, say, a heart murmur or a second prior event the parents mention only when asked directly), they risk discharging a higher-risk infant inappropriately. Correct management for this specific, confirmed low-risk infant is a brief period of observation/monitoring in the ED, shared decision-making with the family, education about BRUE and infant CPR, and outpatient follow-up — not automatic admission or a broad testing cascade.
criticalActions: Obtain a detailed, structured history of the event itself: color change, tone change, breathing pattern, responsiveness, duration, and relation to feeding, plus whether there were any prior similar episodes
Perform a complete physical exam focused on cardiac, neurologic, and growth findings and signs of trauma before applying the BRUE label

Explicitly apply all six 2016 AAP low-risk BRUE criteria: age >60 days; gestational age >=32 weeks and postconceptional age >=45 weeks; first/non-clustered event; duration <1 minute; no CPR by a trained medical provider; no concerning historical or exam findings
Recognize that CPR performed by a panicked parent or bystander does not, by itself, reclassify the infant as higher-risk
Avoid ordering extensive labs, neuroimaging, or hospital admission once the infant is correctly confirmed to meet every low-risk criterion
Engage the family in shared decision-making, provide BRUE/CPR education and reassurance, and arrange appropriate outpatient follow-up rather than defaulting to admission
learningPoints: BRUE (brief resolved unexplained event) replaced the older, nebulous term ALTE and requires the event to have fully resolved with the infant back to baseline and no explanation found after history and exam

The 2016 AAP guideline defines six explicit criteria for a lower-risk BRUE: age >60 days; born at >=32 weeks' gestation with postconceptional age >=45 weeks; a first, isolated event; duration <1 minute; no CPR performed by a trained medical provider; and no concerning historical or exam findings
CPR performed by a frightened parent or bystander does not by itself upgrade an infant to higher-risk -- only CPR performed by a trained medical provider counts against the low-risk criteria

The AAP guideline gives management recommendations only for lower-risk infants; higher-risk infants (who fail any low-risk criterion or have concerning findings) require individualized further evaluation the guideline does not standardize

Unnecessary extensive testing and hospitalization in a truly low-risk infant carries real risk without a high likelihood of finding a treatable cause or preventing a future event -- the exact problem the old ALTE label caused
cases.intro: A visibly shaken young couple carries their swaddled ten-week-old into the resuscitation bay insisting 'she stopped breathing and turned blue — we thought we lost her.' The infant on the monitor now is pink, alert, and rooting eagerly for a bottle, vitals entirely normal, as if nothing happened at all. Nobody in the room can say for certain what caused the twenty seconds that changed everyone's pulse but hers, and the chart's diagnosis line is still blank.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She was eating and all of a sudden she just went limp in my arms, her lips looked blue, and she wasn't breathing — I panicked and started doing CPR on her, and after maybe twenty seconds she just gasped and started crying like normal.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Full vitals just resulted — heart rate's 148, sats 99% on room air, temp's normal, and she's been smiling and rooting for a bottle since we got her on the monitor.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: This has never happened before. She was born right on her due date, no problems, and she's been eating and growing fine. We just don't understand what happened.

cases.title: The paramedic who felt fine at 84% (silent hypoxemia)
cases.chiefComplaint: 34M paramedic — fever and cough x6 days, found hypoxic at triage despite feeling fine
cases.diagnosis: COVID-19 pneumonia with silent ("happy") hypoxemia progressing toward early ARDS
cases.context: Marco is a 34-year-old EMT/paramedic with six days of fever, dry cough, myalgias, and loose stools after working shifts alongside a colleague later confirmed COVID-19 positive, consistent with the article's finding that up to 29% of confirmed COVID-19 cases occur in healthcare workers and that occupational/nosocomial spread is a major transmission route; he also reports mild diarrhea, present in nearly half of COVID-19 patients in the cited Chinese cohort and associated with worse disease outcomes. He is well-appearing, conversational, joking with staff, and denies any sense of breathlessness, but the triage pulse oximeter reads 84% on room air on two separate probes and fingers. This is the case's central can't-miss trap, "silent" or "happy" hypoxemia: per the Bergamo, Italy front-line experience quoted in this article, "patients do not feel dyspneic until they become profoundly hypoxic." His true diagnosis is COVID-19 pneumonia with bilateral interstitial infiltrates progressing toward ARDS; his room-air hypoxia is real and physiologic, not artifact, and must not be dismissed as a faulty probe or explained away by his calm demeanor and normal speech. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively exclude: (1) a self-limited influenza-like illness or viral URI that could be sent home given his reassuring affect and ambulatory status; (2) anxiety/hyperventilation, since he denies dyspnea despite a tachypnea of RR 24; (3) typical bacterial community-acquired pneumonia treated with antibiotics alone, missing the need for COVID-specific isolation and NIV escalation; and (4) a falsely reassuring negative rapid nasal swab, which the article warns must never be trusted alone: "if a patient looks like they have COVID-19 pneumonia, they usually end up having it... treat them as COVID-19 pneumonia, with isolation, and repeat the testing in 3 days." Bedside vitals: HR 98, BP 118/76, RR 24, T 38.6C, SpO2 84% RA, rising only to about 90% on 4L nasal cannula. Lung exam reveals scattered bibasilar crackles; bedside lung ultrasound (more sensitive than chest x-ray per the article) would show a diffuse B-line pattern, which correlates with good PEEP responsiveness. If unrecognized (dismissed as anxiety or probe error, discharged home without pulse-ox counseling, or managed with antibiotics and low-flow oxygen alone without escalation), he will silently desaturate further over hours toward severe ARDS requiring emergent intubation, mirroring the Bergamo surge data cited in the article where roughly 5% of presenting COVID-19 patients developed severe ARDS. The evidence-based approach: isolate him immediately in a negative-pressure room regardless of a pending or negative swab, obtain lung ultrasound, trend oxygenation closely, and, because most hypoxic COVID-19 patients are PEEP responders, escalate promptly to helmet CPAP/noninvasive ventilation as a bridge to a ward or ICU bed, admitting him primarily because of his hypoxia rather than his stated symptoms, and reserving intubation for NIV failure.
criticalActions: Trust the pulse oximeter over the patient's reported comfort — recognize silent/"happy" hypoxemia rather than dismissing a low SpO2 as probe error or anxiety
Place the patient in a negative-pressure isolation room and treat as COVID-19 pneumonia even if the initial nasal swab is negative; plan to repeat testing in 3 days
Obtain a lung ultrasound to look for a diffuse B-line pattern, which is more sensitive than chest x-ray and predicts PEEP responsiveness
Start supplemental oxygen and escalate promptly to helmet CPAP/noninvasive ventilation for hypoxia unresponsive to standard oxygen, using it as a PEEP-responsive bridge to an inpatient/ICU bed
Admit based on the severity of hypoxia, not on the patient's subjective symptoms or comfort, and reserve intubation for NIV failure
Counsel any febrile patient discharged home to check home pulse oximetry and return immediately if respiratory symptoms develop or worsen
learningPoints: COVID-19 pneumonia can cause "silent" or "happy" hypoxemia — patients often do not feel dyspneic until they are profoundly hypoxic, so pulse oximetry, not patient-reported breathlessness, should drive triage and escalation
A single negative nasal swab does not rule out COVID-19: if the clinical picture fits, isolate and treat as COVID-19 pneumonia and repeat testing in 3 days
Lung ultrasound is more sensitive than chest x-ray for COVID-19 pneumonia, and a diffuse B-line pattern correlates with a good response to PEEP
Most hypoxic COVID-19 patients are PEEP responders — helmet CPAP/noninvasive ventilation can safely buy time and bridge patients to an ICU bed, with intubation reserved for NIV failure
During a COVID-19 surge, admission decisions should be driven primarily by the degree of hypoxia, not by how unwell the patient feels or appears
cases.intro: The triage nurse pulls you aside before you even reach the curtain: his pulse ox keeps reading 84, and she has already swapped the probe twice. Inside, a 34-year-old paramedic sits upright, scrolling his phone and chatting easily, insisting he just feels a little run down after a week of fever and a cough that will not quit. He denies any trouble breathing and seems mildly annoyed to be here, saying his crew made him come in. His face and his vitals monitor are telling two very different stories.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Honestly I feel fine, just tired and this cough is annoying. I can breathe just fine, I don't know why everyone is making a big deal out of this.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: His sat read 84 percent twice, on two different fingers with a fresh probe, doctor. He doesn't look short of breath at all, but I really don't like this number.

cases.title: Carotid dissection after a chiropractor visit (the young stroke nobody screens for)
cases.chiefComplaint: 29M neck pain and headache after chiropractic adjustment, now with facial droop and slurred speech
cases.diagnosis: Right internal carotid artery dissection with intramural thrombus causing embolic ischemic stroke of the right MCA territory
cases.context: Marcus Delgado is a 29-year-old competitive-level recreational cyclist and CrossFit enthusiast with no past medical history who is actually suffering an ischemic stroke of the right MCA territory caused by a spontaneous dissection of the right internal carotid artery (ICA), precipitated three days ago by a chiropractic cervical manipulation performed for training-related neck tightness. The manipulation sheared the intimal layer of the ICA, creating a subintimal (intramural) hematoma that both narrowed the vessel lumen and created a nidus for thrombus formation; a fragment of that thrombus has now embolized distally into the right MCA, producing the current left hemiparesis, subtle left visual/spatial neglect, and mild dysarthria. Since the manipulation he has had a constant dull right-sided neck ache and an ipsilateral headache that he and his girlfriend attributed to muscle strain and training soreness (competing diagnosis 1: cervicalgia/muscle strain from lifting). He also reports an intermittent throbbing, whooshing sound in his right ear (pulsatile tinnitus - a classic but frequently overlooked dissection clue from turbulent flow through the narrowed, dissected vessel) and, if specifically examined, has a subtle right partial Horner syndrome (about 2mm ptosis and miosis of the right eye, no anhidrosis, since sympathetic fibers to facial sweat glands travel with the external rather than internal carotid) from disruption of the pericarotid sympathetic plexus. He has no fever, no history of migraine, and no photophobia or phonophobia to suggest migraine (competing diagnosis 2), and a tension-type headache read (competing diagnosis 3) is tempting given the benign-seeming precipitant but does not explain the new focal deficits. The learner must recognize that in a young, previously healthy patient this constellation - minor mechanical neck trauma, followed by neck pain and headache, followed hours to days later by focal neurologic deficits - is the textbook two-stage presentation of cervical artery dissection, one of the most common identifiable causes of ischemic stroke in adults under 45, and that age alone must never be used to take stroke off the differential. If unrecognized and treated as musculoskeletal pain (discharged with NSAIDs and a muscle relaxant), the intramural thrombus will continue to propagate and embolize, and Marcus deficits will progress from mild dysarthria and drift to dense hemiplegia, worsening neglect, and possible malignant MCA edema over the following 24-48 hours - permanent, disabling injury in an otherwise healthy young adult. The correct approach is emergent stroke-code activation, noncontrast CT head, and CT angiography (or MRA) of the head AND neck - essential, since a standard CT head alone will miss the dissection - to identify the intimal flap/false lumen and any distal occlusion; dissection is not a contraindication to standard acute stroke therapy, and Marcus should be evaluated for IV thrombolysis and/or mechanical thrombectomy per usual eligibility criteria if within window, followed by antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation per shared decision-making) for secondary prevention and stroke-unit admission with vascular neurology involvement.
criticalActions: Keep cervical artery dissection and stroke on the differential for a young patient with neck pain/headache plus new focal neuro deficits - do not anchor on muscle strain or migraine
Perform a focused exam for dissection clues: ipsilateral Horner syndrome (ptosis/miosis) and pulsatile tinnitus
Activate a stroke code, establish last-known-well time, and obtain emergent noncontrast CT head plus CTA (or MRA) of the head AND neck
Evaluate for IV thrombolysis and/or mechanical thrombectomy per standard eligibility criteria - dissection is not itself a contraindication to acute stroke therapy
Start antithrombotic therapy (antiplatelet or anticoagulation per shared decision-making) once hemorrhage is excluded, for secondary stroke prevention
Admit to a stroke unit with vascular neurology involvement and manage blood pressure per acute stroke protocol rather than aggressively lowering it
learningPoints: Cervical artery dissection is among the most common identifiable causes of ischemic stroke in adults under 45, but is easily missed because early symptoms mimic benign neck strain or migraine

A trivial-seeming mechanical trigger - chiropractic neck manipulation, sports involving neck hyperextension/rotation, even vigorous coughing - can tear the arterial wall and precede stroke symptoms by hours to days
Ipsilateral Horner syndrome (ptosis and miosis without anhidrosis) and pulsatile tinnitus are classic, frequently overlooked exam findings that point specifically to carotid artery dissection
Age alone should never remove stroke from the differential - a young, previously healthy patient with headache/neck pain plus new focal deficits needs a stroke workup
CT or MR angiography of the head AND neck, not the head alone, is required to diagnose dissection, and dissection does not preclude standard acute stroke therapies like thrombolysis or thrombectomy
cases.intro: Marcus is a 29-year-old cyclist brought in by his girlfriend after she noticed the left side of his face looked droopy over dinner. He mentions his neck has been stiff and achy since a chiropractor cracked it three days ago for training tightness, and he has had a dull, one-sided headache ever since that he chalked up to muscle tension. His girlfriend says he seemed a little off tonight - slurring some words and fumbling when he reached for his water glass - but he insists he is fine and just needs some ibuprofen.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My neck has been sore since Tuesday, I saw my chiropractor for it. Probably just from lifting, I hit legs pretty hard this week.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He was totally fine an hour ago, then all of a sudden he could not hold his fork right and his words got kind of slurry. I thought he was just tired.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Blood pressure is a little up on the monitor, he keeps rubbing the right side of his neck, and I noticed his right eyelid looks a touch droopier than the left.

cases.title: The dog bite to a child's face (the one you actually should close)
cases.chiefComplaint: 5M — dog bite to the face 45 minutes ago
cases.diagnosis: Facial (cheek/upper-lip) laceration-puncture wound from an unprovoked dog bite requiring irrigation and debridement with primary closure, high-risk-wound antibiotic prophylaxis, tetanus status verification, and a structured rabies-exposure risk assessment (PEP deferred pending 10-day observation of the known, apparently healthy dog)
cases.context: A 5-year-old boy, Mason, was bitten on the face by the family's next-door neighbor's dog, Duke, a mid-size mixed-breed retriever, about 45 minutes ago while reaching through the yard fence to pet him. The neighbors are traveling this week and reachable only by phone; they report Duke has never bitten anyone before, is current on rabies vaccination, and is confined to their fenced yard, available for a 10-day observation period. Duke's actual vaccine paperwork cannot be verified today. The bite was essentially unprovoked (petting through a fence, not teasing or cornering). On exam: a 3 cm stellate laceration with an adjacent 1 cm puncture over the left cheek and upper lip, not crossing the vermilion border, sparing the parotid duct and facial nerve branches on exam, with moderate crushed and devitalized wound-edge tissue and mild bleeding controlled by pressure. Full skin exam shows no other bites. Vitals are normal aside from tachycardia from crying; no bony step-off, malocclusion, or crepitus is felt, though a dog's crushing bite force can fracture facial bones or the skull in a small child, so this should be actively excluded, imaging if exam is suspicious. Tetanus-containing vaccine was last given at the 4-year well-child visit (about 13 months ago). The family is anxious, asking whether the cut needs stitches, whether he needs 'a rabies shot right now,' and antibiotics. Pitfalls to actively avoid: (1) Reflexively leaving the wound open or delaying closure the way one might for a hand or heavily-punctured bite elsewhere — facial wounds carry low infection risk from excellent vascularity, and evidence favors primary closure after thorough irrigation and debridement of devitalized tissue for cosmetic outcome, provided there is no prolonged (>6 hour) delay in presentation, which this patient does not have. (2) Either skipping antibiotics entirely because 'it's just a dog bite' or over-treating with IV antibiotics/admission — this wound's crush/devitalized tissue requiring debridement supports oral amoxicillin-clavulanate (45 mg/kg/day divided twice daily, 3-5 days) as an outpatient, since there are no systemic infection signs, immunocompromise, or joint/bone involvement. (3) Panicking into empiric rabies postexposure prophylaxis solely because the vaccination record can't be confirmed today — the correct approach is a structured risk assessment: Duke is a known, apparently healthy domestic dog, the attack was unprovoked but not from a wild or stray animal, and he can be confined and observed for 10 days, so PEP can be deferred while animal control/public health arranges and documents that observation, escalating only if Duke becomes symptomatic or unavailable. (4) Forgetting to verify tetanus status and give a booster if the wound is judged contaminated/crushed (5-year window) rather than clean (10-year window), and forgetting to actively exclude facial or skull fracture given the crushing force involved, since missed cranial injury or an unrecognized skull puncture progressing to brain abscess are described complications of dog bites to a child's head.
criticalActions: Thoroughly irrigate and debride the wound, removing devitalized tissue, before deciding on closure
Close the facial wound primarily rather than leaving it open, given the low infection risk from excellent facial vascularity
Prescribe oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for this crush/devitalized-tissue wound rather than withholding antibiotics or escalating to IV therapy/admission
Confirm the child's tetanus immunization status and give a booster if the wound is contaminated/crushed and immunization is more than 5 years old
Perform a structured rabies risk assessment (species, provoked vs unprovoked, dog's health status, availability for observation) instead of reflexively starting rabies PEP
Arrange 10-day observation of the known, healthy-appearing dog through animal control/public health rather than empiric rabies prophylaxis
learningPoints: Facial bite wounds have a low infection risk because of excellent vascularity and are generally closed primarily after irrigation and debridement, unlike hand or puncture-heavy bites, which are often left open.

Children sustain a disproportionate share of dog bites to the head and face because of their smaller stature — up to 73% of dog bites in children under 9 are facial, versus about 30% of bites overall.

Prophylactic antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) are reserved for higher-risk bite wounds — deep punctures, crush/devitalized tissue needing debridement, hand/foot/joint location, or delayed presentation — not given reflexively to every bite.

Rabies postexposure prophylaxis is unnecessary for a bite from a known, apparently healthy domestic dog that can be confined and observed for 10 days; PEP is reserved for unprovoked attacks by animals that cannot be located or observed.

A dog's crushing bite force (200-450+ psi) can fracture facial bones or the skull even when the visible wound looks minor, so head and face dog bites in small children warrant careful exam and imaging when suspicious, since missed cranial injury or occult brain abscess are recognized complications.
cases.intro: A frightened 5-year-old boy is carried into the room by his mother, a bloodstained washcloth pressed to his cheek. 'He was just petting the neighbor's dog through the fence and it snapped at him out of nowhere,' she says, close to tears. 'They're out of town this week — I don't even know if that dog's shots are up to date. Does he need a rabies shot right now?' The boy whimpers as the nurse gently peels back the washcloth to reveal a jagged wound near his mouth.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He was just petting the neighbor's dog through the fence and it bit him out of nowhere. They're out of town this week, I don't even know if that dog's shots are up to date.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It hurts really bad and I don't want a shot.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Vitals are stable, he's crying but consolable. The cheek wound's still oozing a little but it's not spurting.

cases.title: Two seizures, one fever (when 'complex' doesn't mean catastrophic)
cases.chiefComplaint: 18mo F — fever x3 days + 2 seizures in 8 hours, now postictal
cases.diagnosis: Complex febrile seizure — meets the 2011 AAP complex criteria on two counts (first seizure lasted 18 minutes, exceeding the 15-minute simple-seizure cutoff; second brief generalized seizure recurred within the same 24-hour illness), but both events were generalized without focal features. Underlying trigger is an acute febrile viral illness (clinical picture consistent with an HHV-6-type illness/roseola, rash not yet erupted). Bacterial meningitis/encephalitis must be actively excluded on history and exam given the complex features, but because the child is fully immunized, has had no antibiotic pretreatment, and returns completely to her neurologic baseline with a reassuring exam (no meningismus, no focal deficit, no persistently altered mental status), she does not meet criteria for reflexive neuroimaging or lumbar puncture — workup should instead be directed at identifying the fever source.
cases.context: Per the 2011 AAP guideline this article reviews, a febrile seizure is a seizure with temperature >38°C in a child aged 6-60 months without CNS infection. Simple febrile seizures are generalized, last <15 minutes, and occur only once in 24 hours; complex febrile seizures are defined by meeting ANY of: duration ≥15 minutes, focal onset/features, or recurrence more than once in 24 hours — the 15-minute cutoff exists because seizures beyond that duration are considered more likely to reflect something other than a routine self-limited febrile seizure. This patient, Maya, is an 18-month-old girl on day 3 of fever (Tmax 39.5°C at home, responsive to antipyretics) with no rash yet and only mild pharyngeal erythema on exam — no clear infectious source has been localized, a picture consistent with a viral illness such as roseola (HHV-6, which the article specifically links to increased febrile seizure risk). Her first seizure, witnessed by her mother, was generalized tonic-clonic and lasted about 18 minutes (EMS was called), meeting the DURATION complex criterion; roughly 6 hours later she had a second brief (~3-minute) generalized seizure in triage, meeting the RECURRENCE complex criterion. Neither event had any focal component. She is fully immunized and has had no antibiotics for this illness — unlike the article's own unvaccinated, antibiotic-pretreated case, which raises concern because antibiotics can partially mask evolving meningitis. Three competing frames the learner must actively work through: (1) dismissing this as a routine simple febrile seizure needing no real evaluation — wrong, because duration and recurrence both exceed the simple-seizure thresholds; (2) bacterial meningitis/encephalitis — must be actively excluded by a careful exam (nuchal rigidity, bulging fontanelle, petechiae, persistent obtundation), since postictal drowsiness can superficially mimic a worrisome mental status; (3) an unprovoked seizure/emerging epilepsy — this is not that: there is a clear antecedent measured fever, no prior afebrile seizures, and a maternal history of childhood febrile seizures (the genetic predisposition the article describes, seen in a substantial minority of cases). The can't-miss teaching point: meeting 'complex' criteria by duration and/or recurrence alone, in a fully immunized child with no antibiotic pretreatment who convincingly returns to full neurologic baseline with a normal detailed exam, does not by itself mandate neuroimaging or LP — that escalation is driven by focal findings, incomplete return to baseline, or infection risk factors, not the complex label alone. Vitals on arrival: HR 150, BP 92/58, RR 28, O2 99% RA, Temp 39.3°C, Sinus Tachycardia. Correct management (source-directed workup, no reflexive LP/imaging, no prophylactic antiepileptics, solid anticipatory guidance including the ~25%-40% recurrence risk) leads to a safe, timely discharge. Missing a true red flag, or conversely over-testing a reassuring child, both represent failure modes.
criticalActions: Elicit exact seizure duration, focality, and count within 24h to correctly classify simple vs complex febrile seizure
Perform a detailed, serial neurologic exam confirming full return to baseline mental status before considering discharge
Screen for meningismus, bulging fontanelle, petechiae, immunization status, and any antibiotic pretreatment to risk-stratify for bacterial meningitis/encephalitis
Search for and identify the source of fever rather than reflexively ordering neuroimaging or LP based on the complex label alone
Do not initiate prophylactic antiepileptic medication for febrile seizure recurrence prevention
Counsel family on febrile seizure recurrence risk and return precautions before discharge
learningPoints: AAP 2011 criteria: simple febrile seizure is generalized, <15 minutes, once per 24 hours; complex febrile seizure meets any one of duration ≥15 minutes, focal features, or recurrence more than once in 24 hours
Febrile seizures occur in children 6-60 months with temperature >38°C and no CNS infection; about 25%-40% of children with a febrile seizure will have at least one recurrence
Certain viral illnesses, including HHV-6 (roseola) and influenza A, carry increased risk of triggering febrile seizures
Meeting complex criteria by duration or recurrence alone does not automatically mandate neuroimaging or lumbar puncture in a child who fully returns to neurologic baseline with a reassuring exam
Unvaccinated status and recent antibiotic exposure raise concern for occult or partially masked serious infection and should lower the threshold for further workup
Prophylactic antiepileptic medications are not used to prevent febrile seizure recurrence; family counseling is the mainstay of ED management
cases.intro: Maya is curled against her mother's chest in Bay 4, cheeks flushed and damp, blinking slowly as she comes out of a deep sleep. Her mother is still gripping her phone, the paramedics' number scrawled on her palm in pen. Between rocking Maya and wiping her own eyes, she keeps starting sentences about 'the shaking' and losing her train of thought. A nurse hovers nearby with a temporal thermometer, waiting for your cue.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: She's had this fever for three days and today she just — her whole body started jerking, it felt like forever before it stopped.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: Then it happened again on the way here, a shorter one, and I don't understand, is that normal, is something wrong with her brain?

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Temp's 39.3 now, she's rousable but still pretty groggy. Chart says the first episode ran about eighteen minutes before it broke on its own.

cases.title: Dark urine and puffy eyes, two weeks after a sore throat (the hypertensive one)
cases.chiefComplaint: 9M with tea-colored urine and periorbital swelling x1 day
cases.diagnosis: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) with hypertensive urgency
cases.context: Malik is a 9-year-old boy who presents with one day of dark, tea-colored urine and puffy eyelids noticed by his mother this morning. About two weeks ago he had a sore throat that was never seen by a doctor and resolved on its own; he has also had a few small scabbed sores on his shins for roughly ten days. The true diagnosis is post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN), a form of postinfectious glomerulonephritis: antibodies generated against beta-hemolytic Streptococcus from the preceding throat or skin infection cross-react with host glomerular antigens through molecular mimicry, depositing immune complexes that damage the glomerular capillary wall and basement membrane. This causes red blood cell extravasation into urine (cola-colored macroscopic hematuria), impairs glomerular filtration leading to sodium and water retention (periorbital edema, hypertension), and allows protein leak (proteinuria). The latency between the streptococcal infection and renal symptoms is typically one to three weeks, distinguishing PSGN from IgA nephropathy, in which gross hematuria recurs within one to two days of an upper respiratory infection with persistent microscopic hematuria between episodes; the learner should specifically ask about timing to differentiate these. Other competing diagnoses to actively exclude: a UTI or hemorrhagic cystitis (would show dysuria, frequency, fever, and pyuria/bacteria rather than an RBC-predominant sediment with heavy protein); and benign causes of discolored urine such as vigorous exercise, minor trauma, dehydration, or pigmenturia/food dye, which are urinalysis-negative for blood, unlike this patient. The can't-miss trap is the blood pressure: hypertension in a child with hematuria is a high-risk feature that must never be dismissed as incidental, because untreated postinfectious glomerulonephritis with hypertension can rapidly progress to hypertensive emergency with encephalopathy (headache, vomiting, altered mental status, seizure) plus worsening acute kidney injury, fluid overload, and pulmonary edema. On arrival, vitals show a markedly elevated blood pressure for a 9-year-old, mild tachycardia, and a low-grade temperature; exam shows periorbital and mild pretibial edema, no purpuric rash, no costovertebral angle tenderness, and clear lungs. Urinalysis shows 3+ blood and 2+ protein, with many RBCs and occasional RBC casts on microscopy. Labs would show an elevated BUN and creatinine for age, and a low complement C3. The correct trajectory recognizes the hypertension early, treats it urgently, restricts fluid and sodium, confirms recent streptococcal exposure with low C3, and involves nephrology with admission for close monitoring of blood pressure, renal function, and fluid status — averting hypertensive emergency and further renal injury; missing it risks seizure or renal failure.
criticalActions: Obtain an accurate blood pressure and recognize hypertension as a high-risk feature in a child with hematuria
Send urinalysis with microscopy to confirm glomerular hematuria (RBCs, protein, RBC casts)
Ask specifically about a preceding throat or skin infection and its timing to support postinfectious glomerulonephritis over IgA nephropathy
Check renal function (BUN/creatinine), evidence of recent streptococcal infection, and complement C3
Treat hypertension urgently and avoid aggressive IV fluids that could worsen fluid overload
Consult nephrology and admit for monitoring of blood pressure, renal function, and fluid status
learningPoints: Cola-colored macroscopic hematuria suggests upper urinary tract/glomerular bleeding, while bright-red blood suggests lower urinary tract pathology
Hypertension in a child with hematuria is a high-risk feature that must prompt urgent evaluation, never reassurance
