Postinfectious glomerulonephritis follows streptococcal throat or skin infection by one to three weeks via molecular mimicry, unlike IgA nephropathy's gross hematuria within one to two days of a URI
Untreated hypertension from postinfectious glomerulonephritis can progress to hypertensive emergency with encephalopathy and worsening acute kidney injury
Discolored urine is not always hematuria — always confirm with urinalysis before assuming a glomerular cause
cases.intro: Malik's mother says his pee looked like a Coke can this morning and his eyes were puffy when he woke up, so she brought him straight in. He's quiet and a little tired but answers questions, mentioning his throat hurt 'a couple weeks ago' though nobody took him to a doctor for it. His mother seems more annoyed that he'll miss his soccer game than worried, and is surprised when the triage nurse frowns at the blood pressure cuff readout.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: His pee looked like a Coke can this morning, that's not normal, right? He said his throat was sore a couple weeks ago but it went away on its own.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: My head kind of hurts and I'm really tired. I don't want to get up.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: His blood pressure just came back high for his age, and the repeat reading is even higher than the first one.

cases.title: The eye that keeps bulging (a clock you can't wait out)
cases.chiefComplaint: 29M facial trauma, worsening right eye pressure and blurry vision after a punch
cases.diagnosis: Traumatic retrobulbar hemorrhage causing acute orbital compartment syndrome with optic nerve/central retinal artery compression — requires emergent bedside lateral canthotomy and inferior cantholysis
cases.context: Derek is a 29-year-old man who presents roughly 90 minutes after being punched twice in the right periorbital region during a bar fight; he denies any object striking the eye and there is no glass or metal fragment. The ground truth is traumatic retrobulbar hemorrhage causing acute orbital compartment syndrome: blunt trauma tore an orbital vessel, and blood is accumulating in the retrobulbar space. Per the underlying pathophysiology, the real damage from retrobulbar hemorrhage is optic nerve ischemia — because the bony orbit has limited capacity to expand, the accumulating blood produces a true compartment syndrome that compresses the optic nerve and/or its vascular supply, including the central retinal artery, causing progressive and potentially irreversible vision loss if the pressure is not relieved. Three competing diagnoses must be actively excluded. First, a simple periorbital hematoma ('black eye') or subconjunctival hemorrhage is a non-vision-threatening bruise needing only observation and cold packs, distinguished by a soft, normally-positioned globe with no true proptosis or pressure rise. Second, an isolated orbital (blowout) fracture without compartment syndrome may cause pain, diplopia, and ecchymosis, but the globe stays soft/normotensive and vision is preserved, so it can safely await CT. Third, an open-globe (ruptured globe) injury is also vision-threatening, but the globe is soft or irregularly shaped, intraocular pressure is characteristically LOW rather than high, the anterior chamber may be shallow, the pupil may be peaked/teardrop-shaped, and a positive Seidel sign may be present; it is managed with a protective shield and avoidance of any globe pressure, not canthotomy. Derek's exam instead shows unmistakable compartment-syndrome findings: measurable proptosis (Hertel exophthalmometry ~24mm OD vs 19mm OS), a rock-hard, tense globe that resists gentle retropulsion, visual acuity that has fallen from 20/40 to counting fingers over the last 30 minutes, a grade 2+ relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) on the right, and intraocular pressure by tonometry of roughly 48-52 mmHg (normal 10-21 mmHg). These findings mandate emergent bedside lateral canthotomy and inferior cantholysis — the only ED-performable maneuver that decompresses the orbit — rather than waiting for CT imaging or for ophthalmology to physically arrive, because sustained elevation of orbital/intraocular pressure at this magnitude causes irreversible optic nerve and retinal ischemia and permanent blindness within roughly 60-100 minutes. Adjunctive measures — elevating the head of the bed 30°, and controlling pain, agitation, vomiting, and hypertension, all of which further raise intraocular pressure — buy a little time but never substitute for decompression. Performed correctly, canthotomy/cantholysis produces immediate softening of the globe, a measurable drop in IOP, and stabilizing or improving vision.
criticalActions: Recognize orbital compartment syndrome clinically from proptosis, a tense/firm globe, rising IOP, RAPD, and falling visual acuity — do not wait for CT to confirm
Measure intraocular pressure by bedside tonometry
Perform emergent bedside lateral canthotomy and inferior cantholysis without delaying for imaging or ophthalmology arrival once vision is threatened
Reduce factors that further raise intraocular pressure: elevate the head of bed 30°, control pain, nausea/vomiting, agitation, and hypertension
Obtain emergent ophthalmology consultation in parallel with, not instead of, performing the canthotomy
Reassess visual acuity and IOP after canthotomy/cantholysis to confirm improvement; extend to superior cantholysis if pressure fails to normalize
learningPoints: Retrobulbar hemorrhage causes vision loss through an orbital compartment syndrome, not blood loss itself — the bony orbit's limited capacity to expand lets accumulating blood compress the optic nerve and its vascular supply.

Diagnosis is clinical: proptosis, a tense/firm globe on palpation, worsening visual acuity, a relative afferent pupillary defect, and elevated intraocular pressure — CT can confirm but must never delay treatment.
Lateral canthotomy and inferior cantholysis is an ED-performable bedside procedure that must be done emergently when vision is threatened, without waiting for ophthalmology or imaging.

Distinguish orbital compartment syndrome (tense globe, high IOP) from open-globe injury (soft or irregular globe, low IOP, possible Seidel sign) before intervening, since their management is opposite.
Irreversible vision loss can occur within roughly 60-100 minutes of sustained elevated orbital pressure, making this one of the true can't-wait procedures in emergency medicine.
cases.intro: Derek, 29, sits hunched in the trauma bay pressing an ice pack to his swollen right eye, insisting he just needs a couple of stitches and to go home after getting into it with a guy at a bar. The triage nurse notes his right eye looks noticeably more swollen than it did twenty minutes ago, and he keeps squinting hard against the overhead lights. He mentions the eye feels like it's being pushed out of his skull, but waves it off as just pain from the punch.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: It's not that bad, it just feels really tight back there, like something's pushing on it from the inside, and things look kind of dim out of that side now.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Doctor, his right eye looks a lot more swollen and pushed-out than when we roomed him ten minutes ago, and he says the vision on that side keeps getting worse.

cases.title: The wrist x-ray that lied (occult scaphoid fracture)
cases.chiefComplaint: 22M - right wrist pain after a fall onto an outstretched hand
cases.diagnosis: Occult (radiographically negative) non-displaced fracture of the scaphoid waist
cases.context: This 22-year-old right-hand-dominant man fell onto an outstretched right hand while skateboarding and now has persistent radial-sided wrist pain two days later. The true diagnosis is a non-displaced fracture through the waist of the scaphoid, the most frequently fractured carpal bone, which is NOT visible on the initial plain wrist radiographs (including a dedicated scaphoid view) -- a classic diagnostic trap, since early scaphoid fracture lines are frequently radiographically occult and a meaningful proportion of true fractures are missed on the first set of films. The scaphoid's blood supply is predominantly retrograde, entering distally near the tubercle and coursing proximally, so a waist or proximal-pole fracture can silently devascularize the proximal fragment; if this fracture is not recognized and immobilized, the patient is at high risk for avascular necrosis of the proximal pole, nonunion, and eventual scaphoid nonunion advanced collapse (SNAC wrist) with chronic pain and early degenerative arthritis. On exam he has focal tenderness over the anatomic snuffbox (bounded by extensor pollicis brevis/abductor pollicis longus radially and extensor pollicis longus ulnarly) and over the volar scaphoid tubercle, plus pain with axial loading (telescoping) of the thumb; the wrist is mildly swollen but neurovascularly intact with full digit range of motion and no gross deformity. Two to three plausible distractors the learner must actively rule out: (1) a simple wrist sprain -- tempting because the x-ray is normal and he is otherwise well-appearing, but a sprain should not produce focal, reproducible snuffbox tenderness; (2) a distal radius buckle (torus) fracture -- the film may show a subtle, real but incidental radial metaphyseal buckle, and anchoring on this visible injury risks distracting from the occult carpal injury and prematurely closing the case; (3) a scapholunate ligament sprain, which also causes dorsal wrist pain but localizes to the scapholunate interval rather than the snuffbox. The can-not-miss teaching point mirrored by this article's own teaching vignette (a young athlete with wrist trauma who has films including a scaphoid view and asks when he can return to play) is that a normal initial x-ray does NOT exclude a scaphoid fracture: any patient with snuffbox or scaphoid tubercle tenderness after a fall on an outstretched hand must be treated as a presumed occult scaphoid fracture regardless of imaging, placed in a thumb spica splint, kept non-weight-bearing on that hand, and referred for orthopedic/hand follow-up with repeat radiographs in 10-14 days (once resorption at the fracture line can make it visible) or earlier advanced imaging (CT or MRI) if available. Correct management -- presumptive splinting plus structured follow-up -- protects the fragile blood supply and preserves healing potential; discharging the patient without a splint because "the x-ray is normal" is the error that leads to nonunion and avascular necrosis. Vitals are entirely normal throughout the encounter -- this is an isolated orthopedic injury with no systemic derangement expected.
criticalActions: Palpate the anatomic snuffbox and volar scaphoid tubercle for focal tenderness
Test for pain with axial loading (telescoping) of the thumb
Order wrist radiographs including a dedicated scaphoid view
Apply a thumb spica splint presumptively even though the initial x-ray is normal
Keep the patient non-weight-bearing on the hand and arrange orthopedic/hand follow-up with repeat imaging in 1-2 weeks
Document a neurovascular exam of the hand distal to the injury
learningPoints: A meaningful proportion of true scaphoid fractures are invisible on the initial plain radiographs because early fracture lines are radiographically occult.
Anatomic snuffbox tenderness and scaphoid tubercle tenderness are the key exam findings that should trigger presumptive treatment even when the x-ray looks normal.
The scaphoid receives a predominantly retrograde blood supply entering distally, so a waist or proximal-pole fracture can cut off flow to the proximal fragment.
An unrecognized or unsplinted scaphoid fracture carries a high risk of avascular necrosis and nonunion, leading to chronic wrist dysfunction and early arthritis.
The correct approach to a clinically suspected but radiographically occult scaphoid fracture is thumb spica immobilization plus follow-up or advanced imaging, not discharge without a splint.
cases.intro: A 22-year-old man is wheeled back after landing hard on his outstretched right hand while skateboarding. He is guarding his wrist and winces when the nurse checks his grip strength, asking if he can "just get an x-ray and go" before his shift tomorrow. The tech mutters that the films look basically normal before they have even finished loading on the screen.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: I caught myself on my hand when I fell off my board, and now my wrist is killing me right along my thumb side.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Vitals are all normal, he is just guarding the right wrist. Pain is worse when I press near the base of his thumb.

speech.speaker: patient
speech.text: Can you just tell me if it is broken? I really cannot be in a splint for weeks, I have got a shift tomorrow.

cases.title: The floppy, jittery newborn (it is the formula, not sepsis)
cases.chiefComplaint: 3-day-old girl — jerking episodes, lethargy, sunken fontanelle after vomiting
cases.diagnosis: Acute severe symptomatic hyponatremia with neonatal seizures, caused by improperly (over-diluted) mixed powdered infant formula compounded by vomiting-related hypotonic fluid losses (hyponatremic dehydration)
cases.context: True diagnosis: acute severe symptomatic hyponatremia with neonatal seizures in a 3-day-old girl. Her father has been mixing her powdered formula himself and is unsure his measurements have been exact — most likely adding too much water relative to powder — and today she began vomiting, worsening the free-water load while she keeps being re-fed the same over-diluted, hypotonic formula, driving her serum sodium rapidly downward (a hyponatremic-dehydration picture). Pathophysiology: neonatal total-body water and ion-channel-regulated fluid compartments have almost no buffer for rapid extracellular osmolar shifts; as serum sodium falls, water moves into brain cells faster than the immature brain can compensate, producing cerebral edema that presents as lethargy and intermittent jerking (seizure) activity, with a sunken (not bulging) anterior fontanelle reflecting the concurrent volume depletion from vomiting. Vitals show compensated shock: HR 200 (tachycardia is the primary and earliest compensatory sign of shock in infants — hypotension is a late, ominous finding), BP 60/30, RR 30, afebrile at 37.0C, sat 100% room air. Competing diagnoses to actively exclude: neonatal sepsis/bacterial meningitis (fever is frequently absent in neonatal sepsis, so lethargy plus jerking could easily be read as a septic neonate needing only cultures, a lumbar puncture, and empiric antibiotics); hypoglycemic seizure (a stat fingerstick glucose must be checked, per standard prehospital/ED practice of correcting hypoglycemia immediately in any altered or seizing infant); and dismissing the movements as benign newborn jitteriness, reflux, or an over-anxious first-time father misreading a fussy baby. The can't-miss trap: because she is afebrile and superficially looks like a routine rule-out-sepsis neonate, it is easy to send blood cultures and an LP while never sending a basic metabolic panel/serum sodium — missing the actual, rapidly reversible cause. Deterioration if unrecognized: worsening cerebral edema drives refractory seizures, herniation, respiratory arrest, and death, and continued feeding with the same over-diluted formula keeps pushing sodium lower. Correct approach: treat the seizing, severely hyponatremic neonate as an emergency — hypertonic (3%) saline boluses to acutely raise sodium a few mEq/L and terminate the seizure/reverse cerebral edema, isotonic volume support for the hypovolemic component, IV or IO access if peripheral access fails (vascular access is difficult in a dehydrated, collapsed-vein neonate), airway suctioning given vomiting plus altered mental status, then correcting the remaining sodium deficit slowly (rapid overcorrection risks osmotic demyelination), a careful feeding-preparation history to uncover the improperly mixed formula, caregiver education on correct reconstitution, frequent repeat sodium checks, and PICU admission for continued monitoring.
criticalActions: Obtain a stat point-of-care glucose and a basic metabolic panel/serum sodium in any neonate with seizure-like activity, even without fever
Recognize severe symptomatic hyponatremia (seizure, altered mental status) as an emergency requiring hypertonic (3%) saline, not just a routine sepsis workup
Support the airway and suction as needed given vomiting plus altered mental status; obtain IV or IO access early since neonatal veins are difficult when dehydrated
Correct the sodium deficit cautiously after acute stabilization to avoid rapid overcorrection and osmotic demyelination
Take a careful feeding-preparation history to uncover improperly (over-diluted) mixed formula and counsel the caregiver on safe reconstitution
Avoid anchoring on sepsis, febrile seizure, or reflux without checking electrolytes; admit for PICU-level monitoring
learningPoints: Seizures in a neonate are not always febrile seizures or sepsis — always check a stat glucose and sodium.
Improperly (over-)diluted powdered infant formula is a classic, preventable cause of acute hyponatremic seizures in young infants.
GI losses replaced with more hypotonic formula worsen hyponatremia and can precipitate cerebral edema and seizures.
Severe symptomatic hyponatremia is treated with hypertonic (3%) saline to acutely raise sodium and reverse cerebral edema, then corrected slowly to avoid osmotic demyelination.
Tachycardia is the primary compensatory sign of shock in infants — hypotension is a late, ominous finding.
cases.intro: A young father rushes into triage cradling his three-day-old daughter, who has gone from fussy to eerily floppy over the last few hours, with brief jerking spells that have him terrified. He admits, almost apologetically, that he has been mixing her formula on his own and is not entirely sure he has been getting the scoops right. She is afebrile and tachycardic, and her soft spot looks sunken rather than full, and nobody in the room can yet agree whether this is just a fussy newborn, a seizure, or something more ominous brewing underneath.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: I mix her formula myself every time — I do not know, maybe I got the scoops wrong. She has been throwing up all morning and now she is doing this twitching thing.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Heart rate is up around 200, her soft spot looks sunken, and she has had a couple more jerking episodes since we got her on the monitor.

cases.title: The painless blue scrotal lump in a newborn (the one everyone calls a hydrocele)
cases.chiefComplaint: 3-week-old male, firm bluish scrotal swelling found by mother
cases.diagnosis: Left-sided neonatal (extravaginal) testicular torsion, postnatal-onset and still potentially salvageable, mistaken by the family for a hydrocele
cases.context: This 3-week-old boy is brought in because his mother noticed his left scrotum looked swollen and dusky-blue during a diaper change this morning; he fed and slept normally overnight and is not obviously fussy on exam. The true diagnosis is extravaginal testicular torsion of the left testis -- the neonatal/infantile form of spermatic cord torsion, mechanistically distinct from the intravaginal 'bell-clapper' torsion of adolescents. In neonates the tunica vaginalis has not yet fixed to the scrotal wall, so the entire cord-and-tunica complex twists together above the tunica rather than the testis twisting within a fixed sac. Torsion has a bimodal age distribution -- one peak under a year of age, the other around puberty -- and this case sits squarely in the younger peak. Onset here is presumed postnatal (a new finding on a baby who was normal at birth and at his 2-week check), which is the critical branch point: postnatal-onset neonatal torsion is a true surgical emergency with a real, time-dependent chance of testicular salvage, whereas prenatal (in-utero) torsion typically presents at birth with a testis that is already infarcted and non-salvageable, making that scenario more urgent for consultation but less urgent for the OR. Competing diagnoses the learner must actively exclude: a communicating hydrocele (soft, transilluminates, fluctuates with Valsalva/crying, non-tender), an incarcerated inguinal hernia (may have bowel loops on exam/ultrasound, associated vomiting or a history of intermittent groin bulge), and epididymo-orchitis (rare at this age, usually with overlying erythema/warmth and possible urinary source). The classic diagnostic trap in this age group is that the baby often looks deceptively comfortable -- minimally fussy, feeding well, afebrile -- because neonates localize and express pain poorly, and the cremasteric reflex (relied on heavily in older boys) is physiologically unreliable or absent in normal newborns, so its absence here proves nothing. Exam: left hemiscrotum firm, enlarged (roughly 2x the right), with a dusky blue-purple discoloration of the overlying skin, non-transilluminating, mildly tender to palpation, testis not clearly separable from a thickened cord; right hemiscrotum and testis normal, no inguinal bulge, no bowel sounds in the scrotum, abdomen soft, no fever. If unrecognized or dismissed as a hydrocele/hernia, the delay converts a salvageable postnatal torsion into testicular loss, mirroring the outcome of prenatal torsion, with eventual atrophy and possible orchiectomy; the evidence-based approach is immediate, emergent urology/pediatric surgery consultation and prompt surgical exploration (ultrasound with Doppler can support but should never delay definitive surgical decision-making), since outcome depends heavily on time elapsed from onset of the torsion.
criticalActions: Recognize a firm, discolored, minimally tender scrotal mass in a neonate as testicular torsion until proven otherwise -- do not anchor on hydrocele or hernia
Consult urology or pediatric surgery emergently rather than deferring to outpatient follow-up
Obtain scrotal ultrasound with Doppler only if it will not delay surgical consultation or exploration
Distinguish prenatal (in-utero) from postnatal onset, since this changes salvage likelihood and urgency of exploration
Counsel the family that testicular salvage is time-dependent and often unattainable if torsion occurred prenatally
Discuss surgical exploration and consider contralateral testicular fixation to prevent future asynchronous torsion of the remaining testis
learningPoints: Testicular torsion has a bimodal age distribution -- one peak in the first year of life, the other around puberty -- and the neonatal form is a distinct entity from adolescent torsion.
Neonatal torsion is extravaginal: the spermatic cord and tunica vaginalis twist together above an unfixed tunica, unlike the intravaginal 'bell-clapper' torsion of older boys.
A firm, non-tender or minimally tender, discolored scrotal mass in a newborn is the classic presentation and is easily mistaken for a hydrocele or incarcerated hernia.

Prenatal (in-utero) torsion usually presents with an already-infarcted, non-salvageable testis, while postnatal-onset neonatal torsion is a true surgical emergency with a real chance of salvage if acted on urgently.
The cremasteric reflex is physiologically unreliable in neonates and should not be used to reassure against torsion the way it can be in adolescents.
cases.intro: A tired-looking mother sits in the exam room with her swaddled three-week-old, unwrapping his diaper to show you what she found this morning. The baby is quiet, feeding on demand, and doesn't seem to be in obvious distress, but one side of his scrotum looks swollen and a little bluish compared to the other. She wonders if he just 'slept funny' or if this is something he was born with that's only now gotten your attention.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: He's been eating fine and sleeping fine, he's not even crying about it, so I almost didn't bring him in. I only noticed it this morning when I changed him.

speech.speaker: nurse
speech.text: Vitals are all normal for his age, he's afebrile, and he settled right down once we got him unwrapped. Mom says nothing like this happened at birth, he looked totally normal at his two-week checkup.

speech.speaker: family
speech.text: Is this just one of those hernias or a fluid thing? My sister said her baby had something like that and it went away on its own.
